topics.title: Overview of Machine Learning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Machine Learning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Machine Learning (ML)** is a sub-field of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Computer Science that focuses on developing algorithms and statistical models which enable computer systems to *learn* from data, identify patterns, and make decisions or predictions **without being explicitly programmed** for every specific task.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Canonical Definitions (Mandatory for 2/3 Mark Answers):**
> 
> **Arthur Samuel (1959):** *"Field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed."*
> 
> **Tom Mitchell (1997):** *"A computer program is said to learn from experience **E** with respect to some class of tasks **T** and performance measure **P**, if its performance at tasks in **T**, as measured by **P**, improves with experience **E**."*

In Mitchell's formulation, every ML problem can be rigorously decomposed into three orthogonal components:

- **Task (T):** The objective the system must accomplish (e.g., classification, regression, clustering, control).
- **Experience (E):** The dataset of observations available for learning (e.g., labelled images, transaction records, agent-environment interactions).
- **Performance (P):** A quantifiable metric to measure how well the task is performed (e.g., accuracy, F1-score, Mean Squared Error, cumulative reward).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Highlight:** Any KTU question asking *"Define machine learning"* will fetch **full marks ONLY** if the answer references **Mitchell's formal (T, P, E) definition** along with at least one concrete example. A generic layman definition will be penalised.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "How a Child Learns vs. How a Machine Learns"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Pump (Use this verbatim in your answers for 7-mark questions to score the 'explanation' component):**

Imagine teaching a young child to identify a **cat** versus a **dog**. You don't sit with the child and write down 10,000 rules such as *"if ear_shape = pointed AND tail_length < 15cm AND sound = 'meow' then cat."* Instead, you simply **show the child thousands of pictures** of cats and dogs, and say *"this is a cat," "this is a dog."* Over time, the child develops an internal mental model that can correctly classify a cat or dog it has **never seen before**.

**Machine Learning mimics this exact process:**

| Step in Child's Learning | Equivalent in Machine Learning |
|---|---|
| Show picture (input) | Feed feature vector $x \in \mathbb{R}^{d}$ to algorithm |
| Say "cat/dog" (teacher signal) | Provide label $y$ in the training set |
| Brain adjusts internal model | Algorithm updates internal parameters $\theta$ |
| New unseen picture (test) | Evaluate model on a held-out test set |
| Child correctly identifies it | Model outputs prediction $\hat{y}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The phrase **"without being explicitly programmed"** does **not** mean no code is written — it means **the rules are inferred from data rather than hand-crafted by a human programmer**.

## 1.3 Why Machine Learning? The Motivation

Traditional rule-based programming fails when:

1. The logic is too complex to manually specify (e.g., recognising speech in noisy environments).
2. The data is high-dimensional and multi-modal (e.g., medical imaging with millions of pixels).
3. The environment is dynamic and the rules evolve over time (e.g., stock market prediction, fraud detection).
4. Human expertise is scarce or expensive to encode (e.g., rare disease diagnosis).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foundational Premise of ML:** A pattern exists in the data, we cannot pin it down mathematically, **but** we have enough samples to approximate it statistically. The **Bias-Variance tradeoff** and the **No Free Lunch theorem** govern this approximation.

## 1.4 Key Terminology (Bold = Frequently Asked 2-Mark Glossary)

- **Dataset:** A collection of examples used for learning.
- **Instance / Sample / Example:** A single observation (one row of the dataset).
- **Feature (Attribute):** An individual measurable property of a phenomenon being observed.
- **Label (Target):** The known output or answer corresponding to a sample (in supervised learning).
- **Model (Hypothesis):** The learned function $h : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$.
- **Training Set:** $\mathcal{D}_{train} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$ used to fit parameters.
- **Test Set:** $\mathcal{D}_{test}$ used to estimate generalisation performance.
- **Generalisation:** Ability of a model to perform well on **unseen** data.
- **Overfitting:** Model memorises training data (high variance, low bias).
- **Underfitting:** Model is too simple to capture the pattern (high bias, low variance).
- **Feature Space ($\mathcal{X}$):** The multi-dimensional space in which input vectors reside.
- **Hypothesis Space ($\mathcal{H}$):** The set of all candidate functions the algorithm may select from.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mitchell's (T, P, E) Framework — Expanded

Every ML problem must be reduced to the **(T, P, E)** triplet. The KTU examiner may deliberately give a real-world scenario and ask you to identify these three. Memorise the pattern.

| Element | Symbol | Question It Answers | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Task** | $T$ | *"What is the system supposed to do?"* | Classification, regression, clustering, translation, game-playing |
| **Experience** | $E$ | *"What data does the system learn from?"* | Labelled images, gameplay traces, transaction logs |
| **Performance** | $P$ | *"How is success quantified?"* | Accuracy, MSE, F1, reward, log-loss |

**Worked Example for KTU-style answer:**

> *"A system is fed 50,000 emails marked 'spam' or 'not spam'. It must categorise new incoming emails. Its accuracy is measured on a separate set of 10,000 emails."*

- $T$ = Classify email as spam / not-spam
- $E$ = Database of 50,000 pre-classified emails
- $P$ = Classification accuracy on 10,000 held-out emails

## 2.2 The Five Pillars of a Learning System (Turban's Architecture)

A complete ML pipeline, as per KTU's "Overview of ML" syllabus, consists of five sequential pillars. Examiners love flow-chart questions on this.

1. **Data Acquisition & Curation** — Collection, cleaning, deduplication, anonymisation.
2. **Feature Engineering & Representation** — Selection, extraction, scaling, encoding.
3. **Model Selection & Training** — Choose hypothesis class $\mathcal{H}$; optimise parameters $\theta$.
4. **Evaluation & Validation** — k-fold cross-validation, confusion matrix, ROC-AUC.
5. **Deployment & Monitoring** — Inference at scale, model drift detection, retraining triggers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **quality and quantity of data** typically determines the upper bound of model performance — *better data beats better algorithms.* This is an often-repeated 2-mark KTU question.

## 2.3 Taxonomy of Machine Learning (KTU Mandatory Diagram)

ML algorithms are broadly classified into four paradigms based on the **nature of the feedback signal** available during training.

### A. Supervised Learning
- **Idea:** Learn a mapping $f : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ from labelled pairs $(x_i, y_i)$.
- **Sub-types:**
  - *Classification* — discrete label $y \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$.
  - *Regression* — continuous label $y \in \mathbb{R}$.
- **Canonical algorithms:** Linear/Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, SVM, k-NN, Random Forest, Neural Networks.

### B. Unsupervised Learning
- **Idea:** Discover hidden structure in unlabelled data $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$.
- **Sub-types:**
  - *Clustering* (K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical)
  - *Dimensionality Reduction* (PCA, t-SNE, Autoencoders)
  - *Association Rule Mining* (Apriori, FP-Growth)

### C. Semi-Supervised Learning
- **Idea:** A small labelled set + a large unlabelled set. Leverage unlabelled data to improve the decision boundary.
- **Application:** Medical imaging (few expert annotations + many scans).

### D. Reinforcement Learning (RL)
- **Idea:** An *agent* interacts with an *environment*, takes *actions*, and receives *rewards*. Learns a **policy** $\pi(a \vert s)$ to maximise cumulative reward.
- **Key concepts:** State, action, reward, episode, exploration vs. exploitation, Markov Decision Process (MDP).
- **Application:** AlphaGo, autonomous driving, robotics, recommender systems.

### E. Self-Supervised & Representation Learning (Modern Addition)
- Learns representations by predicting one part of the input from another. Foundation of LLMs (GPT, BERT).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following equations are the mathematical foundation of the "Overview of ML" module. Master them for both 3-mark and 14-mark questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notation Explained |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mitchell's Task | $T : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ | Input space $\mathcal{X}$, output space $\mathcal{Y}$ |
| 2 | Loss Function (0-1) | $L(\hat{y}, y) = \mathbb{I}[\hat{y} \neq y]$ | Indicator loss for classification |
| 3 | Squared Error Loss | $L(\hat{y}, y) = (\hat{y} - y)^2$ | Standard regression loss |
| 4 | Empirical Risk | $\hat{R}(h) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L(h(x_i), y_i)$ | Average loss on training data of size $n$ |
| 5 | Expected Risk | $R(h) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim \mathcal{D}}[L(h(x), y)]$ | True loss over the data distribution $\mathcal{D}$ |
| 6 | Empirical Risk Minimisation (ERM) | $h^{*} = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \hat{R}(h)$ | Select hypothesis minimising training error |
| 7 | Generalisation Bound (PAC) | $R(h) \leq \hat{R}(h) + \sqrt{\frac{\ln \vert \mathcal{H} \vert + \ln(2/\delta)}{2n}}$ | $\delta$ = confidence, $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert$ = hypothesis size |
| 8 | VC Generalisation Bound | $R(h) \leq \hat{R}(h) + \sqrt{\frac{d \ln(2n/d) + \ln(2/\delta)}{n}}$ | $d$ = VC dimension of $\mathcal{H}$ |
| 9 | Bias-Variance Decomposition | $\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | $\sigma^2$ = irreducible noise |
| 10 | Bayes Optimal Classifier | $y^{*} = \arg\max_{y} P(y \vert x)$ | Theoretically best possible classifier |
| 11 | Bayes Error Rate | $\epsilon^{*} = 1 - \mathbb{E}_x[\max_y P(y \vert x)]$ | Irreducible error floor |
| 12 | No Free Lunch | $\sum_{f} \text{Error}(L_a, f) = \sum_{f} \text{Error}(L_b, f)$ | No algorithm is universally superior |
| 13 | Learning Curve | $\text{Error} = f(n)$ where $n$ = training size | Monotonically decreasing in well-behaved cases |
| 14 | Sample Complexity | $n \geq O\left(\frac{VC(\mathcal{H}) + \ln(1/\delta)}{\epsilon^2}\right)$ | Samples needed for error $\leq \epsilon$ |
| 15 | Occam's Razor in ML | Prefer the simplest $h \in \mathcal{H}$ consistent with data | Foundation of regularisation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These equations are not abstract. Equation 6 is what *every* gradient-descent optimiser (SGD, Adam) actually solves. Equation 7 is the theoretical basis of why *more data* reduces error. Equation 9 is why *ensembling* (Random Forest, boosting) reduces variance.

## 2.5 The Learning Process — A Step-by-Step Operational View

1. **Define the problem (T).** Specify the input/output spaces and the success metric (P).
2. **Gather & label data (E).** The more representative, the better.
3. **Choose a hypothesis class $\mathcal{H}$.** Linear, tree-based, kernel, deep, etc.
4. **Define a loss function $L$.** Encodes the cost of a wrong prediction.
5. **Minimise empirical risk** $\hat{R}(h)$ using an optimisation algorithm.
6. **Evaluate on held-out data** to estimate generalisation.
7. **Iterate:** Tune hyperparameters, engineer features, augment data.
8. **Deploy and monitor** for distribution shift and model decay.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse **data** with **information**. Data is raw, information is processed and labelled. The *quality* of labels is more important than raw volume — a fact often tested via short conceptual questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate Active:** No step is skipped. Every algebraic transition is explicitly written.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Empirical Risk and Expected Risk Relationship

The central goal of ML is to find a hypothesis $h \in \mathcal{H}$ that minimises the **expected risk** $R(h)$ over the true (unknown) data distribution $\mathcal{D}$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Expected Risk**

The expected risk (also called generalisation error or true error) is defined as the expectation of the loss function over the joint distribution $\mathcal{D}$:

$$
R(h) = \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim \mathcal{D}} \left[ L(h(x), y) \right]
$$

Written out explicitly as an integral:

$$
R(h) = \int_{\mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{Y}} L(h(x), y) \cdot p(x, y) \, dx \, dy
$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Empirical Risk**

Since $p(x, y)$ is unknown, we approximate the integral by a finite average over a sample of $n$ i.i.d. points $\mathcal{D}_{train} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$:

$$
\hat{R}(h) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L(h(x_i), y_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Relationship by the Law of Large Numbers**

As $n \rightarrow \infty$, the empirical risk converges to the expected risk for a **fixed** hypothesis $h$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \hat{R}(h) = R(h)
$$

**Step 4 — Generalisation Bound (Hoeffding's Inequality applied to finite $\mathcal{H}$)**

For a **single** hypothesis $h$ and any $\delta \in (0, 1)$, with probability at least $1 - \delta$:

$$
R(h) \leq \hat{R}(h) + \sqrt{\frac{\ln(2/\delta)}{2n}}
$$

*Derivation Insight:* This is obtained by applying Hoeffding's inequality to each loss (bounded in $[0, 1]$) and then using a Union Bound over all $h \in \mathcal{H}$ (whose cardinality is $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert$):

$$
\mathbb{P}\left( \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \vert R(h) - \hat{R}(h) \vert > \epsilon \right) \leq 2 \vert \mathcal{H} \vert e^{-2n\epsilon^{2}}
$$

Setting the right-hand side to $\delta$ and solving for $\epsilon$ gives:

$$
\epsilon = \sqrt{\frac{\ln \vert \mathcal{H} \vert + \ln(2/\delta)}{2n}}
$$

Hence the generalisation bound in **Formula 7** of the cheat sheet.

**Step 5 — VC Bound (infinite $\mathcal{H}$)**

For infinite hypothesis classes, the growth function $\Pi_{\mathcal{H}}(n)$ or VC dimension $d$ replaces $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert$, leading to Formula 8.

## 3.2 Derivation of Bias-Variance Decomposition

This is the **single most-tested derivation in the KTU 2024 Module-1 syllabus**. Memorise it.

**Step 1 — Set up the regression model**

Assume the true relationship is:

$$
y = f(x) + \varepsilon, \quad \text{where} \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})
$$

The learning algorithm produces an estimate $\hat{f}(x)$ from training data. We want to compute the expected squared prediction error at a point $x$:

$$
\text{Err}(x) = \mathbb{E}\left[ (y - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Expand the square**

$$
\text{Err}(x) = \mathbb{E}\left[ (f(x) + \varepsilon - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right]
$$

$$
= \mathbb{E}\left[ (f(x) - \hat{f}(x) + \varepsilon)^{2} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Distribute the product**

$$
= \mathbb{E}\left[ (f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right] + 2 \mathbb{E}\left[ (f(x) - \hat{f}(x))\varepsilon \right] + \mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^{2}]
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each term**

*Term 1:* The cross-term vanishes because $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon] = 0$ and $\varepsilon$ is independent of $\hat{f}(x)$:

$$
2 \mathbb{E}\left[ (f(x) - \hat{f}(x))\varepsilon \right] = 0
$$

*Term 2:* $\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon^{2}] = \text{Var}(\varepsilon) = \sigma^{2}$ (irreducible noise).

*Term 3:* Decompose $f(x) - \hat{f}(x) = f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] - \hat{f}(x)$ and recognise the variance identity.

**Step 5 — Apply the variance decomposition identity**

For any random variable $Z$ with mean $\mu$:

$$
\mathbb{E}[(Z - c)^{2}] = \mathbb{E}[(Z - \mu)^{2}] + (\mu - c)^{2} = \text{Var}(Z) + \text{Bias}^{2}(c)
$$

Setting $Z = \hat{f}(x)$, $\mu = \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]$, $c = f(x)$:

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[ (f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right] = \underbrace{\left( f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] \right)^{2}}_{\text{Bias}^{2}} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[ (\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^{2} \right]}_{\text{Variance}}
$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the final decomposition**

$$
\boxed{\text{Err}(x) = \text{Bias}^{2}(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Variance}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^{2}}
$$

This is **Formula 9** from the cheat sheet. KTU examiners award full marks ONLY if all three terms are present and the irreducible noise is correctly identified.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Four Learning Paradigms

| Dimension | Supervised | Unsupervised | Semi-Supervised | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Feedback type** | Label $y$ given | No labels | Few labels + many unlabelled | Scalar reward signal |
| **Goal** | Learn $f : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ | Learn structure of $p(x)$ | Improve $f$ using extra unlabelled data | Maximise $\sum \gamma^{t} r_{t}$ |
| **Performance metric (P)** | Accuracy, MSE, F1 | Silhouette score, reconstruction error | Semi-supervised accuracy | Cumulative reward |
| **Data requirement** | Labelled (expensive) | Unlabelled (cheap) | Mixed | Environment simulator |
| **Canonical algorithm** | SVM, Neural Nets | K-Means, PCA | Self-training, Co-training | Q-Learning, DQN, PPO |
| **Risk of failure** | Overfitting to labels | Discovering spurious structure | Confirmation bias on labels | Reward hacking, unsafe exploration |
| **KTU 2024 example question** | Spam detection | Customer segmentation | Medical diagnosis with few labels | Game-playing AI (Atari, Go) |

## 3.4 Derivation of the No Free Lunch (NFL) Theorem (Conceptual)

Wolpert's NFL theorem states that, averaged over **all possible data distributions**, every classification algorithm has the same expected error. Symbolically:

$$
\sum_{f} \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}} \left[ R(L_{a} \vert \mathcal{D}, f) \right] = \sum_{f} \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}} \left[ R(L_{b} \vert \mathcal{D}, f) \right]
$$

**Engineering Implication:** *No* algorithm is universally superior. The choice of algorithm must be **informed by domain knowledge and prior assumptions** about the data distribution — which is exactly the inductive bias an ML engineer injects.

## 3.5 Sample Complexity Bound (Derivation Outline)

From the VC bound (Formula 8), set $R(h) - \hat{R}(h) \leq \epsilon$ with confidence $1 - \delta$:

$$
\epsilon = \sqrt{\frac{d \ln(2n/d) + \ln(2/\delta)}{n}}
$$

Solving for $n$ (ignoring log terms for order of magnitude):

$$
n = O\left( \frac{d}{\epsilon^{2}} \right)
$$

This is **Formula 14**. The bound confirms the intuitive rule: *sample size grows linearly with model complexity $d$ and quadratically with the desired precision $1/\epsilon$.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Generic Machine Learning Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Problem Definition T P E] --> B0[Data Collection]
    B0 --> C0[Data Cleaning and Preprocessing]
    C0 --> D0[Feature Engineering]
    D0 --> E0[Train Validation Test Split]
    E0 --> F0[Model Selection H]
    F0 --> G0[Model Training minimise R hat]
    G0 --> H0[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    H0 --> I0[Test Set Evaluation]
    I0 --> J0{Generalisation OK?}
    J0 -->|Yes| K0[Deployment]
    J0 -->|No| F0
    K0 --> L0[Monitor and Retrain]
    L0 --> H0
```

## 4.2 Taxonomy of Machine Learning Paradigms (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Machine Learning Paradigms] --> sup[Supervised Learning]
    root --> unsup[Unsupervised Learning]
    root --> semi[Semi Supervised Learning]
    root --> rl[Reinforcement Learning]
    root --> ssl[Self Supervised Learning]

    sup --> sup1[Classification discrete Y]
    sup --> sup2[Regression continuous Y]

    unsup --> unsup1[Clustering KMeans DBSCAN]
    unsup --> unsup2[Dimensionality Reduction PCA tSNE]
    unsup --> unsup3[Association Apriori]

    semi --> semi1[Self Training]
    semi --> semi2[Co Training]
    semi --> semi3[Graph Based Methods]

    rl --> rl1[Value Based Q Learning DQN]
    rl --> rl2[Policy Based REINFORCE PPO]
    rl --> rl3[Actor Critic A2C A3C]

    ssl --> ssl1[Contrastive SimCLR MoCo]
    ssl --> ssl2[Generative BERT GPT MAE]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Learning System (Turban's Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Performance Module P] --> G0[Generalisation Evaluator]
    E0[Experience Module E] --> G0
    T0[Task Module T] --> G0
    G0 --> H0[Hypothesis Space H]
    H0 --> O0[Optimiser gradient descent]
    O0 --> M0[Trained Model h star]
    M0 --> D0[Deployment Engine]
    D0 --> P0
```

## 4.4 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff (Mermaid Visualisation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Model Complexity axis] --> B[Error axis]
    B --> B1[Underfitting region high bias low variance]
    B --> B2[Optimal region sweet spot]
    B --> B3[Overfitting region low bias high variance]
    B1 --> C[Total error decreases]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Generalisation gap increases beyond optimum]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define Machine Learning. State and explain Tom Mitchell's formal definition with one example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Machine Learning is the study of algorithms that improve their performance at a task through experience, without being explicitly programmed.

Tom Mitchell (1997) defined: *"A computer program learns from experience **E** with respect to task **T** and performance measure **P** if its performance at **T**, measured by **P**, improves with **E**."*

**Example:** A spam filter
- $T$: Classify email as spam or not-spam
- $E$: Database of 50,000 pre-labelled emails
- $P$: Classification accuracy on unseen test set

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Writing *only* Arthur Samuel's definition fetches **1 out of 3 marks** at most. KTU 2024 explicitly tests the **(T, P, E)** triplet. Always state *all three* with an example.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised learning. Give one algorithm and one application for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|---|---|---|
| Labels | Uses labelled data $(x, y)$ | Uses unlabelled data $x$ only |
| Goal | Learn mapping to known $y$ | Discover hidden structure |
| Algorithm | K-Nearest Neighbours | K-Means |
| Application | Handwritten digit recognition (MNIST) | Customer segmentation in marketing |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse semi-supervised with supervised. Many students write *semi-supervised* examples for *supervised* questions and lose **1 mark**.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]** 
(a) Explain the five main components of a learning system with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)
(b) Derive the Bias-Variance decomposition for the squared error loss. Explain how this decomposition is used in practice to choose an optimal model. (7 Marks)

#### Solution A(a) — Five Components of a Learning System (7 Marks)

The five canonical components of an ML system (per Turban & Aronson, KTU-recommended reference framework) are:

1. **Data / Experience (E):** Raw and processed data on which learning occurs. Includes training, validation, and test splits.
2. **Feature Extractor / Representation Module:** Transforms raw data into a feature vector $x \in \mathcal{X}$ suitable for the algorithm.
3. **Model / Hypothesis Space ($\mathcal{H}$):** The set of candidate functions $h : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$.
4. **Learning Algorithm / Optimiser:** Searches $\mathcal{H}$ to find $h^{*} = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \hat{R}(h)$.
5. **Performance / Evaluation Module (P):** Quantifies how well the chosen $h$ generalises using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1, MSE, or AUC.

**Block Diagram (recap from Section 4.3):** See the Mermaid block diagram provided in Section 4.3 above.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for 7 marks:** Always *name* the 5 components, *explain* each in 1–2 lines, and *conclude* with a 1-line statement on how they interlock to form a closed-loop system.

#### Solution A(b) — Derivation of Bias-Variance Decomposition (7 Marks)

The derivation is presented **step-by-step** as already derived in Section 3.2. Re-state compactly for the answer script:

**Step 1:** Define the model $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$, $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2})$.

**Step 2:** Expected squared error at point $x$:

$$
\text{Err}(x) = \mathbb{E}\left[ (y - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Expand using $y = f(x) + \varepsilon$:

$$
= \mathbb{E}\left[ (f(x) - \hat{f}(x) + \varepsilon)^{2} \right]
$$

**Step 4:** Distribute the product. The cross-term $\mathbb{E}[(f - \hat{f})\varepsilon] = 0$ because $\varepsilon$ is zero-mean and independent.

**Step 5:** Apply the variance identity $E[(Z - c)^{2}] = \text{Var}(Z) + (E[Z] - c)^{2}$ to $Z = \hat{f}(x)$, $c = f(x)$:

$$
\mathbb{E}\left[ (f(x) - \hat{f}(x))^{2} \right] = \underbrace{\left( f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)] \right)^{2}}_{\text{Bias}^{2}(\hat{f}(x))} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[ (\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)])^{2} \right]}_{\text{Var}(\hat{f}(x))}
$$

**Step 6:** Final assembled form:

$$
\boxed{\text{Err}(x) = \text{Bias}^{2}(\hat{f}(x)) + \text{Variance}(\hat{f}(x)) + \sigma^{2}}
$$

**Practical Use (to fetch the last 2 marks):**

- As model complexity $\uparrow$, **Bias** $\downarrow$ and **Variance** $\uparrow$.
- The optimal model is at the point where their sum is **minimised**.
- Regularisation (L1/L2) and ensembling (bagging, boosting) are practical mechanisms to navigate this tradeoff.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — 3 marks commonly lost:**
> 1. Forgetting the **irreducible noise $\sigma^{2}$** term.
> 2. Writing Bias instead of Bias² in the final equation.
> 3. Failing to interpret the formula *practically* (e.g., explaining how regularisation or bagging reduces variance).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Suggested Model]** 
(a) Explain the different types of machine learning paradigms with suitable examples and algorithms for each. (7 Marks)
(b) What is Empirical Risk Minimisation? Derive the generalisation bound using Hoeffding's inequality for a finite hypothesis class. (7 Marks)

#### Solution B(a) — Types of Machine Learning Paradigms (7 Marks)

**1. Supervised Learning** — Learns from labelled data $(x_i, y_i)$. The algorithm maps an input to a known output.
*Example:* Email spam classification.
*Algorithms:* Linear Regression, Logistic Regression, SVM, Random Forest, Deep Neural Networks.

**2. Unsupervised Learning** — Learns structure from unlabelled data $\{x_i\}$.
*Example:* Grouping customers by purchasing behaviour.
*Algorithms:* K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical Clustering, PCA, Autoencoders.

**3. Semi-Supervised Learning** — Combines a small labelled set with a large unlabelled set. The unlabelled data regularises the decision boundary.
*Example:* Medical image diagnosis with few expert annotations.
*Algorithms:* Self-training, Co-training, Graph-based label propagation.

**4. Reinforcement Learning (RL)** — An agent learns a policy by interacting with an environment, receiving rewards.
*Example:* AlphaGo, autonomous driving.
*Algorithms:* Q-Learning, SARSA, DQN, PPO, A3C.

**5. Self-Supervised Learning (Modern)** — Predicts part of the input from another part; foundation of foundation models.
*Example:* GPT, BERT.
*Algorithms:* Contrastive (SimCLR), Masked (BERT, MAE).

> [!NOTE]
> For full marks, draw a neat comparative table (refer Section 3.3).

#### Solution B(b) — Empirical Risk Minimisation & Hoeffding Bound (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — ERM Definition**

Given training data $\mathcal{D}_{train} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$, the empirical risk of a hypothesis $h$ is:

$$
\hat{R}(h) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L(h(x_i), y_i)
$$

ERM selects:

$$
h^{*} = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \hat{R}(h)
$$

**Step 2 — Hoeffding's Inequality**

For $n$ i.i.d. random variables $Z_i \in [a, b]$ with mean $\mathbb{E}[Z_i] = \mu$:

$$
\mathbb{P}\left( \left\vert \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Z_i - \mu \right\vert \geq \epsilon \right) \leq 2 e^{-\frac{2n\epsilon^{2}}{(b - a)^{2}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to Each Loss**

Each loss $L_i = L(h(x_i), y_i)$ is bounded in $[0, 1]$. Treating $L_i$ as $Z_i$ and the true expected loss $R(h)$ as $\mu$:

$$
\mathbb{P}\left( \vert \hat{R}(h) - R(h) \vert \geq \epsilon \right) \leq 2 e^{-2n\epsilon^{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Union Bound over $\mathcal{H}$**

Applying the union bound over all $h \in \mathcal{H}$ (with $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert$ hypotheses):

$$
\mathbb{P}\left( \sup_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \vert \hat{R}(h) - R(h) \vert \geq \epsilon \right) \leq 2 \vert \mathcal{H} \vert e^{-2n\epsilon^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\epsilon$**

Set the RHS equal to $\delta$ and solve:

$$
\epsilon = \sqrt{\frac{\ln \vert \mathcal{H} \vert + \ln(2/\delta)}{2n}}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Generalisation Bound**

$$
\boxed{R(h) \leq \hat{R}(h) + \sqrt{\frac{\ln \vert \mathcal{H} \vert + \ln(2/\delta)}{2n}}}
$$

**Interpretation:** *More data ($n \uparrow$) tightens the bound. Larger $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert$ loosens it — supporting Occam's razor.*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often forget the **union bound** step, losing 2 marks.
> 2. Many write $\delta$ on the wrong side of the inequality.
> 3. Failing to provide an *interpretation* of the bound loses 1 mark.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning Summary (Module-1 Specific)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU students LOSE marks in the 'Overview of ML' module:**
> 1. **Definition Half-Recall:** Writing only Samuel's or only Mitchell's definition. **Always write BOTH.**
> 2. **Missing the (T, P, E) Triplet:** Scenarios given in questions demand you identify *all three* explicitly.
> 3. **Bias-Variance Forgetfulness:** Forgetting $\sigma^{2}$ (irreducible noise) in the final equation = **−1 mark guaranteed**.
> 4. **NFL Confusion:** Mixing up the No Free Lunch theorem with the Bias-Variance tradeoff.
> 5. **No Diagram in 14-mark answers:** A block diagram of the ML pipeline or Turban's 5-component architecture adds 1–2 marks for visual aid.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Two canonical definitions:** Samuel (1959) and Mitchell (1997). Always cite both.
- [ ] **Mitchell's (T, P, E) triplet:** Task, Performance, Experience — must be identified in every scenario.
- [ ] **Four (now five) paradigms:** Supervised, Unsupervised, Semi-supervised, Reinforcement (+ Self-supervised).
- [ ] **ERM formula:** $\hat{R}(h) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} L(h(x_i), y_i)$.
- [ ] **Hoeffding + Union Bound = PAC Generalisation Bound** for finite $\mathcal{H}$.
- [ ] **VC Bound** is the generalisation for infinite $\mathcal{H}$, parameterised by VC dimension $d$.
- [ ] **Bias-Variance:** $\text{Err}(x) = \text{Bias}^{2} + \text{Variance} + \sigma^{2}$ — never forget $\sigma^{2}$.
- [ ] **No Free Lunch Theorem:** No algorithm is universally best; prior knowledge is essential.
- [ ] **Inductive bias:** Every algorithm embeds assumptions (linearity, smoothness, symmetry, etc.).
- [ ] **Sample complexity:** $n = O(d/\epsilon^{2})$ — grows linearly with model complexity.
- [ ] **Occam's Razor:** Prefer simpler hypotheses; the basis of L1/L2 regularisation.
- [ ] **Overfitting vs. Underfitting:** Diagnose via learning curves (train vs. validation error).
- [ ] **Turban's 5 components:** Data, Feature extractor, Model/Hypothesis, Optimiser, Evaluator.
- [ ] **Bayes Optimal Classifier:** $y^{*} = \arg\max_{y} P(y \vert x)$ — the theoretical ceiling.
- [ ] **KTU's most-asked sub-topic:** *Bias-Variance decomposition derivation* — practise writing it blind.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of Machine Learning Applications, Machine Learning Paradigms - Supervised
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Examples of Machine Learning Applications, Machine Learning Paradigms - Supervised

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Examples of Machine Learning Applications

### Formal Definition
**Machine Learning (ML)** is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence (AI) that enables systems to automatically learn patterns, relationships, and decision rules from data, and improve their performance over time **without being explicitly programmed**. According to Arthur Samuel (1959), ML is the "field of study that gives computers the ability to learn without being explicitly programmed." Tom Mitchell (1997) formally defined a learning problem as: *"A computer program is said to learn from experience E with respect to some class of tasks T and performance measure P, if its performance at tasks in T, as measured by P, improves with experience E."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** This topic builds the foundation for understanding *why* ML is needed and *where* it is applied. Students must be able to categorize real-world applications into the correct ML paradigm (Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement, Semi-Supervised).

### Real-World Application Domains (Domain-Wise Classification)

| Domain | Application | ML Task Type |
|---|---|---|
| **Healthcare** | Cancer detection from X-ray/MRI images, drug discovery, patient risk prediction | Classification, Regression |
| **Finance** | Credit card fraud detection, stock price prediction, loan default prediction | Classification, Regression |
| **Transportation** | Self-driving cars (Tesla, Waymo), route optimization, traffic prediction | Regression, Reinforcement |
| **E-Commerce** | Product recommendation (Amazon, Flipkart), customer churn prediction | Collaborative Filtering, Classification |
| **Natural Language Processing** | Google Translate, ChatGPT, Sentiment Analysis, Spam Filtering | Classification, Sequence Models |
| **Computer Vision** | Face Recognition (Facebook tagging), OCR, Object Detection (YOLO) | Classification, Detection |
| **Speech & Audio** | Siri, Alexa, Google Assistant, Speech-to-Text | Sequence-to-Sequence |
| **Agriculture** | Crop disease detection, yield prediction, soil analysis | Classification, Regression |
| **Cybersecurity** | Intrusion detection, malware classification, phishing URL detection | Anomaly Detection |
| **Manufacturing** | Predictive maintenance, quality control, defect detection | Regression, Classification |

### Conceptual Analogy — "How a Child Learns"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine teaching a child to recognize apples vs. oranges.
> 1. You **show** the child many examples (training data) — "This is an apple (red, round), this is an orange (orange, round)."
> 2. The child **generalizes** the pattern — "Apples are usually red."
> 3. When shown a **new** fruit, the child **predicts** correctly based on prior learning.
>
> This is exactly how supervised ML works! The child is the *model*, the examples are *labeled data*, and the prediction is the *output*.

### Examples of Landmark ML Applications

**1. Email Spam Classification (Gmail)**
- Input: Email text + metadata (sender, subject)
- Output: Binary label — *Spam* or *Not Spam*
- Algorithm Used: Naive Bayes, SVM, or Deep Learning models.

**2. Medical Diagnosis (Skin Cancer Detection — Esteva et al., 2017, Nature)**
- Input: Dermoscopic image of skin lesion
- Output: Benign vs. Malignant classification
- Performance: Matched the accuracy of 21 board-certified dermatologists.

**3. AlphaGo (DeepMind, 2016)**
- Beat the world champion Lee Sedol in the game of Go.
- Used a combination of **Reinforcement Learning** + **Supervised Learning** from expert human moves.
- Demonstrated a landmark achievement of ML in strategic decision-making.

**4. Netflix Recommendation Engine**
- Inputs: Viewing history, ratings, time of day, device.
- Outputs: Ranked list of recommended movies.
- Uses: Collaborative Filtering + Deep Learning.

**5. Autonomous Vehicles (Waymo, Tesla)**
- Inputs: Camera feeds, LiDAR, GPS, radar.
- Outputs: Steering angle, acceleration, braking.
- ML Paradigm: Primarily **Supervised Learning** (object detection) combined with Reinforcement Learning (path planning).

---

## 1.2 Machine Learning Paradigms — Introduction

### The Four Main Paradigms

A **paradigm** in ML refers to the *style* or *approach* of learning from data. The choice of paradigm depends on the **type of data available** (labeled vs. unlabeled) and the **task** to be solved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** The course emphasizes **Supervised Learning** as the primary paradigm. Unsupervised, Semi-Supervised, and Reinforcement Learning are typically covered in later modules.

| Paradigm | Data Type | Goal | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Supervised Learning** | Labeled $(X, Y)$ | Learn mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$ | Spam detection, House price prediction |
| **Unsupervised Learning** | Unlabeled $(X)$ | Discover hidden structure | Customer segmentation, Clustering |
| **Semi-Supervised Learning** | Few labeled + many unlabeled | Leverage both | Web content classification |
| **Reinforcement Learning** | State, Action, Reward | Maximize cumulative reward | Game playing, Robotics |

---

## 1.3 Supervised Learning — Formal Definition

### Academic Definition
**Supervised Learning** is a machine learning paradigm in which a model is trained on a **labeled dataset** — meaning each training example is a pair $(x_i, y_i)$ where $x_i$ is the input (feature vector) and $y_i$ is the corresponding known output (label or target). The objective is to learn a **mapping function** (also called a *hypothesis function*) $h: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ that generalizes well to unseen data.

Formally, given a training set:
$$D = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_N, y_N)\}$$

The learning algorithm searches the **hypothesis space** $\mathcal{H}$ for a function $h^* \in \mathcal{H}$ that minimizes a predefined **loss function** $L(h(x), y)$:
$$h^* = \arg\min_{h \in \mathcal{H}} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(h(x_i), y_i)$$

### Key Terminology (KTU Must-Know)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Feature ($x$)** | An input variable (column) used for prediction. |
| **Label / Target ($y$)** | The output variable to be predicted. |
| **Training Set** | Subset of data used to train the model. |
| **Test Set** | Subset used to evaluate the trained model. |
| **Hypothesis Function ($h$)** | The function $h(x)$ representing the model's prediction. |
| **Loss Function ($L$)** | Measures the error between predicted $\hat{y}$ and true $y$. |
| **Model** | The learned function $h^*$ after training. |
| **Generalization** | Ability of the model to perform well on unseen data. |
| **Overfitting** | Model memorizes training data; performs poorly on test data. |
| **Underfitting** | Model is too simple; performs poorly on both train and test data. |

### Two Main Sub-Tasks of Supervised Learning

**A. Classification (Discrete Output)**
- Output $y$ belongs to a finite set of categories (e.g., {Spam, Not Spam}, {Cat, Dog, Bird}).
- Examples: Email spam detection, MNIST digit recognition, disease diagnosis.
- Algorithms: Logistic Regression, SVM, KNN, Decision Trees, Naive Bayes, Random Forest, Neural Networks.

**B. Regression (Continuous Output)**
- Output $y$ is a real-valued continuous number (e.g., house price, temperature, stock price).
- Examples: Predicting house prices, temperature forecasting, sales prediction.
- Algorithms: Linear Regression, Polynomial Regression, Ridge, Lasso, SVR, Decision Tree Regression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — Supervised Learning as a "Teacher-Student" Relationship:**
> - The **teacher** (supervisor) provides the student (model) with **questions (x)** and the **correct answers (y)**.
> - The student practices repeatedly on these examples.
> - Eventually, the student can answer **new, unseen questions** correctly — this is **generalization**.
> - The "grade" the student receives (loss function) tells them how far off their answers are, so they can improve.

### The Supervised Learning Workflow

$$\text{Data Collection} \rightarrow \text{Preprocessing} \rightarrow \text{Feature Engineering} \rightarrow \text{Train/Test Split} \rightarrow \text{Model Selection} \rightarrow \text{Training} \rightarrow \text{Evaluation} \rightarrow \text{Hyperparameter Tuning} \rightarrow \text{Deployment}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When asked to "explain supervised learning with an example," always include: (1) Definition with labeled data, (2) The mapping function $f: X \rightarrow Y$, (3) At least one Classification and one Regression example, and (4) Mention the loss function concept.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Framework of Supervised Learning

The core idea of supervised learning can be broken into **5 structured steps**:

### Step 1: Define the Hypothesis Space $\mathcal{H}$
We assume the unknown true function is of a certain form. For example, in **Linear Regression**, we assume:
$$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \ldots + \theta_d x_d = \theta^T x$$

where $\theta = (\theta_0, \theta_1, \ldots, \theta_d)$ are the **parameters (weights)** the algorithm must learn.

**Why this matters:** The choice of hypothesis space (linear, polynomial, decision tree, neural network) determines the model's expressive power. A linear hypothesis cannot fit a non-linear relationship — this is the **bias-variance trade-off**.

### Step 2: Define the Loss Function $L(\hat{y}, y)$
A loss function quantifies the error of a single prediction. Common choices:

| Loss Function | Formula | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **Squared Error (SE)** | $L = (y - \hat{y})^2$ | Regression |
| **Absolute Error (AE)** | $L = \vert y - \hat{y} \vert$ | Regression (robust to outliers) |
| **0-1 Loss** | $L = \mathbb{1}[\hat{y} \neq y]$ | Classification (theoretical) |
| **Hinge Loss** | $L = \max(0, 1 - y \cdot \hat{y})$ | SVM |
| **Log Loss / Cross-Entropy** | $L = -[y \log(\hat{y}) + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Logistic Regression |

### Step 3: Define the Cost Function $J(\theta)$ (Empirical Risk)
The cost function is the **average loss** over the entire training set:
$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(h_\theta(x_i), y_i)$$

The goal of training is to find the parameters $\theta^*$ that minimize $J(\theta)$:
$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta} J(\theta)$$

### Step 4: Optimization (Learning the Parameters)
We use an optimization algorithm to find $\theta^*$. The most common method is **Gradient Descent**:

$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate** (a hyperparameter).

**Why this matters:** The learning rate $\alpha$ controls how big a step we take in parameter space. Too large → diverges; too small → slow convergence.

### Step 5: Generalization to Unseen Data
After training, the model is evaluated on the **test set** using metrics like accuracy (classification) or RMSE (regression). The true measure of success is **low test error**, not low training error.

---

## 2.2 Linear Regression — A Detailed Walkthrough

**Problem Setup:** Predict a continuous target $y$ from features $x$.

**Model (Hypothesis):**
$$h_\theta(x) = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \ldots + \theta_d x_d$$

**Cost Function (Mean Squared Error):**
$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (h_\theta(x_i) - y_i)^2$$

**Closed-Form Solution (Normal Equation):**
$$\theta^* = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Normal Equation gives the exact solution in one step but is computationally expensive for large $d$. Gradient Descent is preferred when $d$ is large (e.g., $d > 10,000$).

**Gradient Descent Update Rule:**
$$\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (h_\theta(x_i) - y_i) x_{ij}$$

---

## 2.3 Logistic Regression — Classification Made Probabilistic

For binary classification ($y \in \{0, 1\}$), linear regression is not appropriate because predictions are unbounded. Logistic Regression uses the **sigmoid (logistic) function** to squash outputs into $[0, 1]$:

$$h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}$$

The output $h_\theta(x)$ is interpreted as $P(y=1 \mid x; \theta)$.

**Cost Function (Binary Cross-Entropy / Log Loss):**
$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(h_\theta(x_i)) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - h_\theta(x_i)) \right]$$

**Decision Rule:** Predict $\hat{y} = 1$ if $h_\theta(x) \geq 0.5$, else $\hat{y} = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Despite the name "Regression," Logistic Regression is a **Classification** algorithm. Examiners frequently test this.

---

## 2.4 Evaluation Metrics for Supervised Learning

### A. Regression Metrics

| Metric | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Penalizes large errors heavily. |
| **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same units as $y$. |
| **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)** | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers. |
| **R² (Coefficient of Determination)** | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | $R^2 = 1$ perfect, $R^2 = 0$ predicts mean. |

### B. Classification Metrics

Let **TP** = True Positives, **TN** = True Negatives, **FP** = False Positives, **FN** = False Negatives.

| Metric | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness. |
| **Precision** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Of predicted positives, how many are correct? |
| **Recall (Sensitivity, TPR)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Of actual positives, how many were caught? |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R. |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Of actual negatives, how many were correctly identified? |
| **Confusion Matrix** | 2×2 table of TP, FP, FN, TN | Visual breakdown of errors. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Accuracy is misleading for imbalanced datasets.** Example: In a 99% non-fraud dataset, a model that always predicts "not fraud" achieves 99% accuracy but is useless. Use **Precision, Recall, F1, or AUC-ROC** instead.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU Module 1 question paper.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hypothesis (Linear) | $h_\theta(x) = \theta^T x$ | Linear Regression |
| 2 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Logistic Regression |
| 3 | MSE | $\frac{1}{N}\sum (h_\theta(x_i) - y_i)^2$ | Regression loss |
| 4 | Cross-Entropy | $-\frac{1}{N}\sum [y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})]$ | Classification loss |
| 5 | Gradient Descent | $\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j}$ | Parameter update |
| 6 | Normal Equation | $\theta = (X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty$ | Closed-form linear reg. |
| 7 | Accuracy | $\frac{TP+TN}{Total}$ | Classification metric |
| 8 | Precision | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | Classification metric |
| 9 | Recall | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | Classification metric |
| 10 | F1-Score | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Classification metric |
| 11 | R² | $1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | Regression metric |
| 12 | Bayes' Theorem (Naive Bayes) | $P(y \mid x) = \frac{P(x \mid y)P(y)}{P(x)}$ | Probabilistic classification |
| 13 | Euclidean Distance (KNN) | $d(x, x') = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - x'_j)^2}$ | Nearest neighbor search |
| 14 | Entropy (Decision Tree) | $H(S) = -\sum_{c} p_c \log_2 p_c$ | Information gain |
| 15 | Information Gain | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Feature selection |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of Supervised Learning | ML Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Predicting concrete strength, structural health monitoring | Linear Regression, SVR |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Predicting tool wear, predictive maintenance | Random Forest, LSTM |
| **Electrical Engineering** | Load forecasting, fault detection in power grids | ANN, Gradient Boosting |
| **Biomedical Engineering** | ECG/EEG classification, tumor detection | CNN, SVM |
| **Computer Science (AI/ML)** | Spam filtering, image recognition, NLP | All algorithms |
| **Industrial Engineering** | Quality control, demand forecasting | Time Series + Regression |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The bias-variance trade-off is *the* central concept in supervised learning. High bias (underfitting) = model too simple; high variance (overfitting) = model too complex. **Regularization** (L1/L2) is used to control this trade-off and is a common KTU question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gradient Descent for Linear Regression

**Setup:** We want to derive the parameter update rule for Linear Regression with cost function:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (h_\theta(x_i) - y_i)^2 = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\theta^T x_i - y_i)^2$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Gradient of $J(\theta)$ w.r.t. $\theta_j$

We start by taking the partial derivative of the cost function with respect to a single parameter $\theta_j$:

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j} \left[ \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\theta^T x_i - y_i)^2 \right]$$

The $\frac{1}{2}$ is a mathematical convenience that cancels the **power of 2** from the chain rule.

### Step 2 — Apply the Chain Rule

Using the chain rule $\frac{\partial u^2}{\partial \theta_j} = 2u \cdot \frac{\partial u}{\partial \theta_j}$:

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{2N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2 (\theta^T x_i - y_i) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j}(\theta^T x_i - y_i)$$

The $2$ in the numerator and the $\frac{1}{2}$ in the denominator cancel out, leaving:

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\theta^T x_i - y_i) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j}(\theta^T x_i - y_i)$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate the Hypothesis

Since $h_\theta(x_i) = \theta^T x_i = \sum_{k=0}^{d} \theta_k x_{ik}$, the partial derivative w.r.t. $\theta_j$ is simply $x_{ij}$ (the $j$-th feature of the $i$-th example). The $y_i$ term vanishes because it is a constant:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta_j}(\theta^T x_i - y_i) = x_{ij}$$

### Step 4 — Final Gradient Expression

Substituting back:

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (h_\theta(x_i) - y_i) \cdot x_{ij}$$

### Step 5 — Gradient Descent Update Rule

The update rule moves $\theta_j$ in the opposite direction of the gradient (because we want to **minimize** the cost):

$$\boxed{\theta_j := \theta_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (h_\theta(x_i) - y_i) \cdot x_{ij}}$$

This is called the **Batch Gradient Descent** rule. Variants include:
- **Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD):** Uses one example at a time ($i$ sampled randomly per update).
- **Mini-Batch Gradient Descent:** Uses a small batch (e.g., 32, 64, 128 examples) per update.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Normal Equation (Closed-Form Solution)

**Setup:** For Linear Regression, we want to find $\theta$ that minimizes $J(\theta)$ in closed form (no iteration).

### Step 1 — Express in Matrix Form

Let $X$ be the $N \times (d+1)$ design matrix (with a column of 1s for the bias term), and $y$ be the $N \times 1$ target vector:

$$X = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_{11} & x_{12} & \cdots & x_{1d} \\ 1 & x_{21} & x_{22} & \cdots & x_{2d} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_{N1} & x_{N2} & \cdots & x_{Nd} \end{bmatrix}, \quad y = \begin{bmatrix} y_1 \\ y_2 \\ \vdots \\ y_N \end{bmatrix}$$

The hypothesis vector is $\hat{y} = X\theta$, so the cost function becomes:

$$J(\theta) = \frac{1}{2N} (X\theta - y)^T (X\theta - y)$$

### Step 2 — Set the Gradient to Zero

To find the minimum, we set $\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta} = 0$. Using the matrix calculus identity $\frac{\partial (A\theta - b)^T (A\theta - b)}{\partial \theta} = 2A^T(A\theta - b)$:

$$2 X^T (X\theta - y) = 0$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\theta$

$$X^T X \theta = X^T y$$

$$\boxed{\theta^* = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix $(X^T X)^{-1} X^T$ is called the **Moore-Penrose pseudoinverse** of $X$, denoted $X^+$. So $\theta^* = X^+ y$.

### Comparison: Normal Equation vs. Gradient Descent

| Aspect | Normal Equation | Gradient Descent |
|---|---|---|
| **Solution Type** | Closed-form (one-step) | Iterative |
| **Computation** | $O(d^3)$ for matrix inversion | $O(N \cdot d \cdot \text{iterations})$ |
| **Choice of $\alpha$?** | No | Yes (hyperparameter) |
| **Scalability** | Poor for large $d$ ($d > 10^4$) | Good for large $d$ and $N$ |
| **Convergence** | Exact | Approximate |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Logistic Regression Cost Function

**Why we can't use MSE for classification:** Using MSE with the sigmoid function leads to a **non-convex** cost function with multiple local minima, making optimization difficult. Hence we use **log loss (cross-entropy)**, which is convex.

### Step 1 — Probability Interpretation

We model the probability of class $y=1$ given $x$ as:
$$P(y=1 \mid x; \theta) = h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}$$

The probability of class $y=0$ is therefore:
$$P(y=0 \mid x; \theta) = 1 - h_\theta(x)$$

### Step 2 — Combine into a Single Expression

Both cases can be written compactly using the indicator $y \in \{0, 1\}$:
$$P(y \mid x; \theta) = (h_\theta(x))^y (1 - h_\theta(x))^{1-y}$$

This expression is valid because:
- If $y = 1$: $P = (h)^1 (1-h)^0 = h$
- If $y = 0$: $P = (h)^0 (1-h)^1 = 1 - h$

### Step 3 — Likelihood Function

Assuming training examples are i.i.d. (independent and identically distributed), the likelihood of the entire dataset is:
$$L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} P(y_i \mid x_i; \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{N} (h_\theta(x_i))^{y_i} (1 - h_\theta(x_i))^{1-y_i}$$

### Step 4 — Log-Likelihood

Taking the natural logarithm (monotonic transformation, makes multiplication → addition):
$$\ell(\theta) = \log L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(h_\theta(x_i)) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - h_\theta(x_i)) \right]$$

### Step 5 — Cost Function (Negative Log-Likelihood)

To convert maximization into minimization, we negate:
$$\boxed{J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y_i \log(h_\theta(x_i)) + (1 - y_i) \log(1 - h_\theta(x_i)) \right]}$$

### Step 6 — Gradient

Taking the derivative (using the fact that $\frac{d\sigma(z)}{dz} = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ and the chain rule):
$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (h_\theta(x_i) - y_i) x_{ij}$$

**Notice:** The gradient has the **same elegant form as Linear Regression!** Only $h_\theta$ differs (sigmoid vs. linear).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Supervised Algorithms

| Algorithm | Task Type | Key Idea | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Regression** | Regression | Fit a straight line | Simple, interpretable | Assumes linearity |
| **Logistic Regression** | Classification | Sigmoid + log-loss | Probabilistic, fast | Linear decision boundary only |
| **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** | Both | Vote of $k$ nearest points | No training, intuitive | Slow at prediction, curse of dimensionality |
| **Decision Tree** | Both | Recursive feature splits | Highly interpretable | Prone to overfitting |
| **Random Forest** | Both | Ensemble of decision trees | Robust, accurate | Less interpretable, slow |
| **SVM** | Classification | Maximum margin hyperplane | Works well in high dimensions | Slow for large $N$, kernel choice |
| **Naive Bayes** | Classification | Bayes' theorem + feature independence | Very fast, works with small data | Strong independence assumption |
| **Neural Networks** | Both | Layers of weighted neurons | Universal approximator | Needs lots of data, black box |
| **Gradient Boosting (XGBoost)** | Both | Sequential ensemble correction | State-of-the-art accuracy | Tuning-intensive |

### Bias-Variance Decomposition

The expected prediction error can be decomposed as:
$$\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \text{Bias}^2[\hat{f}(x)] + \text{Var}[\hat{f}(x)] + \sigma^2$$

where:
- $\text{Bias}^2$ = error from wrong model assumptions (underfitting).
- $\text{Variance}$ = error from sensitivity to training data fluctuations (overfitting).
- $\sigma^2$ = **irreducible error** (noise in the data itself).

| Model Complexity | Bias | Variance | Total Error |
|---|---|---|---|
| Very Simple (e.g., linear for non-linear data) | High | Low | High (Underfitting) |
| Sweet Spot | Low | Low | **Lowest (Generalized)** |
| Very Complex (e.g., deep tree on small data) | Low | High | High (Overfitting) |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions test definitions, basic concepts, and short derivations. Answers should be 3–4 sentences with a clear formula/diagram where applicable.

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Machine Learning. List any four real-world applications of Machine Learning.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Machine Learning is a subfield of Artificial Intelligence that enables computers to learn patterns from data and improve their performance on a given task **without being explicitly programmed** (Arthur Samuel, 1959). Tom Mitchell (1997) formally defined it as: *"A program learns from experience E with respect to task T and performance measure P, if its performance at T, measured by P, improves with E."*

**Four Real-World Applications:**

1. **Email Spam Classification** — Classifies incoming emails as spam or not-spam (binary classification).
2. **Medical Diagnosis** — Detects diseases like cancer from X-ray or MRI images (classification).
3. **Product Recommendation Systems** — Suggests products to users on Amazon, Netflix, etc. (collaborative filtering + supervised learning).
4. **Self-Driving Cars** — Detects objects, predicts steering angles from camera/LiDAR data (regression + classification).
5. *(Bonus)* — Voice assistants (Siri, Alexa), stock price prediction, fraud detection, sentiment analysis.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised | Reinforcement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data** | Labeled $(x, y)$ | Unlabeled $(x)$ | State-Action-Reward tuples |
| **Goal** | Learn mapping $f: X \rightarrow Y$ | Discover hidden structure | Maximize cumulative reward |
| **Example** | Email spam detection | Customer segmentation (K-Means) | AlphaGo playing chess |
| **Feedback** | Direct (labels) | None | Delayed (rewards) |
| **Algorithms** | Linear Regression, SVM, KNN, Decision Tree | K-Means, PCA, DBSCAN | Q-Learning, DQN, PPO |

**Examples explained:**
- **Supervised:** Predicting house prices from features (size, location) using labeled historical data.
- **Unsupervised:** Grouping customers into segments based on purchasing behavior using K-Means.
- **Reinforcement:** Training a robot to walk by rewarding forward movement and penalizing falls.
