```

## 4.3 Label Propagation Iteration Cycle (Sequential View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA[Initialize F0 with anchor labels Y]:::step
    stepB[Compute normalized similarity S]:::step
    stepC[Propagate F t+1 equals alpha S F t plus 1 minus alpha Y]:::step
    stepD{Convergence Check diff less than epsilon}:::check
    stepE[Output final soft labels F star]:::out
    stepF[Continue iteration t plus 1]:::loop

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD -->|No| stepF
    stepF --> stepC
    stepD -->|Yes| stepE

    classDef step fill:#1565c0,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0a3676,stroke-width:2px
    classDef check fill:#f9a825,color:#000000,stroke:#a06f00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:3px
    classDef loop fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#3a0d5e,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Semi-Supervised Learning. How does it differ from Supervised and Unsupervised Learning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Semi-Supervised Learning (SSL) is a machine learning paradigm that uses **both a small amount of labeled data** $\mathcal{L} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{l}$ and **a large amount of unlabeled data** $\mathcal{U} = \{x_j\}_{j=l+1}^{l+u}$ during training. **(1 mark)**
- **Supervised Learning** uses *only* labeled data, requires extensive annotations, and is suitable when labels are abundant. **(1 mark)**
- **Unsupervised Learning** uses *only* unlabeled data to discover patterns (e.g., clustering), with no prediction target. **(0.5 mark)**
- **SSL** combines both: it learns from the labeled set for correctness and exploits the structure of the unlabeled set to improve generalization, particularly useful when labeling is expensive. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State and explain the three fundamental assumptions of Semi-Supervised Learning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Smoothness Assumption:** If two points $x_1$ and $x_2$ lie in a high-density region and are close to each other, then their labels $y_1$ and $y_2$ should also be similar. **(1 mark)**
2. **Cluster Assumption:** Data points tend to form discrete clusters, and points belonging to the same cluster typically share the same class label. Consequently, the decision boundary should lie in a low-density region. **(1 mark)**
3. **Manifold Assumption:** High-dimensional input data lies on a lower-dimensional manifold $\mathcal{M}$. The marginal distribution $P(x)$ concentrates near $\mathcal{M}$, and the conditional distribution $P(y \mid x)$ varies smoothly along $\mathcal{M}$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 📕 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **INSTRUCTION (KTU 2024):** Answer **ONE** full question. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the Self-Training algorithm for Semi-Supervised Learning in detail. Discuss its advantages and limitations.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Self-training is a *wrapper-based* SSL technique where a single base learner is iteratively retrained on its own most confident predictions on the unlabeled set.

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
1. Train an initial classifier $f^{(0)}$ using the labeled set $\mathcal{L} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{l}$.
2. Use $f^{(0)}$ to predict labels $\hat{y}_j$ for all $x_j \in \mathcal{U}$.
3. Compute the prediction confidence $c_j = \max_c P(y = c \mid x_j, f^{(0)})$.
4. Select the subset $\mathcal{P} = \{(x_j : c_j \geq \tau)\}$ where $\tau$ is a confidence threshold (typically 0.7–0.95).
5. Add $\mathcal{P}$ with their pseudo-labels $\hat{y}_j$ to the labeled set: $\mathcal{L}^{(1)} = \mathcal{L} \cup \mathcal{P}$.
6. Retrain $f^{(1)}$ on $\mathcal{L}^{(1)}$ and repeat steps 2–5 until convergence or a maximum iteration count.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Model-agnostic: works with any classifier (SVM, Decision Tree, Neural Net).
- Conceptually simple and easy to implement.
- Naturally leverages increasing data sizes as pseudo-labels accumulate.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- **Confirmation bias:** Initial mistakes get reinforced in later iterations, leading to error amplification.
- **Threshold sensitivity:** Performance heavily depends on $\tau$; too low admits noise, too high admits nothing.
- Cannot exploit diverse data views — it is essentially a single-view method.

---

**(b) Derive the iterative update rule for Graph-Based Label Propagation. Show that the sequence converges to a fixed point.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Affinity Construction (1 mark):**
Define edge weights between any two data points $x_i, x_j$ using a Gaussian kernel:
$$w_{ij} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{\Vert x_i - x_j\Vert^2}{2\sigma^2}\right), \quad w_{ii} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Normalization (1 mark):**
Form the degree matrix $D$ with $D_{ii} = \sum_j w_{ij}$, and the symmetrically normalized affinity:
$$S = D^{-1/2} W D^{-1/2}$$

**Step 3 — Anchor Matrix (1 mark):**
Construct $Y \in \mathbb{R}^{(l+u) \times C}$ where $Y_{ic} = 1$ if $x_i \in \mathcal{L}$ has class $c$, else $Y_{ic} = 0$. Initialize $F^{(0)} = Y$.

**Step 4 — Iterative Update (2 marks):**
At each iteration $t$, propagate labels while clamping the anchors:
$$F^{(t+1)} = \alpha S F^{(t)} + (1 - \alpha) Y$$
Here $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ is the propagation rate. The first term spreads labels along the graph; the second term anchors original labels.

**Step 5 — Convergence Proof (2 marks):**
Define $G(F) = \alpha S F + (1 - \alpha) Y$. This is an affine map. The fixed point $F^*$ satisfies $F^* = \alpha S F^* + (1 - \alpha) Y$, which rearranges to:
$$(I - \alpha S) F^* = (1 - \alpha) Y$$
$$F^* = (1 - \alpha)(I - \alpha S)^{-1} Y$$
Since $\Vert \alpha S \Vert_2 < 1$ (by normalization and $\alpha < 1$), the Neumann series $(I - \alpha S)^{-1} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} (\alpha S)^k$ converges, guaranteeing $F^{(t)} \to F^*$.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the Co-Training algorithm. Under what conditions does it outperform Self-Training?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Co-training is a multi-view SSL algorithm that trains **two classifiers** $f_1, f_2$ on **two disjoint feature views** $X^{(1)}, X^{(2)}$ of the same data, and lets them teach each other using unlabeled samples.

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. **Sufficiency:** Each view alone is sufficient to train a strong classifier — i.e., $X^{(1)} \rightarrow Y$ and $X^{(2)} \rightarrow Y$ independently.
2. **Conditional Independence:** Given the label, the two views are conditionally independent: $P(X^{(1)}, X^{(2)} \mid Y) = P(X^{(1)} \mid Y) \cdot P(X^{(2)} \mid Y)$.

**Algorithm Steps (2 marks):**
1. Split each labeled sample $x_i$ into two views: $x_i = (x_i^{(1)}, x_i^{(2)})$.
2. Train $f_1$ on $\{(x_i^{(1)}, y_i)\}$ and $f_2$ on $\{(x_i^{(2)}, y_i)\}$.
3. For each unlabeled sample, $f_1$ predicts and selects its top-$k$ most confident samples per class.
4. These samples are added to $f_2$'s training set as pseudo-labeled data, and vice versa.
5. Iterate until no new samples are added or a maximum iteration is reached.

**Why it outperforms Self-Training (2 marks):**
- **Diverse views reduce confirmation bias:** A misclassification by $f_1$ is unlikely to be replicated by $f_2$ if the views are independent.
- **Disagreement signal:** Samples where $f_1$ and $f_2$ disagree are informative and can be flagged for active learning.
- Empirical results (Blum & Mitchell, 1998) showed Co-Training significantly reduces error compared to Self-Training on webpage classification.

---

**(b) Write the general Semi-Supervised Learning objective function and explain the role of the consistency regularization term. Illustrate with the $\Pi$-Model.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — General Objective (2 marks):**
$$\mathcal{J}_{\text{SSL}}(\theta) = \underbrace{\frac{1}{l}\sum_{i=1}^{l} L_{\text{sup}}\big(f_\theta(x_i), y_i\big)}_{\text{Supervised component}} + \lambda \cdot \underbrace{R_{\text{unsup}}(\theta)}_{\text{Unsupervised regularizer}}$$
where $\lambda$ is a balancing hyperparameter.

**Step 2 — Consistency Regularization Form (2 marks):**
$$R_{\text{unsup}}^{\text{consistency}}(\theta) = \frac{1}{u}\sum_{j=1}^{u} \big\Vert f_\theta(x_j) - f_\theta(\hat{x}_j)\big\Vert^2$$
where $\hat{x}_j = T(x_j)$ is a stochastically perturbed version of $x_j$ (via augmentation, dropout, or noise).

**Step 3 — The $\Pi$-Model (2 marks):**
The $\Pi$-Model evaluates the network **twice** on the same batch — once with augmentation and once with a different augmentation — and minimizes the squared difference between the two output distributions. It adds an **entropy minimization** term to encourage confident predictions on unlabeled data. The total loss is:
$$\mathcal{J}_{\Pi} = \mathcal{J}_{\text{sup}} + \lambda_w(t) \cdot \frac{1}{u}\sum_{j=1}^{u} \text{KL}\big(f_\theta(x_j) \Vert f_\theta(\hat{x}_j)\big) + \beta \cdot \frac{1}{u}\sum_{j=1}^{u} H\big(f_\theta(x_j)\big)$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Significance (1 mark):**
Consistency regularization is the **theoretical engine** behind state-of-the-art SSL methods like Mean Teacher, MixMatch, and FixMatch. It encodes the **smoothness assumption** mathematically: nearby inputs (in input space) should produce nearby outputs (in prediction space).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not confuse "self-supervised" with "semi-supervised".** Self-supervised generates its own labels (e.g., via pretext tasks like rotation prediction); semi-supervised uses *real* labels for a small subset.
> - **Do not write "SSL uses no labels"** — that is unsupervised. SSL *requires* at least some labeled data.
> - **In derivations, students often forget the anchor term** $(1 - \alpha)Y$ in label propagation. Without it, all labels diffuse to a constant. **(−2 marks)**
> - **Confidence threshold $\tau$ must be a number** in self-training, not a vague phrase like "high confidence." Examiners want 0.8, 0.9, etc.
> - **Cluster assumption ≠ smoothness assumption.** Examiners specifically test whether you can distinguish them. Cluster = low-density boundary; Smoothness = local label consistency.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **SSL = Supervised (small) + Unsupervised (large) hybrid paradigm.** Used when labeling is expensive.
- ✅ The **three assumptions** (Smoothness, Cluster, Manifold) are the *theoretical foundation* — name them, distinguish them.
- ✅ The **general SSL objective** is $\mathcal{J} = \mathcal{J}_l + \lambda \mathcal{J}_u$; the value of $\lambda$ controls the regularization strength.
- ✅ **Self-Training:** Single model, confidence-thresholded pseudo-labels. Risk: confirmation bias.
- ✅ **Co-Training:** Two views, two classifiers, mutual teaching. Requires conditional independence.
- ✅ **Label Propagation:** Graph Laplacian-based; update rule is $F^{(t+1)} = \alpha S F^{(t)} + (1 - \alpha) Y$.
- ✅ **Consistency Regularization:** $R_u = \mathbb{E}\Vert f(x) - f(\hat{x})\Vert^2$; powers Mean Teacher, $\Pi$-Model, FixMatch.
- ✅ **Entropy Minimization** encourages confident predictions on unlabeled data: $H(p) = -\sum_c p_c \log p_c$.
- ✅ **Real-world wins:** Medical imaging, NLP, autonomous driving, low-resource speech recognition.
- ✅ **Memory warning:** Graph-based SSL is $O(N^2)$ memory — not scalable beyond $N \approx 10^5$ without approximation.
- ✅ **Stopping criterion:** Confidence drops below $\tau$, validation loss plateaus, or maximum iterations reached.
- ✅ **Exam mantra:** When asked "Why SSL?" — answer: "Labels are expensive, unlabeled data is cheap, and the manifold structure of $\mathcal{U}$ improves the decision boundary."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Unsupervised # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Unsupervised Learning > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > **Unsupervised Learning** is a paradigm of Machine Learning wherein an algorithm is trained on a dataset consisting of input vectors $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ **without** any corresponding target labels $y^{(i)}$. The objective is to discover hidden structure, intrinsic patterns, groupings, or low-dimensional representations directly from the feature space $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$. Mathematically, the learning problem is defined as: $$ \text{Given a dataset } \mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \ldots, \mathbf{x}^{(n)}\}, \quad \text{where } \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^d $$ $$ \text{Learn a function } f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y} \text{ that captures the underlying structure of } \mathcal{D} $$ The fundamental difference between supervised and unsupervised learning is the **absence of a supervisory signal**: $$ \text{Supervised: } \mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{n} \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Unsupervised: } \mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{n} $$ ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Toddler and the Toy Box > [!IMPORTANT] > **Intuitive Real-World Analogy** > Imagine giving a toddler a large box of mixed toys (cars, dolls, blocks, balls) **without telling them any names or categories**. The toddler, through natural curiosity, will: > 1. **Group** similar toys together (clustering). > 2. **Identify** that some toys share a common feature like "rolling" (dimensionality reduction/feature extraction). > 3. Notice that **"when there are more cars, there are fewer dolls"** (association rule mining). This is exactly how unsupervised learning works — the machine is given raw data and must find structure **on its own**, without being told the "right answer." ## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Unsupervised Learning Unsupervised learning problems, as per the KTU 2024 syllabus, are categorized into three principal tasks: | Pillar | Core Objective | Canonical Algorithm | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Clustering** | Group similar data points together | K-Means, Hierarchical, DBSCAN | | **Dimensionality Reduction** | Compress high-$d$ data into low-$d$ representation | PCA, t-SNE, Autoencoders | | **Association Rule Mining** | Discover interesting co-occurrence patterns | Apriori, FP-Growth | > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Note** > Module 1 of PEAET495 emphasizes **Clustering** and **Dimensionality Reduction**. Association mining is typically covered in Module 2 (Data Mining) but the foundational contrast is important for ESE. ## 1.4 Why Unsupervised Learning? (Motivation) - **Labeling is expensive**: In real-world domains (medical imaging, genomics, fraud detection), obtaining labels requires human experts. Unsupervised learning bypasses this bottleneck. - **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)**: Helps visualize and understand data **before** applying supervised methods. - **Feature Engineering**: Generates powerful, compressed features that boost downstream supervised models. - **Anomaly Detection**: Rare points that do not belong to any cluster are often anomalies (e.g., network intrusions, defective products). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Mathematical Framework of Clustering ### 2.1.1 Distance Metrics (Foundational) Clustering algorithms fundamentally rely on a **distance function** $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ that must satisfy four axioms: 1. **Non-negativity**: $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) \geq 0$ 2. **Identity of indiscernibles**: $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = 0 \iff \mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{x}_j$ 3. **Symmetry**: $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = d(\mathbf{x}_j, \mathbf{x}_i)$ 4. **Triangle inequality**: $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) \leq d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_k) + d(\mathbf{x}_k, \mathbf{x}_j)$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The choice of distance metric **drastically changes** the clustering result. Always justify your metric choice based on data type (continuous, categorical, binary). ### 2.1.2 K-Means Clustering: The Centroid-Based Paradigm **K-Means** is the workhorse of clustering. It partitions $n$ data points into $K$ disjoint clusters $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K$ by minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**: $$ J(\mathcal{C}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_k} \lVert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \rVert_2^2 $$ where $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_k} \mathbf{x}$ is the **centroid** (mean) of cluster $C_k$. ### 2.1.3 Hierarchical Clustering: The Agglomerative Approach Builds a **dendrogram** (tree of clusters) by either: - **Agglomerative** (bottom-up): Start with $n$ singleton clusters, merge the two closest at each step. - **Divisive** (top-down): Start with one cluster, recursively split. The **linkage criterion** determines inter-cluster distance: $$ d_{\min}(C_a, C_b) = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in C_a, \mathbf{y} \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \quad \text{(Single Linkage)} $$ $$ d_{\max}(C_a, C_b) = \max_{\mathbf{x} \in C_a, \mathbf{y} \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \quad \text{(Complete Linkage)} $$ $$ d_{\text{avg}}(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \vert C_b \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_a} \sum_{\mathbf{y} \in C_b} d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \quad \text{(Average Linkage)} $$ ### 2.1.4 DBSCAN: Density-Based Clustering **DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise)** defines clusters as **dense regions** separated by sparse regions. It uses two parameters: - $\epsilon$ (epsilon): neighborhood radius - $\text{MinPts}$ (minimum points to form a dense region) Core point condition: a point $\mathbf{x}$ is a **core point** if: $$ \vert N_{\epsilon}(\mathbf{x}) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}, \quad \text{where } N_{\epsilon}(\mathbf{x}) = \{\mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{D} : d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) \leq \epsilon\} $$ ## 2.2 The Mathematical Framework of Dimensionality Reduction ### 2.2.1 Principal Component Analysis (PCA) PCA finds an **orthogonal linear transformation** that projects data onto directions (principal components) of **maximum variance**. Given data matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ (centered, so mean is zero), PCA solves: $$ \max_{\mathbf{w}: \lVert \mathbf{w} \rVert = 1} \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} $$ where $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X}$ is the **covariance matrix**. The optimal $\mathbf{w}^*$ is the **eigenvector** corresponding to the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda$ of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$: $$ \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w} $$ ### 2.2.2 Reconstruction Loss Perspective PCA can equivalently be seen as minimizing reconstruction error. Project $\mathbf{x}$ onto a $k$-dimensional subspace spanned by $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$: $$ \min_{\mathbf{W}: \mathbf{W}^{\top} \mathbf{W} = \mathbf{I}_k} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mathbf{W} \mathbf{W}^{\top} \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \rVert_2^2 $$ The minimum reconstruction error is: $$ \text{Loss} = \sum_{j=k+1}^{d} \lambda_j $$ i.e., the sum of the **discarded eigenvalues** (variance lost by dropping components). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it appears in nearly every KTU ESE paper for this module.** | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Euclidean Distance** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Default metric; scale-sensitive | | **Manhattan Distance** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | Robust to outliers; grid-like paths | | **Minkowski Distance** | $d_p(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalizes both above | | **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \frac{\mathbf{x}^{\top} \mathbf{y}}{\lVert \mathbf{x} \rVert \lVert \mathbf{y} \rVert}$ | For text/high-dim sparse data | | **K-Means Objective (WCSS)** | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_k} \lVert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \rVert_2^2$ | Lower is better; use Elbow Method | | **Silhouette Score** | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Range $[-1, 1]$; higher is better | | **PCA Eigen-equation** | $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}_k = \lambda_k \mathbf{w}_k$ | Sort by $\lambda_1 \geq \lambda_2 \geq \ldots \geq \lambda_d$ | | **Variance Explained Ratio** | $\text{VER}_k = \frac{\lambda_k}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$ | Cumulative VER guides $k$ selection | | **DBSCAN Core Condition** | $\vert N_{\epsilon}(\mathbf{x}) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$ | Border points have fewer neighbors | | **Hopkins Statistic** | $H = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} u_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} u_i + \sum_{i=1}^{m} v_i}$ | $H > 0.75$ indicates strong clustering tendency | > [!NOTE] > **Note on Notation:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, $\lVert \cdot \rVert_2$ denotes the **$L^2$-norm** (Euclidean norm), and $\lVert \mathbf{x} \rVert = \sqrt{\mathbf{x}^{\top}\mathbf{x}}$. Avoid using the pipe symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ inside mathematical tables. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications | Application Domain | Unsupervised Technique | Business/Engineering Utility | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Customer Segmentation** | K-Means | Targeted marketing, CRM analytics | | **Image Compression** | PCA / Vector Quantization | Reduced storage, faster transmission | | **Genomics / Bioinformatics** | Hierarchical Clustering | Phylogenetic tree construction | | **Anomaly Detection (Cybersecurity)** | DBSCAN / Isolation Forest | Intrusion detection, fraud alerts | | **Recommender Systems** | Association Rule Mining (Apriori) | "Customers who bought X also bought Y" | | **NLP / Topic Modeling** | t-SNE + K-Means | Document clustering, search engines | | **Sensor Networks (IoT)** | DBSCAN | Identifying faulty vs. normal sensors | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: K-Means Algorithm Convergence The K-Means algorithm is an instance of the **Expectation-Maximization (EM)** principle applied to a Gaussian Mixture Model with **identity covariance** and **hard assignment**. We will derive why the iterative update rules guarantee non-increasing objective $J$. ### Step 1: Formalize the Objective We aim to partition $\mathcal{D} = \{\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{n}$ into $K$ clusters. Let $r_{ik} \in \{0, 1\}$ be a binary indicator such that $r_{ik} = 1$ if and only if point $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ is assigned to cluster $k$. The constraint is $\sum_{k=1}^{K} r_{ik} = 1$ for every $i$. The objective is: $$ J(\mathbf{r}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{K} r_{ik} \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \rVert_2^2 $$ ### Step 2: Assignment Step (E-step analogue) Fix the centroids $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$. For each point $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$, choose the cluster $k$ that minimizes the distance: $$ r_{ik} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } k = \arg\min_{j} \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \rVert_2^2 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ **Why does this reduce $J$?** Because for each $i$, we are minimizing the inner sum $\sum_k r_{ik} \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \rVert_2^2$ over $r_{ik}$, which cannot increase $J$. ### Step 3: Refinement Step (M-step analogue) Fix the assignments $r_{ik}$. Differentiate $J$ with respect to $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ and set to zero: $$ \frac{\partial J}{\partial \boldsymbol{\mu}_k} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ik} (\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k) = 0 $$ Solving for $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$: $$ \boldsymbol{\mu}_k = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ik} \mathbf{x}^{(i)}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ik}} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_k} \mathbf{x} $$ This is precisely the **mean (centroid)** of all points currently in cluster $k$. The second-order derivative (Hessian) is $2 \mathbf{I} \sum_i r_{ik}$, which is positive-definite, confirming a minimum. ### Step 4: Monotonic Convergence Proof After each E-step, $J$ does not increase (by construction of the argmin). After each M-step, $J$ does not increase (by closed-form minimum). Since $J$ is bounded below by **0**, the algorithm **converges** in a finite number of iterations. However, convergence is only to a **local minimum**, not the global optimum. ### Step 5: Computational Complexity For $n$ points, $d$ dimensions, $K$ clusters, and $I$ iterations: $$ \text{Time complexity} = O(I \cdot K \cdot n \cdot d) $$ $$ \text{Space complexity} = O(n \cdot d + K \cdot d) $$ ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: PCA via Lagrange Multipliers We derive the principal components by solving the constrained optimization: $$ \max_{\mathbf{w}} \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} = 1 $$ ### Step 1: Form the Lagrangian $$ \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - \lambda (\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w} - 1) $$ ### Step 2: Take the Gradient $$ \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = 2 \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - 2 \lambda \mathbf{w} = 0 $$ ### Step 3: Solve the Eigenvalue Equation $$ \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w} $$ This is the **eigenvalue problem**. The optimal $\mathbf{w}^*$ is the eigenvector with the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$. ### Step 4: Justify the Maximum The variance captured by direction $\mathbf{w}$ is: $$ \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}^{\top} (\lambda \mathbf{w}) = \lambda (\mathbf{w}^{\top} \mathbf{w}) = \lambda $$ (using the unit-norm constraint). Maximizing $\mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$ is therefore equivalent to choosing the **largest eigenvalue**. ### Step 5: Multi-Component Extension For $k$ components, we select the top-$k$ eigenvectors $\mathbf{W} = [\mathbf{w}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{w}_k]$. These are mutually orthogonal because $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$ is symmetric, so its eigenvectors form an orthogonal basis. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: K-Means vs. Hierarchical vs. DBSCAN | Property | K-Means | Hierarchical | DBSCAN | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Cluster Shape** | Spherical, convex | Arbitrary | Arbitrary, non-convex | | **Number of Clusters** | Must specify $K$ | Cut dendrogram | Auto-detected | | **Handles Noise** | Poor (forces assignment) | Poor | **Excellent** (marks as outliers) | | **Scalability** | $O(nKdI)$ — scales well | $O(n^3)$ — slow for large $n$ | $O(n \log n)$ with indexing | | **Sensitivity to Initialization** | **High** (use K-Means++) | None | Low | | **Input Parameters** | $K$ | Linkage, distance threshold | $\epsilon$, $\text{MinPts}$ | | **Determinism** | Stochastic (unless seeded) | Deterministic | Deterministic | | **Best For** | Large, isotropic data | Small datasets, taxonomy | Spatial data with noise | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: K-Means on 2D Points > [!NOTE] > **KTU-style numerical problem walkthrough** **Problem:** Apply one iteration of K-Means on the points $\{(2,10), (2,5), (8,4), (5,8), (7,5), (6,4), (1,2), (4,9)\}$ with initial centroids $C_1 = (2,10)$ and $C_2 = (5,8)$. ### Step 1: Compute distance from each point to each centroid | Point | $d$ to $C_1$ | $d$ to $C_2$ | Assigned Cluster | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $(2,10)$ | **0** | $\sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$ | 1 | | $(2,5)$ | **5** | $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.16$ | 2 | | $(8,4)$ | $\sqrt{36} = 6$ | $\sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$ | 2 | | $(5,8)$ | $\sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$ | **0** | 2 | | $(7,5)$ | $\sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$ | $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$ | 2 | | $(6,4)$ | $\sqrt{40} \approx 6.32$ | $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$ | 2 | | $(1,2)$ | $\sqrt{64} = 8$ | $\sqrt{52} \approx 7.21$ | 2 | | $(4,9)$ | $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$ | $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$ | 2 | **Cluster 1** = $\{(2,10)\}$, **Cluster 2** = rest. ### Step 2: Recompute Centroids $$ \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{\text{new}} = (2, 10) $$ $$ \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{\text{new}} = \frac{1}{7}\left[(2,5) + (8,4) + (5,8) + (7,5) + (6,4) + (1,2) + (4,9)\right] $$ $$ = \frac{1}{7}(33, 37) = \left(\frac{33}{7}, \frac{37}{7}\right) \approx (4.71, 5.29) $$ ### Step 3: Verify Convergence Compute $J_{\text{old}}$ and $J_{\text{new}}$ and check the difference. Repeat until centroids stabilize or $\vert J_{\text{old}} - J_{\text{new}} \vert < 10^{-4}$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Unsupervised Learning Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Unsupervised Learning] --> B[Clustering] A --> C[Dimensionality Reduction] A --> D[Association Mining] B --> B1[Partition-Based
K-Means, K-Medoids] B --> B2[Hierarchical
Agglomerative, Divisive] B --> B3[Density-Based
DBSCAN, OPTICS] B --> B4[Model-Based
Gaussian Mixture] C --> C1[Linear
PCA, LDA, ICA] C --> C2[Non-Linear
t-SNE, UMAP, Autoencoders] D --> D1[Apriori Algorithm] D --> D2[FP-Growth] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style B2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style B3 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style B4 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style C1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style C2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style D1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style D2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.2 K-Means Algorithm Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Input dataset X, K, init centroids] --> B[Initialize K centroids
randomly or via K-Means++] B --> C[Assignment Step
Assign each x_i to nearest centroid] C --> D{Convergence Check
Centroids stable or
max iterations?} D -->|No| E[Update Step
Recompute centroids as cluster means] E --> C D -->|Yes| F[Output: Final cluster labels
and centroids] F --> G[End] style A fill:#264653,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#000 style D fill:#f4a261,stroke:#000 style E fill:#e76f51,stroke:#000,color:#fff style F fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G fill:#264653,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 PCA Projection Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Original Feature Space] X1[High-Dim Data
X in R to the n x d] end subgraph PROC[PCA Pipeline] S1[Step 1: Mean Center
x_i minus mean] S2[Step 2: Covariance Matrix
Sigma equals X transpose X] S3[Step 3: Eigendecomposition
Sigma W equals Lambda W] S4[Step 4: Select Top k
eigenvectors] end subgraph OUTPUT[Reduced Space] Y1[Low-Dim Projection
Y in R to the n x k] Y2[Reconstruction
X tilde equals Y W transpose] end X1 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> Y1 Y1 --> Y2 style X1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Y1 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff style Y2 fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff style S1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style S2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style S3 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style S4 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 DBSCAN Point Classification Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Point x] --> B{More than MinPts
points within epsilon?} B -->|Yes| C[Core Point
Density reachable] B -->|No| D{Any core point
within epsilon?} D -->|Yes| E[Border Point
Density reachable] D -->|No| F[Noise Point
Outlier] C --> G[Forms/expands
a cluster] E --> G F --> H[Marked as
outlier] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#f4a261,stroke:#000 style C fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D fill:#f4a261,stroke:#000 style E fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#000 style F fill:#e76f51,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#000,color:#fff style H fill:#e76f51,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define unsupervised learning. How does it differ from supervised learning?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Unsupervised learning is a machine learning paradigm where the model is trained on input data $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ **without any corresponding output labels** $y^{(i)}$. The goal is to discover hidden patterns, groupings, or low-dimensional structure in the data. **Difference (1.5 marks):** | Aspect | Supervised | Unsupervised | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Training data | $\{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}$ | $\{\mathbf{x}^{(i)}\}$ | | Goal | Learn $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ | Discover structure in $\mathcal{X}$ | | Feedback | Labeled correction signal | No explicit feedback | | Examples | Classification, Regression | Clustering, PCA, Apriori | --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain the Elbow Method for determining the optimal number of clusters in K-Means.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Elbow Method (1 mark) plots the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** $J(K) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_k} \lVert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \rVert_2^2$ against the number of clusters $K$ (1 mark). As $K$ increases, $J(K)$ monotonically decreases. The **optimal $K$** is at the "elbow" — the point where the rate of decrease sharply shifts, indicating diminishing returns from adding more clusters (1 mark). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often confuse the Elbow Method with the Silhouette Method. The Elbow Method uses **WCSS** (lower is better, look for bend), while Silhouette uses **$s(i) \in [-1, 1]$** (higher is better). Mixing these up costs 1 mark. --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **K-Means clustering algorithm** in detail. State its objective function and discuss its step-by-step procedure. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **limitations of K-Means** and explain how **DBSCAN** overcomes them. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Objective Function (2 marks):** K-Means partitions $n$ data points into $K$ clusters $C_1, \ldots, C_K$ by minimizing the **WCSS**: $$ J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \in C_k} \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \rVert_2^2 $$ **Algorithm Steps (5 marks):** 1. **Initialize** $K$ cluster centroids $\boldsymbol{\mu}_1, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2, \ldots, \boldsymbol{\mu}_K$ (randomly or using K-Means++). 2. **Assignment Step:** For each data point $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$, assign it to the cluster with the nearest centroid: $C_k \leftarrow \{\mathbf{x}^{(i)} : k = \arg\min_j \lVert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j \rVert_2^2\}$. 3. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to it: $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k \leftarrow \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_k} \mathbf{x}$. 4. **Convergence Check:** Repeat steps 2–3 until centroids do not change, or maximum iterations are reached. #### Model Solution for (b): **Limitations of K-Means (3.5 marks):** - Requires **pre-specifying $K$**. - Sensitive to **initial centroid placement** (may converge to local minima). - Assumes **spherical, equal-sized clusters**; fails on elongated or irregular shapes. - **Sensitive to outliers** (centroids are dragged). - Performs **poorly on non-linear** data structures. **DBSCAN and its advantages (3.5 marks):** - **Density-based**: clusters are dense regions separated by sparse regions. - Parameters: $\epsilon$ (radius), $\text{MinPts}$ (density threshold). - Automatically **detects noise/outliers** — addresses K-Means' outlier problem. - Does **not require pre-specifying** the number of clusters — addresses K-Means' $K$ requirement. - Discovers **arbitrarily shaped** clusters (e.g., moons, rings) — addresses K-Means' spherical assumption. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive the **principal components** of a dataset using the **eigenvalue decomposition of the covariance matrix**. Show mathematically that the direction of maximum variance corresponds to the eigenvector of the largest eigenvalue. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Silhouette Coefficient** as an internal cluster validation metric. Describe its formula and interpretation with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): **Setup (1.5 marks):** Given centered data $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ with covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}^{\top} \mathbf{X}$, we seek a unit vector $\mathbf{w}$ maximizing projected variance: $$ \max_{\mathbf{w}: \mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} = 1} \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} $$ **Lagrangian Formulation (2 marks):** $$ \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^{\top} \boldsymbol{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - \lambda(\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} - 1) $$ **Setting gradient to zero (2 marks):** $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} - 2\lambda\mathbf{w} = 0 \implies \boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \lambda\mathbf{w} $$ **Conclusion (1.5 marks):** The optimal $\mathbf{w}$ is an eigenvector of $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}$. The variance captured equals $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \lambda$. To **maximize** this, we pick the eigenvector with the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_1$. The first $k$ principal components are the top-$k$ eigenvectors. #### Model Solution for (b): **Definition (2 marks):** The Silhouette Coefficient measures how well each point lies within its own cluster vs. the next nearest cluster. **Formula (3 marks):** For point $i$ in cluster $C_I$: $$ a(i) = \frac{1}{\vert C_I \vert - 1} \sum_{j \in C_I, j \neq i} d(i, j) \quad \text{(mean intra-cluster distance)} $$ $$ b(i) = \min_{J \neq I} \frac{1}{\vert C_J \vert} \sum_{j \in C_J} d(i, j) \quad \text{(smallest mean inter-cluster distance)} $$ $$ s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}} $$ **Interpretation (2 marks):** - $s(i) \approx 1$: Point is well-clustered (far from neighboring clusters). - $s(i) \approx 0$: Point is on the boundary between two clusters. - $s(i) \approx -1$: Point is likely in the wrong cluster. The **average silhouette score** over all points indicates the overall quality of clustering. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When asked to "derive" PCA, students often skip the **Lagrangian step** and jump directly to the eigenvalue equation. This loses **2 marks** because the constraint $\mathbf{w}^{\top}\mathbf{w} = 1$ is essential to the proof. Always show: (1) objective, (2) Lagrangian, (3) gradient, (4) solution, (5) interpretation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Unsupervised Learning** ### Core Concepts - Unsupervised learning = learning **without labels**; goal is to discover **structure**. - Three main branches: **Clustering, Dimensionality Reduction, Association Mining**. - Always **standardize/normalize** features before applying distance-based algorithms (K-Means, DBSCAN, PCA). ### K-Means - Minimizes **WCSS**: $J = \sum_k \sum_{\mathbf{x} \in C_k} \lVert \mathbf{x} - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \rVert^2$. - **Suffers from**: local minima, $K$ required, spherical assumption, outlier sensitivity. - **Use K-Means++ initialization** to avoid poor random starts. - **Elbow Method** for $K$ selection; **Silhouette Score** for validation. ### Hierarchical Clustering - Output is a **dendrogram** — cut at desired level to obtain $K$ clusters. - Linkage types: **Single** (min), **Complete** (max), **Average** (mean), **Ward's** (variance). - Time complexity $O(n^3)$ — not suitable for large datasets. ### DBSCAN - Parameters: $\epsilon$ (radius) and $\text{MinPts}$ (density). - Identifies **core**, **border**, and **noise** points. - Detects **arbitrary-shaped clusters** and handles **outliers natively**. ### PCA - Linear, **unsupervised** dimensionality reduction. - Solves $\boldsymbol{\Sigma}\mathbf{w} = \lambda\mathbf{w}$ and takes top-$k$ eigenvectors. - **Variance Explained Ratio** = $\frac{\lambda_k}{\sum_j \lambda_j}$ guides $k$ choice. - Reconstruction loss = sum of discarded eigenvalues. ### Evaluation Metrics (for ESE) - **Internal**: Silhouette, Davies-Bouldin Index, Dunn Index. - **External** (when labels available): Adjusted Rand Index, Normalized Mutual Information. - **Clustering Tendency**: Hopkins Statistic. ### KTU Board Exam Tips 1. **Always define** the objective function mathematically before explaining any algorithm. 2. Mention **time and space complexity** — examiners award marks for it. 3. Compare algorithms in a **table** — it is high-yield and shows conceptual clarity. 4. For numerical problems, **show all distance calculations** explicitly. 5. Discuss **limitations** of every algorithm you present — this is a frequently-tested KTU pattern. 6. Use **diagrams** (dendrograms, scatter plots with clusters) wherever possible — visual aids score well.

topics.title: Reinforcement Learning, Types of Supervised Learning - Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reinforcement Learning, Types of Supervised Learning - Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Supervised Learning – Classification

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification** is a sub-category of **Supervised Learning** in which the target variable (label) is a **discrete, categorical value** drawn from a finite set of classes. The learning algorithm maps an input feature vector $X \in \mathbb{R}^n$ to a discrete output $Y \in \{C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k\}$ by learning a decision boundary from a labeled training dataset $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$.

In simpler terms, classification is the process of **predicting a category** (e.g., Spam / Not Spam, Disease / Healthy, Digit 0–9) rather than a continuous number.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Post Office Sorting Room**
> Imagine a postal worker who must sort thousands of letters into different country bins. The worker is shown thousands of **already-sorted** letters (training data), studies the patterns on the envelopes (features such as city, stamp, address format), and eventually learns to place new, unseen letters into the correct bin. This supervised, pattern-matching activity is exactly what a **Classifier** does.

The four pillars of classification are:
- **Discrete Output** $\Rightarrow$ A finite set of categories
- **Labeled Training Data** $\Rightarrow$ Each example is tagged with the correct class
- **Decision Boundary** $\Rightarrow$ A surface separating the feature space into regions
- **Generalization** $\Rightarrow$ Correct prediction on *unseen* data

### Types of Classification Tasks

> [!NOTE]
> **Three major variants of classification** must be memorized for KTU exams:

| # | Type | # of Classes | # of Labels per Sample | Example |
|---|------|---------------|------------------------|---------|
| 1 | **Binary Classification** | 2 | 1 | Spam vs. Ham |
| 2 | **Multiclass Classification** | $k \geq 3$ | 1 (mutually exclusive) | Digit recognition (0–9) |
| 3 | **Multilabel Classification** | $k \geq 2$ | $\geq 1$ (non-exclusive) | Movie tagging (Action + Comedy) |

---

## 1.2 Reinforcement Learning (RL)

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reinforcement Learning (RL)** is a paradigm of **Machine Learning** in which an **agent** learns an optimal **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ by interacting with an **environment**, performing **actions**, and receiving **rewards** (or penalties). Unlike supervised learning, RL does not require explicit input–output pairs; instead, it learns from the **consequences of its actions** through a trial-and-error search guided by a scalar reward signal $R_t$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — Training a Pet Dog**
> When you teach a dog to fetch a ball, you don't show it a dataset of "correct" and "incorrect" fetches. Instead, you give it a **treat (reward)** every time it does the right thing, and ignore (or say "no") when it does the wrong thing. Over time, the dog figures out the **sequence of actions** that maximize the number of treats. This is precisely how an RL agent behaves — it learns a **policy** that maximizes **cumulative reward**.

### The Five Building Blocks of RL

Every RL system is built from these **five mandatory components**:

1. **Agent** — The learner / decision-maker (e.g., the robot, the dog, the chess engine).
2. **Environment** — The external world the agent interacts with.
3. **State ($S_t$)** — A complete description of the environment at time $t$.
4. **Action ($A_t$)** — The move chosen by the agent from action space $\mathcal{A}(s)$.
5. **Reward ($R_{t+1}$)** — A scalar feedback signal indicating how good the action was.

The **policy** $\pi$ and the **value function** $V(s)$ are *learned* quantities that guide the agent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Supervised Learning:** In supervised learning the correct answer (label) is provided for every input. In RL, **no supervisor** is present — the agent must *discover* which actions yield the most reward by trying them.
> 

---

## 1.3 Where Classification & RL Sit in the ML Family

```
            Machine Learning
            /      |       \
   Supervised  Unsupervised  Reinforcement
   Learning     Learning       Learning
   /      \         |              |
Regression  Classification  Clustering
   (Continuous  (Discrete       Dimensionality
    output)      output)        Reduction
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU One-Liner:** *"Classification predicts a **category**; Reinforcement Learning learns a **strategy** to maximize reward."*
> 

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Classification Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

A classification system is built in **five structured stages**:

1. **Data Collection** $\Rightarrow$ Gather a labeled dataset $D = \{(x^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{m}$.
2. **Feature Engineering** $\Rightarrow$ Extract informative, discriminative features from raw inputs.
3. **Model Selection** $\Rightarrow$ Choose a hypothesis family $h_\theta(x)$ (e.g., logistic regression, decision tree, SVM).
4. **Training / Loss Minimization** $\Rightarrow$ Optimize parameters $\theta$ by minimizing a **loss function** $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$.
5. **Inference & Evaluation** $\Rightarrow$ Predict class for new $x$ and evaluate using **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1**.

### Core 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Step 1** — Supervised learning is *data-hungry*; quality of data dictates the upper bound of model performance.
- **Step 2** — The **No Free Lunch Theorem** states that no algorithm is universally best; features are often more important than the algorithm itself.
- **Step 3** — Different hypothesis families (linear vs. non-linear) imply different inductive biases.
- **Step 4** — The loss function encodes *how wrong* a prediction is (e.g., 0–1 loss, log-loss, hinge loss).
- **Step 5** — Accuracy alone is misleading for **imbalanced datasets**; hence we use Precision/Recall/F1.

---

## 2.2 Canonical Classification Algorithms (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Six Algorithms** that KTU examiners love to test on:

### (a) Logistic Regression
- A **linear classifier** that models the probability $P(y=1 \mid x)$ using the **sigmoid (logistic) function**.
- Output: a probability in $[0,1]$; decision rule: predict class $1$ if $P \geq 0.5$, else $0$.

### (b) $k$-Nearest Neighbors ($k$-NN)
- A **non-parametric, instance-based** learner. For a query point, it finds the $k$ closest training points (using Euclidean / Manhattan distance) and assigns the **majority class label**.

### (c) Naive Bayes
- A **probabilistic classifier** based on **Bayes' Theorem** with the *naive* assumption of conditional independence between features.
- Fast, works well on high-dimensional text data (spam filtering).

### (d) Decision Tree
- A **non-parametric, hierarchical** model. Splits the feature space using criteria such as **Gini Impurity** or **Information Gain (Entropy)**.
- Highly interpretable; prone to overfitting (controlled via pruning).

### (e) Support Vector Machine (SVM)
- A **maximum-margin classifier** that finds the hyperplane which **maximizes the geometric margin** between two classes.
- Uses **kernel trick** (linear, polynomial, RBF) for non-linear decision boundaries.

### (f) Artificial Neural Networks (ANN)
- A **non-linear, multi-layer** function approximator. Each neuron computes a weighted sum followed by a non-linear activation (ReLU, sigmoid, tanh).
- The output layer uses **softmax** for multiclass classification.

---

## 2.3 Reinforcement Learning — Mathematical Framework

### Markov Decision Process (MDP)

An RL problem is formally modeled as an **MDP** defined by the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $\mathcal{S}$ | Set of all possible **states** |
| $\mathcal{A}$ | Set of all possible **actions** |
| $P(s' \mid s, a)$ | **Transition probability** — probability of moving to $s'$ from $s$ after action $a$ |
| $R(s, a, s')$ | **Reward function** — immediate reward for the transition |
| $\gamma \in [0,1]$ | **Discount factor** — weight for future rewards |

### Key Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core RL Equations (KTU Must-Know):**

**1. Policy $\pi(a \mid s)$:**
$$
\pi(a \mid s) = P(A_t = a \mid S_t = s)
$$
This is the probability that the agent picks action $a$ when in state $s$.

**2. Return $G_t$ (Cumulative discounted reward):**
$$
G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}
$$

**3. State-Value Function $V_\pi(s)$:**
$$
V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]
$$
Expected return when starting in state $s$ and following policy $\pi$.

**4. Action-Value Function $Q_\pi(s,a)$:**
$$
Q_\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ G_t \mid S_t = s, A_t = a \right]
$$
Expected return when taking action $a$ in state $s$ and then following $\pi$.

**5. Bellman Optimality Equation:**
$$
V^*(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a)\!\left[ R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^*(s') \right]
$$

**6. Q-Learning Update Rule (off-policy TD learning):**
$$
Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha \!\left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \right]
$$
where $\alpha$ is the **learning rate**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **The following table summarizes every high-yield formula for this topic.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Used For |
|---|---------|---------|----------|
| 1 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Logistic Regression |
| 2 | Log-Loss (Binary) | $\mathcal{L} = -\!\left[ y \log \hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y}) \right]$ | Classification loss |
| 3 | Softmax | $P(y_i \mid x) = \dfrac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}$ | Multiclass output |
| 4 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(y \mid x) = \dfrac{P(x \mid y) P(y)}{P(x)}$ | Naive Bayes |
| 5 | Euclidean Distance | $d(x, x') = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n} (x_j - x'_j)^2}$ | $k$-NN |
| 6 | Gini Impurity | $G = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$ | Decision Tree |
| 7 | Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Decision Tree |
| 8 | Information Gain | $IG = H(\text{parent}) - \sum \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | Feature split |
| 9 | SVM Margin | $\text{margin} = \dfrac{2}{\Vert w \Vert}$ | Max-margin theory |
| 10 | Return (RL) | $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}$ | Cumulative reward |
| 11 | Bellman Eqn | $V^*(s) = \max_{a} \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) [R + \gamma V^*(s')]$ | Optimal value |
| 12 | Q-Update | $Q \leftarrow Q + \alpha [r + \gamma \max Q - Q]$ | Q-Learning |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Healthcare** | Tumor classification (malignant / benign) — Logistic Regression, SVM |
| **Email Systems** | Spam filtering — Naive Bayes |
| **Finance** | Credit card fraud detection — Decision Trees, Random Forests |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Self-driving cars learn lane-keeping via **Deep RL** |
| **Robotics** | Robotic arm grasping — Q-Learning, Policy Gradient |
| **Game AI** | AlphaGo, Atari agents — Deep Q-Networks (DQN) |
| **NLP** | Sentiment analysis (positive/negative) — Multilabel text classification |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why the Sigmoid Function for Logistic Regression?

### Setup

We want a function $h_\theta(x) \in [0, 1]$ that represents $P(y=1 \mid x; \theta)$ for binary classification.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Start with the linear regression hypothesis:**
$$
z = \theta^T x
$$

**Step 2 — We need to map $z \in (-\infty, +\infty)$ to a probability in $[0, 1]$.**

**Step 3 — Use the logistic (sigmoid) function:**
$$
h_\theta(x) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}
$$

**Step 4 — Show the logistic transform comes from the log-odds (logit).** The log-odds of an event is $\log\!\left(\dfrac{P}{1-P}\right)$. Setting log-odds equal to a linear function:
$$
\log\!\left(\frac{P(y=1 \mid x)}{1 - P(y=1 \mid x)}\right) = \theta^T x
$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate both sides:**
$$
\frac{P(y=1 \mid x)}{1 - P(y=1 \mid x)} = e^{\theta^T x}
$$

**Step 6 — Let $p = P(y=1 \mid x)$. Solve for $p$:**
$$
\frac{p}{1-p} = e^{\theta^T x} \quad\Rightarrow\quad p = e^{\theta^T x} (1-p)
$$

**Step 7 — Collect $p$ terms on the left:**
$$
p + p \cdot e^{\theta^T x} = e^{\theta^T x}
$$

**Step 8 — Factor $p$:**
$$
p \left( 1 + e^{\theta^T x} \right) = e^{\theta^T x}
$$

**Step 9 — Divide:**
$$
p = \frac{e^{\theta^T x}}{1 + e^{\theta^T x}}
$$

**Step 10 — Multiply numerator & denominator by $e^{-\theta^T x}$ to recover the canonical sigmoid form:**
$$
p = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}} = \sigma(\theta^T x)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The sigmoid naturally arises from the assumption that the **log-odds** of the class label is a linear function of the input features. This is the foundational derivation for **Logistic Regression**.
> 

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Logistic Regression Cost Function (Cross-Entropy)

### Why Not Use MSE?

If we used Mean Squared Error, the loss surface becomes **non-convex** for sigmoid outputs, making optimization hard.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Likelihood of the dataset (i.i.d. samples):**
$$
L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(y^{(i)} \mid x^{(i)}; \theta)
$$

**Step 2 — For a single example, $P(y \mid x; \theta) = h_\theta(x)^y \cdot (1 - h_\theta(x))^{1-y}$:**
$$
L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} h_\theta(x^{(i)})^{y^{(i)}} \cdot (1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)}))^{1-y^{(i)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the log-likelihood (to convert products to sums):**
$$
\ell(\theta) = \log L(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]
$$

**Step 4 — We want to *maximize* the log-likelihood, equivalent to *minimize* the negative:**
$$
J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1-y^{(i)}) \log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — The gradient of $J(\theta)$ has a beautifully simple form:**
$$
\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** The gradient of cross-entropy loss has the same elegant form as the gradient of MSE in linear regression — namely, (prediction $-$ truth) $\times$ input. This is a favourite KTU viva question.
> 

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bellman Equation for $V_\pi(s)$

### Setup

We want to express the value of a state recursively — in terms of the value of the *next* state.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of $V_\pi(s)$:**
$$
V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ G_t \mid S_t = s \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the definition of the return $G_t$:**
$$
V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^2 R_{t+3} + \cdots \;\middle|\; S_t = s \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Split the immediate reward and the discounted future return:**
$$
V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma \!\left( R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \cdots \right) \;\middle|\; S_t = s \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the bracketed term as $G_{t+1}$:**
$$
V_\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1} \;\middle|\; S_t = s \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the law of iterated expectations; condition first on $A_t$, then on $S_{t+1}$:**
$$
V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s,a,s') + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_\pi\!\left[ G_{t+1} \mid S_{t+1} = s' \right] \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the inner expectation as $V_\pi(s')$:**
$$
V_\pi(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \mid s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s,a,s') + \gamma V_\pi(s') \right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bellman Equation** is the recursive backbone of nearly every RL algorithm. It expresses the value of "now" in terms of the value of "next."
> 

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Q-Learning Update Rule

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Start with the tabular estimate $Q(s,a)$.** We want to nudge it toward the **TD target**:**
$$
\text{TD Target} = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a')
$$

**Step 2 — The TD error (also called the *advantage of surprise*):**
$$
\delta_t = r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply a learning rate $\alpha$ update:**
$$
Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha \cdot \delta_t
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $\delta_t$ explicitly:**
$$
Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha \!\left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convergence Guarantee:** Q-Learning is proven to converge to the optimal $Q^*$ as long as **all state-action pairs are visited infinitely often** and $\alpha$ is decayed appropriately (Robbins-Monro conditions).
> 

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Classification vs Reinforcement Learning

| Dimension | Classification | Reinforcement Learning |
|-----------|----------------|------------------------|
| **Learning signal** | Ground-truth label $y$ | Scalar reward $r$ |
| **Feedback type** | Direct, immediate | Delayed, sparse |
| **Goal** | Predict $y$ from $x$ | Maximize $\sum R_t$ |
| **Data** | Static dataset $D$ | Sequential experience $(s, a, r, s')$ |
| **Exploration** | Not required | Essential (explore vs. exploit) |
| **Time** | IID samples assumed | Sequential, time-dependent |
| **Output** | Class label | Policy $\pi$ / Action |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, F1, AUC | Cumulative reward, regret |
| **Examples** | Spam detection, MNIST | Game AI, robotics |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Reinforcement Learning Agent-Environment Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Agent] -->|Action A_t| B[Environment]
    B -->|Reward R_{t+1}| A
    B -->|Next State S_{t+1}| A
    A -->|Policy pi updates| A
    A -->|Value V updates| A
    classDef agentBox fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef envBox fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A agentBox
    class B envBox
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the loop:** At time $t$, the agent observes state $S_t$, picks action $A_t$ via policy $\pi$, the environment transitions to $S_{t+1}$ and returns reward $R_{t+1}$. The cycle continues until a terminal state.
> 

---

## 4.2 Flowchart: Classification Pipeline

```mermaid

    A[Raw Data] --> B[Data Preprocessing]
    B --> C[Feature Engineering]
    C --> D[Train/Test Split]

    F --> G[Predict on Test Set]
    G --> H[Compute Metrics]
    H --> I{Performance OK?}
    I -->|No| J[Tune Hyperparameters]
    J --> F
    I -->|Yes| K[Deploy Model]

    classDef decisionBox fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G,H,J,K stepBox
    class I decisionBox
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Types of Supervised Learning — Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Q-Learning Module

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Initialize Q-Table: Q s a = 0] --> S2[Observe current state S_t]
    S2 --> S3{Explore or Exploit}
    S3 -->|epsilon-greedy| S4[Select Action A_t]
    S3 -->|epsilon-greedy| S4
    S4 --> S5[Execute A_t in Environment]
    S5 --> S6[Observe R_t and S_{t+1}]
    S6 --> S7[Compute TD Target]
    S7 --> S8[Update Q s a]
    S8 --> S9{Terminal State?}
    S9 -->|No| S2
    S9 -->|Yes| S10[Episode End: Decay epsilon and alpha]
    S10 --> S11{Convergence Reached?}
    S11 -->|No| S2
    S11 -->|Yes| S12[Output Optimal Policy pi star]

    class S12 ioBox
```

## 4.5 Decision Boundary Visualization (Conceptual Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Class A Region] --- B[Linear Decision Boundary] --- C[Class B Region]
    A --> A1[Feature 1 lt threshold]
    C --> C1[Feature 1 gt threshold]
    style A fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style B fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,stroke-dasharray:5 5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** The decision boundary is the *surface* where the classifier is *equally uncertain* between two classes. For binary classification with sigmoid output, this is the contour $P(y=1 \mid x) = 0.5$, equivalently $\theta^T x = 0$.
>
