<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between supervised learning and reinforcement learning. List any two applications of reinforcement learning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Reinforcement Learning |
|--------|--------------------|------------------------|
| Feedback | Labeled input–output pairs | Scalar reward signal |
| Goal | Learn mapping $f: X \to Y$ | Learn policy $\pi$ to maximize $\sum R$ |

**Two applications of RL:**
1. **Autonomous driving** — agent learns lane-keeping and obstacle avoidance.
2. **Game playing AI** — e.g., AlphaGo, chess engines that learn by self-play.

*(1 mark for table/differentiation, 2 marks for applications.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is classification? Explain binary and multiclass classification with an example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Classification (1 mark):** A supervised learning task where the output variable $Y$ is a discrete category.
- **Binary classification (1 mark):** Two mutually exclusive classes. *Example:* Email → Spam / Not Spam.
- **Multiclass classification (1 mark):** $k \geq 3$ classes, one label per sample. *Example:* Handwritten digit recognition (0–9).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When answering Part B questions on RL, students *frequently* confuse the **policy**, **value function**, and **Q-function**. Memorize the three as **distinct** entities: $\pi$ = *what to do*, $V$ = *how good is this state*, $Q$ = *how good is this state-action*. Mixing them up costs **3–4 marks** instantly.
> 

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **reinforcement learning** framework. Define the **Markov Decision Process (MDP)** and discuss its components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Bellman Optimality Equation** for the state-value function $V^*(s)$. Explain how the **discount factor $\gamma$** influences the agent's behaviour with $\gamma = 0$, $\gamma = 1$, and $0 < \gamma < 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Reinforcement Learning Framework (3 marks):**

The RL framework consists of an **agent** that interacts with an **environment** in discrete time steps. The block diagram from Section 4.1 should be drawn.

**MDP Definition (2 marks):**

An MDP is defined by the 5-tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$:
- $\mathcal{S}$ — finite set of states
- $\mathcal{A}$ — finite set of actions
- $P(s' \mid s, a)$ — transition probability
- $R(s, a, s')$ — reward function
- $\gamma \in [0,1]$ — discount factor

**Markov Property (1 mark):**

> The future depends only on the present state, not on the history of past states.

**Working cycle (1 mark):**

At each step $t$: agent observes $S_t$ $\to$ selects $A_t = \pi(S_t)$ $\to$ receives $R_{t+1}$ and $S_{t+1}$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**Derivation of Bellman Optimality (4 marks):**

Starting from $V^*(s) = \max_\pi V_\pi(s)$ and following the same steps as in Section 3.3, but taking the $\max$ over actions:

$$
V^*(s) = \max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \sum_{s' \in \mathcal{S}} P(s' \mid s, a) \left[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^*(s') \right]
$$

**Effect of $\gamma$ (3 marks):**

| Value of $\gamma$ | Behaviour |
|-------------------|-----------|
| $\gamma = 0$ | **Myopic** — agent cares only about immediate reward $R_{t+1}$; ignores the future. |
| $\gamma = 1$ | **Far-sighted** — agent values future rewards as much as immediate ones (no discounting). |
| $0 < \gamma < 1$ | **Balanced** — finite geometric series ensures the return is bounded, future rewards decay exponentially. |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the different **types of classification algorithms** in supervised learning. Compare **Logistic Regression** and **Naive Bayes** classifiers with respect to assumptions, decision boundary, and computational complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a binary classification dataset with two features $x_1, x_2$. The trained logistic regression model has parameters $\theta = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ -2.0 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$ (including bias). For a test point $x = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}$, compute $P(y = 1 \mid x; \theta)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**Types of Classification Algorithms (3 marks):**

1. **Linear** — Logistic Regression, Linear SVM.
2. **Tree-based** — Decision Tree, Random Forest.
3. **Instance-based** — $k$-NN.
4. **Probabilistic** — Naive Bayes, Bayesian Networks.
5. **Neural** — Multi-layer Perceptron, Deep Networks.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Logistic Regression | Naive Bayes |
|--------|---------------------|-------------|
| **Assumption** | Linear decision boundary in log-odds; no feature independence | Conditional independence of features given class |
| **Decision Boundary** | Linear: $\theta^T x = 0$ | Linear in log-probability space (in the binary case) |
| **Complexity** | $O(nd)$ per iteration (gradient descent) | $O(nd)$ training, $O(d)$ prediction (very fast) |
| **Data Need** | Large dataset for stable estimates | Works well with small data |
| **Output** | Calibrated probability | Probability (with independence caveat) |

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute the linear score $z$:**
$$
z = \theta^T x = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values:**
$$
z = 1.5 + (-2.0)(1) + (0.5)(2) = 1.5 - 2.0 + 1.0 = 0.5
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the sigmoid function:**
$$
P(y=1 \mid x; \theta) = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-0.5}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation:**
$$
e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer:**
$$
P(y=1 \mid x; \theta) = \frac{1}{1 + 0.6065} = \frac{1}{1.6065} \approx 0.6225
$$

**Decision (for full credit):** Since $0.6225 \geq 0.5$, classify the point as $y = 1$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfalls for Q-B(b):**
> 1. Forgetting the **bias term** $\theta_0 = 1.5$ (lose 1 mark).
> 2. Computing $e^{0.5}$ instead of $e^{-0.5}$ — a careless sign error (lose 1 mark).
> 3. Failing to make the **classification decision** (predict class label) — examiners deduct 1 mark if only the probability is stated.
> 

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorize before entering the exam hall):**

### **Classification Essentials**
- [x] Classification = supervised learning with **discrete output**.
- [x] Three types: **Binary**, **Multiclass**, **Multilabel**.
- [x] Six canonical algorithms: Logistic Regression, $k$-NN, Naive Bayes, Decision Tree, SVM, ANN.
- [x] Sigmoid maps $\mathbb{R} \to [0,1]$; softmax generalizes to $k$ classes.
- [x] Cross-entropy loss has a *convex* surface for logistic regression.
- [x] Naive Bayes assumes **conditional independence** of features.
- [x] Decision trees use **Gini** or **Entropy** as split criteria.
- [x] SVM finds the **maximum-margin** hyperplane.

### **Reinforcement Learning Essentials**
- [x] RL = learning from **interaction**, not from labeled data.
- [x] Five components: **Agent, Environment, State, Action, Reward**.
- [x] An MDP is defined by $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$.
- [x] **Policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ = the agent's strategy.
- [x] **Return** $G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^k R_{t+k+1}$.
- [x] **Bellman Equation** decomposes value recursively.
- [x] **Q-Learning** is an *off-policy* TD control algorithm.
- [x] **Exploration vs Exploitation** is resolved via $\epsilon$-greedy.
- [x] RL applications: game AI, robotics, autonomous vehicles, recommendation systems.

### **High-Yield Numerical Facts to Remember**
- [x] Sigmoid: $\sigma(0) = 0.5$
- [x] $e^{-1} \approx 0.368$, $e^{-0.5} \approx 0.607$
- [x] Discount factor $\gamma$ is **always in $[0, 1]$**.
- [x] When $\gamma = 0$, the agent is **short-sighted (greedy)**.

### **Frequently Confused Pairs (Examiners Love These)**
- [x] **Policy** vs **Plan** — Policy is a *function* from states to actions.
- [x] **Value** $V(s)$ vs **Q-Value** $Q(s,a)$ — $V$ is for a state, $Q$ is for a state-action pair.
- [x] **Exploration** vs **Exploitation** — Trying new actions vs. using known best action.
- [x] **On-policy** (SARSA) vs **Off-policy** (Q-Learning) — Learn from own action vs. learn from optimal action.
- [x] **Classification** vs **Regression** — Discrete vs continuous output.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regression, Bayes' Theorem, Classification - Bayes' Decision Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regression, Bayes' Theorem, Classification - Bayes' Decision Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Regression

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
Regression is a **supervised learning** paradigm that models the relationship between a continuous scalar (or vector) dependent variable $y$ and one or more independent variables $x$, by learning a mapping function $f: X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ from a labeled training dataset $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, such that the prediction $\hat{y} = f(x)$ minimizes a chosen loss function (typically the squared error).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the syllabus, regression is positioned as the *first* supervised paradigm to be studied because it sets the foundation for loss-function thinking (MSE, SSE, MLE), gradient optimization, and the bias-variance trade-off — concepts that re-appear across ML.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Cricket Coach" Story:**
Imagine a cricket coach who records how many hours each of his 100 students practised (`x`) and what their final match score was (`y`). After a season, he plots a graph. He notices a *trend*: "More practice → higher score." He now draws a single straight line through the cloud of points that best summarizes this trend. That line — the **regression line** — lets him predict any new student's score from their practice hours. That is regression in plain English: *fitting a smooth rule to noisy data so you can predict a number.*

---

### 1.2 Bayes' Theorem

**Formal Definition:**
Bayes' Theorem is a fundamental rule of probability that describes how to update the probability of a hypothesis $\omega$ after observing evidence $x$. It expresses the **posterior** probability $P(\omega \vert x)$ in terms of the **prior** $P(\omega)$, the **likelihood** $p(x \vert \omega)$, and the **evidence** $p(x)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Equation:** $P(\omega \vert x) = \dfrac{p(x \vert \omega) \, P(\omega)}{p(x)}$

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Doctor's Diagnosis" Story:**
Suppose a disease affects 1 in 1000 people. A test is 99% accurate (correctly flags sick people 99% of the time, and correctly clears healthy people 99% of the time). You test positive. *Are you definitely sick?* Common sense screams "YES!" But Bayes' theorem whispers "Wait." Because the disease is rare, the *vast majority* of the population is healthy — and even a 1% false-positive rate on a huge healthy population produces many more false alarms than true cases. Bayes' theorem lets the doctor compute the **real** probability: roughly 9%! This counterintuitive result is exactly why Bayes' theorem is revolutionary: it forces us to combine *prior belief* with *new evidence* rigorously.

---

### 1.3 Classification

**Formal Definition:**
Classification is a supervised learning task where the goal is to assign an input vector $x$ to one of a finite set of $c$ discrete categories (classes) $\omega_1, \omega_2, \dots, \omega_c$. The output is a categorical label rather than a continuous value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Binary vs Multi-class:** When $c = 2$, it is *binary classification* (e.g., spam vs ham). When $c > 2$, it is *multi-class* (e.g., digit recognition 0–9).

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Mailroom Sorter" Story:**
A post-office worker looks at every letter and decides: "Domestic or International?" Then "Local, National, or Overseas?" Each letter is a vector $x$ (weight, dimensions, address pattern), and each category is a class $\omega_j$. The worker has internalized a *decision rule* — a function from features to class — that may be either deterministic (a hard rule) or probabilistic (a soft rule that says "70% sure it's international, decide it"). That worker is performing classification.

---

### 1.4 Bayes' Decision Theory (The Core of Probabilistic Classification)

**Formal Definition:**
Bayes' Decision Theory is the **probabilistic framework that provides the optimal decision rule for classification under uncertainty**, by minimizing the expected loss (risk) associated with a decision. When the loss is the **0–1 loss** (correct decision costs 0, every error costs 1), the Bayes-optimal rule reduces to choosing the class $\omega_j$ that **maximizes the posterior probability** $P(\omega_j \vert x)$ — also known as the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** rule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Theorem (KTU High-Yield):** *For minimum probability of error, classify $x$ into $\omega_j$ if*
> $$P(\omega_j \vert x) > P(\omega_k \vert x) \quad \forall \, k \neq j$$
> *Equivalently, the Bayes' error rate is the irreducible floor of any classifier.*

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Insurance Underwriter" Story:**
An insurance underwriter must decide: should a 40-year-old male with a 20-a-day smoking habit be charged a "low risk" or "high risk" premium? The underwriter does not know with certainty whether he will fall sick. She uses two ingredients: (a) the **base rate** of illness in smokers (prior), and (b) the **test/medical data** for this specific person (likelihood). Bayes' decision theory tells her exactly how to *combine* these to make the *expected-loss-minimizing* decision. If the cost of misclassifying a sick person as healthy (large claim!) is far higher than the opposite error, the optimal threshold *shifts* — this is the heart of decision theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Spam filtering (Gmail), medical diagnosis (CAD systems), credit-card fraud detection, autonomous-vehicle pedestrian recognition, and biometric authentication all rely on Bayes' decision theory either directly or as a *theoretical benchmark* against which other classifiers (SVM, neural networks) are evaluated.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Linear Regression — Operational Logic

1. **Model Assumption:** The true relationship is approximately linear — $y_i = w_0 + w_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i$, where $\varepsilon_i$ is zero-mean Gaussian noise.
2. **Loss Function:** Minimize the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**
   $$J(w) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_i - \hat{y}_i\right)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_i - (w_0 + w_1 x_i)\right)^2$$
3. **Optimization:** Take partial derivatives with respect to $w_0, w_1$ and set them to zero → **Normal Equations** (analytical closed-form).
4. **Prediction:** For a new $x^*$, output $\hat{y}^* = \hat{w}_0 + \hat{w}_1 x^*$.
5. **Quality Metric:** $R^2$ (coefficient of determination) measures the fraction of variance explained.
6. **Why squared error?** Under Gaussian noise, minimizing SSE is equivalent to **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** — the most probable parameters under the data.

### 2.2 Bayes' Theorem — Operational Logic

1. **Product Rule of Probability:** $P(\omega, x) = P(\omega \vert x) P(x) = P(x \vert \omega) P(\omega)$.
2. **Equate and Rearrange:** Since the joint probability has two valid expansions,
   $$P(\omega \vert x) \, p(x) = p(x \vert \omega) \, P(\omega)$$
3. **Solve for Posterior:** Divide by $p(x)$ to obtain Bayes' formula.
4. **Evidence via Total Probability:** $p(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} p(x \vert \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$ — the denominator ensures posterior probabilities sum to 1.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bayes' Theorem lets us reason "in reverse":** we observe the *effect* $x$ and want to infer the *cause* $\omega$.

### 2.3 Bayes' Decision Theory — Operational Logic

1. **Setup:** $c$ classes $\omega_1, \dots, \omega_c$ with prior probabilities $P(\omega_j)$ and class-conditional densities $p(x \vert \omega_j)$.
2. **Loss Function $\lambda_{ij}$:** Cost of deciding $\omega_i$ when the true class is $\omega_j$. For 0-1 loss: $\lambda_{ij} = 0$ if $i=j$, else $1$.
3. **Conditional Risk:** $R(\alpha_i \vert x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda_{ij} \, P(\omega_j \vert x)$ — expected loss if we take action $\alpha_i$ (decide $\omega_i$) on observation $x$.
4. **Bayes' Decision Rule:** Choose $\alpha_i$ that **minimizes** $R(\alpha_i \vert x)$.
5. **0-1 Loss Simplification:** $R(\alpha_i \vert x) = \sum_{j \neq i} P(\omega_j \vert x) = 1 - P(\omega_i \vert x)$. Minimizing this is equivalent to **maximizing** the posterior — i.e., **MAP decision**.
6. **Two-class special case:** Decide $\omega_1$ if $P(\omega_1 \vert x) > P(\omega_2 \vert x)$; the **decision boundary** is the locus where $P(\omega_1 \vert x) = P(\omega_2 \vert x)$.

---

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Notation Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Linear Regression Model | $\hat{y} = w_0 + w_1 x$ | $w_0$: intercept, $w_1$: slope |
| 2 | SSE Loss | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | units: (output)$^2$ |
| 3 | Slope (1-D) | $w_1 = \dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | least-squares closed form |
| 4 | Intercept | $w_0 = \bar{y} - w_1 \bar{x}$ | where $\bar{x}, \bar{y}$ are sample means |
| 5 | Product Rule | $P(\omega, x) = P(\omega \vert x)\, p(x)$ | joint = conditional × marginal |
| 6 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(\omega_j \vert x) = \dfrac{p(x \vert \omega_j)\, P(\omega_j)}{p(x)}$ | posterior in terms of prior × likelihood / evidence |
| 7 | Evidence (Total Prob.) | $p(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} p(x \vert \omega_j)\, P(\omega_j)$ | denominator of Bayes |
| 8 | Conditional Risk | $R(\alpha_i \vert x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda_{ij}\, P(\omega_j \vert x)$ | expected loss of action $\alpha_i$ |
| 9 | Overall Risk | $R = \int R(\alpha(x) \vert x)\, p(x)\, dx$ | integrated over input space |
| 10 | MAP Rule (0-1 Loss) | Decide $\omega^*$ = $\arg\max_j P(\omega_j \vert x)$ | minimum error rate classifier |
| 11 | Likelihood Ratio Test | $\dfrac{p(x \vert \omega_1)}{p(x \vert \omega_2)} \gtrless \dfrac{\lambda_{12} - \lambda_{22}}{\lambda_{21} - \lambda_{11}} \cdot \dfrac{P(\omega_2)}{P(\omega_1)}$ | two-class discriminant |
| 12 | Bayes' Error | $P(\text{error}) = 1 - \int \max_j P(\omega_j \vert x)\, p(x)\, dx$ | irreducible minimum error |
| 13 | Discriminant Function | $g_i(x) = P(\omega_i \vert x)$ (or any monotonic transform) | equivalent MAP decisions |
| 14 | $R^2$ Score | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | ranges in $(-\infty, 1]$, $1$ is perfect fit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Mantra:** *Bayes' theorem inverts the dependency; Bayes' decision rule turns the inversion into an optimal action.* Together they form the **theoretical gold standard** of classification.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Bayes' Decision Theory |
|--------|---------------------------------------|
| Medical AI | Diagnose disease $j$ given symptoms $x$ by maximizing $P(\omega_j \vert x)$ |
| Finance | Fraud vs legitimate transaction classifier with asymmetric loss (fraud-miss is costlier) |
| Autonomous Vehicles | Pedestrian / vehicle / sign classification with safety-critical loss |
| NLP | Spam filter — Bernoulli/multinomial Naive Bayes |
| Manufacturing | Defect classification on production line with prior shift over time |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Least-Squares Coefficients of Simple Linear Regression

**Goal:** Find $w_0$ and $w_1$ that minimize $J(w_0, w_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_i - w_0 - w_1 x_i\right)^2$.

**Step 1 — Take partial derivative with respect to $w_0$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_i - w_0 - w_1 x_i\right) = 0
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the sum:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i - N w_0 - w_1 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $N$ and recognize the sample means $\bar{y}$ and $\bar{x}$:**

$$
\bar{y} - w_0 - w_1 \bar{x} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w_0 = \bar{y} - w_1 \bar{x}
$$

This is the **intercept formula** — it says the regression line always passes through the centroid $(\bar{x}, \bar{y})$.

**Step 4 — Take partial derivative with respect to $w_1$:**

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \left(y_i - w_0 - w_1 x_i\right) = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $w_0$ from Step 3 and expand:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i y_i - w_0 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i - w_1 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2 = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Use the centered variables $x_i' = x_i - \bar{x}$, $y_i' = y_i - \bar{y}$:**

After substitution and simplification, we obtain the closed-form slope:

$$
w_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:**

- **Numerator** = sample covariance between $x$ and $y$ (× $N$).
- **Denominator** = sample variance of $x$ (× $N$).
- So $w_1 = \dfrac{\text{Cov}(x, y)}{\text{Var}(x)}$ — the slope measures *how much $y$ moves per unit change in $x$*, scaled by their joint co-movement.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Bayes' Theorem from First Principles

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of conditional probability:**

$$
P(\omega \vert x) = \frac{P(\omega \cap x)}{p(x)} \quad \text{and} \quad p(x \vert \omega) = \frac{P(\omega \cap x)}{P(\omega)}
$$

**Step 2 — Both expressions describe the same joint event $P(\omega \cap x)$. Equate them:**

$$
P(\omega \vert x) \, p(x) = p(x \vert \omega) \, P(\omega)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the posterior $P(\omega \vert x)$ by dividing both sides by $p(x)$:**

$$
P(\omega \vert x) = \frac{p(x \vert \omega)\, P(\omega)}{p(x)}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the evidence $p(x)$ using the law of total probability:**

$$
p(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} p(x \vert \omega_j)\, P(\omega_j)
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back for the multi-class posterior:**

$$
P(\omega_j \vert x) = \frac{p(x \vert \omega_j)\, P(\omega_j)}{\sum_{k=1}^{c} p(x \vert \omega_k)\, P(\omega_k)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The denominator is a *normalizing constant* that ensures the posteriors of all classes sum to 1. When we are only interested in **which class wins**, the denominator is identical for all classes and can be dropped — yielding the famous **MAP decision** with just the numerator.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Bayes' Decision Rule for Minimum Error (0-1 Loss)

**Step 1 — Define the loss matrix $\lambda_{ij}$:**

For 0-1 loss: $\lambda_{ij} = 0$ if $i = j$, else $1$.

**Step 2 — Write the conditional risk for deciding class $\omega_i$ given observation $x$:**

$$
R(\alpha_i \vert x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda_{ij}\, P(\omega_j \vert x)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute 0-1 loss and split the sum:**

$$
R(\alpha_i \vert x) = \sum_{j \neq i} 1 \cdot P(\omega_j \vert x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} P(\omega_j \vert x) - P(\omega_i \vert x)
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that $\sum_{j=1}^{c} P(\omega_j \vert x) = 1$ (posteriors sum to 1):**

$$
R(\alpha_i \vert x) = 1 - P(\omega_i \vert x)
$$

**Step 5 — Bayes' decision rule — minimize the conditional risk:**

Choose $\omega^*$ such that $R(\alpha^* \vert x)$ is minimum, i.e., $P(\omega^* \vert x)$ is **maximum**.

$$
\omega^* = \arg\max_{j \in \{1, \dots, c\}} P(\omega_j \vert x)
$$

**Step 6 — For the special two-class case, the decision boundary is the locus where:**

$$
P(\omega_1 \vert x) = P(\omega_2 \vert x)
$$

i.e., where the posterior probabilities of the two classes are exactly equal.

**Step 7 — Using Bayes' theorem, this boundary can be rewritten purely in terms of likelihoods and priors:**

$$
\frac{p(x \vert \omega_1)}{p(x \vert \omega_2)} = \frac{P(\omega_2)}{P(\omega_1)}
$$

The left side is called the **likelihood ratio** $\Lambda(x)$, and the right side is the **threshold** $\theta$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: General Loss Matrix — Likelihood Ratio Test

**Setup:** In some applications the cost of false-positive ≠ cost of false-negative. The general loss matrix has four entries for two classes:

$$
\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda_{11} & \lambda_{12} \\ \lambda_{21} & \lambda_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$

where $\lambda_{ij}$ = loss of deciding $\omega_i$ when truth is $\omega_j$.

**Step 1 — Compute conditional risks:**

$$
R(\alpha_1 \vert x) = \lambda_{11} P(\omega_1 \vert x) + \lambda_{12} P(\omega_2 \vert x)
$$

$$
R(\alpha_2 \vert x) = \lambda_{21} P(\omega_1 \vert x) + \lambda_{22} P(\omega_2 \vert x)
$$

**Step 2 — Decide $\omega_1$ if $R(\alpha_1 \vert x) < R(\alpha_2 \vert x)$:**

$$
\lambda_{11} P(\omega_1 \vert x) + \lambda_{12} P(\omega_2 \vert x) < \lambda_{21} P(\omega_1 \vert x) + \lambda_{22} P(\omega_2 \vert x)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute posteriors by Bayes and simplify:**

After algebraic rearrangement we obtain the **likelihood ratio test**:

$$
\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x \vert \omega_1)}{p(x \vert \omega_2)} \gtrless \frac{\lambda_{12} - \lambda_{22}}{\lambda_{21} - \lambda_{11}} \cdot \frac{P(\omega_2)}{P(\omega_1)} = \theta
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** When $\lambda_{12} - \lambda_{22} = \lambda_{21} - \lambda_{11}$ (symmetric loss), the loss-fraction simplifies to 1, and we recover the pure MAP rule. In **asymmetric** applications (e.g., cancer screening — missing a tumour is far costlier than a false alarm), the threshold $\theta$ shifts, biasing the classifier toward the more critical class.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Decision Boundaries

| Loss Type | Threshold $\theta$ | Decision Rule |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|
| 0-1 Loss (equal cost) | $\theta = P(\omega_2)/P(\omega_1)$ | $\Lambda(x) \gtrless \theta$ (LRT) |
| Symmetric loss | $\theta = P(\omega_2)/P(\omega_1)$ | identical to MAP |
| Asymmetric (asymmetric loss) | $\theta = \dfrac{(\lambda_{12}-\lambda_{22})}{(\lambda_{21}-\lambda_{11})} \cdot \dfrac{P(\omega_2)}{P(\omega_1)}$ | threshold shifts |
| Minimum-risk Bayes | minimize $R(\alpha_i \vert x)$ directly | choose class with smallest risk |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bayes' Decision Theory — Functional Flow Architecture

The block diagram below traces the journey from raw observation $x$ to the optimal decision, with explicit intermediate quantities.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Observation x] --> B[Class-Conditional Densities p(x given omega j)]
    C[Prior Probabilities P omega j] --> D[Combine with likelihoods]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Compute Posterior P omega j given x via Bayes Theorem]
    E --> F[Compute Conditional Risk R alpha i given x]
    G[Loss Matrix lambda ij] --> F
    F --> H{Choose action minimizing R}
    H --> I[Bayes Optimal Decision omega star]
    E --> J[MAP shortcut: argmax of posterior]
    J --> I
    H --> K[Bayes Error irreducible floor]
    I --> K
```

### 4.2 Linear Regression — Pipeline Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Training Data x i, y i] --> B[Define Model y hat = w0 + w1 x]
    B --> C[Define Loss J = sum y i minus y hat i squared]
    C --> D[Minimize J via Normal Equations]
    D --> E[Closed Form w0, w1]
    E --> F[Predict y hat for new x star]
    F --> G[Evaluate R squared or MSE]
```

### 4.3 Three-Way Comparison: Regression, Bayes' Theorem, Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Regression] --> R1[Continuous output y]
    R1 --> R2[Minimize squared error]
    R1 --> R3[Closed form normal equations]
    BT[Bayes Theorem] --> BT1[Probabilistic inversion]
    BT1 --> BT2[Posterior = likelihood x prior / evidence]
    BT2 --> BT3[Foundation for probabilistic classifiers]
    C[Classification] --> C1[Discrete class label omega j]
    C1 --> C2[Bayes Decision Rule: MAP]
    C1 --> C3[Discriminant functions g i x]
    BT3 --> C2
    BT3 --> C3
    R2 --> S[Supervised Learning Family]
    C2 --> S
    C3 --> S
```

### 4.4 Decision Boundary Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Space X] --> B{Is x in Region R1?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Decide omega 1]
    B -- No --> D[Decide omega 2]
    E[P omega 1 given x equals P omega 2 given x] --> B
    E -.defines.-> F[Decision Boundary]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Region $\mathcal{R}_1$ is the locus where $P(\omega_1 \vert x) > P(\omega_2 \vert x)$. The decision boundary is the hypersurface on which the two posteriors are *equal*. For Gaussian class-conditionals with equal covariance, this boundary is **linear**; with unequal covariances it is **quadratic** — a key result of Bayes' decision theory.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
*State Bayes' theorem. Explain each term with one example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Bayes' theorem states that the posterior probability of a hypothesis $\omega_j$ given observation $x$ is:
$$P(\omega_j \vert x) = \frac{p(x \vert \omega_j)\, P(\omega_j)}{p(x)}$$
- $P(\omega_j \vert x)$: **Posterior** — probability of class $\omega_j$ after seeing evidence $x$.
- $P(\omega_j)$: **Prior** — initial belief about class $\omega_j$ before seeing $x$.
- $p(x \vert \omega_j)$: **Likelihood** — probability of observing $x$ assuming class $\omega_j$.
- $p(x)$: **Evidence** — overall probability of observing $x$ under all classes.

**Example:** Given a patient's symptoms $x$, $P(\text{Flu} \vert \text{symptoms})$ uses the prior prevalence of flu, the likelihood of these symptoms in flu patients, and the overall probability of such symptoms in the population.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
*What is the Bayes' decision rule for minimum error rate classification? Derive it from the conditional risk under 0-1 loss.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For 0-1 loss ($\lambda_{ij}=0$ if $i=j$, else $1$), the conditional risk of deciding $\omega_i$ is:
$$R(\alpha_i \vert x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda_{ij}\, P(\omega_j \vert x) = 1 - P(\omega_i \vert x)$$
To minimize risk, we must maximize $P(\omega_i \vert x)$. Therefore the **Bayes' decision rule for minimum error** is:
$$\text{Decide } \omega^* = \arg\max_{j} P(\omega_j \vert x)$$
This is the **Maximum A Posteriori (MAP)** rule.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: each module question is 14 marks, with internal choice between two questions (Q1A / Q1B). You must attempt **one**. Each long question typically has two sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style]*

**(a)** *Derive the least-squares estimates of the slope $w_1$ and intercept $w_0$ in simple linear regression. Clearly state the cost function and the normal equations. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Model form:**
The simple linear regression model is $\hat{y}_i = w_0 + w_1 x_i$.

**Step 2 — Cost function (SSE):**
$$J(w_0, w_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_i - w_0 - w_1 x_i\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Partial derivative w.r.t. $w_0$ and set to zero:**
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_0} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_i - w_0 - w_1 x_i\right) = 0 \Rightarrow \sum y_i = N w_0 + w_1 \sum x_i$$
Dividing by $N$: $\bar{y} = w_0 + w_1 \bar{x}$ ⇒ $w_0 = \bar{y} - w_1 \bar{x}$ (Normal equation 1).

**Step 4 — Partial derivative w.r.t. $w_1$ and set to zero:**
$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_1} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i (y_i - w_0 - w_1 x_i) = 0$$
$$\Rightarrow \sum x_i y_i = w_0 \sum x_i + w_1 \sum x_i^2$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $w_0$ and simplify (centered form):**
Using $x_i' = x_i - \bar{x}$, $y_i' = y_i - \bar{y}$:
$$w_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2} = \frac{\text{Cov}(x, y)}{\text{Var}(x)}$$

**Step 6 — Final formulas:**
$$\boxed{w_1 = \frac{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})}{\sum(x_i-\bar{x})^2}, \quad w_0 = \bar{y} - w_1 \bar{x}}$$

**(b)** *Consider the following 5 data points: $(1, 2), (2, 2.8), (3, 3.6), (4, 4.5), (5, 5.1)$. Fit a linear regression model $y = w_0 + w_1 x$ using least squares. Predict the value at $x = 6$. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the means:**
$\bar{x} = (1+2+3+4+5)/5 = 3$, $\bar{y} = (2+2.8+3.6+4.5+5.1)/5 = 18/5 = 3.6$.

**Step 2 — Build the table of centered values:**

| $i$ | $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i-\bar{x}$ | $y_i-\bar{y}$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})(y_i-\bar{y})$ | $(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ |
|-----|-------|-------|----------------|----------------|------------------------------|-------------------|
| 1   | 1     | 2.0   | -2             | -1.6           | 3.2                          | 4                 |
| 2   | 2     | 2.8   | -1             | -0.8           | 0.8                          | 1                 |
| 3   | 3     | 3.6   | 0              | 0.0            | 0.0                          | 0                 |
| 4   | 4     | 4.5   | 1              | 0.9            | 0.9                          | 1                 |
| 5   | 5     | 5.1   | 2              | 1.5            | 3.0                          | 4                 |
| **Σ** |       |       |                |                | **7.9**                      | **10**            |

**Step 3 — Compute slope and intercept:**
$w_1 = 7.9 / 10 = 0.79$
$w_0 = 3.6 - 0.79 \times 3 = 3.6 - 2.37 = 1.23$

**Step 4 — Regression equation:**
$\hat{y} = 1.23 + 0.79 x$

**Step 5 — Predict at $x = 6$:**
$\hat{y}(6) = 1.23 + 0.79 \times 6 = 1.23 + 4.74 = 5.97$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** The model predicts that at $x=6$, the dependent variable is approximately **5.97**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Style, Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** *Explain Bayes' Decision Theory. For a two-class problem with prior probabilities $P(\omega_1) = 0.6$ and $P(\omega_2) = 0.4$, and class-conditional densities $p(x \vert \omega_1) = 0.5$ and $p(x \vert \omega_2) = 0.3$ at a particular $x$, determine the Bayes-optimal decision using the 0-1 loss criterion. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bayes' Decision Theory (Brief Definition, ~2 marks):**
Bayes' Decision Theory is the probabilistic framework that minimizes the expected loss (risk) when classifying an observation $x$ into one of $c$ classes. The decision rule is:
$$\text{Choose } \omega_i \text{ that minimizes } R(\alpha_i \vert x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda_{ij}\, P(\omega_j \vert x)$$

**Step 2 — Under 0-1 loss, this reduces to MAP:**
$$\omega^* = \arg\max_j P(\omega_j \vert x)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' theorem to compute posteriors:**

Evidence:
$$p(x) = p(x \vert \omega_1) P(\omega_1) + p(x \vert \omega_2) P(\omega_2) = (0.5)(0.6) + (0.3)(0.4) = 0.30 + 0.12 = 0.42$$

Posterior for $\omega_1$:
$$P(\omega_1 \vert x) = \frac{0.5 \times 0.6}{0.42} = \frac{0.30}{0.42} = 0.714$$

Posterior for $\omega_2$:
$$P(\omega_2 \vert x) = \frac{0.3 \times 0.4}{0.42} = \frac{0.12}{0.42} = 0.286$$

**Step 4 — Verification:** $0.714 + 0.286 = 1.000$ ✓

**Step 5 — Apply MAP rule:**
Since $P(\omega_1 \vert x) = 0.714 > 0.286 = P(\omega_2 \vert x)$, the Bayes-optimal decision is $\omega^* = \omega_1$.

**Step 6 — Equivalent likelihood-ratio test:**
Likelihood ratio $\Lambda(x) = 0.5/0.3 = 1.667$. Threshold $\theta = P(\omega_2)/P(\omega_1) = 0.4/0.6 = 0.667$. Since $1.667 > 0.667$, decide $\omega_1$. ✓ Consistent.

**(b)** *For the two-class classification problem with loss matrix*
$$\begin{bmatrix} \lambda_{11}=0 & \lambda_{12}=10 \\ \lambda_{21}=1 & \lambda_{22}=0 \end{bmatrix}$$
*and priors $P(\omega_1) = 0.7, P(\omega_2) = 0.3$, derive the decision rule in terms of the likelihood ratio. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute conditional risks (1 mark):**
$$R(\alpha_1 \vert x) = 0 \cdot P(\omega_1 \vert x) + 10 \cdot P(\omega_2 \vert x) = 10 P(\omega_2 \vert x)$$
$$R(\alpha_2 \vert x) = 1 \cdot P(\omega_1 \vert x) + 0 \cdot P(\omega_2 \vert x) = P(\omega_1 \vert x)$$

**Step 2 — Decide $\omega_1$ if $R(\alpha_1 \vert x) < R(\alpha_2 \vert x)$ (1 mark):**
$$10 P(\omega_2 \vert x) < P(\omega_1 \vert x)$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of likelihoods via Bayes' theorem (1 mark):**
$$P(\omega_j \vert x) = \frac{p(x \vert \omega_j) P(\omega_j)}{p(x)}$$
Substituting:
$$10 \cdot \frac{p(x \vert \omega_2) \cdot 0.3}{p(x)} < \frac{p(x \vert \omega_1) \cdot 0.7}{p(x)}$$
The $p(x)$ cancels:
$$3 \, p(x \vert \omega_2) < 0.7 \, p(x \vert \omega_1)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into likelihood ratio (1 mark):**
$$\frac{p(x \vert \omega_1)}{p(x \vert \omega_2)} > \frac{3}{0.7} = 4.286$$

**Step 5 — Final decision rule (1 mark):**
$$\boxed{\Lambda(x) = \frac{p(x \vert \omega_1)}{p(x \vert \omega_2)} \gtrless 4.286}$$
- If $\Lambda(x) > 4.286$, decide $\omega_1$.
- If $\Lambda(x) < 4.286$, decide $\omega_2$.

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation (1 mark):**
Since $\lambda_{12} = 10$ (cost of misclassifying $\omega_2$ as $\omega_1$) is much greater than $\lambda_{21} = 1$, the classifier is biased **toward declaring $\omega_1$** (because the cost of missing a true $\omega_2$ is high). This is typical in medical screening applications.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalize:**
> 1. **Forgetting the evidence denominator:** Many students write $P(\omega_j \vert x) = p(x \vert \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$ and stop. This is **wrong** unless explicitly comparing and the denominator cancels. Always include $p(x) = \sum p(x \vert \omega_k) P(\omega_k)$.
> 2. **Confusing posterior with likelihood:** $P(\omega \vert x) \neq p(x \vert \omega)$. The first is "cause given effect"; the second is "effect given cause". Bayes' theorem is precisely what lets you swap the conditioning.
> 3. **Sign error in regression derivation:** A single sign mistake in $\partial J / \partial w_1$ cascades to wrong slope and intercept — examiners check the centroid property $\hat{y}(\bar{x}) = \bar{y}$ to catch this.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of Gaussian noise / 0-1 loss:** Marks are reserved for explicitly mentioning the underlying assumption of the SSE loss (homoscedastic Gaussian) and the 0-1 loss when deriving the MAP rule.
> 5. **Confusing "risk" with "error":** Risk is the expected loss under a general loss function; error is the special case of 0-1 loss. Use the correct term.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Regression** models a continuous target $y$ from features $x$ via a learned function $f$.
- [x] **Simple linear regression** assumes $y \approx w_0 + w_1 x$; the cost is **SSE**; the closed-form solution is the **normal equation** $w_1 = \text{Cov}(x,y)/\text{Var}(x)$, $w_0 = \bar{y} - w_1 \bar{x}$.
- [x] **Bayes' Theorem** inverts conditional probabilities: $\text{Posterior} \propto \text{Likelihood} \times \text{Prior}$, normalized by the **evidence** $p(x) = \sum_j p(x \vert \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$.
- [x] **Classification** predicts a discrete class label from $c$ categories $\omega_1, \dots, \omega_c$.
- [x] **Bayes' Decision Theory** is the optimal classification framework — choose the action that minimizes **conditional risk** $R(\alpha_i \vert x) = \sum_j \lambda_{ij} P(\omega_j \vert x)$.
- [x] **0-1 Loss ⇒ MAP Rule:** Decide $\omega^* = \arg\max_j P(\omega_j \vert x)$ for minimum error.
- [x] **Two-class decision boundary** is the locus where $P(\omega_1 \vert x) = P(\omega_2 \vert x)$ — equivalently $\Lambda(x) = P(\omega_2)/P(\omega_1)$.
- [x] **General Loss ⇒ Likelihood Ratio Test:** $\Lambda(x) = \dfrac{p(x \vert \omega_1)}{p(x \vert \omega_2)} \gtrless \dfrac{\lambda_{12} - \lambda_{22}}{\lambda_{21} - \lambda_{11}} \cdot \dfrac{P(\omega_2)}{P(\omega_1)}$.
- [x] **Bayes' error** $P(\text{error}) = 1 - \int \max_j P(\omega_j \vert x) p(x)\, dx$ is the **irreducible floor** of any classifier.
- [x] **Discriminant function** $g_i(x)$ is any monotonic transform of $P(\omega_i \vert x)$ — the decision rule is unchanged.
- [x] **Engineering takeaway:** Bayes' decision theory gives the *theoretical ceiling*; Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression, and LDA are practical approximations of it.
- [x] **Exam mantra:** "Posterior wins. Risk decides. Prior sets the scene."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discriminant Function (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A discriminant function $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ is a scalar-valued function defined on the feature space, used to assign a pattern $\mathbf{x}$ to one of $c$ classes $\omega_1, \omega_2, \dots, \omega_c$. The classifier assigns $\mathbf{x}$ to class $\omega_i$ if $g_i(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x})$ for all $j \neq i$. The points in the feature space where $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$ for some pair $(i, j)$ form the **decision surface** (or **decision boundary**).

Mathematically, for a $c$-class problem, the decision rule is:

$$\mathbf{x} \in \omega_i \quad \text{if} \quad g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \max_{j=1,\dots,c} \, g_j(\mathbf{x})$$

The decision boundary separating class $\omega_i$ from class $\omega_j$ is the locus:

$$g_{ij}(\mathbf{x}) = g_i(\mathbf{x}) - g_j(\mathbf{x}) = 0$$

For the special two-class case, we often collapse this into a single function $g(\mathbf{x}) = g_1(\mathbf{x}) - g_2(\mathbf{x})$, with the rule $\mathbf{x} \in \omega_1$ if $g(\mathbf{x}) > 0$, and $\mathbf{x} \in \omega_2$ otherwise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Surface (Geometric Viewpoint):** A decision surface is a $(d-1)$-dimensional hypersurface in a $d$-dimensional feature space that partitions the space into regions, each associated with one class. For linear discriminants, these surfaces are **hyperplanes**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **postal worker sorting parcels into three bins** based on weight: Light, Medium, Heavy. You develop a simple mental rule:
- If weight $< 1$ kg → Light bin
- If $1 \le$ weight $< 5$ kg → Medium bin  
- If weight $\ge 5$ kg → Heavy bin

The functions "$<1$", "$<5$" are your **discriminant functions**, and the values $1$ kg and $5$ kg are the **decision boundaries** (thresholds) in 1D feature space. If we now use two features (weight **and** volume), the boundaries become **lines or curves in 2D** — these are decision surfaces. Discriminant functions simply output a "score" for each class, and the highest score wins.

> [!TIP]
> **Why we care:** Designing a good discriminant function $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ is *the* central problem of pattern recognition. The shape of $g$ (linear, quadratic, etc.) determines whether the resulting decision surface is a hyperplane, a conic section, or a more general hypersurface.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structure of a Linear Discriminant Function

The simplest and most important form is the **linear discriminant function**:

$$g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0$$

- $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_d)^T$ is the **weight vector** (also called *parameter vector*).
- $w_0$ is the **bias** (or *threshold*).
- $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_d)^T$ is the feature vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Augmented Vector Form (KTU Board Favourite):** To handle $w_0$ uniformly with the weights, we augment $\mathbf{x} \to \mathbf{y} = (1, x_1, x_2, \dots, x_d)^T$ and define $\mathbf{a} = (w_0, w_1, \dots, w_d)^T$, so that $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}$. This homogenizes the notation and is used heavily in perceptron and LMS derivations.

### 2.2 Two-Class Decision Geometry

The decision boundary for a two-class linear classifier is a **hyperplane** defined by:

$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$$

Key geometric properties of this hyperplane $H$:

- **$\mathbf{w}$ is normal to $H$**: The weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ is perpendicular to the decision surface. This is because for any two points $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2 \in H$:
$$\mathbf{w}^T(\mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_2) = 0$$
- **Distance from origin to $H$**: $d_0 = \dfrac{w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$
- **Signed distance from any point $\mathbf{x}$ to $H$**: $r = \dfrac{g(\mathbf{x})}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \dfrac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$

### 2.3 Multi-Class Classification (c > 2)

There are **three canonical strategies** in KTU syllabus:

1. **$c$ discriminant functions $\,g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_i^T \mathbf{x} + w_{i0}$**, $i = 1, \dots, c$. Assign $\mathbf{x}$ to $\omega_i$ if $g_i$ is largest. This produces $c(c-1)/2$ pairwise decision boundaries.
2. **$c(c-1)/2$ pairwise dichotomizers** $\,g_{ij}(\mathbf{x})$, with arbitrary tie-breaking.
3. **One-vs-rest (OVR)**: $c$ separate two-class problems, $\omega_i$ vs *not* $\omega_i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Strategy 1 can produce **ambiguous (undefined) regions** if the $g_i$'s are not consistent. This is a popular 7-mark question: "Explain how ambiguous regions arise and how to avoid them." We will revisit this in SECTION_5.

### 2.4 Generalization to Non-Linear Decision Surfaces

To obtain **non-linear** decision boundaries (e.g., quadratic, in the form of conic sections), we apply a non-linear transformation $\mathbf{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$ and use a linear discriminant in the transformed space:

$$g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) + w_0$$

For example, for a **quadratic discriminant** in 2D, we use $\mathbf{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) = (1, x_1, x_2, x_1^2, x_2^2, x_1 x_2)^T$, and the resulting decision surface in $\mathbf{x}$-space is a general conic (ellipse, parabola, hyperbola, or pair of lines).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Remarks** |
|:-----:|:------------|:------------|:------------|
| 1 | Linear discriminant (2-class) | $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0$ | $\mathbf{w}$ weights, $w_0$ bias |
| 2 | Augmented form | $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}$, $\mathbf{y} = (1, \mathbf{x}^T)^T$ | Homogeneous notation |
| 3 | Two-class decision rule | $g(\mathbf{x}) > 0 \Rightarrow \omega_1$; else $\omega_2$ | Sign-based |
| 4 | Multi-class decision rule | $\mathbf{x} \in \omega_i$ if $g_i(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x}) \ \forall j \neq i$ | Max-wins |
| 5 | Hyperplane (decision surface) | $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$ | $(d-1)$-D in $d$-D space |
| 6 | Normal vector | $\mathbf{w}$ | Perpendicular to $H$ |
| 7 | Distance from origin | $d_0 = \dfrac{w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Signed |
| 8 | Distance from point $\mathbf{x}$ | $r = \dfrac{g(\mathbf{x})}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ | Algebraic distance |
| 9 | Point on hyperplane (closest to origin) | $\mathbf{x}_p = -\dfrac{w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2}\,\mathbf{w}$ | Lies on $H$ |
| 10 | Pairwise boundary $\omega_i$ vs $\omega_j$ | $(\mathbf{w}_i - \mathbf{w}_j)^T \mathbf{x} + (w_{i0} - w_{j0}) = 0$ | $c(c-1)/2$ such lines |
| 11 | Non-linear discriminant (kernel form) | $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) + w_0$ | Non-linear surface in $\mathbf{x}$-space |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Linear discriminant functions underpin classical techniques such as the **Perceptron algorithm**, **Fisher's Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)**, **Logistic Regression**, and the **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** (which is a refined linear discriminant that maximizes the margin). Non-linear extensions are foundational to **kernel methods** and **deep neural networks**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Weight Vector $\mathbf{w}$ is Normal to the Hyperplane

**Goal:** Prove that $\mathbf{w}$ is perpendicular to the decision surface $H: \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$.

**Step 1 — Choose two arbitrary points on $H$.**
Let $\mathbf{x}_1$ and $\mathbf{x}_2$ be any two points satisfying the hyperplane equation. Then:

$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_1 + w_0 = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_2 + w_0 = 0$$

**Step 2 — Subtract the two equations.**

$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_2 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Factor using linearity of the inner product.**

$$\mathbf{w}^T (\mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_2) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Interpret geometrically.**
The vector $(\mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_2)$ lies *within* the hyperplane $H$ (it connects two points on $H$). The dot product of $\mathbf{w}$ with this in-plane vector being zero means $\mathbf{w}$ is **orthogonal** to every vector lying in $H$. Therefore, $\mathbf{w}$ is the **normal vector** of $H$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Distance from a Point $\mathbf{x}$ to the Hyperplane

**Goal:** Show that the perpendicular (signed) distance from an arbitrary point $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$ is $r = \dfrac{g(\mathbf{x}_0)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$.

**Step 1 — Project $\mathbf{x}_0$ onto the line through the origin in the direction of $\mathbf{w}$.**
Decompose $\mathbf{x}_0$ as:

$$\mathbf{x}_0 = \mathbf{x}_p + r \cdot \frac{\mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

where $\mathbf{x}_p$ is the *foot of the perpendicular* from $\mathbf{x}_0$ onto $H$, and $r$ is the signed scalar distance along the unit normal $\mathbf{w}/\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the hyperplane equation.**
Since $\mathbf{x}_p$ lies on $H$, we have $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + w_0 = 0$. Substituting:

$$\mathbf{w}^T \left( \mathbf{x}_p + r \cdot \frac{\mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} \right) + w_0 = 0$$

**Step 3 — Expand and group terms.**

$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + r \cdot \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} + w_0 = 0$$

**Step 4 — Use $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + w_0 = 0$.**

$$0 + r \cdot \frac{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} + 0 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad r \cdot \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = -\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p - w_0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $r$.**

$$r = \frac{-(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + w_0)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Step 6 — Re-derive using the original point.**
Plugging $\mathbf{x}_0 = \mathbf{x}_p + r \mathbf{w}/\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ back into the discriminant:

$$g(\mathbf{x}_0) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + w_0 = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + w_0 + r \cdot \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

Since $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + w_0 = 0$ and $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$:

$$g(\mathbf{x}_0) = r \cdot \frac{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = r \cdot \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$$

**Step 7 — Final closed form.**

$$r = \frac{g(\mathbf{x}_0)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign of $r$:** If $r > 0$, the point is on the *positive* side of the hyperplane (where $g(\mathbf{x}) > 0$, i.e., the side of $\omega_1$); if $r < 0$, it lies on the $\omega_2$ side. This sign is **the basis of geometric margin** in SVMs.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Closest Point on $H$ to the Origin

**Goal:** Find the point $\mathbf{x}_p$ on $H$ that minimizes $\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert$.

**Step 1 — The closest point must lie along the normal direction $\mathbf{w}$.**
By symmetry of the minimization, $\mathbf{x}_p = \lambda \mathbf{w}$ for some scalar $\lambda \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the hyperplane equation.**

$$\mathbf{w}^T (\lambda \mathbf{w}) + w_0 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 = -w_0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\lambda$ and $\mathbf{x}_p$.**

$$\lambda = -\frac{w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2}$$

$$\mathbf{x}_p = -\frac{w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2}\, \mathbf{w}$$

**Step 4 — Distance from origin (verification).**

$$\Vert \mathbf{x}_p \Vert = \vert \lambda \vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = \frac{\vert w_0 \vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2} = \frac{\vert w_0 \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

This matches $d_0 = w_0/\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ from the formula sheet. $\blacksquare$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Pipeline of a Discriminant-Based Classifier

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the *operational architecture* of a discriminant-based pattern recognition system, from raw input to classified output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Input Pattern x] --> B[Feature Extraction Module]
    B --> C[Feature Vector x in R to the d]
    C --> D[Discriminant Function Bank]
    D --> D1[g1 of x]
    D --> D2[g2 of x]
    D --> D3[gc of x]
    D1 --> E[Score Comparison Engine]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Argmax Selector]
    F --> G[Predicted Class Label omega i]
    E --> H[Decision Surface Computation]
    H --> I[Pairwise Boundaries gij of x equals 0]
    I --> J[Decision Region Map]
    J --> K[Geometric Interpretation and Margin]
```

### 4.2 Multi-Class Decision Region Schematic (Conceptual Block Map)

This block diagram illustrates the logical structure of how $c$ discriminant functions carve the feature space into decision regions, including the **ambiguity zone** phenomenon.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FS[Feature Space X]
        direction TB
        R1[Region R1 for class omega 1]:::region
        R2[Region R2 for class omega 2]:::region
        R3[Region R3 for class omega 3]:::region
        RA[Ambiguous Region RA]:::amb
    end
    G1[g1 of x] -->|dominant| R1
    G2[g2 of x] -->|dominant| R2
    G3[g3 of x] -->|dominant| R3
    G1 & G2 & G3 -->|tie or none dominant| RA
    H12[g1 minus g2 equals 0]:::bnd -.-> R1
    H12 -.-> R2
    H13[g1 minus g3 equals 0]:::bnd -.-> R1
    H13 -.-> R3
    H23[g2 minus g3 equals 0]:::bnd -.-> R2
    H23 -.-> R3
    classDef region fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    classDef amb fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#a04000,color:#000
    classDef bnd fill:#fff2a8,stroke:#806000,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Two-Class Hyperplane Geometry (Vector Decomposition)

This block-flow diagram shows the geometric relationship between an arbitrary point $\mathbf{x}_0$, the hyperplane $H$, the normal vector $\mathbf{w}$, and the signed distance $r$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    O[Origin O] -->|vector w0 normal| P[Closest Point xp on H]
    P -->|offset direction w per w| X0[Arbitrary Point x0]
    H[Hyperplane H colon wT x plus w0 equals 0] -.perpendicular.-> P
    X0 -->|g of x0 equals wT x0 plus w0| S[Scalar Discriminant Value]
    S -->|divide by norm w| R[Signed Distance r]
    R -->|sign positive| C1[Class omega 1]
    R -->|sign negative| C2[Class omega 2]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
Define a discriminant function. How is the decision rule formulated for a $c$-class classification problem using discriminant functions?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A discriminant function $g_i(\mathbf{x})$ is a scalar function of the feature vector $\mathbf{x}$ used to decide the class membership of $\mathbf{x}$. For a $c$-class problem with classes $\omega_1, \omega_2, \dots, \omega_c$, we define $c$ such functions $g_1(\mathbf{x}), g_2(\mathbf{x}), \dots, g_c(\mathbf{x})$. The **decision rule** assigns $\mathbf{x}$ to class $\omega_i$ if:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x}) \quad \forall \, j \neq i, \quad j \in \{1, 2, \dots, c\}$$

In other words, $\mathbf{x}$ belongs to the class whose discriminant function yields the **largest value**. Ties are broken arbitrarily.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional]**
What is a decision surface? Show that the weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ is normal to the linear decision boundary in a two-class classifier.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **decision surface** is the locus of points in the feature space where the classifier is indifferent between two or more classes; mathematically, it is the set $\{ \mathbf{x} : g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x}) \}$. For a two-class linear discriminant $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0$, the decision boundary is the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$. For any two points $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2$ on this hyperplane, $\mathbf{w}^T(\mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_2) = 0$, which shows that $\mathbf{w}$ is **perpendicular** to every in-plane vector. Hence $\mathbf{w}$ is the **normal vector** of the decision surface.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **forget the bias term $w_0$** when writing the hyperplane equation — a 1-mark direct loss.
> - In multi-class problems, students must state that **ambiguous regions can exist** when using $c$ discriminant functions; this is a frequently asked sub-question.
> - Always specify that the **distance formula $r = g(\mathbf{x})/\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$** is a *signed* (algebraic) distance; sign indicates class side.
> - The augmented form $\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}$ is examiner-favoured — use it to fetch full marks.

---

**Q3. Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the perpendicular distance from an arbitrary point $\mathbf{x}_0$ to the hyperplane $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$. State clearly the geometric role of $\mathbf{w}$ and $w_0$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Consider the hyperplane $H: \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$. Let $\mathbf{x}_0$ be any point not on $H$, and let $\mathbf{x}_p$ be the foot of the perpendicular from $\mathbf{x}_0$ onto $H$. Then we can write:

$$\mathbf{x}_0 = \mathbf{x}_p + r \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}$$

where $\hat{\mathbf{n}} = \mathbf{w}/\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ is the unit normal and $r$ is the signed distance.

Since $\mathbf{x}_p \in H$, we have $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + w_0 = 0$. Now compute:

$$g(\mathbf{x}_0) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + w_0 = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_p + w_0 + r \cdot \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w}}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

The first two terms cancel (since $\mathbf{x}_p \in H$), and $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$. So:

$$g(\mathbf{x}_0) = r \cdot \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = \frac{g(\mathbf{x}_0)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} = \frac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_0 + w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

**Geometric role of $\mathbf{w}$:** The weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ is the **normal vector** of the hyperplane; its direction is perpendicular to $H$, and its magnitude scales the discriminant.

**Geometric role of $w_0$:** The bias $w_0$ controls the **offset of the hyperplane from the origin**. If $w_0 = 0$, the hyperplane passes through the origin; otherwise, it is shifted along the direction of $\mathbf{w}$.

---

**(b) Explain the multi-class classification strategy using $c$ linear discriminant functions. Discuss how the decision regions are formed and when ambiguous regions arise. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

For a $c$-class problem, we define $c$ linear discriminant functions:

$$g_i(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_i^T \mathbf{x} + w_{i0}, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, c$$

The **decision rule** assigns $\mathbf{x}$ to class $\omega_i$ if $g_i(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x})$ for all $j \neq i$.

**Formation of Decision Regions:** The decision region for class $\omega_i$ is:

$$R_i = \{\mathbf{x} : g_i(\mathbf{x}) > g_j(\mathbf{x}) \ \forall j \neq i\}$$

The boundary between $R_i$ and $R_j$ is the hyperplane:

$$(\mathbf{w}_i - \mathbf{w}_j)^T \mathbf{x} + (w_{i0} - w_{j0}) = 0$$

There are $c(c-1)/2$ such pairwise hyperplanes, but only some of them contribute to the actual region boundaries. The full set of regions partitions the feature space into (at most) $c$ convex polyhedral regions.

**Ambiguous Regions:** In certain configurations, there may exist points $\mathbf{x}$ where:
- Two or more discriminant functions tie (e.g., $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x}) > g_k(\mathbf{x})$ for all other $k$). Such points lie on a decision surface and are assigned arbitrarily.
- **No** discriminant function is uniquely maximum, even after tie-breaking — this is the *unclassifiable* region, which is rare in linear cases but common in piecewise or non-linear setups.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> To **avoid** ambiguous regions in linear discriminant classifiers, the simplest fix is to assign tied points to *any* of the tied classes (consistent with the loss function). In multiclass SVMs, this issue is handled by the *crammer-singer* formulation.

---

**Q4. Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a) Define a linear discriminant function. Explain its geometric interpretation in a 2D feature space with a suitable diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A **linear discriminant function** is a function of the form:

$$g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \dots + w_d x_d + w_0$$

where $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2, \dots, w_d)^T$ is the weight vector and $w_0$ is the bias. The decision rule for two classes is:

$$g(\mathbf{x}) \begin{cases} > 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{x} \in \omega_1 \\ < 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{x} \in \omega_2 \\ = 0 \Rightarrow \mathbf{x} \in H \end{cases}$$

**Geometric Interpretation in 2D:** In a 2D feature space $(x_1, x_2)$, the linear discriminant $g(\mathbf{x}) = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + w_0$ defines a **straight line** as the decision boundary. The vector $\mathbf{w} = (w_1, w_2)^T$ is normal to this line, and $w_0$ determines the offset from the origin. The line divides the plane into two half-planes: the **positive half-plane** (assigned to $\omega_1$) and the **negative half-plane** (assigned to $\omega_2$).

The **distance** of any point $(x_1, x_2)$ from the decision line is given by:

$$r = \frac{w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + w_0}{\sqrt{w_1^2 + w_2^2}}$$

The closest point to the origin on the line is at $\left( -\dfrac{w_0 w_1}{w_1^2 + w_2^2}, -\dfrac{w_0 w_2}{w_1^2 + w_2^2} \right)$.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Tip:** A neat 2D sketch with the line, normal vector $\mathbf{w}$, distance $r$, and shaded half-planes will easily score the full 7 marks.

---

**(b) Derive the equation of the decision boundary between two classes $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ using linear discriminant functions. Show that the distance from the origin to this boundary is $\dfrac{\vert w_0 \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Let $g_1(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_1^T \mathbf{x} + w_{10}$ and $g_2(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}_2^T \mathbf{x} + w_{20}$ be the discriminant functions for $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$.

**Step 1 — Define the difference discriminant.**

$$g(\mathbf{x}) = g_1(\mathbf{x}) - g_2(\mathbf{x}) = (\mathbf{w}_1 - \mathbf{w}_2)^T \mathbf{x} + (w_{10} - w_{20})$$

**Step 2 — Define the new weight and bias.**

$$\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}_1 - \mathbf{w}_2, \quad w_0 = w_{10} - w_{20}$$

Then $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0$.

**Step 3 — Set up the decision boundary equation.** The decision boundary is the locus where $g(\mathbf{x}) = 0$:

$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0 = 0$$

This is a **hyperplane** in the $d$-dimensional feature space.

**Step 4 — Distance from the origin.** The distance from the origin to the hyperplane is the length of the projection of *any* point on $H$ onto the normal $\mathbf{w}$. Let $\mathbf{x}_p$ be the closest point to the origin on $H$. Then $\mathbf{x}_p = \lambda \mathbf{w}$ (along the normal). Substituting:

$$\mathbf{w}^T (\lambda \mathbf{w}) + w_0 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 = -w_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda = -\frac{w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\mathbf{x}_p = -\frac{w_0}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2}\,\mathbf{w}$$

and the distance from the origin to the hyperplane is:

$$\Vert \mathbf{x}_p \Vert = |\lambda| \cdot \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = \frac{\vert w_0 \vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2} = \frac{\vert w_0 \vert}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam!):**

- **Discriminant function $g_i(\mathbf{x})$** → assigns a score to $\mathbf{x}$ for class $\omega_i$.
- **Decision rule** → assign to the class with the maximum $g_i(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Decision surface** → locus where two discriminant functions are equal: $g_i(\mathbf{x}) = g_j(\mathbf{x})$.
- **Linear discriminant** → $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + w_0$; produces a **hyperplane** boundary in $d$-D space.
- **Augmented form** → $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{y}$, where $\mathbf{y} = (1, x_1, \dots, x_d)^T$. Used in perceptron/LMS proofs.
- **$\mathbf{w}$ is normal to the hyperplane** — a 1-line proof, always remember: $\mathbf{w}^T(\mathbf{x}_1 - \mathbf{x}_2) = 0$ for $\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2 \in H$.
- **Distance from origin** → $d_0 = w_0 / \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ (signed; absolute value gives the magnitude).
- **Distance from any point** → $r = g(\mathbf{x}) / \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$ (this is the **algebraic / signed** distance).
- **Multi-class**: $c$ discriminant functions give $c(c-1)/2$ pairwise boundaries; can produce **ambiguous regions**.
- **Non-linear extension**: $g(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) + w_0$ — linear in feature space, non-linear in input space (kernel trick foundation).
- **Real-world applications**: Spam filtering, medical diagnosis, image classification, biometric authentication, fault detection in engineering systems.
- **Common exam mistakes** to avoid: forgetting $w_0$, mixing up $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}$ with $\mathbf{x}^T \mathbf{w}$ (same scalar, but notation matters), and treating $g(\mathbf{x})$ as a *probability* (it is only a *score*).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bayesian Classification for Normal Distributions
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bayesian Classification for Normal Distributions

# 🎯 Bayesian Classification for Normal Distributions — KTU Premier Notes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bayesian Classification (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A statistical pattern recognition framework that classifies an unknown feature vector $x$ into one of $c$ classes $\omega_1, \omega_2, \dots, \omega_c$ by computing the **posterior probability** $P(\omega_i \mid x)$ and assigning the input to the class with the **maximum a posteriori (MAP)** probability. When the class-conditional densities $p(x \mid \omega_i)$ are assumed to follow **Multivariate Normal (Gaussian) distributions**, the resulting decision boundaries are either **linear** or **quadratic**, leading to analytically tractable classifiers.

Mathematically, the **Bayes decision rule** for minimum error rate is:

$$\text{Decide } \omega_i \iff P(\omega_i \mid x) = \max_{j=1,\dots,c} P(\omega_j \mid x)$$

Expanding via Bayes' theorem:

$$P(\omega_i \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_i) \, P(\omega_i)}{p(x)} = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_i) \, P(\omega_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{c} p(x \mid \omega_j) \, P(\omega_j)}$$

Where:
- $P(\omega_i)$ = **Prior probability** of class $\omega_i$
- $p(x \mid \omega_i)$ = **Class-conditional probability density function (likelihood)**
- $P(\omega_i \mid x)$ = **Posterior probability**
- $p(x)$ = **Evidence (normalizing constant)**

### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Doctor Diagnosis" Analogy:**
> Imagine a doctor examining a patient with symptoms (the feature vector $x$). The doctor has prior experience about how common each disease is ($P(\omega_i)$). For each possible disease, the doctor knows how likely those symptoms are ($p(x \mid \omega_i)$). Combining both pieces of evidence gives the posterior probability — the doctor's final belief about which disease the patient has. The doctor then prescribes the most likely disease (MAP rule). The Gaussian assumption simply means: "the symptoms for each disease are bell-curve distributed around an average."

### 📌 Real-World Engineering Significance

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Classifying tumors as benign or malignant from imaging features.
- **Spam Filtering:** Bayesian email classifiers (e.g., spam vs. ham) — the foundation of Gmail's filtering.
- **Speech Recognition:** Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) use Bayesian classification on phonetic features.
- **Quality Control:** Classifying manufactured products as defective or non-defective from sensor measurements.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Classifying pedestrians, vehicles, and road signs from LiDAR/camera features.
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression classification using Gaussian likelihoods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Why Gaussian Models?**
> The Central Limit Theorem justifies Gaussian assumptions in many real-world systems. Moreover, the analytical tractability of the Gaussian leads to **closed-form decision boundaries**, making them a cornerstone of classical pattern recognition — heavily emphasized in the KTU Machine Learning syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🔬 The Three Foundational Pillars of Bayesian Classification

#### **Pillar 1: Bayesian Decision Theory (The Philosophy)**

- **Goal:** Minimize the **probability of misclassification** (error rate).
- **Mechanism:** Choose the class that maximizes the posterior probability.
- **Key Insight:** Since $p(x)$ is independent of the class index $i$, the decision rule simplifies to:

$$\text{Decide } \omega_i \iff p(x \mid \omega_i) \, P(\omega_i) = \max_{j=1,\dots,c} \, p(x \mid \omega_j) \, P(\omega_j)$$

- **Why?** Because maximizing $P(\omega_i \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)}{p(x)}$ over $i$ is equivalent to maximizing the numerator (since $p(x) > 0$ is constant for all $i$).

#### **Pillar 2: Minimum-Risk Bayesian Decision (Loss Function Extension)**

> [!NOTE]
> **The Generalization Beyond Minimum Error:**
> In many real-world applications (medical diagnosis, financial fraud), the cost of a misclassification is **asymmetric**. KTU expects students to understand the **Bayesian Risk** formulation.

- **Loss function** $\lambda_{ij}$ = cost incurred when the true class is $\omega_j$ but we decide $\omega_i$.
- **Conditional risk** (expected loss) for deciding $\omega_i$:

$$R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda_{ij} \, P(\omega_j \mid x)$$

- **Bayes Risk Minimization Rule:**

$$\text{Decide } \omega_i \iff R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \min_{k=1,\dots,c} R(\alpha_k \mid x)$$

- **Special case** (0–1 loss, where $\lambda_{ij} = 1 - \delta_{ij}$): Reduces to the minimum error rate rule (Pillar 1).

#### **Pillar 3: Discriminant Functions (The Computational Engine)**

A **discriminant function** $g_i(x)$ is any function such that:

$$g_i(x) > g_j(x) \quad \forall \, j \neq i \implies \text{Decide } \omega_i$$

- The simplest choice: $g_i(x) = P(\omega_i \mid x)$.
- For the Gaussian case, taking the logarithm (monotonic transformation) simplifies computation:

$$g_i(x) = \ln p(x \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

### 🧮 Multivariate Normal (Gaussian) Density — The Foundation

The **d-dimensional multivariate normal distribution** is:

$$p(x \mid \omega_i) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \, \vert\Sigma_i\vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (x - \mu_i)\right)$$

Where:
- $\mu_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ = mean vector of class $\omega_i$
- $\Sigma_i \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ = covariance matrix of class $\omega_i$ (symmetric, positive definite)
- $\vert\Sigma_i\vert$ = determinant of $\Sigma_i$ (measures volume of the covariance ellipsoid)
- $(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (x - \mu_i)$ = **Mahalanobis distance squared** (denoted $r^2$)

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Interpretation of $\Sigma_i$:**
> - The **diagonal elements** $\sigma_{kk}^2$ represent the variance along each feature dimension.
> - The **off-diagonal elements** $\sigma_{ij}$ represent the covariance (correlation) between features.
> - The **eigenvectors** of $\Sigma_i$ define the principal axes of the probability ellipsoid.
> - The **eigenvalues** define the spread (variance) along those axes.

### 📊 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Decision Boundary Type** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Bayes' Theorem | $P(\omega_i \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)}{p(x)}$ | — |
| Total Probability | $p(x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} p(x \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$ | — |
| MAP Decision Rule | $\text{Decide } \omega_i \iff p(x \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i) = \max_j$ | Depends on $p(x \mid \omega_i)$ |
| Conditional Risk | $R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_{j=1}^{c} \lambda_{ij} P(\omega_j \mid x)$ | Minimize over $i$ |
| **Case 1:** $\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$ | $g_i(x) = -\frac{\Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2}{2\sigma^2} + \ln P(\omega_i)$ | **Linear** (minimum distance classifier) |
| **Case 2:** $\Sigma_i = \Sigma$ (arbitrary, identical) | $g_i(x) = W_i^T x + W_{i0}$, where $W_i = \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i$, $W_{i0} = -\frac{1}{2}\mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i + \ln P(\omega_i)$ | **Linear** |
| **Case 3:** $\Sigma_i$ arbitrary (distinct) | $g_i(x) = -\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1}(x-\mu_i) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma_i\vert + \ln P(\omega_i)$ | **Quadratic** |
| Mahalanobis Distance | $D_M(x, \mu_i) = \sqrt{(x-\mu_i)^T \Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu_i)}$ | Distance metric |
| Discriminant for two classes (case 2) | $g_1(x) - g_2(x) = 0 \Rightarrow$ hyperplane | Linear |
| Discriminant for two classes (case 3) | $g_1(x) - g_2(x) = 0 \Rightarrow$ quadric surface | Quadratic |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake — Pipes in Tables:**
> Always use $\vert x \vert$ (or $\lvert x \rvert$) in LaTeX for determinants/absolute values. Using the raw keyboard pipe $\vert$ inside a markdown table will **break the table rendering**. KTU examiners expect clean, well-formatted answers.

### 🌍 Real-World Engineering Applications of Each Case

- **Case 1 ($\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$):** Used in **template matching**, **nearest centroid classifiers**, radar signal classification with isotropic noise.
- **Case 2 ($\Sigma_i = \Sigma$):** Used in **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** for face recognition, customer segmentation, dimensionality reduction.
- **Case 3 ($\Sigma_i$ distinct):** Used in **Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (QDA)**, medical diagnostics where each disease has unique feature correlations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🔍 Derivation 1: From Posterior Maximization to Discriminant Function

**Goal:** Show that maximizing $P(\omega_i \mid x)$ is equivalent to a simpler algebraic form.

**Step 1 — Start with Bayes' Theorem:**

$$P(\omega_i \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)}{p(x)}$$

**Step 2 — Since $p(x) > 0$ is the same for all classes, it does not affect the argmax:**

$$\omega^* = \arg\max_i P(\omega_i \mid x) = \arg\max_i \, p(x \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm (monotonic, preserves order):**

$$g_i(x) \triangleq \ln \left[ p(x \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i) \right] = \ln p(x \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the multivariate Gaussian density:**

$$p(x \mid \omega_i) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert\Sigma_i\vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (x - \mu_i)\right)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the logarithm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln p(x \mid \omega_i) &= -\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma_i\vert - \frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (x - \mu_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Form the complete discriminant function (drop the constant $-\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi)$ since it is identical for all classes):**

$$g_i(x) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma_i\vert - \frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (x - \mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

This is the **master discriminant function** for Bayesian classification under Gaussian assumptions. The simplification in each of the three cases follows by substituting the appropriate structure of $\Sigma_i$.

---

### 🔍 Derivation 2: Case 1 — $\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$ (Spherical, Identical Covariance)

**Assumption:** $\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$ for all $i$ (all classes have the same variance $\sigma^2$ in every direction).

**Step 1 — Substitute into the master discriminant function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_i(x) &= -\frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\sigma^2 I\vert - \frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T (\sigma^2 I)^{-1} (x - \mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the determinant term:** $\vert\sigma^2 I\vert = \sigma^{2d}$, so $\ln \vert\sigma^2 I\vert = 2d \ln \sigma$. This is constant across all classes and can be dropped:

$$g_i(x) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(x - \mu_i)^T (x - \mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the quadratic form** using $(x-\mu_i)^T(x-\mu_i) = \Vert x \Vert^2 - 2\mu_i^T x + \Vert \mu_i \Vert^2$:

$$g_i(x) = -\frac{\Vert x \Vert^2}{2\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_i^T x}{\sigma^2} - \frac{\Vert \mu_i \Vert^2}{2\sigma^2} + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

**Step 4 — Drop the term $-\frac{\Vert x \Vert^2}{2\sigma^2}$ (independent of $i$):**

$$g_i(x) = \frac{\mu_i^T x}{\sigma^2} - \frac{\Vert \mu_i \Vert^2}{2\sigma^2} + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

**Step 5 — Define** $W_i = \frac{\mu_i}{\sigma^2}$ and $W_{i0} = -\frac{\Vert \mu_i \Vert^2}{2\sigma^2} + \ln P(\omega_i)$:

$$g_i(x) = W_i^T x + W_{i0}$$

This is a **linear discriminant function**. The decision boundary between classes $\omega_i$ and $\omega_j$ is:

$$g_i(x) = g_j(x) \implies (\mu_i - \mu_j)^T x = \frac{\Vert \mu_i \Vert^2 - \Vert \mu_j \Vert^2}{2} + \sigma^2 \ln \frac{P(\omega_i)}{P(\omega_j)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Interpretation (Case 1):**
> The classifier assigns $x$ to the class whose mean $\mu_i$ is **closest in Euclidean distance** (scaled by $\sigma$). When $P(\omega_i) = P(\omega_j)$, it is the classic **minimum-distance classifier** (template matcher).

---

### 🔍 Derivation 3: Case 2 — $\Sigma_i = \Sigma$ (General Identical Covariance)

**Assumption:** $\Sigma_i = \Sigma$ for all $i$ (same covariance, but possibly anisotropic — different variance per feature, plus correlations).

**Step 1 — Substitute into the master discriminant function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_i(x) &= -\frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma\vert - \frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Drop the constant** $-\frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma\vert$:

$$g_i(x) = -\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the quadratic form:**

$$(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu_i) = x^T \Sigma^{-1} x - 2\mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} x + \mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i$$

**Step 4 — Drop the constant** $x^T \Sigma^{-1} x$ (independent of $i$):

$$g_i(x) = \mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} x - \frac{1}{2}\mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

**Step 5 — Define** $W_i = \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i$ and $W_{i0} = -\frac{1}{2}\mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i + \ln P(\omega_i)$:

$$g_i(x) = W_i^T x + W_{i0}$$

**Decision boundary for two classes** $g_1(x) = g_2(x)$:

$$(\mu_1 - \mu_2)^T \Sigma^{-1} x = \frac{1}{2}(\mu_1^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_1 - \mu_2^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_2) - \ln \frac{P(\omega_1)}{P(\omega_2)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Insight:**
> Case 2 yields the **Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)** classifier. The decision surface is a **hyperplane** perpendicular to the line connecting the means, weighted by $\Sigma^{-1}$. If $P(\omega_1) = P(\omega_2)$, the hyperplane passes through the **midpoint** of the two means (in the Mahalanobis metric).

---
