### 🔍 Derivation 4: Case 3 — $\Sigma_i$ Arbitrary (Distinct Covariances)

**Assumption:** Each class has its own covariance matrix $\Sigma_i$. The $-\frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma_i\vert$ term **cannot be dropped** because it varies across classes.

**Step 1 — The full discriminant function:**

$$g_i(x) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma_i\vert - \frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (x - \mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

**Step 2 — Expanding the quadratic form (this time all terms must be retained because $x^T \Sigma_i^{-1} x$ depends on $i$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_i(x) &= -\frac{1}{2}x^T \Sigma_i^{-1} x + \mu_i^T \Sigma_i^{-1} x - \frac{1}{2}\mu_i^T \Sigma_i^{-1} \mu_i - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma_i\vert + \ln P(\omega_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — The decision boundary $g_i(x) = g_j(x)$ is now quadratic in $x$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&-\frac{1}{2}x^T(\Sigma_i^{-1} - \Sigma_j^{-1})x + (\mu_i^T \Sigma_i^{-1} - \mu_j^T \Sigma_j^{-1})x \\
&\quad + \left(-\frac{1}{2}\mu_i^T \Sigma_i^{-1} \mu_i + \frac{1}{2}\mu_j^T \Sigma_j^{-1} \mu_j - \frac{1}{2}\ln \frac{\vert\Sigma_i\vert}{\vert\Sigma_j\vert} + \ln \frac{P(\omega_i)}{P(\omega_j)}\right) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Interpretation (Case 3):**
> This is the **Quadratic Discriminant Analysis (QDA)** classifier. The decision boundary is a general **quadric surface** (ellipsoid, hyperboloid, parabola, etc.) in 2D, or a hyperquadric in higher dimensions. The class-specific covariance allows each class to have its own elliptical "spread" pattern.

---

### 🔍 Worked Numerical Example (Case 2 — Two-Class LDA)

**Given:**
- $\mu_1 = [0, 0]^T$, $\mu_2 = [4, 0]^T$
- $\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$ (i.e., $\Sigma = 2I$)
- $P(\omega_1) = P(\omega_2) = 0.5$

**Question:** Classify the point $x = [1, 1]^T$.

**Step 1 — Compute discriminant values** using Case 1 (or Case 2, equivalent here since $\Sigma = \sigma^2 I$ with $\sigma^2 = 2$):

$$g_1(x) = -\frac{1}{2 \cdot 2}\left[(1-0)^2 + (1-0)^2\right] + \ln 0.5 = -\frac{2}{4} + (-0.693) = -0.5 - 0.693 = -1.193$$

$$g_2(x) = -\frac{1}{2 \cdot 2}\left[(1-4)^2 + (1-0)^2\right] + \ln 0.5 = -\frac{10}{4} + (-0.693) = -2.5 - 0.693 = -3.193$$

**Step 2 — Compare:** $g_1(x) = -1.193 > g_2(x) = -3.193$, so **decide $\omega_1$**.

**Step 3 — Verification with Mahalanobis distance:**

$$D_M(x, \mu_1)^2 = (1)^2 + (1)^2 = 2$$

$$D_M(x, \mu_2)^2 = (-3)^2 + (1)^2 = 10$$

Since $D_M(x, \mu_1)^2 < D_M(x, \mu_2)^2$, the point $x$ is closer to $\mu_1$ → **classify as $\omega_1$** ✓

---

### 📊 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Case

| **Property** | **Case 1** $\Sigma = \sigma^2 I$ | **Case 2** $\Sigma$ identical | **Case 3** $\Sigma_i$ distinct |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Decision boundary | Linear hyperplane | Linear hyperplane | Quadratic surface |
| Parameters to estimate per class | $\mu_i$ ($d$ params) | $\mu_i$ ($d$ params) | $\mu_i$ + $\Sigma_i$ ($d + d(d+1)/2$ params) |
| Total parameters ($c$ classes) | $cd$ | $cd + d(d+1)/2$ | $cd + c \cdot d(d+1)/2$ |
| Sample efficiency | Highest | Medium | Lowest (more data needed) |
| When to use | Isotropic noise, few features | Features correlated equally across classes | Each class has unique feature correlations |
| Classifier name | Minimum distance / Euclidean | LDA (Linear Discriminant) | QDA (Quadratic Discriminant) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗺️ Diagram 1: Bayesian Classification Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Feature Vector x"] --> B["Compute Prior P omega_i"]
    B --> C["Compute Likelihood p x given omega_i"]
    C --> D["Apply Bayes Theorem to get Posterior P omega_i given x"]
    D --> E{"Compare Posteriors across all classes"}
    E --> F["Class with Maximum Posterior = Winner"]
    F --> G["Output Predicted Class Label"]

    H["Training Data with Gaussian Assumption"] --> I["Estimate mu_i and Sigma_i per class"]
    I --> C

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
    style G fill:#d4f4dd
    style I fill:#fff4d4
```

### 🗺️ Diagram 2: Three Cases of Gaussian Bayesian Classification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Multivariate Gaussian Assumption p x given omega_i"] --> B["Inspect Covariance Structure Sigma_i"]
    B --> C{"Covariance Type?"}
    C --> C1["Case 1: Sigma_i equals sigma squared I"]
    C --> C2["Case 2: Sigma_i equals Sigma identical"]
    C --> C3["Case 3: Sigma_i arbitrary distinct"]

    C1 --> D1["Linear Boundary"]
    C1 --> E1["Minimum Distance Classifier"]
    C2 --> D2["Linear Boundary Hyperplane"]
    C2 --> E2["LDA Classifier"]
    C3 --> D3["Quadratic Boundary"]
    C3 --> E3["QDA Classifier"]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#ffe1e1
    style D1 fill:#d4f4dd
    style D2 fill:#d4f4dd
    style D3 fill:#fff4d4
    style E1 fill:#f0e1ff
    style E2 fill:#f0e1ff
    style E3 fill:#f0e1ff
```

### 🗺️ Diagram 3: Discriminant Function Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["x in R^d"] --> B["Linear Term W_i transpose x"]
    A --> C["Quadratic Term x transpose Sigma_i inverse x"]
    A --> D["Constant Term W_i0"]
    B --> E["Sum all three contributions"]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F["g_i x = Discriminant for class omega_i"]
    F --> G{"g_i x greater than g_j x for all j?"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Decide omega_i"]
    G -- No --> I["Try next class"]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
    style H fill:#d4f4dd
    style I fill:#fff4d4
```

### 🗺️ Diagram 4: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Bayesian Classifier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1["Training Phase"]
        T1["Collect Labeled Data D equals x_k, y_k"] --> T2["Group Samples by Class Label"]
        T2 --> T3["Compute Class Mean mu_i"]
        T2 --> T4["Compute Class Covariance Sigma_i"]
        T3 --> T5["Store Parameters mu_i, Sigma_i, P omega_i"]
        T4 --> T5
    end

    subgraph S2["Inference Phase"]
        I1["New Observation x_new"] --> I2["For each class i, compute g_i x_new"]
        T5 --> I2
        I2 --> I3["Apply MAP Rule: argmax g_i x_new"]
        I3 --> I4["Output Predicted Class omega_hat"]
    end

    S1 --> S2

    style T5 fill:#fff4d4
    style I4 fill:#d4f4dd
    style S1 fill:#f0f8ff
    style S2 fill:#fff0f8
```

### 🗺️ Diagram 5: Decision Boundary Geometry Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Bayesian Decision Boundary Geometry"] --> B["2D Feature Space x1, x2"]
    B --> C["Case 1: Sphereical Equal Variance"]
    B --> D["Case 2: Identical Elliptical"]
    B --> E["Case 3: Different Elliptical"]

    C --> F["Linear boundary perpendicular to mean line"]
    D --> G["Linear hyperplane weighted by Sigma inverse"]
    E --> H["General conic section parabola hyperbola ellipse"]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#d4f4dd
    style G fill:#fff4d4
    style H fill:#ffe1e1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📚 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**Q:** State and explain Bayes' theorem for pattern classification. Define prior, likelihood, and posterior probabilities with examples.

**Model Answer:**

Bayes' theorem provides a principled way to update beliefs about class membership upon observing evidence. For a feature vector $x$ and class $\omega_i$:

$$P(\omega_i \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)}{p(x)}$$

The components are:
- **Prior probability $P(\omega_i)$:** The initial belief about class $\omega_i$ *before* observing $x$. Example: Probability that a randomly chosen email is spam = 0.3.
- **Likelihood $p(x \mid \omega_i)$:** The probability density of observing $x$ given the class is $\omega_i$. Example: The probability density of seeing the word "FREE" given the email is spam.
- **Posterior $P(\omega_i \mid x)$:** The updated belief about $\omega_i$ *after* observing $x$. Example: Probability email is spam given that it contains "FREE".
- **Evidence $p(x)$:** Normalizing constant ensuring probabilities sum to 1, computed as $p(x) = \sum_j p(x \mid \omega_j) P(\omega_j)$.

The classifier decides $\omega^*$ such that $P(\omega^* \mid x) = \max_i P(\omega_i \mid x)$.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**Q:** Differentiate between the three cases of Bayesian classification under Gaussian assumption based on the structure of the covariance matrix $\Sigma_i$.

**Model Answer:**

| **Case** | **Covariance Assumption** | **Decision Boundary** | **Discriminant Function** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Case 1 | $\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$ (spherical, identical) | Linear hyperplane | $g_i(x) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\Vert x - \mu_i\Vert^2 + \ln P(\omega_i)$ |
| Case 2 | $\Sigma_i = \Sigma$ (identical, arbitrary) | Linear hyperplane | $g_i(x) = W_i^T x + W_{i0}$ with $W_i = \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i$ |
| Case 3 | $\Sigma_i$ distinct per class | Quadratic surface | $g_i(x) = -\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1}(x-\mu_i) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma_i\vert + \ln P(\omega_i)$ |

**Key distinctions:** Case 1 assumes isotropic (direction-independent) noise; Case 2 allows feature correlations but assumes all classes share the same correlation structure; Case 3 allows each class to have its own unique covariance structure, yielding the most flexible but parameter-heavy classifier.

---

### 📘 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the discriminant function for the Bayesian classifier when the class-conditional densities are multivariate Gaussian with **identical covariance matrices** $\Sigma_i = \Sigma$ for all classes. Show that the resulting decision boundary is linear. State the geometric interpretation of the classifier.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a two-class problem with $d = 2$ features, the following parameters are given:
- $\mu_1 = [2, 2]^T$, $\mu_2 = [6, 4]^T$
- $\Sigma = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}$
- $P(\omega_1) = 0.4$, $P(\omega_2) = 0.6$

Find the equation of the decision boundary and classify the test point $x = [3, 3]^T$.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Starting from the multivariate Gaussian density:

$$p(x \mid \omega_i) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert\Sigma\vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu_i)\right)$$

Form the discriminant function (monotonic log transformation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_i(x) &= \ln p(x \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i) \\
&= -\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma\vert - \frac{1}{2}(x-\mu_i)^T \Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

Drop the constant terms $-\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma\vert$ (identical for all $i$):

$$g_i(x) = -\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu_i)^T \Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

Expand the quadratic form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x-\mu_i)^T \Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu_i) &= x^T \Sigma^{-1} x - 2\mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} x + \mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i
\end{aligned}
$$

The term $x^T \Sigma^{-1} x$ is independent of $i$, so it drops out:

$$g_i(x) = \mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} x - \frac{1}{2}\mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

Let $W_i = \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i$ and $W_{i0} = -\frac{1}{2}\mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i + \ln P(\omega_i)$:

$$\boxed{g_i(x) = W_i^T x + W_{i0}}$$

This is **linear in $x$**, hence the decision boundary $g_i(x) = g_j(x)$ is a **hyperplane**:

$$W^T (x - x_0) = 0$$

where $W = \Sigma^{-1}(\mu_i - \mu_j)$ and $x_0$ is the point on the hyperplane.

**Geometric Interpretation:** The decision boundary is a hyperplane perpendicular to $\Sigma^{-1}(\mu_1 - \mu_2)$. If priors are equal, the hyperplane passes through the midpoint of $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ in the **Mahalanobis metric** induced by $\Sigma$. The classifier generalizes the Euclidean minimum-distance classifier to handle correlated, anisotropic noise.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\Sigma^{-1}$:**

$$\det(\Sigma) = (3)(2) - (1)(1) = 5$$

$$\Sigma^{-1} = \frac{1}{5}\begin{pmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 3 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 & -0.2 \\ -0.2 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the weight vectors:**

$$
W_1 = \Sigma^{-1} \mu_1 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 & -0.2 \\ -0.2 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.8 \end{pmatrix}
$$

$$
W_2 = \Sigma^{-1} \mu_2 = \begin{pmatrix} 0.4 & -0.2 \\ -0.2 & 0.6 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 1.6 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bias terms:**

$$
\mu_1^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_1 = [2, 2]\begin{pmatrix} 0.4 \\ 0.8 \end{pmatrix} = 0.8 + 1.6 = 2.4
$$

$$
\mu_2^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_2 = [6, 4]\begin{pmatrix} 1.6 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix} = 9.6 + 0 = 9.6
$$

$$
W_{10} = -\frac{1}{2}(2.4) + \ln(0.4) = -1.2 + (-0.916) = -2.116
$$

$$
W_{20} = -\frac{1}{2}(9.6) + \ln(0.6) = -4.8 + (-0.511) = -5.311
$$

**Step 4 — Form discriminant functions:**

$$g_1(x) = 0.4 x_1 + 0.8 x_2 - 2.116$$

$$g_2(x) = 1.6 x_1 + 0 \cdot x_2 - 5.311 = 1.6 x_1 - 5.311$$

**Step 5 — Decision boundary** $g_1(x) = g_2(x)$:

$$0.4 x_1 + 0.8 x_2 - 2.116 = 1.6 x_1 - 5.311$$

$$0.8 x_2 = 1.2 x_1 - 3.195$$

$$\boxed{x_2 = 1.5 x_1 - 3.994}$$

**Step 6 — Classify $x = [3, 3]^T$:**

$$g_1(3, 3) = 0.4(3) + 0.8(3) - 2.116 = 1.2 + 2.4 - 2.116 = 1.484$$

$$g_2(3, 3) = 1.6(3) - 5.311 = 4.8 - 5.311 = -0.511$$

Since $g_1(x) = 1.484 > g_2(x) = -0.511$, the point is classified as $\omega_1$. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the general discriminant function for Bayesian classification with multivariate Gaussian densities where each class has its own arbitrary covariance matrix $\Sigma_i$. Show that the decision boundary is a quadratic function of $x$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a two-class problem, derive the special case where both classes have covariance matrices of the form $\Sigma_i = \sigma_i^2 I$ (spherical but with **different** variances). Find the equation of the decision boundary. Discuss how this differs from the case of identical covariances.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Starting from:

$$p(x \mid \omega_i) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{d/2} \vert\Sigma_i\vert^{1/2}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (x - \mu_i)\right)$$

Taking the logarithm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_i(x) &= -\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma_i\vert - \frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1}(x - \mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

Drop $-\frac{d}{2}\ln(2\pi)$:

$$g_i(x) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma_i\vert - \frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1}(x - \mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

Expand the quadratic form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1}(x - \mu_i) &= x^T \Sigma_i^{-1} x - 2\mu_i^T \Sigma_i^{-1} x + \mu_i^T \Sigma_i^{-1} \mu_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Crucial observation:** Unlike Case 2, the term $x^T \Sigma_i^{-1} x$ **cannot be dropped** because $\Sigma_i^{-1}$ differs across classes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_i(x) &= -\frac{1}{2}x^T \Sigma_i^{-1} x + \mu_i^T \Sigma_i^{-1} x - \frac{1}{2}\mu_i^T \Sigma_i^{-1} \mu_i - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma_i\vert + \ln P(\omega_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

The decision boundary $g_1(x) = g_2(x)$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&-\frac{1}{2}x^T(\Sigma_1^{-1} - \Sigma_2^{-1})x + (\mu_1^T \Sigma_1^{-1} - \mu_2^T \Sigma_2^{-1})x \\
&\quad + \left(\frac{1}{2}\mu_2^T \Sigma_2^{-1} \mu_2 - \frac{1}{2}\mu_1^T \Sigma_1^{-1} \mu_1\right) + \frac{1}{2}\ln \frac{\vert\Sigma_2\vert}{\vert\Sigma_1\vert} + \ln \frac{P(\omega_1)}{P(\omega_2)} = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **quadratic equation in $x$**, hence the decision boundary is a **quadric surface** (in 2D: parabola, hyperbola, or ellipse; in 3D: paraboloid, hyperboloid, or ellipsoid). This is the **QDA classifier**.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

With $\Sigma_i = \sigma_i^2 I$:

$$g_i(x) = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(\sigma_i^{2d}) - \frac{1}{2\sigma_i^2}\Vert x - \mu_i\Vert^2 + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

$$g_i(x) = -d \ln \sigma_i - \frac{\Vert x - \mu_i\Vert^2}{2\sigma_i^2} + \ln P(\omega_i)$$

Setting $g_1(x) = g_2(x)$:

$$-d \ln \sigma_1 - \frac{\Vert x - \mu_1\Vert^2}{2\sigma_1^2} + \ln P(\omega_1) = -d \ln \sigma_2 - \frac{\Vert x - \mu_2\Vert^2}{2\sigma_2^2} + \ln P(\omega_2)$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\Vert x - \mu_2\Vert^2}{2\sigma_2^2} - \frac{\Vert x - \mu_1\Vert^2}{2\sigma_1^2} = d \ln \frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2} + \ln \frac{P(\omega_1)}{P(\omega_2)}$$

Expanding $\Vert x - \mu_i\Vert^2 = \Vert x \Vert^2 - 2\mu_i^T x + \Vert \mu_i\Vert^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\frac{\Vert x \Vert^2 - 2\mu_2^T x + \Vert \mu_2\Vert^2}{2\sigma_2^2} - \frac{\Vert x \Vert^2 - 2\mu_1^T x + \Vert \mu_1\Vert^2}{2\sigma_1^2} = d \ln \frac{\sigma_1}{\sigma_2} + \ln \frac{P(\omega_1)}{P(\omega_2)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Factoring the $\Vert x \Vert^2$ coefficient (which depends on $x$ since $\sigma_1 \neq \sigma_2$):

$$\left(\frac{1}{2\sigma_2^2} - \frac{1}{2\sigma_1^2}\right)\Vert x \Vert^2 + \left(\frac{\mu_1^T}{\sigma_1^2} - \frac{\mu_2^T}{\sigma_2^2}\right)x + \text{const} = 0$$

**Decision boundary:** This is a **quadratic (hyperbola/sphere)** in $x$ — the term $\Vert x \Vert^2$ appears with a non-zero coefficient when $\sigma_1 \neq \sigma_2$.

**Difference from Case 2:** When $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma$, the $\Vert x \Vert^2$ coefficient vanishes and the boundary becomes **linear** (Case 2). When the variances differ, the boundary curves, reflecting the different "spread" of each class.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the determinant term** $-\frac{1}{2}\ln \vert\Sigma_i\vert$ in Case 3 is the #1 cause of lost marks. KTU examiners *specifically* check this term.
> 2. **Dropping $x^T \Sigma_i^{-1} x$ prematurely:** This is valid only in Case 1 and Case 2 (when $\Sigma_i$ is identical). In Case 3, this term varies with $i$ and must be retained.
> 3. **Confusing MAP with ML:** Maximum Likelihood ignores priors; MAP does not. KTU questions often test this distinction.
> 4. **Sign errors in the bias term $W_{i0}$:** The factor is $-\frac{1}{2}\mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i$, not $+\frac{1}{2}$.
> 5. **Units mismatch:** $P(\omega_i)$ is dimensionless, $p(x \mid \omega_i)$ has units of $1/(\text{feature units}^d)$. The log of a probability (dimensionless) is fine, but $\log$ of a density needs care. KTU expects students to use Bayes' theorem, not maximum likelihood alone.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions explicitly:** Always state the Gaussian assumption, the covariance structure, and the prior assumptions at the start of the solution.

---

### 🔥 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Bayes' Theorem** is the mathematical heart of Bayesian classification: $P(\omega_i \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)}{p(x)}$.
- ✅ The **MAP (Maximum A Posteriori) rule** decides the class with the highest posterior probability.
- ✅ The **discriminant function** $g_i(x) = \ln p(x \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$ is monotonic in the posterior — the argmax is preserved.
- ✅ The **multivariate Gaussian** density has two key parameters per class: mean $\mu_i$ and covariance $\Sigma_i$.
- ✅ **Three cases** of Gaussian Bayesian classification:
  - **Case 1** ($\Sigma_i = \sigma^2 I$): **Linear** boundary, **minimum distance** classifier.
  - **Case 2** ($\Sigma_i = \Sigma$): **Linear** boundary, **LDA** classifier.
  - **Case 3** ($\Sigma_i$ distinct): **Quadratic** boundary, **QDA** classifier.
- ✅ The **Mahalanobis distance** $(x-\mu_i)^T \Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu_i)$ generalizes Euclidean distance to handle correlations.
- ✅ The **Bayes Risk** $R(\alpha_i \mid x) = \sum_j \lambda_{ij} P(\omega_j \mid x)$ allows asymmetric loss modeling.
- ✅ **0-1 loss** (equal cost for all errors) reduces Bayesian risk to minimum error rate.
- ✅ The **log transformation** is the standard simplification trick for Gaussian discriminant functions.
- ✅ **Parameter count** scales as $O(cd^2)$ for QDA versus $O(cd + d^2)$ for LDA — choose based on dataset size.
- ✅ **Decision boundary type is a function of covariance assumption**, not the underlying distribution's complexity.
- ✅ KTU **frequently tests** the derivation of discriminant functions and the classification of test points in numerical problems.
- ✅ **Always state assumptions** (Gaussian, covariance structure, prior knowledge) at the beginning of any solution.
- ✅ **Compute carefully**: determinant, matrix inverse, quadratic expansion — these are where most marks are lost.
- ✅ The **Bayes classifier is optimal** in the sense of minimizing classification error (for 0-1 loss) — no other classifier using the same features and class distributions can beat it on average.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Naive Bayes Classifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Naive Bayes Classifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Naive Bayes Classifiers** are a family of simple yet highly effective **probabilistic machine learning algorithms** based on applying **Bayes' Theorem** with a strong (hence "naive") assumption of **conditional independence** between every pair of features, given the value of the class label. Formally, for a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ and a class variable $C_k$, the classifier assigns the class that maximizes the posterior probability:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k \in \{1, \ldots, K\}} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

Despite the oversimplified independence assumption, Naive Bayes classifiers have demonstrated surprisingly strong performance, especially in **high-dimensional problems** such as text classification, spam filtering, and medical diagnosis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Naive" in Naive Bayes refers to the assumption that **all features are mutually independent** given the class label. This is almost never true in real-world data, yet the classifier still performs remarkably well, making it a benchmark (baseline) model in supervised learning.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **doctor diagnosing a disease** based on three observed symptoms: fever, cough, and body ache. The doctor knows:
- The general probability that any random person has the flu (the **prior**).
- How likely each symptom appears in flu patients (the **likelihoods**).

Even though in reality fever, cough, and body ache are correlated (a flu patient with fever is more likely to also have a cough), the doctor **pretends** they are independent to make a quick probabilistic judgment. This is exactly what Naive Bayes does — it ignores feature correlations and just multiplies individual probabilities to estimate the most likely class.

Think of it as a **fast, rule-of-thumb jury** that ignores the conspiracy between clues and just counts votes weighted by their typical reliability for each verdict.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Probabilistic Components (MUST MEMORIZE):**
> - **Prior Probability $P(C_k)$**: The initial belief about a class before observing any feature.
> - **Likelihood $P(x \mid C_k)$**: The probability of observing feature $x$ given class $C_k$.
> - **Posterior $P(C_k \mid x)$**: The updated belief about class $C_k$ after seeing evidence $x$.
> - **Evidence $P(x)$**: The marginal probability of observing $x$ across all classes (a normalizing constant).
> - **Conditional Independence**: $P(x_1, x_2 \mid C_k) = P(x_1 \mid C_k) \cdot P(x_2 \mid C_k)$.

### 1.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application Domain | Use Case | Why Naive Bayes Works |
|---|---|---|
| **Email Filtering** | Spam vs. Ham classification | Words are treated independently; small training data suffices |
| **Sentiment Analysis** | Positive vs. Negative review | Bag-of-words features are conditionally independent given sentiment |
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Disease prediction from symptoms | Probabilistic reasoning handles uncertainty in symptoms |
| **Document Categorization** | News article topic tagging | Scales well to thousands of features (words) |
| **Real-time Prediction** | IoT sensor fault detection | Extremely fast inference — no iterative optimization required |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundation: Bayes' Theorem

The classifier is rooted in the **Bayes' Theorem** of conditional probability. For two events $A$ and $B$ with $P(B) > 0$:

$$P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}$$

Extending this to a class label $C_k$ and a feature vector $\mathbf{x}$:

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

### 2.2 The "Naive" Independence Assumption

Computing $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$ directly is **intractable** for high-dimensional $\mathbf{x}$ because it requires estimating a joint distribution over all possible feature combinations. The naive assumption simplifies this:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

This means the joint probability becomes a simple product of individual conditional probabilities.

### 2.3 Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) Decision Rule

Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant across all classes (it does not depend on $C_k$), it can be dropped during maximization:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

The class with the **highest posterior probability** wins.

### 2.4 Handling Numerical Underflow with Log Probabilities

Multiplying many small probabilities (e.g., 10 words × probability 0.001) can underflow to zero. The standard fix is to take the **logarithm**, converting the product into a sum:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} \left[ \log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid C_k) \right]$$

This is **monotonically equivalent** to the original (log is a monotonic function) and is numerically stable.

### 2.5 The Zero Probability Problem & Laplace Smoothing

If a categorical feature value $x_i$ never appears with class $C_k$ in training data, then $P(x_i \mid C_k) = 0$, which **zeroes out the entire product**. **Additive (Laplace) smoothing** prevents this:

$$\hat{P}(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, C_k) + \alpha}{\text{count}(C_k) + \alpha \cdot V}$$

where $V$ is the vocabulary size and $\alpha \geq 0$ is the smoothing parameter (typically $\alpha = 1$).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Notation Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$ | Bayes' Theorem | $\mathbf{x}$ is feature vector |
| 2 | $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$ | Conditional Independence Assumption | $n$ = number of features |
| 3 | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$ | MAP Classification Rule | Ignore $P(\mathbf{x})$ |
| 4 | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} \left[ \log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid C_k) \right]$ | Log-Space Decision Rule | Prevents underflow |
| 5 | $P(C_k) = \frac{N_k}{N}$ | Prior Probability | $N_k$ = count of class $k$, $N$ = total |
| 6 | $\hat{P}(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, C_k) + \alpha}{\text{count}(C_k) + \alpha \cdot V}$ | Laplace-Smoothed Likelihood | $\alpha = 1$ is standard |
| 7 | $P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_k^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu_k)^2}{2\sigma_k^2}\right)$ | Gaussian NB Likelihood | For continuous features |

### 2.7 Variants of Naive Bayes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Variants — Know the Differences:**
>
> 1. **Gaussian Naive Bayes**: Assumes features follow a **Normal (Gaussian) distribution**. Used for continuous data. Likelihood computed using the Probability Density Function.
> 2. **Multinomial Naive Bayes**: Assumes feature vectors represent **counts/frequencies** (e.g., word counts in text). Used for document classification.
> 3. **Bernoulli Naive Bayes**: Assumes features are **binary (0/1)** indicating presence/absence. Used for short-text classification.
> 4. **Complement Naive Bayes**: Adapted for **imbalanced datasets** in text classification.

### 2.8 Engineering Utility

- **Baseline model**: Naive Bayes is the first model to try in any classification problem because it is fast, requires minimal training data, and gives surprisingly competitive results.
- **Online/Streaming systems**: Updates are simple — just maintain class and feature counts. No retraining of weights required.
- **Embedded systems**: Tiny memory footprint; perfect for IoT edge devices performing local inference.
- **Explainability**: Predictions can be explained in plain probabilistic terms — important for **medical, legal, and regulatory** applications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation: From Bayes' Theorem to Naive Bayes

We start from the general classification problem. Given a feature vector $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$, we want to find the class $C_k$ that maximizes the posterior probability $P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$.

**Step 1:** Begin with Bayes' Theorem applied to classification.

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

*Logic:* This is the direct statement of Bayes' Theorem, where event $A$ is "class is $C_k$" and event $B$ is "feature vector is $\mathbf{x}$."

**Step 2:** Define the Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) classifier.

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$$

*Logic:* The optimal class is the one with the highest posterior probability. This is the definition of the Bayes Optimal Classifier.

**Step 3:** Substitute Bayes' Theorem into the MAP rule.

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

*Logic:* Direct substitution to express the posterior in terms of likelihood, prior, and evidence.

**Step 4:** Drop the evidence term $P(\mathbf{x})$ since it is constant with respect to $k$.

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)$$

*Logic:* The denominator $P(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j} P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_j) P(C_j)$ does not depend on $k$, so removing it does not change which $k$ maximizes the expression.

**Step 5:** Apply the chain rule of probability to expand $P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$.

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C_k) = P(x_1 \mid C_k) \cdot P(x_2 \mid x_1, C_k) \cdots P(x_n \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1}, C_k)$$

*Logic:* The full joint conditional probability expands as a chain of conditional dependencies.

**Step 6:** Apply the naive (strong) conditional independence assumption.

$$P(x_i \mid x_1, \ldots, x_{i-1}, C_k) = P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

*Logic:* Each feature is assumed independent of all others, given the class. This is the "naive" assumption that gives the algorithm its name.

**Step 7:** Replace the joint conditional with a product of marginals.

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

*Logic:* The chain rule simplifies dramatically to a single product of $n$ single-feature likelihoods.

**Step 8:** Write the final Naive Bayes decision rule.

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

*Logic:* This is the canonical form of the Naive Bayes classifier. ☐

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Spam Classification

Suppose we have a training corpus with the following statistics:

| Class | Total Emails | Contains "free" | Contains "offer" |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spam | 20 | 15 | 12 |
| Ham | 80 | 5 | 8 |

A new email contains both "free" and "offer". Classify it.

**Step 1: Compute the priors.**

$$P(\text{Spam}) = \frac{20}{100} = 0.20$$

$$P(\text{Ham}) = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80$$

**Step 2: Compute the likelihoods (with Laplace smoothing, $\alpha = 1$, $V = 2$).**

$$P(\text{free} \mid \text{Spam}) = \frac{15 + 1}{20 + 2} = \frac{16}{22} = 0.727$$

$$P(\text{offer} \mid \text{Spam}) = \frac{12 + 1}{20 + 2} = \frac{13}{22} = 0.591$$

$$P(\text{free} \mid \text{Ham}) = \frac{5 + 1}{80 + 2} = \frac{6}{82} = 0.073$$

$$P(\text{offer} \mid \text{Ham}) = \frac{8 + 1}{80 + 2} = \frac{9}{82} = 0.110$$

**Step 3: Compute unnormalized posteriors (Spam).**

$$P(\text{Spam} \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto 0.20 \times 0.727 \times 0.591 = 0.0859$$

**Step 4: Compute unnormalized posteriors (Ham).**

$$P(\text{Ham} \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto 0.80 \times 0.073 \times 0.110 = 0.00642$$

**Step 5: Compare and classify.**

$$0.0859 \gg 0.00642 \implies \hat{y} = \text{Spam}$$

*Logic:* The Spam posterior is over 13× higher, so the email is classified as Spam. The large gap demonstrates how Naive Bayes can produce confident predictions even with limited features.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification via Log-Space:** The same classification can be obtained using log probabilities:
> Spam: $\log(0.20) + \log(0.727) + \log(0.591) = -1.609 + -0.319 + -0.526 = -2.454$
> Ham: $\log(0.80) + \log(0.073) + \log(0.110) = -0.223 + -2.617 + -2.207 = -5.047$
> Since $-2.454 > -5.047$, the email is classified as Spam. Both methods yield the same answer, confirming equivalence.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Naive Bayes Conceptual Architecture

The following block diagram illustrates how a new test instance flows through the Naive Bayes classifier and how the posterior is computed per class.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Input Feature Vector x] --> nodeB[Feature Decomposition]
    nodeB --> nodeC1[Feature x1]
    nodeB --> nodeC2[Feature x2]
    nodeB --> nodeC3[Feature xi]
    nodeB --> nodeC4[Feature xn]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD[Per-Class Likelihood Lookup]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD
    nodeC3 --> nodeD
    nodeC4 --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Training Data Statistics]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Prior P of Ck]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Likelihood P of xi given Ck]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Posterior Computation]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Argmax over Classes]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Predicted Class Label]
```

### 4.2 Training and Inference Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Training Dataset] --> nodeB[Compute Class Priors P of Ck]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Compute Feature Likelihoods P of xi given Ck]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Naive Bayes Model]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[New Test Instance x]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Compute Posterior per Class]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Apply Argmax Decision Rule]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Final Class Prediction]
```

### 4.3 Bayes' Theorem Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Prior P of Ck] --> nodeC[Posterior P of Ck given x]
    nodeB[Likelihood P of x given Ck] --> nodeC
    nodeD[Evidence P of x] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE[Classification via Argmax]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture for Text Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw Text Document] --> nodeB[Tokenization and Lowercasing]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Stopword Removal]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Vocabulary Mapping]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Feature Vector Construction]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Naive Bayes Classifier Module]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Posterior Comparison}
    nodeG --> nodeH[Class Label Output]
    nodeI[Training Corpus] --> nodeJ[Vocabulary Builder]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Class Prior and Likelihood Tables]
    nodeK --> nodeF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The arrows represent **data flow and dependencies**, not physical wiring. Each rectangular node is a processing stage, and the diamond node represents a **decision branch** (the argmax operation that selects the winning class).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

*State Bayes' Theorem and explain the meaning of each term in the context of a classification problem.*

**Model Answer:**

Bayes' Theorem states that for a class $C_k$ and feature vector $\mathbf{x}$:

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

- **$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$** — Posterior: probability of class $C_k$ after seeing features $\mathbf{x}$.
- **$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k)$** — Likelihood: probability of observing $\mathbf{x}$ given class $C_k$.
- **$P(C_k)$** — Prior: initial probability of class $C_k$ without seeing features.
- **$P(\mathbf{x})$** — Evidence: marginal probability of $\mathbf{x}$ across all classes (normalizing constant).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

*What is the "naive" assumption in Naive Bayes? Why is it called "naive"?*

**Model Answer:**

The "naive" assumption is that **all features are conditionally independent given the class label**. Mathematically:

$$P(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

It is called "naive" because in real-world data, features are almost always correlated (e.g., the words "rain" and "umbrella" co-occur frequently). The classifier naively ignores these dependencies, which simplifies computation enormously. Despite this unrealistic assumption, the classifier often performs competitively in practice.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Naive Bayes classification rule starting from Bayes' Theorem. Clearly state each step and the assumption made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a training set with two classes $C_1$ and $C_2$. The prior probabilities are $P(C_1) = 0.4$ and $P(C_2) = 0.6$. For a test instance with two binary features $x_1$ and $x_2$, the likelihoods are:

- $P(x_1 = 1 \mid C_1) = 0.8$, $P(x_1 = 1 \mid C_2) = 0.3$
- $P(x_2 = 1 \mid C_1) = 0.5$, $P(x_2 = 1 \mid C_2) = 0.7$

For the test instance with $x_1 = 1$ and $x_2 = 0$, compute $P(x_2 = 0 \mid C_1) = 0.5$ and $P(x_2 = 0 \mid C_2) = 0.3$. Predict the class.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Step 1: Bayes' Theorem for classification:

$$P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Step 2: MAP rule — choose class that maximizes the posterior:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(C_k \mid \mathbf{x})$$

Step 3: Substitute Bayes' Theorem:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} \frac{P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)}{P(\mathbf{x})}$$

Step 4: Drop $P(\mathbf{x})$ since it is constant with respect to $k$:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) \cdot P(C_k)$$

Step 5: Apply the **conditional independence assumption** — each feature is independent of others given the class:

$$P(\mathbf{x} \mid C_k) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

Step 6: Final Naive Bayes decision rule:

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Step 1: Compute the prior (already given):
- $P(C_1) = 0.4$
- $P(C_2) = 0.6$

Step 2: Extract the relevant likelihoods for the test instance $(x_1 = 1, x_2 = 0)$:
- $P(x_1 = 1 \mid C_1) = 0.8$
- $P(x_2 = 0 \mid C_1) = 0.5$
- $P(x_1 = 1 \mid C_2) = 0.3$
- $P(x_2 = 0 \mid C_2) = 0.3$

Step 3: Compute unnormalized posterior for $C_1$:

$$P(C_1 \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto 0.4 \times 0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.16$$

Step 4: Compute unnormalized posterior for $C_2$:

$$P(C_2 \mid \mathbf{x}) \propto 0.6 \times 0.3 \times 0.3 = 0.054$$

Step 5: Apply the argmax rule:

$$0.16 > 0.054 \implies \hat{y} = C_1$$

The test instance is classified as $C_1$.

---

**Q4. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Zero Frequency Problem** in Naive Bayes. How does **Laplace Smoothing** address it? Provide the formula and explain each term.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Gaussian**, **Multinomial**, and **Bernoulli** Naive Bayes classifiers. Give one practical application scenario for each.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Zero Frequency Problem and Laplace Smoothing:**

The **Zero Frequency Problem** occurs when a categorical feature value $x_i$ never co-occurs with a class $C_k$ in the training data. In this case, $P(x_i \mid C_k) = 0$, which **zeroes out the entire product** in the Naive Bayes formula, regardless of how strong the other features are. This makes the classifier overly confident in rejecting that class.

**Laplace (Additive) Smoothing** solves this by adding a small constant $\alpha \geq 0$ to every count:

$$\hat{P}(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, C_k) + \alpha}{\text{count}(C_k) + \alpha \cdot V}$$

Where:
- $\text{count}(x_i, C_k)$ = number of times feature value $x_i$ appears with class $C_k$ in training data.
- $\text{count}(C_k)$ = total count of class $C_k$ in training data.
- $V$ = number of unique feature values (vocabulary size).
- $\alpha$ = smoothing parameter (typically $\alpha = 1$).

With $\alpha = 1$, even unseen feature-class combinations get a small non-zero probability, ensuring no posterior is ever exactly zero.

**(b) Comparison of Naive Bayes Variants:**

| Variant | Feature Type | Likelihood Formula | Application Scenario |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gaussian NB** | Continuous (real-valued) | $P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma_k^2}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x_i - \mu_k)^2}{2\sigma_k^2}\right)$ | Predicting disease from continuous lab measurements (e.g., blood pressure, cholesterol) |
| **Multinomial NB** | Discrete counts | $P(x_i \mid C_k) = \frac{\text{count}(x_i, C_k) + \alpha}{\sum \text{count}(x_j, C_k) + \alpha \cdot V}$ | Document classification using word frequencies (e.g., news topic tagging) |
| **Bernoulli NB** | Binary (0/1) | $P(x_i \mid C_k) = p_{ik}^{x_i}(1 - p_{ik})^{1-x_i}$ | Spam filtering using presence/absence of specific keywords |

**Detailed Explanations:**

- **Gaussian Naive Bayes**: Assumes that continuous features within each class follow a **Normal distribution**. The mean $\mu_k$ and variance $\sigma_k^2$ are estimated from training data. Best suited for sensor data or biometric measurements.

- **Multinomial Naive Bayes**: Models the feature vector as a **count vector** — e.g., how many times each word appears in a document. The likelihood is the normalized count with smoothing. This is the go-to model for **text classification with bag-of-words**.

- **Bernoulli Naive Bayes**: Models features as **binary indicators** — whether a word appears (1) or does not appear (0) in a document. Useful for **short-text classification** and **keyword-based spam detection**, where the multiplicity of a word matters less than its presence.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to drop $P(\mathbf{x})$**: Students often compute the full posterior including the evidence term, wasting time and marks. Since $P(\mathbf{x})$ is constant, comparing unnormalized products is sufficient.
> 2. **Not applying Laplace Smoothing**: In numerical problems with words or categorical features, examiners often expect $\alpha = 1$ smoothing. Omitting it can yield zero probabilities and is a common error.
> 3. **Confusing log-likelihood with cross-entropy**: When using log-space, do not confuse $\log P(\cdot)$ with the cross-entropy loss function. Naive Bayes uses log-likelihood only for **inference**, not training.
> 4. **Mixing up Gaussian vs. Multinomial formulas**: Many students mistakenly use the Gaussian PDF for text (count) data. Always match the formula to the data type.
> 5. **Forgetting the conditional independence assumption**: If you derive Naive Bayes without explicitly stating this assumption, full marks for derivation will not be awarded.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Naive Bayes = Bayes' Theorem + Conditional Independence Assumption**.
- ✅ The "naive" assumption is that features are mutually independent **given the class label**.
- ✅ The decision rule is $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k} P(C_k) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C_k)$.
- ✅ **Prior** $P(C_k) = N_k / N$; **Likelihood** $P(x_i \mid C_k)$ is estimated from training counts.
- ✅ The evidence term $P(\mathbf{x})$ is **constant** and can be dropped during classification.
- ✅ Use **log-probabilities** to prevent numerical underflow: $\log P(C_k) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid C_k)$.
- ✅ **Laplace Smoothing** ($\alpha = 1$) prevents the **zero frequency problem** in categorical features.
- ✅ **Gaussian NB** → continuous data; **Multinomial NB** → text count data; **Bernoulli NB** → binary feature data.
- ✅ Advantages: **fast, simple, scalable, works with small data, handles high dimensions**.
- ✅ Disadvantages: **independence assumption often violated, poor probability calibration, zero-frequency problem**.
- ✅ Naive Bayes is often the **first baseline model** to try in any classification task.
- ✅ Applications: **spam filtering, sentiment analysis, document categorization, medical diagnosis, real-time prediction**.
- ✅ The model is **generative** — it models the joint distribution $P(x, C_k)$, not just $P(C_k \mid x)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Logistic Regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Logistic Regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logistic Regression** is a fundamental **supervised learning** algorithm used primarily for **classification tasks** (despite its name containing "regression"). It models the **probability** that a given input vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ belongs to a particular class $y \in \{0, 1\}$ by fitting a linear decision boundary to a transformed version of the data using the **logistic (sigmoid) function**.

Mathematically, for a binary classification problem, logistic regression estimates:

$$P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{w}, b) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b)}}$$

where $\sigma(\cdot)$ is the **sigmoid (logistic) function**, $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the weight vector, and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bias term. The decision rule is simply:

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 1.2 Why Not Use Linear Regression for Classification?

| Issue | Linear Regression | Logistic Regression |
|---|---|---|
| Output range | $(-\infty, +\infty)$ | $[0, 1]$ (probabilities) |
| Assumes error structure | Gaussian/Normal | Bernoulli |
| Sensitive to outliers | High | Moderate |
| Decision boundary | Linear regression line | Linear (in transformed space) |
| Loss function | Mean Squared Error (MSE) | Log Loss (Cross-Entropy) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight):** Linear regression assumes a continuous, unbounded target and Gaussian-distributed errors. For classification, the target is **discrete** (categorical), and the error distribution is **Bernoulli** (or Multinoulli for multiclass). Logistic regression is the **discriminative** counterpart to **Generative Discriminative pairings** like Gaussian Discriminant Analysis.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Medical Diagnosis Engine**
>
> Imagine a doctor trying to predict whether a patient has a disease ($y=1$) or not ($y=0$) based on symptoms (age, blood pressure, cholesterol). The doctor assigns a *weight* to each symptom based on its importance. If the total weighted score is high, the patient is likely sick. However, the score itself isn't a probability — it's just a number. To convert this number into a probability (0 to 100%), the doctor passes it through a **squashing function** (the sigmoid) that compresses any large positive value toward 1 and any large negative value toward 0.
>
> **Logistic regression does exactly this**: it computes a linear combination of features, then "squashes" the result through the sigmoid to obtain a calibrated probability estimate.

### 1.4 The Sigmoid (Logistic) Function — Heart of the Algorithm

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Properties** (essential for KTU viva/derivations):
- **Range:** $\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)$ for all $z \in \mathbb{R}$
- **Monotonically increasing**
- **Symmetry:** $\sigma(-z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$
- **Derivative:** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$
- **Passes through:** $(0, 0.5)$
- **Asymptotes:** $\sigma(z) \to 0$ as $z \to -\infty$ and $\sigma(z) \to 1$ as $z \to +\infty$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **sigmoid derivative property** $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ is the most critical mathematical identity in logistic regression. It is repeatedly used in gradient descent derivations and is a guaranteed **2-mark question** in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logistic Regression Model — End-to-End

**Step 1 — Linear Combination (Score):** Compute the weighted sum of inputs.

$$z = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j x_j + b$$

This is a real-valued "logit" or "score."

**Step 2 — Probabilistic Mapping:** Pass the score through the sigmoid to obtain a probability.

$$P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{w}, b) = \hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

By the complement rule:

$$P(y=0 \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{w}, b) = 1 - \hat{y}$$

**Step 3 — Decision:** Apply a threshold (default 0.5) for class assignment.

### 2.2 The Logit Link Function & Odds Ratio

The **logit** is the inverse of the sigmoid and provides the natural link between linear regression and probability:

$$\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b$$

The term $\frac{p}{1-p}$ is called the **odds ratio**. Thus, logistic regression is a **linear model for the log-odds**:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P(y=1)}{P(y=0)}\right) = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "logit" matters:** It is the *canonical link function* for the Bernoulli distribution in the Generalized Linear Model (GLM) framework. This is high-yield for KTU derivations linking logistic regression to the **exponential family** of distributions.

### 2.3 Cost Function: Log Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy)

Since the output is a probability, we need a loss function that penalizes wrong probabilistic predictions heavily. The **Log Loss** (also called **Binary Cross-Entropy**) is derived from the principle of **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**.

For a single training example $(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})$:

$$\mathcal{L}^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\Big[y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})\Big]$$

**Total cost** over $m$ training examples:

$$J(\mathbf{w}, b) = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mathcal{L}^{(i)}(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\Big[y^{(i)} \log(\hat{y}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})\Big]$$

**Why not MSE?** The log loss cost function is **convex** in $(\mathbf{w}, b)$, ensuring a unique global minimum. MSE combined with sigmoid produces a **non-convex** loss surface riddled with local minima.

### 2.4 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) Derivation Connection

The likelihood of the dataset under the model is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(y^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \mathbf{w}, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \big[\hat{y}^{(i)}\big]^{y^{(i)}} \big[1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}\big]^{1-y^{(i)}}$$

Taking the log-likelihood:

$$\ell(\mathbf{w}, b) = \log \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\Big[y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})\Big]$$

Maximizing $\ell$ is equivalent to minimizing $J(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{m}\ell$.

### 2.5 Gradient Descent Updates

The partial derivatives of the cost function with respect to each parameter (the gradients) are:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\big(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\big)\, x_j^{(i)}$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\big(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\big)$$

**Update rule** (with learning rate $\alpha$):

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} \qquad b := b - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial b}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Elegant fact:** The gradient has the same form as in linear regression — $\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}$ multiplied by $x_j^{(i)}$ — but $\hat{y}^{(i)}$ is now a sigmoid output (nonlinear), not a linear output. This is a recurring **3-mark KTU question**.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sigmoid function | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Output $\in (0, 1)$ |
| 2 | Sigmoid derivative | $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)\big(1 - \sigma(z)\big)$ | Used in backprop |
| 3 | Hypothesis | $\hat{y} = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b)$ | Probability estimate |
| 4 | Logit (log-odds) | $\ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right) = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b$ | Linear in $\mathbf{x}$ |
| 5 | Log Loss (single) | $\mathcal{L} = -\big[y\log\hat{y} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{y})\big]$ | $\geq 0$ |
| 6 | Total Cost | $J = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i}\big[y^{(i)}\log\hat{y}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})\big]$ | Convex in $\mathbf{w},b$ |
| 7 | Likelihood | $\mathcal{L} = \prod_{i}\hat{y}_i^{y_i}(1-\hat{y}_i)^{1-y_i}$ | Product form |
| 8 | Gradient (weight) | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_i(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$ | Vector form: $\nabla_\mathbf{w}J = \frac{1}{m}\mathbf{X}^T(\hat{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y})$ |
| 9 | Gradient (bias) | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_i(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})$ | Scalar |
| 10 | Decision boundary | $\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Where $P=0.5$ |
| 11 | L2 Regularization | $J_{reg} = J + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_j w_j^2$ | Prevents overfitting |
| 12 | L1 Regularization | $J_{reg} = J + \frac{\lambda}{2m}\sum_j \vert w_j \vert$ | Sparsity inducing |
| 13 | Multiclass (Softmax) | $P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{\mathbf{w}_k^T\mathbf{x}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{\mathbf{w}_j^T\mathbf{x}}}$ | One-vs-All alternative |
| 14 | Multiclass loss | $J = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_i \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}\{y^{(i)}=k\}\log\hat{y}_k^{(i)}$ | Categorical CE |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Healthcare:** Disease risk prediction (diabetes, cancer, heart disease)
- **Finance:** Credit card fraud detection, loan default prediction
- **NLP:** Spam email classification, sentiment analysis
- **Manufacturing:** Defect detection, predictive maintenance
- **Marketing:** Customer churn prediction, click-through rate estimation
- **A/B Testing:** Conversion probability estimation in online experimentation

### 2.8 Extensions & Variants

| Variant | Use Case | Key Difference |
|---|---|---|
| **Binary Logistic Regression** | Two classes | Standard sigmoid |
| **Multinomial Logistic Regression** | $K \geq 2$ classes | Softmax activation |
| **Regularized Logistic Regression** | High-dimensional data | Adds $\ell_1$ or $\ell_2$ penalty |
| **Ridge Logistic Regression** | Correlated features | $\ell_2$ penalty |
| **Lasso Logistic Regression** | Feature selection | $\ell_1$ penalty |
| **Elastic Net** | Combined | $\ell_1 + \ell_2$ mixture |
| **Polynomial Logistic Regression** | Non-linear boundaries | Polynomial features in $z$ |
| **Bayesian Logistic Regression** | Uncertainty quantification | Prior on $\mathbf{w}$ |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Sigmoid Derivative $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$

**Step 1:** Start with the definition of the sigmoid.

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 2:** Rewrite using a positive exponent for easier differentiation. Let $u = 1 + e^{-z}$.

$$\sigma(z) = u^{-1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{du}{dz} = -e^{-z}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the chain rule.

$$\sigma'(z) = \frac{d\sigma}{dz} = -u^{-2} \cdot \frac{du}{dz} = -u^{-2} \cdot (-e^{-z}) = \frac{e^{-z}}{u^2}$$

**Step 4:** Expand the denominator.

$$\sigma'(z) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})^2}$$

**Step 5:** Factor the numerator and denominator to obtain the elegant form.

$$\sigma'(z) = \frac{e^{-z}}{(1 + e^{-z})} \cdot \frac{1}{(1 + e^{-z})}$$

**Step 6:** Notice that $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = \frac{e^z}{1 + e^z}$ (multiply top and bottom by $e^z$). Also note that:

$$1 - \sigma(z) = 1 - \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}} = \frac{e^{-z}}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

**Step 7:** Therefore, substituting back:

$$\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z) \cdot \big(1 - \sigma(z)\big) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This derivative identity dramatically simplifies the gradient computation in logistic regression and is also fundamental in the backpropagation algorithm of neural networks (since sigmoid was historically a common activation function).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Gradient of the Log Loss with respect to $w_j$

**Step 1:** Write the cost function for a single example (we will average later).

$$\mathcal{L}^{(i)} = -\Big[y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})\Big]$$

where $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \sigma(z^{(i)})$ and $z^{(i)} = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b$.

**Step 2:** Compute the partial derivative of $\mathcal{L}^{(i)}$ with respect to $z^{(i)}$.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = -\Big[\frac{y^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} - \frac{1 - y^{(i)}}{1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}}\Big]$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\frac{\partial \hat{y}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = \sigma'(z^{(i)}) = \hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})$.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = -\Big[\frac{y^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}} - \frac{1 - y^{(i)}}{1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}}\Big] \cdot \hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})$$

**Step 4:** Expand the bracket.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = -\Big[\frac{y^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}) - (1 - y^{(i)})\hat{y}^{(i)}}{\hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})}\Big] \cdot \hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $\hat{y}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})$.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = -\Big[y^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\hat{y}^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)} + y^{(i)}\hat{y}^{(i)}\Big] = -\Big[y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}\Big] = \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}$$

**Step 6:** Apply the chain rule to get $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial w_j^{(i)}}$.

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)}$$

**Step 7:** Average over the entire training set.

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \, x_j^{(i)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Likelihood to Log Loss

**Step 1:** For a single training example, the probability of observing label $y^{(i)}$ given input $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ is the **Bernoulli PMF**:

$$P(y^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \mathbf{w}) = \big[\hat{y}^{(i)}\big]^{y^{(i)}} \big[1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}\big]^{1 - y^{(i)}}$$

**Step 2:** Assuming **i.i.d.** training examples, the joint likelihood over $m$ samples is:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} P(y^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \mathbf{w}, b) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \big[\hat{y}^{(i)}\big]^{y^{(i)}} \big[1 - \hat{y}^{(i)}\big]^{1 - y^{(i)}}$$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm of the likelihood (the log-likelihood is easier to optimize and the log is a monotonic transformation).

$$\ell(\mathbf{w}, b) = \log \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\Big[y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})\Big]$$

**Step 4:** The cost function is the **negative** of the mean log-likelihood (negative because we minimize cost, not maximize likelihood).

$$J(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{m}\ell(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\Big[y^{(i)} \log \hat{y}^{(i)} + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{y}^{(i)})\Big]$$

This is the **Log Loss / Binary Cross-Entropy**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Softmax Function for Multiclass Logistic Regression

**Step 1:** For $K$ classes, generalize the sigmoid to a vector-valued function (the softmax):

$$P(y = k \mid \mathbf{x}; \mathbf{W}) = \frac{e^{\mathbf{w}_k^T \mathbf{x}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{\mathbf{w}_j^T \mathbf{x}}} \quad \text{for } k = 1, 2, \dots, K$$

**Step 2:** Verify that the probabilities sum to 1.

$$\sum_{k=1}^{K} P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{K} e^{\mathbf{w}_k^T \mathbf{x}}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{\mathbf{w}_j^T \mathbf{x}}} = 1$$

**Step 3:** Verify the binary case ($K=2$). For $k=1$:

$$P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{\mathbf{w}_1^T \mathbf{x}}}{e^{\mathbf{w}_1^T \mathbf{x}} + e^{\mathbf{w}_2^T \mathbf{x}}} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{(\mathbf{w}_2 - \mathbf{w}_1)^T \mathbf{x}}} = \sigma\big((\mathbf{w}_1 - \mathbf{w}_2)^T \mathbf{x}\big)$$

This reduces to standard logistic regression with $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}_1 - \mathbf{w}_2$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Stability Trick (KTU 2024 Highlight):** In practice, we subtract $\max_j \mathbf{w}_j^T \mathbf{x}$ from every exponent to prevent overflow:
> $$\text{softmax}(\mathbf{z})_i = \frac{e^{z_i - \max_j z_j}}{\sum_{k=1}^{K} e^{z_k - \max_j z_j}}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Example

**Sample problem:** Given data $\mathbf{x} = (1, 2)^T$, $\mathbf{w} = (0.5, -0.3)^T$, $b = 0.1$, $y = 1$. Compute $\hat{y}$ and the loss.

| Step | Computation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $z = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b = (0.5)(1) + (-0.3)(2) + 0.1$ | $z = 0.0$ |
| 2 | $\hat{y} = \sigma(0) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{0}}$ | $\hat{y} = 0.5$ |
| 3 | $\mathcal{L} = -[1 \cdot \log(0.5) + 0 \cdot \log(0.5)]$ | $\mathcal{L} = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ |
| 4 | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_1} = (0.5 - 1)(1) = -0.5$ | Gradient for $w_1$ |
| 5 | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_2} = (0.5 - 1)(2) = -1.0$ | Gradient for $w_2$ |
| 6 | $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0.5 - 1 = -0.5$ | Gradient for $b$ |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Logistic Regression End-to-End Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Features X] --> B[Linear Combination z = W transpose X + b]
    B --> C[Sigmoid Activation sigma z]
    C --> D[Probability P y=1 given X]
    D --> E{Threshold 0.5}
    E -->|Yes| F[Predicted Class 1]
    E -->|No| G[Predicted Class 0]
    D --> H[Log Loss Computation]
    H --> I[Gradient Calculation]
    I --> J[Parameter Update W b]
    J --> B
    K[Training Data y_true] --> H
```

### 4.2 Sigmoid Function — Input/Output Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Zminus[z equals minus infinity] --> S0[sigma z approaches 0]
    Zneg[z equals minus 5] --> S1[sigma z equals 0.0067]
    Zzero[z equals 0] --> S2[sigma z equals 0.5]
    Zpos[z equals 5] --> S3[sigma z equals 0.9933]
    Zinf[z equals plus infinity] --> S4[sigma z approaches 1]
```

### 4.3 Training Loop Architecture (Gradient Descent)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize W and b randomly] --> B[For each epoch]
    B --> C[Forward Pass: Compute y_hat]
    C --> D[Compute Cost J]
    D --> E[Backward Pass: Compute Gradients]
    E --> F[Update W := W minus alpha dJ/dW]
    F --> G[Update b := b minus alpha dJ/db]
    G --> H{Converged or Max Iterations}
    H -->|No| B
    H -->|Yes| I[Return Optimal W and b]
    J[Training Dataset] --> C
    K[Learning Rate alpha] --> F
    L[Regularization lambda] --> D
```

### 4.4 Binary Classification Decision Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Data with Two Classes] --> B[Fit Logistic Regression Model]
    B --> C[Compute Weight Vector W and Bias b]
    C --> D[Derive Decision Boundary: W transpose X + b equals 0]
    D --> E[Plot Linear Boundary Separating Classes]
    E --> F[New Data Point X_new]
    F --> G[Compute P y=1 given X_new]
    G --> H{P greater than 0.5}
    H -->|Yes| I[Classify as Positive]
    H -->|No| J[Classify as Negative]
```

### 4.5 Connection: Logistic Regression vs Neural Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Layer x1 x2 ... xn] --> B[Linear Layer: z equals W transpose x plus b]
    B --> C[Activation: Sigmoid or Softmax]
    C --> D[Output Probability]
    E[Single Layer Perceptron] --> A
    F[Neural Network Deep Model] --> A
    E -.->|Identical to| B
    F -.->|Stacked Layers| B
```

### 4.6 Regularization Effect on Decision Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Decision Boundary] --> B{High Variance Overfitting}
    B --> C[Add L2 Penalty lambda/2m times sum W squared]
    B --> D[Add L1 Penalty lambda/2m times sum absolute W]
    C --> E[Smooth Boundary: Smaller Weights]
    D --> F[Sparse Boundary: Few Active Features]
    E --> G[Better Generalization]
    F --> G
```
