## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Define the sigmoid function and state any three of its properties relevant to logistic regression.

**Model Answer:**

The **sigmoid (logistic) function** is defined as:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

where $z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$ is the linear score.

**Three key properties:**
1. **Bounded output range:** $\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)$ for all $z \in \mathbb{R}$, making it suitable for probability estimation.
2. **Smooth and differentiable:** The derivative is $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$, which is required for gradient-based optimization.
3. **Monotonically increasing and point-symmetric about $(0, 0.5)$:** Specifically, $\sigma(-z) = 1 - \sigma(z)$, ensuring the function has a unique transition midpoint at $z = 0$.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Why is the Mean Squared Error (MSE) cost function unsuitable for logistic regression? What is used in its place?

**Model Answer:**

**Why MSE fails:**
1. **Non-convexity:** The composition of the sigmoid function with the MSE loss produces a **non-convex** cost surface with multiple local minima, making gradient descent prone to getting stuck in suboptimal solutions.
2. **Poor gradient behavior:** The gradient of MSE combined with sigmoid vanishes (becomes very small) when $\hat{y}$ is close to 0 or 1, causing slow convergence (plateau/saturation problem).
3. **Mismatch with distribution:** MSE assumes Gaussian-distributed errors, but classification targets follow a **Bernoulli distribution**.

**Alternative used:** The **Log Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy)** cost function is used, which is:

$$J(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\Big[y^{(i)}\log\hat{y}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})\Big]$$

This is **convex** in $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ and arises naturally from Maximum Likelihood Estimation under the Bernoulli assumption.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam – Module 1, ESE Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the gradient of the binary cross-entropy loss function with respect to the weight vector $\mathbf{w}$ and bias $b$ in logistic regression. State the final update equations.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a binary classification dataset, the following table gives 4 data points with two features. Train logistic regression for **one iteration** of gradient descent with learning rate $\alpha = 0.1$ and initial weights $\mathbf{w} = (0, 0)^T$, $b = 0$. (You may leave final values to 4 decimal places.)

| $i$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $y$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| 2 | 2 | 3 | 0 |
| 3 | 3 | 1 | 1 |
| 4 | 4 | 3 | 1 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation [7 Marks]:**

We have $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \sigma(z^{(i)})$ where $z^{(i)} = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b$.

**Step 1 — Write the loss:**

$$\mathcal{L}^{(i)} = -\big[y^{(i)}\log\hat{y}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-\hat{y}^{(i)})\big]$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $z^{(i)}$:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} = \hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \quad \text{(derived in SECTION 3.2)}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $z^{(i)}$ with respect to $w_j$:**

$$\frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = x_j^{(i)} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial b} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Apply chain rule:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial z^{(i)}} \cdot \frac{\partial z^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})\, x_j^{(i)}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial b} = (\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})$$

**Step 5 — Average over $m$ examples and negate for gradient descent:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})\, x_j^{(i)}$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})$$

**Step 6 — Update rules:**

$$w_j := w_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} \qquad b := b - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial b}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Computation [7 Marks]:**

With initial $\mathbf{w} = (0, 0)^T$ and $b = 0$:

**Step 1 — Compute $z^{(i)} = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}^{(i)} + b = 0$ for all $i$ (since $\mathbf{w}=0, b=0$).**

**Step 2 — Compute predictions** $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \sigma(0) = 0.5$ for all $i$.

**Step 3 — Compute errors** $\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}$:

| $i$ | $\hat{y}^{(i)}$ | $y^{(i)}$ | $\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.5 | 0 | $+0.5$ |
| 2 | 0.5 | 0 | $+0.5$ |
| 3 | 0.5 | 1 | $-0.5$ |
| 4 | 0.5 | 1 | $-0.5$ |

**Step 4 — Compute gradients:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_1} = \frac{1}{4}\big[0.5(1) + 0.5(2) + (-0.5)(3) + (-0.5)(4)\big] = \frac{1}{4}(0.5 + 1.0 - 1.5 - 2.0) = \frac{-2.0}{4} = -0.5$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_2} = \frac{1}{4}\big[0.5(1) + 0.5(3) + (-0.5)(1) + (-0.5)(3)\big] = \frac{1}{4}(0.5 + 1.5 - 0.5 - 1.5) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial b} = \frac{1}{4}(0.5 + 0.5 - 0.5 - 0.5) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Update parameters with $\alpha = 0.1$:**

$$w_1 = 0 - 0.1(-0.5) = 0.05$$
$$w_2 = 0 - 0.1(0) = 0$$
$$b = 0 - 0.1(0) = 0$$

**Final Answer (After 1 iteration):** $\mathbf{w} = (0.05, 0)^T, \; b = 0$.

---

> **Q3 (B). [Alternative 14-Mark Question]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **log-odds** (logit) in logistic regression. Show that the logit transformation maps probabilities from $(0,1)$ to $(-\infty, +\infty)$. Derive the logit function from the sigmoid.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable diagram, explain the **Multinomial Logistic Regression (Softmax Regression)** model for a $K$-class classification problem. Derive the cross-entropy loss function for it.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Log-Odds / Logit [7 Marks]:**

The **odds** of an event with probability $p$ is defined as the ratio of the probability of the event occurring to the probability of it not occurring:

$$\text{Odds} = \frac{p}{1-p}$$

For logistic regression, $p = P(y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b)$. Since $p \in (0,1)$, the odds lie in $(0, +\infty)$.

The **log-odds (logit)** is the natural logarithm of the odds:

$$\text{logit}(p) = \ln\!\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Show the range:** For $p \in (0,1)$:
- As $p \to 0^+$: $\frac{p}{1-p} \to 0$, so $\ln(\text{odds}) \to -\infty$
- As $p \to 1^-$: $\frac{p}{1-p} \to +\infty$, so $\ln(\text{odds}) \to +\infty$
- Hence, $\text{logit}(p) \in (-\infty, +\infty)$. ✓

**Step 2 — Derive the logit from the sigmoid.** Given $p = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$,

$$\frac{p}{1-p} = \frac{1/(1+e^{-z})}{1 - 1/(1+e^{-z})} = \frac{1/(1+e^{-z})}{e^{-z}/(1+e^{-z})} = e^{z}$$

Therefore:

$$\text{logit}(p) = \ln(e^z) = z = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b$$

**Conclusion:** The logit is the **inverse of the sigmoid**, and logistic regression is precisely a **linear model for the log-odds** of the positive class. This justifies the linear-in-features assumption in the transformed (logit) space.

---

**(b) Multinomial Logistic Regression [7 Marks]:**

**Architecture Diagram (text-based):**

```
Input x (n-dim) ──► Linear Scores (z1, z2, ..., zK) where zk = wk^T x + bk
                          │
                          ▼
                  Softmax Activation
                          │
                          ▼
            Probabilities (p1, p2, ..., pK) with sum = 1
                          │
                          ▼
                  Predicted Class = argmax(pk)
```

**Step 1 — Softmax function:** For $K$ classes, the class probability is:

$$P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} e^{z_j}} \quad \text{where } z_k = \mathbf{w}_k^T \mathbf{x} + b_k$$

**Step 2 — Indicator (one-hot) encoding:** Each true label $y^{(i)} \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$ is represented as a one-hot vector $\mathbf{y}^{(i)}$ where:

$$\mathbb{1}\{y^{(i)} = k\} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } y^{(i)} = k \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Likelihood for one sample:** Under the **Categorical distribution**:

$$P(y^{(i)} \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)}; \mathbf{W}) = \prod_{k=1}^{K} \big[P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)})\big]^{\mathbb{1}\{y^{(i)}=k\}}$$

**Step 4 — Negative log-likelihood (Categorical Cross-Entropy):**

$$J(\mathbf{W}) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}\{y^{(i)}=k\} \log P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)})$$

**Step 5 — Gradient:** Differentiating $J$ w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}_k$ yields the simple update:

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}_k} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\big(P(y=k \mid \mathbf{x}^{(i)}) - \mathbb{1}\{y^{(i)}=k\}\big) \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

This mirrors the binary case and is a common 7-mark derivation in KTU exams.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing cost function and loss function:** The cost $J$ is the **average** loss over all training examples (factor of $1/m$). The loss $\mathcal{L}$ is for a **single** example. Forgetting the $1/m$ factor loses **1–2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Sign error in gradient:** Students often write $\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum(y - \hat{y})x$ instead of $(\hat{y} - y)$. This affects the **direction** of the weight update. The correct gradient descent update is $w_j := w_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the bias term:** When computing $z = \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b$, missing $b$ is a critical error.
>
> 4. **Using MSE in the derivation:** Examiners explicitly ask "Why not MSE?" Skipping this justification loses marks.
>
> 5. **Not justifying convexity:** The log loss is convex because the negative log-likelihood of a Bernoulli with sigmoid link is convex. Simply stating "it is convex" without justification may cost **1 mark**.
>
> 6. **Confusing multinomial and one-vs-all:** In multiclass problems, distinguish **multinomial logistic regression (softmax)** from **one-vs-all** strategy. They are NOT identical.
>
> 7. **Numerical stability in softmax:** Always subtract $\max(z)$ from exponents — forgetting this in implementation loses practical marks in coding questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Logistic regression is a **classification** algorithm (despite the name).
- ✅ The **sigmoid** function $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ squashes real values to $(0,1)$.
- ✅ The **sigmoid derivative** identity: $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1-\sigma(z))$ — **MEMORIZE**.
- ✅ The **logit** $\ln(p/(1-p))$ is the inverse of the sigmoid and is linear in features.
- ✅ The **Log Loss (Binary Cross-Entropy)** is convex and is the negative log-likelihood under Bernoulli.
- ✅ The gradient $\frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m}\sum_i(\hat{y}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$ is the same form as linear regression but with sigmoid $\hat{y}$.
- ✅ The **decision boundary** is $\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x} + b = 0$ (a hyperplane).
- ✅ **Multinomial logistic regression** uses the **softmax** activation and **categorical cross-entropy** loss.
- ✅ **Regularization** (L1/L2/Elastic Net) prevents overfitting; ridge ($\ell_2$) is most common.
- ✅ Logistic regression is a **Generalized Linear Model (GLM)** with **canonical link** being the logit, derived from the **Bernoulli distribution** which is a member of the **exponential family**.
- ✅ Numerical stability: subtract $\max(z)$ in softmax; clip predictions in log to avoid $\log(0)$.
- ✅ Performance metrics: **accuracy**, **precision**, **recall**, **F1-score**, **ROC-AUC**, **confusion matrix**.
- ✅ Assumptions: linearity in log-odds, independence of observations, no multicollinearity, large sample size.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formulation of the Optimization Problem and Solutions, Multiclass Case
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Formulation of the Optimization Problem and Solutions, Multiclass Case

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is the "Optimization Problem" in SVM?

In the context of Support Vector Machines (SVM), the **optimization problem** is the mathematical heart of the algorithm. It is a **convex quadratic programming (QP)** problem whose goal is to find the **maximum-margin hyperplane** that separates data points belonging to two or more classes with the largest possible decision boundary (margin) while allowing for a controlled amount of misclassification through slack variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> The SVM optimization problem is a **constrained convex quadratic minimization** of the norm of the weight vector subject to classification margin constraints. For the **multiclass case**, it is generalized into multiple binary sub-problems (decomposition strategies) or solved as a single joint QP problem that simultaneously considers all class labels.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Widest Road in a Crowded Market"

Imagine you are a city planner asked to build a **wide road** through a crowded marketplace that has two groups of stalls: **Vegetable vendors (Class +1)** on the left and **Fruit vendors (Class −1)** on the right.

- You want the **widest possible road** so that the marketplace remains spacious and safe.
- But the road cannot be infinitely wide because the stalls are densely packed.
- Some stalls may even slightly **encroach** into the road — these are the **misclassified points (slack variables ξᵢ)**.
- The **width of the road** is mathematically `2 / ‖w‖`, where `w` is the "road-direction" vector.
- To make the road wider, you must **minimize ‖w‖**, which is exactly what the optimization problem does.

For a **multiclass case** (say 3 groups: Veg, Fruit, Flower), you have several options:
1. Build **three separate roads**, each separating one group from the rest (One-vs-All).
2. Build **three roads pairwise** — Veg-vs-Fruit, Veg-vs-Flower, Fruit-vs-Flower (One-vs-One).
3. Build a **single unified road network** that separates all three groups simultaneously (Crammer-Singer).

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Constants & Symbols)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **w** | Weight vector (normal to the separating hyperplane) |
| **b** | Bias term (offset of hyperplane from origin) |
| **C** | Regularization parameter (penalty for misclassification) |
| **ξᵢ** | Slack variable for i-th training point |
| **αᵢ** | Lagrange multiplier for the i-th constraint |
| **K** | Number of classes (multiclass case) |
| **M** | Total number of training samples |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The multiclass extension is **not** a single closed-form formula. It involves **decomposition strategies** that reduce a K-class problem into K(K−1)/2 or K binary sub-problems, each solved using the standard binary SVM QP solver.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Binary SVM Optimization Problem (Foundation)

For linearly separable data with training set `{(xᵢ, yᵢ)}` where `yᵢ ∈ {+1, −1}`, the **primal optimization problem** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{w, b} \quad & \frac{1}{2} \lVert w \rVert^2 \\
\text{subject to} \quad & y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, M
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **hard-margin SVM**. For non-separable data, slack variables `ξᵢ ≥ 0` are introduced (**soft-margin SVM**):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{w, b, \xi} \quad & \frac{1}{2} \lVert w \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i \\
\text{subject to} \quad & y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i \\
& \xi_i \geq 0, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, M
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.2 The Lagrangian Dual Form

By introducing Lagrange multipliers `αᵢ ≥ 0` (for margin constraints) and `μᵢ ≥ 0` (for slack constraints), the **Lagrangian** becomes:

$$
L(w, b, \xi, \alpha, \mu) = \frac{1}{2}\lVert w\rVert^2 + C\sum_{i=1}^{M}\xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{M}\alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_{i=1}^{M}\mu_i \xi_i
$$

Setting partial derivatives to zero gives the **KKT conditions**:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; w = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \alpha_i y_i x_i
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_{i=1}^{M} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \xi_i} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0
$$

Substituting back yields the **dual problem**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\alpha} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{M} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^T x_j) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{M} \alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.3 Extension to Multiclass Case

The binary SVM must be extended when **K > 2 classes** exist. Three principal strategies exist:

### Strategy A: One-vs-All (OvA) / One-vs-Rest (OvR)

Train **K separate binary SVMs**. The k-th SVM separates class k from all other classes.

$$
f_k(x) = w_k^T x + b_k, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, K
$$

**Decision rule:**

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k \in \{1, \dots, K\}} f_k(x)
$$

**Drawback:** Class imbalance (one class has M/M data; the "rest" has (K−1)M/M data).

### Strategy B: One-vs-One (OvO)

Train **K(K−1)/2** binary SVMs — one for every pair of classes.

$$
f_{ij}(x) = w_{ij}^T x + b_{ij}, \quad \forall \, 1 \leq i < j \leq K
$$

**Decision rule (Voting):** The class with the maximum number of votes wins. Ties are broken by weighted voting using confidence scores.

### Strategy C: Crammer-Singer Single-Machine Formulation (Joint QP)

This is a **single optimization problem** that handles all K classes simultaneously. Let `W = [w₁, w₂, …, w_K]` be the matrix of K weight vectors.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{W, \xi} \quad & \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \lVert w_k \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i \\
\text{subject to} \quad & w_{y_i}^T x_i - w_k^T x_i \geq \Delta_{y_i, k} - \xi_i, \quad \forall i, \, \forall k \neq y_i
\end{aligned}
$$

where `Δ_{y_i, k} = 1` if `k ≠ y_i` and `0` otherwise (the **margin rescaling function**).

> [!TIP]
> **Why Crammer-Singer matters:** It avoids the heuristic voting of OvO/OvA. However, it solves a **larger QP** (K classes × M samples), making it computationally expensive for large K. In practice, OvO is preferred for moderate K (K ≤ 10), while OvA is preferred for large K with many classes.

### 2.4 Weston-Watkins Multiclass Formulation (Alternative Joint)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{W, \xi} \quad & \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \lVert w_k \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{k \neq y_i} \xi_i^k \\
\text{subject to} \quad & w_{y_i}^T x_i - w_k^T x_i \geq 1 - \xi_i^k, \quad \xi_i^k \geq 0, \, \forall i, \, \forall k \neq y_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, each sample has **K−1 slack variables** (one per non-true class), giving a much richer optimization landscape.

## 2.5 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `min (1/2)‖w‖² + C Σ ξᵢ` | Soft-margin binary SVM primal | Binary classification |
| 2 | `w = Σ αᵢ yᵢ xᵢ` | Weight vector reconstruction from support vectors | Dual → Primal conversion |
| 3 | `Σ αᵢ yᵢ = 0` | Equality constraint of dual | Dual feasibility |
| 4 | `0 ≤ αᵢ ≤ C` | Box constraint for soft-margin | Dual feasible region |
| 5 | `αᵢ [yᵢ(wᵀxᵢ+b) − 1 + ξᵢ] = 0` | **KKT complementarity** | Identifies support vectors |
| 6 | `ŷ = argmax_k (w_kᵀx + b_k)` | OvA decision rule | Multiclass inference |
| 7 | `K(K−1)/2` classifiers | OvO classifier count | Multiclass decomposition |
| 8 | `w_{yᵢ}ᵀxᵢ − w_kᵀxᵢ ≥ 1 − ξᵢ` | Crammer-Singer margin | Joint multiclass QP |
| 9 | `αᵢ (C − αᵢ) = 0` | Active set condition for support vectors | Determines SVs in soft margin |
| 10 | `Margin = 2 / ‖w‖` | Geometric margin width | Theoretical analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** In KTU exams, students often confuse `αᵢ` (dual variable) with `ξᵢ` (slack variable). Remember: `αᵢ` is the **Lagrange multiplier**, while `ξᵢ` is the **slack variable** introduced to relax the margin constraint. They play very different roles in the optimization.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Multiclass SVM (OvO) classifies tumor types: Benign, Malignant-Type-1, Malignant-Type-2.
- **Optical Character Recognition (OCR):** Crammer-Singer SVM distinguishes handwritten digits 0–9 (K = 10).
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein fold classification (K = 27 SCOP classes) using Weston-Watkins.
- **Remote Sensing:** Land-cover classification from satellite imagery (Water, Forest, Urban, Bare Soil).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: From Binary Primal to Dual (Full KKT Walkthrough)

### Step 1 — Start with the Soft-Margin Primal Problem

The binary soft-margin SVM optimization problem is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{w, b, \xi} \quad & \frac{1}{2} \lVert w \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i \\
\text{subject to} \quad & y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad i = 1, \dots, M \\
& \xi_i \geq 0, \quad i = 1, \dots, M
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** We are minimizing a convex function (sum of squared norm of weights and a linear penalty on slacks), subject to **two sets of inequality constraints** — the margin constraint and the non-negativity of slacks.

### Step 2 — Form the Lagrangian

Introduce `αᵢ ≥ 0` for each margin constraint and `μᵢ ≥ 0` for each slack non-negativity constraint:

$$
L = \frac{1}{2} \lVert w \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{M} \alpha_i \left[ y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_{i=1}^{M} \mu_i \xi_i
$$

**Textual Logic:** The Lagrangian combines the objective with a **weighted penalty** for each violated constraint. The signs are negative because we want to penalize violation.

### Step 3 — Compute Partial Derivatives (Stationarity)

For the dual function `θ(α, μ) = min_{w, b, ξ} L`, we set each partial to zero:

**Derivative w.r.t. w:**

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = w - \sum_{i=1}^{M} \alpha_i y_i x_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad w = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \alpha_i y_i x_i
$$

**Textual Logic:** The optimal weight vector is a **linear combination of training samples**, weighted by their labels and dual coefficients. Samples with `αᵢ = 0` do not contribute — these are non-support vectors.

**Derivative w.r.t. b:**

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = -\sum_{i=1}^{M} \alpha_i y_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{M} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

**Textual Logic:** The dual variables must be **balanced** — the sum of positive-class weights must equal the sum of negative-class weights. This is the equality constraint of the dual.

**Derivative w.r.t. ξᵢ:**

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \xi_i} = C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu_i = C - \alpha_i
$$

**Textual Logic:** Since `μᵢ ≥ 0`, we get `αᵢ ≤ C`. Combined with `αᵢ ≥ 0`, this is the **box constraint** `0 ≤ αᵢ ≤ C`.

### Step 4 — Substitute Back into L

Substitute `w = Σ αᵢ yᵢ xᵢ` and `μᵢ = C − αᵢ` into the Lagrangian:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{1}{2} \left( \sum_{i} \alpha_i y_i x_i \right)^T \left( \sum_{j} \alpha_j y_j x_j \right) + C \sum_{i} \xi_i \\
&\quad - \sum_{i} \alpha_i \left[ y_i \left( \sum_{j} \alpha_j y_j x_j \right)^T x_i + b y_i - 1 + \xi_i \right] - \sum_{i} (C - \alpha_i) \xi_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Simplify term-by-term:**

The `Σ ξᵢ` terms: `C Σ ξᵢ − Σ αᵢ ξᵢ − (C − αᵢ) Σ ξᵢ = 0` (they cancel exactly — this is the magic of the dual substitution).

The `b` term: `− Σ αᵢ yᵢ b = −b (Σ αᵢ yᵢ) = 0` (by the equality constraint).

This leaves:

$$
L = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^T x_j) - \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^T x_j) + \sum_{i} \alpha_i
$$

$$
L = \sum_{i} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j (x_i^T x_j)
$$

### Step 5 — Write the Dual Problem

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\alpha} \quad & W(\alpha) = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \sum_{j=1}^{M} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, (x_i^T x_j) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{M} \alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The dual is also a **convex QP** but in variables `αᵢ` rather than `w`. The dot products `xᵢᵀxⱼ` can be replaced by kernels `K(xᵢ, xⱼ)` for non-linear classification — this is the **kernel trick**.

### Step 6 — Recover w and b from the Dual

Once `α*` is found via QP solver (e.g., SMO — Sequential Minimal Optimization):

$$
w^* = \sum_{i=1}^{M} \alpha_i^* y_i x_i
$$

$$
b^* = y_i - w^{*T} x_i, \quad \text{for any } i \text{ with } 0 < \alpha_i^* < C
$$

**Textual Logic:** `b` is recovered from any **margin support vector** (one that lies exactly on the margin, not a non-margin SV).

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Crammer-Singer Multiclass Formulation

### Step 1 — Define the Joint Margin

For K classes, define a single weight matrix `W = [w₁, w₂, …, w_K] ∈ ℝ^{d×K}`. For sample `i` with true label `yᵢ`, the constraint is that the score for the true class must exceed the score for every other class by at least 1 (margin rescaling):

$$
w_{y_i}^T x_i - w_k^T x_i \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \forall k \neq y_i
$$

**Textual Logic:** We have **K − 1 constraints per sample** instead of 1 (binary case). Total constraints = M × (K − 1).

### Step 2 — Write the Primal

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{W, \xi} \quad & \frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \lVert w_k \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{M} \xi_i \\
\text{subject to} \quad & w_{y_i}^T x_i - w_k^T x_i \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \forall i, \, \forall k \neq y_i
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Build the Dual

Introduce Lagrange multipliers `α_{i,k} ≥ 0` for each margin constraint. The dual becomes a maximization of:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\alpha} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{M} \alpha_{i, y_i} - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j=1}^{M} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \alpha_{i,k} \alpha_{j,k} \, (x_i^T x_j) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & \alpha_{i,k} \leq C, \quad \alpha_{i,k} \geq 0, \quad \alpha_{i,y_i} = \sum_{k \neq y_i} \alpha_{i,k}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual Logic:** The dual has **M × K variables** (vs. M in binary) and a coupling constraint per sample linking the `K − 1` non-true-class coefficients to the true-class coefficient.

### Step 4 — Decision Rule

$$
\hat{y} = \arg\max_{k \in \{1, \dots, K\}} w_k^T x
$$

where `w_k = Σᵢ αᵢ,k xᵢ`.

## 3.3 Computational Complexity Comparison

| Strategy | # of QPs | Variables per QP | Constraints per QP | Training Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| OvA | K | M | M | O(K · M² to M³) |
| OvO | K(K−1)/2 | Mᵢⱼ (subset) | Mᵢⱼ | O(K² · M²) |
| Crammer-Singer | 1 | M·K | M·(K−1) | O((MK)² to (MK)³) |
| Weston-Watkins | 1 | M·(K−1) | M·(K−1) | O((MK)² to (MK)³) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** For K = 1000 classes (e.g., ImageNet), OvA with K QPs is the only feasible option, even though it suffers from class imbalance. Crammer-Singer becomes intractable because (MK)³ grows cubically.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Binary SVM Optimization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Data x_i, y_i] --> B[Formulate Primal QP Problem]
    B --> C{Primal Variables: w, b, xi}
    C --> D[Construct Lagrangian L w, b, xi, alpha, mu]
    D --> E[Apply KKT Stationarity Conditions]
    E --> F[Derive Dual QP Problem]
    F --> G[Dual Variables: alpha_i only]
    G --> H[Solve via SMO / QP Solver]
    H --> I{Solution Status}
    I -->|alpha_i = 0| J[Non Support Vector Discarded]
    I -->|0 less alpha_i less C| K[Margin Support Vector b recovery point]
    I -->|alpha_i = C| L[Inside Margin Point Bounded SV]
    K --> M[Reconstruct w star from alpha star]
    M --> N[Final Classifier sign w Tx plus b]
    J --> N
    L --> N
```

## 4.2 Multiclass Decomposition Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Multiclass Problem K classes] --> Q{Choose Strategy}
    Q -->|Strategy 1| OvA[One vs All OvA]
    Q -->|Strategy 2| OvO[One vs One OvO]
    Q -->|Strategy 3| CS[Crammer Singer Joint QP]

    OvA --> OvAStep1[Train K Binary SVMs]
    OvAStep1 --> OvAStep2[Class k vs Rest for k 1 to K]
    OvAStep2 --> OvAStep3[Decision argmax over K scores]
    OvAStep3 --> OvAResult[Final Class Prediction]

    OvO --> OvOStep1[Train K K minus 1 over 2 Binary SVMs]
    OvOStep1 --> OvOStep2[Pairwise class separation]
    OvOStep2 --> OvOStep3[Majority Voting]
    OvOStep3 --> OvOResult[Final Class Prediction]

    CS --> CSStep1[Formulate Joint QP over W matrix]
    CSStep1 --> CSStep2[Solve Single Large QP]
    CSStep2 --> CSStep3[Decision argmax over K weight vectors]
    CSStep3 --> CSResult[Final Class Prediction]
```

## 4.3 KKT Complementary Slackness Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sample i] --> B{alpha_i value}
    B -->|equals 0| C[Non Support Vector y_i f x_i greater than 1]
    B -->|between 0 and C| D[Margin Support Vector y_i f x_i equals 1]
    B -->|equals C| E[Inside Margin y_i f x_i less than 1]
    C --> F[Far from hyperplane]
    D --> G[On the margin boundary]
    E --> H[Misclassified or close to boundary]
```

## 4.4 OvA vs OvO Visual Comparison

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph OvA[One vs All K equal 3]
        A1[SVM 1 Class A vs B plus C]
        A2[SVM 2 Class B vs A plus C]
        A3[SVM 3 Class C vs A plus B]
    end

    subgraph OvO[One vs One K equal 3]
        O1[SVM 12 Class A vs Class B]
        O2[SVM 13 Class A vs Class C]
        O3[SVM 23 Class B vs Class C]
    end

    OvA --> VoteA[Argmax Voting]
    OvO --> VoteO[Majority Voting]
```
