Variance Analysis (2 Marks): As $K$ decreases, the training sets across folds become more similar, and the validation sets overlap more, causing the resulting error estimates to be more correlated. This inflates variance. **Lower $K$ ⇒ higher variance**.

Special Cases (2 Marks):
- $K=2$: High bias and high variance. Useful only for huge datasets.
- $K=5$ or $K=10$: Best trade-off; recommended default per KTU conventions.
- $K=N$ (LOOCV): Lowest bias but highest variance; expensive for large $N$.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

Step 1: Compute fold sizes (1 Mark):
- Total samples: $N = 500$
- Number of folds: $K = 10$
- Validation set size per fold: $N / K = 500 / 10 = 50$ samples.
- Training set size per fold: $N - 50 = 450$ samples.

Step 2: Sum the accuracies (2 Marks):

$$\sum_{k=1}^{10} a_k = 0.82 + 0.85 + 0.80 + 0.88 + 0.83 + 0.86 + 0.84 + 0.81 + 0.87 + 0.84 = 8.40$$

Step 3: Compute mean (1 Mark):

$$\bar{a} = \frac{8.40}{10} = 0.84$$

Step 4: Compute squared deviations and variance (2 Marks):

| $a_k$ | $a_k - \bar{a}$ | $(a_k - \bar{a})^2$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.82 | -0.02 | 0.0004 |
| 0.85 | +0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 0.80 | -0.04 | 0.0016 |
| 0.88 | +0.04 | 0.0016 |
| 0.83 | -0.01 | 0.0001 |
| 0.86 | +0.02 | 0.0004 |
| 0.84 | 0.00 | 0.0000 |
| 0.81 | -0.03 | 0.0009 |
| 0.87 | +0.03 | 0.0009 |
| 0.84 | 0.00 | 0.0000 |

$$\text{Sum} = 0.0060, \quad \text{Variance} = \frac{0.0060}{10} = 0.0006, \quad \text{Std. Dev.} = \sqrt{0.0006} \approx 0.0245$$

Step 5: Final answer (1 Mark):

$$\boxed{\text{CV Accuracy} = 0.84 \; (84\%), \quad \sigma \approx 0.0245}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse $\mathbf{S}_B$ definitions:** Some students write $\mathbf{S}_B = (\mathbf{m}_2 - \mathbf{m}_1)$ as a *vector*; the correct form is the *outer product* $(\mathbf{m}_2 - \mathbf{m}_1)(\mathbf{m}_2 - \mathbf{m}_1)^T$, which is a *matrix*. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Do NOT forget to compute $\mathbf{S}_W^{-1}$:** For numerical problems, omitting the matrix inversion or getting the determinant wrong costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **LOOCV vs. K-Fold confusion:** State clearly that LOOCV is a *special case* of K-Fold with $K=N$, *not* a separate technique.
> 4. **K-Fold variance calculation:** Use the *population* formula $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{K} \sum (a_k - \bar{a})^2$ for CV reporting. Some examiners accept sample std-dev $\sqrt{\frac{1}{K-1} \sum \cdots}$ — write both forms if unsure.
> 5. **Threshold computation:** The threshold $y_0$ for Fisher's classification is often incorrectly placed at the midpoint of the projected *class means* without normalizing for class prior probabilities. Use the correct formula as given.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

**Fisher's LDA — Core Points:**
- LDA is **supervised**, unlike PCA (which is unsupervised).
- Objective: Maximize $\dfrac{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_B \mathbf{w}}{\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{S}_W \mathbf{w}}$ — between-class variance over within-class variance.
- Optimal projection for **2 classes**: $\mathbf{w}^{*} = \mathbf{S}_W^{-1} (\mathbf{m}_2 - \mathbf{m}_1)$.
- For **$c$ classes**, at most $c-1$ discriminant directions exist.
- Assumes **Gaussian-distributed classes** with **equal covariance matrices** for optimality; if violated, LDA is still the *best linear* projection but not Bayes-optimal.
- Projection $y = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}$ reduces $d$-dimensional data to 1-D (binary case) or $(c-1)$-D (multi-class case).
- Decision rule: threshold $y_0 = \tfrac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}^T (\mathbf{m}_1 + \mathbf{m}_2)$ for equal priors.

**Model Selection — Core Points:**
- Goal: pick the model with the best *generalization* performance, not the best *training* performance.
- Tools: cross-validation, AIC, BIC, adjusted $R^2$.
- Trade-off: simple models underfit; complex models overfit.

**Cross-Validation — Core Points:**
- **Hold-out**: One split, fast, high variance.
- **K-Fold**: $K$ splits, balanced trade-off; default $K = 5$ or $10$.
- **LOOCV**: $K = N$, low bias, high variance, expensive.
- **Stratified K-Fold**: Preserves class ratios per fold; mandatory for imbalanced data.
- **Time-Series CV**: Uses forward chaining, not random shuffling, to respect temporal order.

**Numerical-Solving Reminders for KTU:**
- Always show **matrix inversions explicitly** with determinant and cofactor.
- In $J(\mathbf{w})$, state whether you are computing the **eigenvector** form or the **closed-form** form.
- For $K$-Fold, the **per-fold accuracy** values must be reported individually before averaging.
- Use $\sigma^2 = \tfrac{1}{K} \sum (a_k - \bar{a})^2$ for population variance unless the problem explicitly says "sample standard deviation."

**Common KTU-Asked Mnemonics:**
- **S_W = Within** → think "**W**ithin the class walls"
- **S_B = Between** → think "**B**etween the walls, far apart"
- **J(w) ↑** → means **Good separation** (large numerator, small denominator)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Leave One Out
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Leave One Out

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV)** is a special deterministic instance of the *K-Fold Cross-Validation* procedure in which the number of folds is set equal to the total number of training instances, i.e., $K = N$, where $N$ is the cardinality of the dataset. In this scheme, the model is trained on $N-1$ observations and tested on the single, held-out observation. This single-observation hold-out is repeated $N$ times, ensuring that every data point serves exactly once as the validation sample.

Formally, given a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$, the LOOCV estimate of the generalization (test) error is defined as:

$$E_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L\left(y_i, \hat{f}^{-i}(x_i)\right)$$

where $\hat{f}^{-i}$ denotes the model trained on the dataset with the $i^{th}$ observation removed, and $L(\cdot,\cdot)$ is the loss function (e.g., squared error for regression, 0-1 loss for classification).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** LOOCV is explicitly listed under Module 4 (Model Evaluation and Validation) of the PEAET495 syllabus. It is the most **bias-optimal** but the most **computationally expensive** cross-validation variant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a strict school examiner who has to grade a class of **100 students** on a difficult mathematics problem.

- **Hold-Out Validation** is like the examiner picking just 10 students randomly and judging the whole class based on those 10.
- **K-Fold Cross-Validation (e.g., k=5)** is like splitting the class into 5 groups of 20 students, testing on each group in turn.
- **Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV)** is the *obsessive perfectionist* examiner who tests **99 students**, then asks the **100th student** alone, then re-tests **99 different students** leaving the **99th student** out... and repeats this **100 times** so that *every single student* gets tested individually.

The examiner (the algorithm) thus "sees" almost the entire class every time, making the judgment of each student highly reliable — but the examiner must grade 100 separate times, which is exhausting (computationally costly).

## 1.3 Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Nomenclature used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **$N$** → Total number of samples in the dataset.
> - **$k$** → Number of folds in K-Fold CV (here $k=N$).
> - **Training Set** at iteration $i$ → $\mathcal{D} \setminus \{(x_i, y_i)\}$, with size $N-1$.
> - **Test Set** at iteration $i$ → $\{(x_i, y_i)\}$, with size $1$.
> - **Bias** → Error from erroneous assumptions in the learning algorithm.
> - **Variance** → Error from sensitivity to fluctuations in the training set.
> - **$E_{LOOCV}$** → The averaged LOOCV error (the final reported metric).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Algorithm (The "Why" and "How")

LOOCV operates on a deterministic, exhaustive partitioning logic. The procedure is as follows:

1. **Initialization:** Load the dataset $\mathcal{D}$ of size $N$. Initialize an empty list (or accumulator) for the per-iteration error scores.
2. **Iteration Loop (for $i = 1$ to $N$):**
   - **Partition:** Form the training subset $\mathcal{D}_{train}^{-i} = \mathcal{D} \setminus \{(x_i, y_i)\}$.
   - **Form the test subset:** $\mathcal{D}_{test}^{i} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}$.
   - **Model Training:** Fit the chosen model $\mathcal{M}$ on $\mathcal{D}_{train}^{-i}$, yielding $\hat{f}^{-i}$.
   - **Prediction:** Compute the prediction $\hat{y}_i = \hat{f}^{-i}(x_i)$ on the held-out sample.
   - **Error Computation:** Compute the instantaneous loss $L_i = L(y_i, \hat{y}_i)$.
3. **Aggregation:** Average the $N$ individual loss values to obtain the final LOOCV error:

$$E_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i$$

4. **Reporting:** $E_{LOOCV}$ is reported as the unbiased estimate of the true out-of-sample (generalization) error.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this works:** Because each training fold uses **99% of the data** (when $N=100$), the trained model is virtually identical in bias terms to a model trained on the full dataset, making the LOOCV error a near-unbiased estimate of the true test error.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Formula** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Meaning / Interpretation** | **Typical Value / Unit** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of Folds in LOOCV | $k = N$ | Folds equal total samples | Dimensionless |
| Training Set Size per fold | $N - 1$ | Samples used to fit the model | Samples |
| Test Set Size per fold | $1$ | Held-out observation | Samples |
| LOOCV Error (Regression) | $E_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y_i - \hat{y}_i^{-i})^2$ | Mean Squared Error across $N$ iterations | Squared units of $y$ |
| LOOCV Error (Classification) | $E_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}\{y_i \neq \hat{y}_i^{-i}\}$ | Misclassification Rate | Probability (0 to 1) |
| Bias Relationship | $Bias_{LOOCV} < Bias_{K\text{-}Fold}$ | LOOCV has lower bias than K-Fold ($k < N$) | Qualitative |
| Variance Relationship | $Var_{LOOCV} > Var_{K\text{-}Fold}$ | LOOCV has higher variance (overlapping training sets) | Qualitative |
| Computational Cost | $\mathcal{O}(N \cdot C_{train})$ | $N$ model fits required | Model fits |
| Stratified Variant | $k = N$ with class preservation | Used for imbalanced classification | Optional |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** In KTU examinations, when asked to "comment on the bias-variance trade-off of LOOCV," always mention: **Low Bias, High Variance.** This single sentence alone fetches 2 marks consistently.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnostics:** When the dataset of confirmed patient cases is small (e.g., rare diseases), LOOCV maximizes the use of every labeled case.
- **Bioinformatics & Genomics:** Gene expression datasets with $N < 100$ samples and thousands of features rely on LOOCV for unbiased error estimation.
- **Financial Risk Modeling:** Credit scoring models where each default event is valuable use LOOCV to avoid discarding any signal.
- **Hyperparameter Tuning:** Used as the inner-loop evaluator in `GridSearchCV` for small datasets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the LOOCV Error Estimator

Let the true underlying function generating the data be $f(x)$ with additive noise $\epsilon$, such that $y_i = f(x_i) + \epsilon_i$, where $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon_i] = 0$ and $\text{Var}(\epsilon_i) = \sigma^2$.

**Step 1:** Consider any deterministic loss function $L$. For a single iteration $i$, the prediction is $\hat{f}^{-i}(x_i)$. The instantaneous loss is:

$$L_i = L\left(y_i, \hat{f}^{-i}(x_i)\right)$$

**Step 2:** The LOOCV estimator averages these $N$ losses:

$$\hat{E}_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i$$

**Step 3:** Taking the expectation (to study its statistical properties), we analyze the bias relative to the true expected prediction error of a model trained on $N-1$ samples:

$$Err_\tau = \mathbb{E}_{(x_0, y_0)}\left[ L\left(y_0, \hat{f}^{-1}(x_0)\right) \right]$$

The LOOCV estimator is *almost* unbiased because each fold's training set is of size $N-1$, not $N$. The bias decays as $\mathcal{O}(1/N)$.

## 3.2 Closed-Form Shortcut for Linear Models (The "HAT Matrix" Trick)

For ordinary least squares (OLS) regression, LOOCV has a beautiful algebraic shortcut. Define the **hat matrix** (also called the influence / projection matrix):

$$H = X(X^T X)^{-1} X^T$$

The diagonal element $h_{ii}$ is the *leverage* of the $i^{th}$ observation, representing how much $x_i$ influences its own prediction. The fitted value for the $i^{th}$ observation is $\hat{y}_i = h_{ii} y_i + \sum_{j \neq i} h_{ij} y_j$.

**Derivation of the LOOCV shortcut:**

**Step 1:** The OLS coefficients trained without the $i^{th}$ observation are $\hat{\beta}^{(-i)}$. The corresponding fitted value is:

$$\hat{y}_i^{(-i)} = x_i^T \hat{\beta}^{(-i)}$$

**Step 2:** The well-known relationship between full-data fit and leave-one-out fit is:

$$\hat{y}_i^{(-i)} = \frac{\hat{y}_i - h_{ii} y_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$

**Step 3:** The LOOCV residual is then:

$$r_i^{LOO} = y_i - \hat{y}_i^{(-i)} = \frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{1 - h_{ii}} = \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the LOOCV Mean Squared Error for OLS can be computed in a **single full-dataset fit** (no iterative retraining needed):

$$E_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} \right)^2$$

This is the famous formula implemented in statistical packages (e.g., the `PRESS` statistic — **P**rediction **R**esidual **S**um of **S**quares).

> [!NOTE]
> **PRESS Statistic:** $PRESS = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} \right)^2$. This single equation encapsulates the entire LOOCV procedure for linear regression in $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ once (matrix inversion), instead of $\mathcal{O}(N^4)$ (N retraining iterations).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **Hold-Out (Single Split)** | **K-Fold ($k=5$ or $10$)** | **LOOCV ($k=N$)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bias of Test Error Estimate** | High (training set much smaller) | Moderate | **Very Low** |
| **Variance of Test Error Estimate** | High (depends on lucky/unlucky split) | Lower | **High** (overlapping folds) |
| **Computational Cost** | **Very Low** (1 model fit) | Moderate ($k$ fits) | **Very High** ($N$ fits) |
| **Data Efficiency** | Low (typically 70-80% used for training) | Good ($80-90\%$) | **Maximum** (99%+) |
| **Determinism** | Stochastic (depends on random split) | Stochastic | **Fully Deterministic** |
| **Suitability for Small Datasets** | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| **Suitability for Large Datasets** | Good | Good | **Poor (impractical)** |
| **Ease of Stratification** | Easy | Easy | Difficult (only 1 sample per fold) |

## 3.4 Why LOOCV has High Variance

Consider the OLS case. Each pair of LOOCV training sets $\mathcal{D}_{train}^{-i}$ and $\mathcal{D}_{train}^{-j}$ differ by only **2 observations**. Consequently, the trained models $\hat{f}^{-i}$ and $\hat{f}^{-j}$ are highly correlated:

$$\text{Corr}\left(\hat{f}^{-i}, \hat{f}^{-j}\right) \rightarrow 1 \text{ as } N \rightarrow \infty$$

Averaging many highly correlated quantities yields a sum whose variance does not diminish as fast as averaging independent quantities. This is the formal reason behind the "Low Bias, High Variance" property of LOOCV.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of LOOCV

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the leave-one-out procedure for a dataset of size $N=5$. Each row represents one iteration; the yellow cell is the held-out (test) sample, while the blue cells form the training set.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Dataset D of size N]) --> Init[Initialize empty error list L]
    Init --> LoopStart{i = 1, 2, ..., N}
    LoopStart --> Partition[Partition D into D_train minus i and D_test containing only sample i]
    Partition --> Train[Train model M on D_train minus i to get f-hat minus i]
    Train --> Predict[Predict y-hat sub i for the held out sample]
    Predict --> Loss[Compute L sub i = Loss y sub i, y-hat sub i]
    Loss --> Store[Append L sub i to error list]
    Store --> Cond{i less than N?}
    Cond -- Yes --> LoopStart
    Cond -- No --> Aggregate[Compute E sub LOOCV as mean of all L sub i]
    Aggregate --> Report([Report E sub LOOCV as test error estimate])
    Train -.LOOCV with N iterations.-> Partition
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: LOOCV Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Input Pipeline]
        RawData[Raw Dataset D] --> PreProc[Preprocessing and Normalization]
        PreProc --> SplitIndex[Generate N Hold Out Indices]
    end
    subgraph CoreEngine[LOOCV Engine]
        SplitIndex --> IterCtrl[Iteration Controller i equals 1 to N]
        IterCtrl --> TrainMod[Model Training Module]
        TrainMod --> HyperParams[Hyperparameter Vector theta]
        HyperParams --> TrainMod
        TrainMod --> PredMod[Prediction Module]
        PredMod --> LossMod[Loss Computation Module]
        LossMod --> AccBuf[Error Accumulator Buffer]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer[Output and Reporting]
        AccBuf --> AvgComp[Mean Error Aggregator]
        AvgComp --> StdEst[Standard Error Estimator]
        StdEst --> FinalReport[E sub LOOCV plus or minus SE Report]
    end
    IterCtrl -.feedback.-> TrainMod
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Variance Accumulation in LOOCV

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Fold1[Fold 1: Train on samples 2 to N, Test on 1] --> Pred1[Prediction f-hat minus 1]
    Fold2[Fold 2: Train on samples 1, 3 to N, Test on 2] --> Pred2[Prediction f-hat minus 2]
    Fold3[Fold 3: Train on samples 1, 2, 4 to N, Test on 3] --> Pred3[Prediction f-hat minus 3]
    Dot1[Dots] --> PredN[Prediction f-hat minus N]
    FoldN[Fold N: Train on samples 1 to N-1, Test on N] --> PredN
    Pred1 --> Avg[Average of N Predictions]
    Pred2 --> Avg
    Pred3 --> Avg
    PredN --> Avg
    Avg --> HighVar[Result: High Variance due to Correlated Training Sets]
    Pred1 -.highly correlated.-> Pred2
    Pred2 -.highly correlated.-> Pred3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV). Mention one advantage and one disadvantage.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation is a resampling technique in which the dataset of size $N$ is split into $N$ folds. In each iteration, the model is trained on $N-1$ samples and tested on the single held-out sample. The procedure is repeated $N$ times, and the final reported error is the average of all $N$ test errors.

**Advantage (1 Mark):** LOOCV has very low bias because each training set uses almost the entire dataset (99% of data when $N=100$). It is also fully deterministic — no random splitting is involved.

**Disadvantage (1 Mark):** It is computationally very expensive, as the model must be retrained $N$ times. It also has higher variance compared to $k$-fold CV with $k=5$ or $k=10$.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**State the formula for LOOCV error in a regression setting and explain the term $\hat{f}^{-i}$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Formula (2 Marks):**

$$E_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(y_i - \hat{f}^{-i}(x_i)\right)^2$$

**Explanation of $\hat{f}^{-i}$ (1 Mark):** The symbol $\hat{f}^{-i}$ denotes the regression function (model) that has been *fit* using all training data **except** the $i^{th}$ observation. The superscript "$-i$" indicates the removal of the $i^{th}$ sample from the training set.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the Leave-One-Out Cross-Validation (LOOCV) procedure. List its **two key advantages** and **two key disadvantages** when compared to the standard 10-fold cross-validation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a linear regression model, derive the **closed-form expression** (PRESS statistic) for computing the LOOCV error from a *single* full-dataset fit. Clearly state the role of the **hat matrix** $H$ and the **leverage** $h_{ii}$.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

LOOCV is a special case of K-Fold cross-validation where $k = N$. The dataset is partitioned $N$ times; in the $i^{th}$ iteration, sample $(x_i, y_i)$ is held out as the test set and the remaining $N-1$ samples form the training set. The model is fit, tested on the held-out sample, and the error is recorded. The LOOCV estimate is the mean of all $N$ recorded errors.

**Advantages over 10-Fold CV:**

1. **Lower Bias:** Since each fold trains on $N-1$ samples (≈99% of data), the model is virtually as complex as a model trained on the full data. Hence, the estimated test error is nearly unbiased, whereas 10-Fold CV trains on only 90% of the data, introducing slight bias.

2. **Determinism:** The partitioning is completely deterministic — no random seed is required. The same LOOCV result is obtained every time the procedure is run on the same dataset. 10-Fold CV depends on the random fold assignment.

**Disadvantages compared to 10-Fold CV:**

1. **High Computational Cost:** LOOCV requires $N$ model fits, while 10-Fold CV requires only 10. For large $N$ (e.g., $N=10^5$), LOOCV is infeasible.

2. **Higher Variance:** Because the training sets in LOOCV differ by only 2 samples, the resulting models are highly correlated, leading to high variance in the test error estimate. 10-Fold CV, with more distinct training partitions, has lower variance.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 (Define the Hat Matrix):** For OLS regression with design matrix $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times p}$, the hat matrix projects $y$ onto the column space of $X$:

$$H = X(X^T X)^{-1} X^T$$

**Step 2 (Fitted Values and Residuals):** The fitted values are $\hat{y} = H y$, and the residuals are $e = y - \hat{y} = (I - H) y$. The diagonal element $h_{ii}$ is the **leverage** of the $i^{th}$ observation.

**Step 3 (Leave-One-Out Prediction):** When the $i^{th}$ observation is removed, the new coefficients satisfy:

$$\hat{\beta}^{(-i)} = \hat{\beta} - \frac{(X^T X)^{-1} x_i e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$

The leave-one-out prediction is therefore:

$$\hat{y}_i^{(-i)} = x_i^T \hat{\beta}^{(-i)} = \frac{\hat{y}_i - h_{ii} y_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$

**Step 4 (LOOCV Residual):** The LOOCV residual (also called the *PRESS residual*) is:

$$r_i^{LOO} = y_i - \hat{y}_i^{(-i)} = \frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{1 - h_{ii}} = \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}}$$

**Step 5 (Final PRESS Statistic):** The LOOCV Mean Squared Error becomes:

$$E_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} \right)^2 = \frac{PRESS}{N}$$

Thus, only **one** full-dataset fit is required; LOOCV errors are computed analytically from the residuals and leverages.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **bias-variance trade-off** in cross-validation. Compare LOOCV with 5-Fold and 10-Fold cross-validation in terms of bias, variance, and computational cost. Use a suitable table.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a small dataset of $N=5$ samples. Demonstrate the **step-by-step execution** of LOOCV for a simple linear regression model, showing all five training sets, the corresponding fitted models, and the averaged LOOCV error.

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The bias-variance trade-off in cross-validation concerns the tension between the *accuracy* of the test error estimate (bias) and the *stability* of the estimate across different random partitions (variance).

- **Bias** is minimized when the training set in each fold is as large as possible (closer to the full dataset $N$). LOOCV, with $N-1$ training samples, has the *lowest* bias.
- **Variance** is minimized when the training sets across folds are sufficiently different. LOOCV folds are nearly identical (differ by 1 sample), so the resulting test errors are highly correlated, giving *high* variance. 5-Fold or 10-Fold have more diverse training sets, yielding *lower* variance.

| **Criterion** | **5-Fold CV** | **10-Fold CV** | **LOOCV ($k=N$)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Bias | Moderate | Slightly Lower | **Lowest** |
| Variance | Moderate | Lower than 5-Fold | **Highest** |
| Computational Cost | 5 fits | 10 fits | **N fits** |
| Determinism | Stochastic | Stochastic | **Deterministic** |
| Recommended Use | Large datasets | Industry default | Small datasets |

**Empirical Finding (from KTU-referenced text):** For most practical purposes, **10-Fold CV offers the best balance** between bias, variance, and computational cost. LOOCV is preferred only when $N$ is small or when the model has high variance / high smoothness.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

Let the dataset be $\mathcal{D} = \{(1,2), (2,3), (3,5), (4,4), (5,6)\}$, so $N=5$.

**Linear Regression Model:** $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$.

**Iteration 1 (Hold out sample 1: $(1,2)$):** Train on $(2,3), (3,5), (4,4), (5,6)$. Compute $\hat{\beta}_0^{-1}, \hat{\beta}_1^{-1}$. Predict $\hat{y}_1^{-1}$ for $x=1$. Record $e_1 = (2 - \hat{y}_1^{-1})^2$.

**Iteration 2 (Hold out sample 2: $(2,3)$):** Train on $(1,2), (3,5), (4,4), (5,6)$. Compute $\hat{\beta}_0^{-2}, \hat{\beta}_1^{-2}$. Predict $\hat{y}_2^{-2}$ for $x=2$. Record $e_2$.

**Iteration 3 (Hold out sample 3: $(3,5)$):** Train on $(1,2), (2,3), (4,4), (5,6)$. Record $e_3$.

**Iteration 4 (Hold out sample 4: $(4,4)$):** Train on $(1,2), (2,3), (3,5), (5,6)$. Record $e_4$.

**Iteration 5 (Hold out sample 5: $(5,6)$):** Train on $(1,2), (2,3), (3,5), (4,4)$. Record $e_5$.

**Final LOOCV Error:**

$$E_{LOOCV} = \frac{e_1 + e_2 + e_3 + e_4 + e_5}{5}$$

**Numerical Example (assume computed values):** If the five residuals are $e_1=0.25, e_2=0.16, e_3=0.09, e_4=0.04, e_5=0.01$, then:

$$E_{LOOCV} = \frac{0.25 + 0.16 + 0.09 + 0.04 + 0.01}{5} = \frac{0.55}{5} = 0.11$$

The reported test MSE for the LOOCV procedure is **0.11**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing $k$ and $N$:** Many students incorrectly write "$k=10$" when describing LOOCV. LOOCV is defined by $k=N$, not $k=10$.
> 2. **Forgetting the bias-variance nuance:** Stating that LOOCV is "best" without acknowledging the high variance loses 1 mark. Always present **both** sides of the trade-off.
> 3. **Sign error in PRESS statistic:** The denominator is $(1 - h_{ii})$, NOT $(h_{ii} - 1)$. The latter gives a negative squared value, which is mathematically invalid.
> 4. **Mixing up stratified LOOCV:** If the question mentions imbalanced classes, students often forget that plain LOOCV with $k=N$ may produce folds with no positive class. Always mention *stratified* LOOCV as a remedy.
> 5. **Computational cost underestimation:** Writing "LOOCV is slow" without quantifying the cost ($N$ fits vs. $k$ fits) loses the technical depth required for 14-mark answers.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LOOCV** is K-Fold Cross-Validation with $k = N$, where $N$ is the total number of samples.
- Each iteration uses **$N-1$ training samples** and **1 test sample**; the model is retrained **$N$ times**.
- The LOOCV error is the **mean of $N$ individual losses**: $E_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(y_i, \hat{f}^{-i}(x_i))$.
- **Key Property 1 — Low Bias:** Because each training fold is nearly the full dataset, the estimated test error is nearly unbiased.
- **Key Property 2 — High Variance:** Folds differ by only 2 samples, making trained models highly correlated and the error estimate unstable.
- **Key Property 3 — Deterministic:** No random splitting is involved; LOOCV always produces the same result on the same dataset.
- **Key Property 4 — Expensive:** Requires $N$ model fits; infeasible for large $N$ (e.g., $N > 10^4$).
- **PRESS Shortcut (OLS only):** $E_{LOOCV} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{e_i}{1 - h_{ii}} \right)^2$, where $h_{ii}$ is the leverage from the hat matrix $H = X(X^T X)^{-1} X^T$.
- **Practical Recommendation:** Use LOOCV for **small datasets** ($N < 100$); use **10-Fold CV** for medium-to-large datasets.
- **Variance Mitigation:** *Repeated K-Fold CV* or *5x2 CV* are often preferred over LOOCV when variance reduction is critical.
- **KTU Exam Buzzwords to Memorize:** "Low Bias, High Variance", "Deterministic Resampling", "PRESS Statistic", "Hat Matrix", "$k=N$", "N-1 training samples per fold".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bootstrapping, Model Evaluation - Classification - Confusion Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bootstrapping, Model Evaluation - Classification - Confusion Matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bootstrapping — The Statistical "Time Loop"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Bootstrapping* is a **non-parametric, resampling-based statistical inference technique** introduced by **Bradley Efron (1979)**. It involves drawing samples *with replacement* from an observed dataset of size $n$ to form $B$ independent "bootstrap samples" (each also of size $n$), thereby approximating the **sampling distribution** of a statistic without assuming any underlying parametric distribution of the population.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Survey Mirror"

Imagine you have a small jar containing **100 marbles** (your dataset), and you want to estimate the *true average weight* of *all marbles in the factory* (the population). You cannot weigh every marble in the factory, but you can perform a clever trick:

1. Pick **one marble at random**, **note its weight**, and **put it back** into the jar.
2. Repeat step 1 exactly **100 times** to form *one "virtual" sample* of 100 marbles (some marbles will be picked multiple times, some not at all).
3. Calculate the average weight of these 100 picks — call it $\bar{x}^{*}_1$.
4. **Reset the jar** (since we always replace) and repeat the entire process $B$ times (say $B = 1000$).
5. Now you have **1000 averages** — and the *spread* of these averages tells you how confident you should be in your estimate.

> [!NOTE]
> The "jar reset" is the key insight. Because we sample **with replacement**, every bootstrap sample is independent and identically distributed, mimicking the act of going back to the population and drawing a fresh sample.

### Why Bootstrapping Matters in Machine Learning

- **Model Stability**: Quantifies how much your model's predictions vary if the training data were slightly different.
- **Confidence Intervals**: Provides empirical confidence intervals for *any* statistic (accuracy, AUC, regression coefficients) without distributional assumptions.
- **Bagging Foundation**: It is the mathematical backbone of ensemble methods like **Random Forests** and **Bagging classifiers**.

---

## 1.2 Confusion Matrix — The "Report Card" of Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A *Confusion Matrix* (also called an *Error Matrix* or *Contingency Table*) is an **$N \times N$ square matrix** that visualizes the performance of a classification algorithm by comparing **predicted labels** ($\hat{y}$) against **actual ground-truth labels** ($y$), where $N$ is the number of distinct classes. For binary classification ($N=2$), it is a $2 \times 2$ matrix with entries **True Positive (TP)**, **True Negative (TN)**, **False Positive (FP)**, and **False Negative (FN)**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Medical Test Verdict"

Picture a doctor running a diagnostic test for a disease on **1000 patients** (500 truly sick, 500 truly healthy). The test gives a "Yes/No" verdict for each patient. The doctor now compares verdicts with reality:

| Reality says SICK, Test says SICK | Reality says HEALTHY, Test says SICK |
|:---:|:---:|
| **True Positive (TP)** — Doctor got it right | **False Positive (FP)** — False alarm! |

| Reality says SICK, Test says HEALTHY | Reality says HEALTHY, Test says HEALTHY |
|:---:|:---:|
| **False Negative (FN)** — Missed it! | **True Negative (TN)** — Doctor got it right |

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix is called "confusion" because it shows *where* and *how* the model is getting "confused" between classes — distinguishing **Type I errors (FP)** from **Type II errors (FN)**.

### Why the Confusion Matrix is the Foundation of Model Evaluation

Unlike a single number (e.g., accuracy), the confusion matrix preserves the **distributional information** of errors, allowing domain-specific metric selection. In KTU examinations and in industry, the confusion matrix is the **first artifact** produced after training any classifier.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bootstrapping — Operational Algorithm

The bootstrapping procedure follows a precise sequence:

1. **Original Dataset**: Let $D = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ be the observed dataset of size $n$.
2. **Bootstrap Sample Generation**: For $b = 1, 2, \dots, B$:
   - Draw $n$ samples *with replacement* from $D$.
   - Denote the bootstrap sample as $D^{*(b)} = \{x_1^{*(b)}, x_2^{*(b)}, \dots, x_n^{*(b)}\}$.
3. **Statistic Computation**: Compute the statistic of interest on each sample: $\hat{\theta}^{*(b)} = s(D^{*(b)})$.
4. **Aggregation**: The bootstrap estimate of the statistic is the mean:
   $$\hat{\theta}_{\text{boot}} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{\theta}^{*(b)}$$
5. **Uncertainty Quantification**: The bootstrap estimate of the **standard error** is:
   $$\widehat{SE}_{\text{boot}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \left(\hat{\theta}^{*(b)} - \hat{\theta}_{\text{boot}}\right)^2}$$

### The "Why" and "How" — Deeper Insights

- **Why "with replacement"?** Without replacement, every bootstrap sample would be identical to $D$. Replacement ensures variability and independence between samples.
- **Probability Insight**: For a single observation $x_i$, the probability of being selected in a bootstrap sample of size $n$ is:
  $$P(x_i \in D^{*(b)}) = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^n \approx 1 - \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.632$$
  This means each bootstrap sample contains approximately **63.2% unique observations** from $D$ — a result known as the **0.632 rule** (used in 0.632 bootstrap cross-validation).

### Types of Bootstrap Variants

| Variant | Description | KTU Relevance |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Non-parametric (Standard) Bootstrap** | Resamples from empirical distribution | Most common in exams |
| **Parametric Bootstrap** | Fits a parametric model, then resamples from the fitted distribution | Advanced inference |
| **Block Bootstrap** | Preserves temporal/spatial dependence in time series | Used in sequential data |
| **0.632 Bootstrap** | Combines training resubstitution error with bootstrap error | For small-sample classification error estimation |

---

## 2.2 Confusion Matrix — Structural Anatomy

### Binary Classification ($2 \times 2$)

The matrix is laid out with **actual** labels on rows and **predicted** labels on columns (a convention, though KTU may use either axis):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Confusion Matrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 
\text{TP} & \text{FN} \\ 
\text{FP} & \text{TN} 
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In some KTU textbook conventions, rows represent **Predicted** and columns represent **Actual**. Always verify axis labels in the question.

### The Four Cardinal Outcomes

| Symbol | Name | Meaning |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $\text{TP}$ | True Positive | Model predicted Positive, and it IS Positive |
| $\text{TN}$ | True Negative | Model predicted Negative, and it IS Negative |
| $\text{FP}$ | False Positive (Type I Error) | Model predicted Positive, but it IS Negative |
| $\text{FN}$ | False Negative (Type II Error) | Model predicted Negative, but it IS Positive |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the Module 4 examination. All symbols use absolute value notation as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation | Range |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Accuracy** | $\text{Acc} = \frac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{TP} + \text{TN} + \text{FP} + \text{FN}}$ | Fraction of all correct predictions | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Error Rate (Misclassification Rate)** | $\text{Error} = 1 - \text{Acc} = \frac{\text{FP} + \text{FN}}{\text{Total}}$ | Fraction of all incorrect predictions | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Precision (Positive Predictive Value)** | $\text{Precision} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$ | Of all predicted positives, how many are correct? | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Recall (Sensitivity, True Positive Rate, TPR)** | $\text{Recall} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ | Of all actual positives, how many did we catch? | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Specificity (True Negative Rate, TNR)** | $\text{Specificity} = \frac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN} + \text{FP}}$ | Of all actual negatives, how many did we correctly reject? | $[0, 1]$ |
| **F1-Score (Harmonic Mean)** | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Balanced measure of Precision and Recall | $[0, 1]$ |
| **F-beta Score (Generalized)** | $F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\beta^2 \cdot \text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Weighted harmonic mean ($\beta > 1$ favors Recall) | $[0, 1]$ |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\text{FPR} = \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$ | Proportion of negatives incorrectly flagged | $[0, 1]$ |
| **False Negative Rate (FNR)** | $\text{FNR} = \frac{\text{FN}}{\text{FN} + \text{TP}} = 1 - \text{Recall}$ | Proportion of positives missed | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Prevalence** | $\text{Prevalence} = \frac{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}{\text{Total}}$ | Prior probability of the positive class | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)** | $\text{MCC} = \frac{\text{TP} \cdot \text{TN} - \text{FP} \cdot \text{FN}}{\sqrt{(\text{TP}+\text{FP})(\text{TP}+\text{FN})(\text{TN}+\text{FP})(\text{TN}+\text{FN})}}$ | Balanced metric for imbalanced classes | $[-1, +1]$ |
| **Cohen's Kappa** | $\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | Agreement adjusted for chance | $[-1, +1]$ |
| **Bootstrap Mean Estimate** | $\hat{\theta}_{\text{boot}} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{\theta}^{*(b)}$ | Average statistic across $B$ bootstrap samples | — |
| **Bootstrap Standard Error** | $\widehat{SE}_{\text{boot}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \left(\hat{\theta}^{*(b)} - \hat{\theta}_{\text{boot}}\right)^2}$ | Estimated variability of the statistic | — |
| **0.632 Bootstrap Error** | $\text{err}_{0.632} = 0.632 \cdot \text{err}_{\text{boot}} + 0.368 \cdot \text{err}_{\text{train}}$ | Optimistic-bias corrected error estimate | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **medical diagnosis**, Recall is critical (we cannot miss sick patients). In **spam detection**, Precision is critical (we cannot block legitimate emails). The **F1-score** balances both and is the most common single metric in KTU practical examinations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Bootstrap Mean and Standard Error

### Problem Statement

Given the dataset $D = \{4, 7, 9, 12, 15\}$ ($n = 5$), perform bootstrapping with $B = 4$ to estimate the **mean** and its **standard error**. Use the following bootstrap samples drawn with replacement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D^{*(1)} &= \{4, 9, 9, 15, 7\} \\
D^{*(2)} &= \{12, 12, 4, 7, 9\} \\
D^{*(3)} &= \{7, 4, 15, 4, 12\} \\
D^{*(4)} &= \{9, 15, 7, 4, 7\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Original Sample Mean (Reference)

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{4 + 7 + 9 + 12 + 15}{5} = \frac{47}{5} = 9.4
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Bootstrap Sample Means

**For $D^{*(1)} = \{4, 9, 9, 15, 7\}$:**

$$
\bar{x}^{*(1)} = \frac{4 + 9 + 9 + 15 + 7}{5} = \frac{44}{5} = 8.8
$$

**For $D^{*(2)} = \{12, 12, 4, 7, 9\}$:**

$$
\bar{x}^{*(2)} = \frac{12 + 12 + 4 + 7 + 9}{5} = \frac{44}{5} = 8.8
$$

**For $D^{*(3)} = \{7, 4, 15, 4, 12\}$:**

$$
\bar{x}^{*(3)} = \frac{7 + 4 + 15 + 4 + 12}{5} = \frac{42}{5} = 8.4
$$

**For $D^{*(4)} = \{9, 15, 7, 4, 7\}$:**

$$
\bar{x}^{*(4)} = \frac{9 + 15 + 7 + 4 + 7}{5} = \frac{42}{5} = 8.4
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Bootstrap Mean Estimate

$$
\hat{\theta}_{\text{boot}} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \bar{x}^{*(b)} = \frac{8.8 + 8.8 + 8.4 + 8.4}{4} = \frac{34.4}{4} = 8.6
$$

**Interpretation:** The bootstrap point estimate of the population mean is **8.6**, close to the sample mean of **9.4**.

### Step 4 — Compute the Bootstrap Standard Error

First, compute squared deviations from the bootstrap mean:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(8.8 - 8.6)^2 &= (0.2)^2 = 0.04 \\
(8.8 - 8.6)^2 &= (0.2)^2 = 0.04 \\
(8.4 - 8.6)^2 &= (-0.2)^2 = 0.04 \\
(8.4 - 8.6)^2 &= (-0.2)^2 = 0.04
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of squared deviations:

$$
\sum_{b=1}^{B} \left(\bar{x}^{*(b)} - \hat{\theta}_{\text{boot}}\right)^2 = 0.04 + 0.04 + 0.04 + 0.04 = 0.16
$$

Now compute the standard error:

$$
\widehat{SE}_{\text{boot}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.16}{4 - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.16}{3}} = \sqrt{0.0533} \approx 0.231
$$

**Interpretation:** The estimated standard error of the mean is **0.231**. A 95% confidence interval would be approximately $\hat{\theta}_{\text{boot}} \pm 1.96 \cdot \widehat{SE}_{\text{boot}} = 8.6 \pm 0.453$, i.e., $[8.15,\ 9.05]$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Confusion Matrix — Full Metric Derivation

### Problem Statement

A binary classifier is tested on a dataset of **200 samples**. The results are:
- **TP** = 50
- **TN** = 120
- **FP** = 15
- **FN** = 15

Construct the confusion matrix and compute **all** standard evaluation metrics.

### Step 1 — Construct the Confusion Matrix

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Confusion Matrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 
\text{TP} = 50 & \text{FN} = 15 \\ 
\text{FP} = 15 & \text{TN} = 120 
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Verification: $50 + 15 + 15 + 120 = 200$ ✓

### Step 2 — Compute Accuracy

$$
\text{Acc} = \frac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{Total}} = \frac{50 + 120}{200} = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85
$$

**Result:** The model is **85% accurate**.

### Step 3 — Compute Error Rate

$$
\text{Error} = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15 \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Error} = \frac{\text{FP} + \text{FN}}{\text{Total}} = \frac{15 + 15}{200} = 0.15
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Precision

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} = \frac{50}{50 + 15} = \frac{50}{65} \approx 0.7692
$$

**Result:** When the model predicts "Positive", it is correct **~76.92%** of the time.

### Step 5 — Compute Recall (Sensitivity)

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{50}{50 + 15} = \frac{50}{65} \approx 0.7692
$$

**Result:** The model catches **~76.92%** of all actual positives.

### Step 6 — Compute Specificity

$$
\text{Specificity} = \frac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN} + \text{FP}} = \frac{120}{120 + 15} = \frac{120}{135} \approx 0.8889
$$

**Result:** The model correctly identifies **~88.89%** of all actual negatives.

### Step 7 — Compute F1-Score

First, the numerator:

$$
2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall} = 2 \cdot \frac{50}{65} \cdot \frac{50}{65} = 2 \cdot \frac{2500}{4225} = \frac{5000}{4225} \approx 1.1834
$$

Then the denominator:

$$
\text{Precision} + \text{Recall} = \frac{50}{65} + \frac{50}{65} = \frac{100}{65} \approx 1.5385
$$

Final F1:

$$
F_1 = \frac{1.1834}{1.5385} \approx 0.7692
$$

**Result:** $F_1 \approx 0.7692$ (or **76.92%**).

> [!NOTE]
> Precision equals Recall in this example because $\text{FP} = \text{FN} = 15$, so the class-wise error distribution is symmetric. This is a special case — in imbalanced scenarios they will differ.

### Step 8 — Compute False Positive Rate and False Negative Rate

$$
\text{FPR} = \frac{\text{FP}}{\text{FP} + \text{TN}} = \frac{15}{15 + 120} = \frac{15}{135} \approx 0.1111
$$

$$
\text{FNR} = \frac{\text{FN}}{\text{FN} + \text{TP}} = \frac{15}{15 + 50} = \frac{15}{65} \approx 0.2308
$$

### Step 9 — Compute Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)

Numerator:

$$
\text{TP} \cdot \text{TN} - \text{FP} \cdot \text{FN} = (50)(120) - (15)(15) = 6000 - 225 = 5775
$$

Denominator components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\text{TP} + \text{FP}) &= 50 + 15 = 65 \\
(\text{TP} + \text{FN}) &= 50 + 15 = 65 \\
(\text{TN} + \text{FP}) &= 120 + 15 = 135 \\
(\text{TN} + \text{FN}) &= 120 + 15 = 135
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = \sqrt{65 \times 65 \times 135 \times 135} = 65 \times 135 = 8775
$$

Therefore:

$$
\text{MCC} = \frac{5775}{8775} \approx 0.658
$$

**Interpretation:** $\text{MCC} \approx 0.658$ indicates a **moderately strong** positive correlation between predictions and reality.

### Step 10 — Compute Cohen's Kappa

Observed agreement:

$$
p_o = \text{Accuracy} = 0.85
$$

Expected agreement (by chance):

$$
p_e = \frac{(\text{TP} + \text{FP})(\text{TP} + \text{FN}) + (\text{TN} + \text{FP})(\text{TN} + \text{FN})}{\text{Total}^2}
$$

$$
p_e = \frac{(65)(65) + (135)(135)}{200^2} = \frac{4225 + 18225}{40000} = \frac{22450}{40000} = 0.56125
$$

$$
\kappa = \frac{0.85 - 0.56125}{1 - 0.56125} = \frac{0.28875}{0.43875} \approx 0.658
$$

> [!NOTE]
> MCC and Kappa yield the **same value** in $2 \times 2$ binary cases — this is a known mathematical identity.

### Summary Table of All Computed Metrics

| Metric | Value |
|:---|:---:|
| Accuracy | $0.8500$ |
| Error Rate | $0.1500$ |
| Precision | $0.7692$ |
| Recall (Sensitivity) | $0.7692$ |
| Specificity | $0.8889$ |
| F1-Score | $0.7692$ |
| FPR | $0.1111$ |
| FNR | $0.2308$ |
| MCC | $0.6580$ |
| Cohen's Kappa | $0.6580$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bootstrap Resampling Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete bootstrapping workflow, from the original dataset through resampling, statistic computation, and aggregation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Original Dataset D of size n"] --> B["Initialize Counter b = 1"]
    B --> C{"b less than or equal to B?"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Draw n samples with replacement from D"]
    D --> E["Form Bootstrap Sample D superscript asterisk b"]
    E --> F["Compute Statistic theta superscript asterisk b on D superscript asterisk b"]
    F --> G["Store theta superscript asterisk b in list"]
    G --> H["Increment Counter b = b + 1"]
    H --> C
    C -- No --> I["Compute Bootstrap Mean theta sub boot = mean of all theta superscript asterisk b"]
    I --> J["Compute Bootstrap SE as square root of variance"]
    J --> K["Construct Confidence Interval theta sub boot plus or minus 1.96 times SE sub boot"]
    K --> L["Report Final Estimate and Uncertainty"]

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79
    style D fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#B45309
    style F fill:#E6F7EC,stroke:#2D6A4F
    style I fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#5B21B6
    style L fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D
```

### Interpretation of the Flow

- The **blue** node represents the **input** (original dataset).
- The **orange** node represents the **resampling action** (the heart of bootstrapping).
- The **green** node represents the **statistic computation** on each resample.
- The **purple** nodes represent the **aggregation** phase.
- The **pink** node represents the **output** (final inference).

---

## 4.2 Confusion Matrix Structure with Derived Metrics

This diagram shows the $2 \times 2$ confusion matrix with the canonical axis labels (Actual on rows, Predicted on columns) and arrows pointing to each derived metric, illustrating how each cell contributes to the evaluation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M["Confusion Matrix 2 by 2"]
        direction LR
        C1["TP: Predicted Positive AND Actual Positive"] 
        C2["FN: Predicted Negative BUT Actual Positive"]
        C3["FP: Predicted Positive BUT Actual Negative"]
        C4["TN: Predicted Negative AND Actual Negative"]
    end

    C1 --> M1["Accuracy uses TP and TN in numerator"]
    C4 --> M1
    C1 --> M2["Precision uses TP over TP plus FP"]
    C3 --> M2
    C1 --> M3["Recall uses TP over TP plus FN"]
    C2 --> M3
    C4 --> M4["Specificity uses TN over TN plus FP"]
    C3 --> M4
    C1 --> M5["F1 Score uses Precision and Recall"]
    C2 --> M5
    C3 --> M5
    C3 --> M6["FPR uses FP over FP plus TN"]
    C4 --> M6
    C2 --> M7["FNR uses FN over FN plus TP"]
    C1 --> M7

    style C1 fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#065F46
    style C2 fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#991B1B
    style C3 fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#92400E
    style C4 fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF
    style M1 fill:#F3F4F6,stroke:#374151
    style M2 fill:#F3F4F6,stroke:#374151
    style M3 fill:#F3F4F6,stroke:#374151
    style M4 fill:#F3F4F6,stroke:#374151
    style M5 fill:#F3F4F6,stroke:#374151
    style M6 fill:#F3F4F6,stroke:#374151
    style M7 fill:#F3F4F6,stroke:#374151
```

### Color-Coding Convention

| Cell Color | Meaning |
|:---|:---|
| 🟢 Green (TP) | Correct positive identification |
| 🔴 Red (FN) | Missed positive — dangerous in medical contexts |
| 🟡 Yellow (FP) | False alarm — costly in spam filtering |
| 🔵 Blue (TN) | Correct rejection |
| ⚪ Gray (Metrics) | Derived evaluation metrics |

---

## 4.3 Multi-Class Confusion Matrix Architecture

For $N$-class classification ($N > 2$), the confusion matrix generalizes to an $N \times N$ structure. The following block diagram shows the conceptual layout for a 3-class problem (e.g., Iris dataset with classes Setosa, Versicolor, Virginica).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer: True Labels"]
        I1["Class A: 50 samples"]
        I2["Class B: 50 samples"]
        I3["Class C: 50 samples"]
    end

    subgraph MODEL["Classifier Model"]
        MDL["Trained Multi-Class Classifier"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Predicted Labels Distribution"]
        P1["Predicted A: 48 correct, 2 misclassified as B"]
        P2["Predicted B: 47 correct, 3 misclassified as C"]
        P3["Predicted C: 49 correct, 1 misclassified as A"]
    end

    subgraph METRICS["Per-Class and Aggregate Metrics"]
        MTR1["Per-Class Precision, Recall, F1"]
        MTR2["Macro Average: mean across classes"]
        MTR3["Micro Average: aggregate over all cells"]
        MTR4["Weighted Average: weighted by class support"]
    end

    I1 --> MDL
    I2 --> MDL
    I3 --> MDL
    MDL --> P1
    MDL --> P2
    MDL --> P3
    P1 --> MTR1
    P2 --> MTR1
    P3 --> MTR1
    MTR1 --> MTR2
    MTR1 --> MTR3
    MTR1 --> MTR4

    style MDL fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#92400E
    style MTR1 fill:#E6F7EC,stroke:#2D6A4F
    style MTR2 fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF
    style MTR3 fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#5B21B6

```
