# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define bootstrapping. List any two advantages of the bootstrap method over traditional parametric inference.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Bootstrapping is a non-parametric resampling technique in which multiple samples of size $n$ are drawn **with replacement** from an original dataset of size $n$ to estimate the sampling distribution of a statistic. Let $D = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ be the original sample. For $b = 1, 2, \dots, B$, we draw $D^{*(b)}$ by sampling with replacement, compute $\hat{\theta}^{*(b)} = s(D^{*(b)})$, and aggregate these to obtain the bootstrap mean and standard error.

**Advantages (1 Mark — any two):**
1. **No distributional assumption required** — works for any statistic (median, percentile, AUC) without needing normality.
2. **Applicable to complex statistics** — useful for estimators whose variance formula is mathematically intractable.
3. **Provides empirical confidence intervals** directly from data.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**What is a confusion matrix? Construct the confusion matrix for a binary classifier and define the terms TP, FP, TN, and FN.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A confusion matrix is an $N \times N$ table that reports the counts of **correct** and **incorrect** predictions broken down by class, comparing predicted labels $\hat{y}$ against actual labels $y$.

**Matrix (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Confusion Matrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 
\text{TP} & \text{FN} \\ 
\text{FP} & \text{TN} 
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Definitions (1 Mark — 0.25 each):**
- **TP (True Positive):** Model predicted Positive; actual class is Positive.
- **FP (False Positive):** Model predicted Positive; actual class is Negative. (Type I Error)
- **TN (True Negative):** Model predicted Negative; actual class is Negative.
- **FN (False Negative):** Model predicted Negative; actual class is Positive. (Type II Error)

---

## 📝 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**A logistic regression model was evaluated on a test set of 500 patients for diabetes prediction. The confusion matrix obtained is as follows:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Confusion Matrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 
180 & 20 \\ 
30 & 270 
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where rows represent Predicted and columns represent Actual classes (Positive = Diabetic, Negative = Non-Diabetic).**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Compute the following metrics with proper formulas:**
(i) Accuracy
(ii) Precision
(iii) Recall (Sensitivity)
(iv) Specificity
(v) F1-Score

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Compute Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) and Cohen's Kappa. Comment on the agreement quality.**
**(ii) If the model is intended for a medical screening test, which metric — Precision or Recall — should be prioritized, and why? Justify with a one-sentence threshold argument.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $\text{TP} = 180$, $\text{FN} = 20$, $\text{FP} = 30$, $\text{TN} = 270$, $\text{Total} = 500$.

**(i) Accuracy** [1.5 Marks]:

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{Total}} = \frac{180 + 270}{500} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90
$$

**(ii) Precision** [1.5 Marks]:

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}} = \frac{180}{180 + 30} = \frac{180}{210} \approx 0.8571
$$

**(iii) Recall (Sensitivity)** [1.5 Marks]:

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90
$$

**(iv) Specificity** [1.5 Marks]:

$$
\text{Specificity} = \frac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN} + \text{FP}} = \frac{270}{270 + 30} = \frac{270}{300} = 0.90
$$

**(v) F1-Score** [1 Mark]:

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8571 \times 0.90}{0.8571 + 0.90} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7714}{1.7571} \approx 0.8780
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**(i) MCC and Cohen's Kappa** [4 Marks]:

**MCC Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= \text{TP} \cdot \text{TN} - \text{FP} \cdot \text{FN} = (180)(270) - (30)(20) = 48600 - 600 = 48000 \\
\text{Denominator} &= \sqrt{(\text{TP}+\text{FP})(\text{TP}+\text{FN})(\text{TN}+\text{FP})(\text{TN}+\text{FN})} \\
&= \sqrt{(210)(200)(300)(290)} \\
&= \sqrt{210 \times 200 \times 300 \times 290} \\
&= \sqrt{3654000000} \\
&\approx 60448
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{MCC} = \frac{48000}{60448} \approx 0.794
$$

**Cohen's Kappa Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_o &= \text{Accuracy} = 0.90 \\
p_e &= \frac{(\text{TP}+\text{FP})(\text{TP}+\text{FN}) + (\text{TN}+\text{FP})(\text{TN}+\text{FN})}{\text{Total}^2} \\
&= \frac{(210)(200) + (300)(290)}{500^2} \\
&= \frac{42000 + 87000}{250000} \\
&= \frac{129000}{250000} = 0.516
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\kappa = \frac{0.90 - 0.516}{1 - 0.516} = \frac{0.384}{0.484} \approx 0.793
$$

**Comment:** Both MCC ($\approx 0.794$) and Kappa ($\approx 0.793$) indicate **substantial agreement** between predicted and actual classes (Landis & Koch benchmark: $\kappa \in [0.61, 0.80]$ = Substantial).

**(ii) Metric Prioritization in Medical Screening** [3 Marks]:

**Recall should be prioritized.** In medical screening, a **False Negative (FN)** means a sick patient is incorrectly classified as healthy — this patient will not receive treatment and may suffer severe consequences. A **False Positive (FP)** only means a healthy patient undergoes further (often non-invasive) confirmatory tests. Therefore, the **cost asymmetry** ($C_{\text{FN}} \gg C_{\text{FP}}$) demands a model with **high Recall** (i.e., low Type II error rate), even at the cost of slightly reduced Precision. As a threshold argument: lowering the classification threshold from $0.5$ to $0.3$ increases Recall by catching more true diabetics, at the acceptable cost of a few additional false alarms.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**A data scientist collects a sample dataset containing 5 observations: $\{2, 5, 8, 11, 14\}$. She performs bootstrap resampling with $B = 5$ iterations using the following samples drawn with replacement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= \{2, 8, 8, 14, 5\} \\
S_2 &= \{11, 11, 2, 5, 8\} \\
S_3 &= \{5, 2, 14, 2, 11\} \\
S_4 &= \{8, 14, 5, 2, 5\} \\
S_5 &= \{14, 11, 8, 5, 2\}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**(i) Compute the bootstrap mean estimate $\hat{\theta}_{\text{boot}}$.**
**(ii) Compute the bootstrap estimate of the standard error $\widehat{SE}_{\text{boot}}$.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Construct a 95% bootstrap percentile confidence interval for the mean.**
**(ii) Explain how bootstrapping is used in Random Forest algorithms for model evaluation. Mention the concept of "Out-of-Bag (OOB) error" in your answer.**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute original sample mean (reference):**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{2 + 5 + 8 + 11 + 14}{5} = \frac{40}{5} = 8.0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each bootstrap sample mean** [4 Marks]:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{x}^{*(1)} &= \frac{2 + 8 + 8 + 14 + 5}{5} = \frac{37}{5} = 7.4 \\
\bar{x}^{*(2)} &= \frac{11 + 11 + 2 + 5 + 8}{5} = \frac{37}{5} = 7.4 \\
\bar{x}^{*(3)} &= \frac{5 + 2 + 14 + 2 + 11}{5} = \frac{34}{5} = 6.8 \\
\bar{x}^{*(4)} &= \frac{8 + 14 + 5 + 2 + 5}{5} = \frac{34}{5} = 6.8 \\
\bar{x}^{*(5)} &= \frac{14 + 11 + 8 + 5 + 2}{5} = \frac{40}{5} = 8.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**(i) Bootstrap Mean Estimate** [1.5 Marks]:

$$
\hat{\theta}_{\text{boot}} = \frac{7.4 + 7.4 + 6.8 + 6.8 + 8.0}{5} = \frac{36.4}{5} = 7.28
$$

**(ii) Bootstrap Standard Error** [1.5 Marks]:

Squared deviations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(7.4 - 7.28)^2 &= (0.12)^2 = 0.0144 \\
(7.4 - 7.28)^2 &= (0.12)^2 = 0.0144 \\
(6.8 - 7.28)^2 &= (-0.48)^2 = 0.2304 \\
(6.8 - 7.28)^2 &= (-0.48)^2 = 0.2304 \\
(8.0 - 7.28)^2 &= (0.72)^2 = 0.5184
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum $= 0.0144 + 0.0144 + 0.2304 + 0.2304 + 0.5184 = 1.008$

$$
\widehat{SE}_{\text{boot}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.008}{5 - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.008}{4}} = \sqrt{0.252} \approx 0.502
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**(i) 95% Bootstrap Percentile Confidence Interval** [3 Marks]:

Sort the bootstrap means in ascending order:

$$
\{6.8,\ 6.8,\ 7.4,\ 7.4,\ 8.0\}
$$

For a 95% percentile CI with $B = 5$, the lower bound is the $\frac{0.025 \times 5}{}$ = 0.125-th value (round up to 1st), and the upper bound is the $\frac{0.975 \times 5}{}$ = 4.875-th value (round down to 5th).

In practice with $B = 5$ (small), we use the empirical quantiles:

$$
\text{CI}_{95\%} = [6.8,\ 8.0]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> With larger $B$ (typically $B \geq 1000$), the percentile CI is taken as $[\hat{\theta}^{*(0.025B)},\ \hat{\theta}^{*(0.975B)}]$.

**(ii) Bootstrapping in Random Forest and OOB Error** [4 Marks]:

**Random Forest and Bagging:**
Random Forest is an **ensemble learning** method that constructs $B$ decision trees, each trained on a **bootstrap sample** of the original training data. This is known as **Bootstrap Aggregation (Bagging)**. Each tree sees a slightly different subset of data, introducing diversity that reduces variance when the trees' predictions are averaged (regression) or voted (classification).

**Out-of-Bag (OOB) Error:**
Because each bootstrap sample is drawn **with replacement** from $n$ observations, approximately **36.8%** of the original data points are **left out** of each bootstrap sample (as $1 - 0.632 = 0.368$). These left-out samples are called **Out-of-Bag (OOB) samples** for that particular tree.

For each observation $x_i$ in the original dataset, we can collect predictions from **only those trees** for which $x_i$ was OOB. The aggregated OOB prediction is then compared to the true label $y_i$. The **OOB error** is computed as:

$$
\text{Error}_{\text{OOB}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}\left[\hat{y}_{i}^{\text{OOB}} \neq y_i\right]
$$

**Significance:**
- OOB error provides an **internal cross-validation estimate** of the generalization error **without needing a separate validation set**.
- This makes Random Forest computationally efficient — you get an unbiased test error estimate "for free" during training.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Axis Mislabeling Trap:** KTU questions may present confusion matrices with **rows = Predicted** OR **rows = Actual**. Always re-read the axis labels — swapping TP with FP changes **every** derived metric.
> 2. **Division by Zero:** When computing Precision or Recall, if the denominator (TP+FP or TP+FN) equals zero, the metric is **undefined**, not zero. State this explicitly in the answer.
> 3. **Bootstrap "With Replacement" Violation:** Sampling *without* replacement defeats the entire purpose of bootstrapping — every sample becomes identical to the original. Examiners specifically test this conceptual understanding.
> 4. **Forgetting the $B-1$ Denominator:** The bootstrap variance uses $B-1$ in the denominator (Bessel's correction), not $B$. This is a 1-mark deduction trap.
> 5. **Confusing "Error Rate" with "Accuracy":** A model with 85% accuracy has a 15% error rate — they are complements, not independent metrics.
> 6. **Forgetting Class Imbalance Context:** Reporting only Accuracy in imbalanced datasets (e.g., 95% negative class) is misleading. Always pair Accuracy with **Precision, Recall, and F1-Score** for KTU full-mark answers.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 4 Examination:**

### Bootstrapping — Key Takeaways
- ✅ Bootstrapping = **resampling with replacement** to estimate the **sampling distribution** of a statistic.
- ✅ Introduced by **Bradley Efron (1979)**; non-parametric and distribution-free.
- ✅ Each bootstrap sample has the **same size $n$** as the original dataset.
- ✅ Approximately **63.2% unique observations** appear in each bootstrap sample (the **0.632 rule**).
- ✅ Bootstrap mean: $\hat{\theta}_{\text{boot}} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{\theta}^{*(b)}$
- ✅ Bootstrap SE: $\widehat{SE}_{\text{boot}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{B-1} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \left(\hat{\theta}^{*(b)} - \hat{\theta}_{\text{boot}}\right)^2}$
- ✅ **Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregation)** is the foundation of **Random Forests**.
- ✅ **OOB (Out-of-Bag) error** uses the ~36.8% of unselected samples per tree as an internal validation set.
- ✅ Standard choice of $B$: at least **1000** iterations for stable estimates; $B = 50$–$100$ is acceptable for KTU numerical problems.

### Confusion Matrix — Key Takeaways
- ✅ A **$2 \times 2$ matrix** for binary classification; **$N \times N$** for $N$-class problems.
- ✅ **TP, TN, FP, FN** are the four cardinal cells — memorize their definitions cold.
- ✅ **Accuracy** = $\frac{\text{TP} + \text{TN}}{\text{Total}}$ — single most reported metric, but misleading under class imbalance.
- ✅ **Precision** = $\frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FP}}$ — focus on prediction quality.
- ✅ **Recall (Sensitivity, TPR)** = $\frac{\text{TP}}{\text{TP} + \text{FN}}$ — focus on detection quality.
- ✅ **Specificity (TNR)** = $\frac{\text{TN}}{\text{TN} + \text{FP}}$ — true negative detection rate.
- ✅ **F1-Score** = harmonic mean of Precision and Recall — preferred single metric for imbalanced data.
- ✅ **MCC** and **Cohen's Kappa** are equivalent for $2 \times 2$ matrices — both correct for chance agreement.
- ✅ **Domain determines metric choice:** Medical → Recall; Spam → Precision; Balanced → F1.

### KTU-Specific Exam Tips
- ✅ Always show the **formula** before substituting values (1 mark reserved for this).
- ✅ For multi-part questions, **label each sub-answer** (a-i, a-ii, b-i, b-ii) clearly.
- ✅ Carry intermediate results to **4 decimal places** to avoid rounding-off errors.
- ✅ If given **only the confusion matrix**, verify the total $N$ matches the given dataset size before computing.
- ✅ For bootstrap problems, always state the **"with replacement"** assumption explicitly in the first line of your answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Precision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Precision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In Machine Learning, **Precision** is a fundamental **classification evaluation metric** that quantifies the accuracy of *positive predictions* made by a classifier. Formally, it is defined as the ratio of correctly predicted positive observations (True Positives) to the total number of observations predicted as positive (True Positives + False Positives).

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} $$

Where:
- **TP (True Positives):** Cases where the model correctly predicted the *positive* class.
- **FP (False Positives):** Cases where the model incorrectly predicted the *positive* class (Type I Error).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precision is also commonly referred to as "Positive Predictive Value (PPV)"** in statistical literature. It answers the question: *"Of all the instances the model labeled as positive, how many were actually positive?"*

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **metal detector at an airport security checkpoint**. Out of 100 people the detector beeps for (predicts "positive" — carrying metal), suppose 95 are actually carrying prohibited metal items and 5 are false alarms (e.g., belt buckles, wedding rings).

- The detector's **Precision = 95/100 = 95%**
- This means: *"When the alarm rings, you can be 95% confident that there is genuinely a threat."*

In contrast, if the detector beeps for 1000 people but only 50 are real threats, the precision drops to 5% — the alarm has become useless because it cries wolf too often.

## 1.3 Why Precision is Critical in Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **High Precision is required when the cost of a False Positive is very high.**

| Domain | Application | Cost of False Positive |
|---|---|---|
| Medical Diagnosis | Cancer detection | Unnecessary biopsy, patient anxiety |
| Email Filtering | Spam classification | Legitimate email sent to junk folder |
| Information Retrieval | Web search ranking | Irrelevant results shown to user |
| Manufacturing | Defect detection | Discarding good products (waste) |
| Finance | Fraud detection | Blocking legitimate transactions |

## 1.4 Range and Interpretation

- **Range:** $0 \leq \text{Precision} \leq 1$ (or 0% to 100%)
- **Precision = 1:** Every positive prediction is correct (no False Positives)
- **Precision = 0:** No positive prediction is correct (all predictions are wrong)
- **Higher Precision → Fewer False Alarms → More Trustworthy Positive Predictions**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix Foundation

Precision is derived directly from the **Confusion Matrix**, which tabulates the performance of a classification model on a set of test data for which the true values are known.

|  | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

The four quadrants represent the four possible outcomes of a binary classifier.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The operational flow to compute precision follows this structured logic:

1. **Step 1 — Train the Classifier:** Fit a model (e.g., Logistic Regression, SVM, Decision Tree) on the training data.
2. **Step 2 — Generate Predictions:** Apply the trained model to test/unseen data to obtain class labels (Positive or Negative).
3. **Step 3 — Construct the Confusion Matrix:** Compare predicted labels with ground-truth labels to count TP, FP, FN, TN.
4. **Step 4 — Isolate the "Predicted Positive" Column:** Precision focuses only on the predicted positive outcomes (TP + FP).
5. **Step 5 — Compute the Ratio:** Divide correctly predicted positives (TP) by the total predicted positives (TP + FP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** Precision is *column-oriented* (focuses on predicted positive column), whereas **Recall (Sensitivity)** is *row-oriented* (focuses on actual positive row). This structural difference is the single most important conceptual point examiners test.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Precision** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Accuracy of positive predictions |
| **Recall (Sensitivity / TPR)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Coverage of actual positives |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Accuracy of negative predictions |
| **F1-Score** | $\frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Harmonic mean of P and R |
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + FN + TN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Overall correctness |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Probability of false alarm |
| **Total Population (N)** | $TP + FP + FN + TN$ | $\mathbb{Z}^+$ | Sum of all test samples |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In real-world ML pipelines, Precision is preferred for **imbalanced datasets** where the positive class is rare but critical (e.g., disease screening, fraud detection, defect identification in semiconductor wafers). Accuracy alone becomes misleading in such cases.

## 2.4 The Precision-Recall Trade-off

There is an **inverse relationship** between Precision and Recall as the classification threshold varies:

- **Raising the threshold** (e.g., from 0.5 to 0.8) → Model becomes more conservative → **Precision increases**, Recall decreases.
- **Lowering the threshold** (e.g., from 0.5 to 0.2) → Model predicts "positive" more freely → **Recall increases**, Precision decreases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A model with Precision = 1.0 and Recall = 0.0 is useless (predicts nothing positive). A model with both = 1.0 is perfect. The **F1-Score** balances both.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example — Computing Precision from a Confusion Matrix

### Problem Statement

A binary classifier is evaluated on **200 test samples**. The resulting confusion matrix is given below. Compute the Precision and all related metrics.

|  | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** | **Row Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | 50 | 10 | 60 |
| **Actual Negative** | 5 | 135 | 140 |
| **Column Total** | 55 | 145 | 200 |

### Step 1: Extract the Four Cardinal Values

From the matrix, we identify:

$$ TP = 50, \quad FP = 5, \quad FN = 10, \quad TN = 135 $$

**Textual Logic:** Reading the table — the cell where "Actual Positive" meets "Predicted Positive" gives TP. The cell where "Actual Negative" meets "Predicted Positive" gives FP (Type I errors). The cell where "Actual Positive" meets "Predicted Negative" gives FN (Type II errors). The cell where "Actual Negative" meets "Predicted Negative" gives TN.

### Step 2: Compute Precision

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 5} = \frac{50}{55} $$

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{10}{11} \approx 0.9091 \approx 90.91\% $$

**Textual Logic:** The model made 55 total positive predictions. Of these, 50 were correct. The ratio 50/55 represents the precision.

### Step 3: Compute Recall (for cross-verification)

$$ \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 83.33\% $$

**Textual Logic:** There were 60 truly positive samples in reality. The model correctly identified 50 of them. The ratio is 50/60.

### Step 4: Compute F1-Score

$$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9091 \cdot 0.8333}{0.9091 + 0.8333} $$

$$ F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7576}{1.7424} = 2 \cdot 0.4348 = 0.8696 $$

**Textual Logic:** The harmonic mean penalizes extreme imbalances. Even though precision (0.91) is high, the lower recall (0.83) pulls the F1 down to 0.87, providing a more honest single-number summary.

### Step 5: Compute Accuracy (to demonstrate its weakness here)

$$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + FN + TN} = \frac{50 + 135}{200} = \frac{185}{200} = 92.5\% $$

**Textual Logic:** The model appears excellent at 92.5% accuracy. However, this is misleading because the dataset has more negatives (140) than positives (60). A dummy classifier predicting "Negative" always would achieve 140/200 = 70% accuracy. Precision is the *more informative* metric.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of F1 from Precision and Recall

The **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of Precision (P) and Recall (R). We derive it step-by-step:

$$ F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}} $$

This is the definition of the harmonic mean of two numbers. Expanding the denominator:

$$ \frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R} = \frac{R + P}{P \cdot R} $$

Substituting back:

$$ F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{P + R}{P \cdot R}} = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} $$

**Textual Logic:** The harmonic mean is used (instead of arithmetic mean) because it punishes extreme values. If either P or R is near zero, F1 collapses to near zero, accurately reflecting poor model performance.

## 3.3 Multi-Class Extension — Macro and Micro Precision

For a **k-class problem**, precision is computed per class and then averaged.

### Macro Precision (unweighted average)

$$ P_{\text{macro}} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{TP_i}{TP_i + FP_i} $$

**Textual Logic:** Treats every class equally regardless of support size. Suitable when all classes are equally important.

### Micro Precision (weighted by instance count)

$$ P_{\text{micro}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} TP_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} (TP_i + FP_i)} $$

**Textual Logic:** Pools all TP and FP across classes before computing the ratio. In multi-class single-label problems, micro-precision equals accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Where Precision Fits in the Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Test Data] --> B[Trained Classifier]
    B --> C[Predicted Labels]
    C --> D[Compare with Ground Truth]
    D --> E[Build Confusion Matrix]
    E --> F[Compute TP, FP, FN, TN]
    F --> G[Precision: TP / TP+FP]
    F --> H[Recall: TP / TP+FN]
    F --> I[F1-Score: 2PR / P+R]
    F --> J[Accuracy: TP+TN / Total]
    G --> K[Final Model Evaluation Report]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

**Reading Guide:** This flowchart illustrates the hierarchical evaluation pipeline. Notice that all four metrics branch from the same node (TP, FP, FN, TN), emphasizing that **the confusion matrix is the universal foundation** of classification metrics.

## 4.2 Visualizing the Precision-Recall Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Low Threshold] -->|More Positives Predicted| M[Recall UP]
    M --> N[Precision DOWN]
    H[High Threshold] -->|Fewer Positives Predicted| O[Recall DOWN]
    O --> P[Precision UP]
    N --> Q[Trade-off Curve]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Optimal Operating Point]
    R --> S[Balanced F1-Score]
```

**Reading Guide:** The trade-off curve plots Precision (y-axis) against Recall (x-axis). The optimal operating point balances both metrics and is often selected by maximizing the F1-Score.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Precision in an ML System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        A1[Raw Features] --> A2[Preprocessing]
        A2 --> A3[Train-Test Split]
    end
    subgraph MODEL[Model Stage]
        A3 --> B1[Algorithm Selection]
        B1 --> B2[Logistic Regression]
        B1 --> B3[Random Forest]
        B1 --> B4[Neural Network]
    end
    subgraph PREDICT[Prediction Stage]
        B2 --> C1[Probability Scores]
        B3 --> C1
        B4 --> C1
        C1 --> C2[Apply Threshold T]
    end
    subgraph EVAL[Evaluation Stage]
        C2 --> D1[Predicted Class Labels]
        D1 --> D2[Confusion Matrix]
        D2 --> E1[Precision Metric]
        D2 --> E2[Recall Metric]
        D2 --> E3[F1 Score]
    end
    E1 --> F1[Threshold Tuning]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    F1 --> C2
```

**Reading Guide:** This block diagram shows the closed-loop nature of ML evaluation. The threshold tuning loop (F1 → C2) demonstrates that **precision is not a fixed property of a model** — it changes with the decision threshold. This is a frequent KTU exam trap.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Precision. How is it different from Accuracy? When would you prefer Precision over Accuracy?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Precision is the ratio of True Positives to the total number of positive predictions made by the classifier. It is given by:

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} $$

**Difference from Accuracy (1 mark):** Accuracy measures the proportion of correct predictions (both positive and negative) out of the total predictions. Precision focuses only on the correctness of *positive* predictions. Mathematically, Accuracy = (TP + TN)/N, while Precision = TP/(TP + FP).

**When to Prefer Precision (1 mark):** Precision is preferred over Accuracy in **imbalanced classification problems** where the cost of False Positives is high, such as spam detection, fraud detection, or medical diagnosis. In such cases, Accuracy can be misleadingly high due to the majority class.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the term "False Positive" with a suitable example. How does it affect Precision?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A False Positive (FP), also called a Type I Error, occurs when the model predicts the *positive* class for an instance that is actually *negative*.

**Example (1 mark):** In a cancer screening test, if a healthy patient is incorrectly diagnosed as having cancer, this is a False Positive. Another example: a legitimate email being marked as spam.

**Effect on Precision (1 mark):** False Positives appear in the **denominator** of the Precision formula. Therefore, increasing FP decreases Precision. The relationship is inversely proportional. To improve Precision, the model must reduce FP without significantly sacrificing TP.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Confusion Matrix with a neat diagram. List and define the four outcomes: TP, FP, FN, TN.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A classifier was tested on 1000 samples. The confusion matrix obtained is:

|  | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | 200 | 50 |
| **Actual Negative** | 30 | 720 |

Calculate the Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Accuracy. Comment on the model's performance.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]

**Confusion Matrix Definition (2 marks):** A Confusion Matrix is an N×N table (N = number of classes) that summarizes the performance of a classification algorithm by comparing predicted labels against actual labels.

**Four Outcomes (4 marks):**

- **True Positive (TP):** Model predicts *Positive*, and the actual class is *Positive*. ✓ Correct detection.
- **False Positive (FP):** Model predicts *Positive*, but the actual class is *Negative*. ✗ Type I Error (False Alarm).
- **False Negative (FN):** Model predicts *Negative*, but the actual class is *Positive*. ✗ Type II Error (Miss).
- **True Negative (TN):** Model predicts *Negative*, and the actual class is *Negative*. ✓ Correct rejection.

**Diagram (1 mark):** A 2×2 matrix with rows = Actual and columns = Predicted, showing all four cells labeled correctly.

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Extract Values (1 mark)**

$$ TP = 200, \quad FN = 50, \quad FP = 30, \quad TN = 720 $$

**Step 2: Compute Precision (1.5 marks)**

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{200}{200 + 30} = \frac{200}{230} \approx 0.8696 = 86.96\% $$

**Step 3: Compute Recall (1.5 marks)**

$$ \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{200}{200 + 50} = \frac{200}{250} = 0.80 = 80\% $$

**Step 4: Compute F1-Score (1.5 marks)**

$$ F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8696 \cdot 0.80}{0.8696 + 0.80} = \frac{1.3914}{1.6696} \approx 0.8334 = 83.34\% $$

**Step 5: Compute Accuracy (1 mark)**

$$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{200 + 720}{1000} = \frac{920}{1000} = 0.92 = 92\% $$

**Step 6: Comment (0.5 mark):** The model has high overall accuracy (92%) and good precision (86.96%), meaning positive predictions are reliable. However, recall is 80%, indicating the model misses 20% of actual positive cases. The F1 of 83.34% suggests a reasonably balanced model, but threshold tuning could improve recall if needed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the Precision-Recall trade-off in classification. Explain with a suitable graph how varying the decision threshold affects these two metrics.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a multi-class problem with 3 classes, the per-class TP, FP, and FN are given. Compute the Macro Precision and Micro Precision.

| Class | TP | FP | FN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cat | 40 | 10 | 5 |
| Dog | 35 | 8 | 12 |
| Bird | 25 | 7 | 3 |

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]

**Definition of Trade-off (2 marks):** Precision and Recall are inversely related. As one increases, the other typically decreases. This is called the **Precision-Recall Trade-off** and is controlled by the decision threshold of the classifier.

**Effect of Threshold (3 marks):**
- **Low threshold (e.g., 0.3):** Model is liberal in predicting "Positive." This catches more actual positives (Recall ↑) but also includes more false positives (Precision ↓).
- **High threshold (e.g., 0.8):** Model is conservative. It only predicts "Positive" when very confident, leading to fewer false positives (Precision ↑) but missing some positives (Recall ↓).

**Graph Description (2 marks):** A Precision-Recall curve plots Precision (y-axis) vs Recall (x-axis) as the threshold varies from 0 to 1. The curve typically starts high (top-left) and descends toward the right. The **optimal point** is chosen by maximizing F1-Score or by domain-specific requirements (e.g., medical diagnosis may prioritize recall).

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Compute Per-Class Precision (2 marks)**

$$ P_{\text{Cat}} = \frac{40}{40+10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.80 $$

$$ P_{\text{Dog}} = \frac{35}{35+8} = \frac{35}{43} \approx 0.8140 $$

$$ P_{\text{Bird}} = \frac{25}{25+7} = \frac{25}{32} \approx 0.7813 $$

**Step 2: Compute Macro Precision (1.5 marks)**

$$ P_{\text{macro}} = \frac{1}{3}(0.80 + 0.8140 + 0.7813) = \frac{2.3953}{3} \approx 0.7984 = 79.84\% $$

**Step 3: Compute Total TP and Total FP (1.5 marks)**

$$ \sum TP = 40 + 35 + 25 = 100 $$

$$ \sum FP = 10 + 8 + 7 = 25 $$

**Step 4: Compute Micro Precision (2 marks)**

$$ P_{\text{micro}} = \frac{\sum TP}{\sum TP + \sum FP} = \frac{100}{100 + 25} = \frac{100}{125} = 0.80 = 80\% $$

**Comparison (Optional, 0.5 mark):** In this case, Macro Precision (79.84%) ≈ Micro Precision (80%) because the class distribution is relatively balanced. If classes were imbalanced, these values would diverge significantly.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Precision with Accuracy:** Students frequently mix up the formula. Remember — Precision uses *predicted* positives in the denominator; Accuracy uses *total* samples.
> 2. **Forgetting to add FP to TP:** A common arithmetic error is writing Precision = TP/Total. The correct denominator is **TP + FP**, not the full sample size.
> 3. **Using Arithmetic Mean instead of Harmonic Mean for F1:** F1-Score uses the harmonic mean to penalize extreme values. Using arithmetic mean will cost full marks.
> 4. **Ignoring Multi-Class Scenarios:** KTU frequently tests Macro vs. Micro averaging. Students who only know binary precision lose 3-4 marks.
> 5. **Not commenting on results:** A numerical answer without interpretation loses the final 0.5-1 mark. Always add a "comment on model performance" sentence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Precision (Module 4):**

- **Core Formula:** Precision = TP / (TP + FP). Memorize this as the foundation of all classification metrics.
- **Range:** Always between 0 and 1. Precision = 1 means zero false positives.
- **Column-Oriented Metric:** Precision focuses on the *Predicted Positive* column of the confusion matrix.
- **Alternative Name:** Precision = Positive Predictive Value (PPV).
- **When to Use:** Choose Precision when **False Positives are more costly** than False Negatives.
- **Trade-off Partner:** Recall = TP / (TP + FN). The two metrics are inversely related via the decision threshold.
- **F1-Score:** Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall = 2PR / (P+R). Use it when you need a single balanced metric.
- **Imbalanced Data:** Accuracy is misleading; Precision (and Recall) are the correct metrics.
- **Multi-Class Variants:** Macro Precision = simple average of per-class precisions. Micro Precision = pooled TP / pooled (TP+FP).
- **Threshold Dependency:** Precision is NOT a fixed model property — it changes when you change the classification threshold.
- **Common Error:** Precision ≠ TP / Total. Always include FP in the denominator.
- **Engineering Examples:** Spam filters (precision matters), cancer screening (recall often matters more), fraud detection (precision matters).
- **Pairing Metrics:** In KTU exams, always compute and report Precision, Recall, and F1-Score together for a complete picture.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme Tip:** Expect 3-mark direct definition questions and 7-14 mark numerical/computational questions involving confusion matrices.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recall
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recall

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Recall (R)** — formally known as **Sensitivity**, **Hit Rate**, or **True Positive Rate (TPR)** — is a fundamental performance metric in binary and multi-class classification that quantifies the proportion of *actual positive instances* in the dataset that the classifier successfully identified as positive.

Mathematically, **Recall** measures the model's ability to **find all relevant positive cases** within a dataset. It is one of the four canonical metrics derived from the **Confusion Matrix**, alongside Accuracy, Precision, and Specificity.

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

Where:
- **TP** = True Positives (correctly predicted positive cases)
- **FN** = False Negatives (positive cases missed by the model)

The value of Recall lies in the closed interval **[0, 1]**, with **1.0 representing perfect recall** (every positive instance captured) and **0.0 representing complete failure** (no positive instance detected).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Recall is a cornerstone metric under *Module 4: Model Evaluation and Performance Metrics*. Students must understand its derivation, its distinction from Precision, and its practical implications in high-stakes domains like medical diagnostics, fraud detection, and security screening.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **fisherman casting a net** into a lake filled with fish (the positive cases) and weeds (the negative cases).

- Your **net's job** is to catch *all the fish* in the lake.
- **Recall** asks: *"Out of all the fish actually swimming in the lake, how many did your net successfully scoop up?"*

If the lake contains **100 fish** and your net catches **80 of them**, your Recall is **80/100 = 0.8 (or 80%)**. The 20 fish that escaped represent the **False Negatives** — actual positives that the model failed to catch.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Recall does **not** punish the model for mistakenly catching weeds (that is what **Precision** measures). Recall is solely concerned with **completeness** — did the model miss any genuine positives?

### Real-World Engineering Applications

| Domain | Positive Class | Why Recall Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Cancer / Disease present | Missing a sick patient (FN) can be fatal |
| **Spam Filtering** | Spam email | Missing spam is annoying but not catastrophic |
| **Fraud Detection** | Fraudulent transaction | Missing fraud causes direct financial loss |
| **Defect Manufacturing** | Defective product | Missing defects leads to product failure |
| **Security Screening** | Threat detected | Missing a threat is unacceptable |

In all these domains, the cost of a **False Negative** vastly outweighs the cost of a **False Positive** — making Recall the metric of choice.

## 1.3 Standard Statistical Aliases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Recall carries multiple synonymous names depending on the academic field. The KTU examiner may use any of these terms interchangeably:
> - **Sensitivity** (medical/biostatistics literature)
> - **True Positive Rate (TPR)** (signal detection theory)
> - **Hit Rate** (information retrieval)
> - **Detection Rate** (machine learning literature)
> - **Completeness** (search engine evaluation)

All of these are computed using the identical formula: $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix — Foundation of Recall

The **Confusion Matrix** is a 2×2 (for binary classification) or N×N (for multi-class) tabular structure that records the count of correct and incorrect predictions made by a classifier.

### 2.1.1 Binary Classification Confusion Matrix

|                 | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | **TP** (True Positive) | **FN** (False Negative) |
| **Actual Negative** | **FP** (False Positive) | **TN** (True Negative) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Convention:** Rows represent the *Ground Truth (Actual)* labels, while columns represent the *Model Predicted* labels. This is the standard convention used in KTU board examinations and scikit-learn documentation.

### 2.1.2 Anatomy of Each Cell

- **TP (True Positive)**: Model predicted *Positive*, and the actual label was *Positive*. ✅ Correct.
- **TN (True Negative)**: Model predicted *Negative*, and the actual label was *Negative*. ✅ Correct.
- **FP (False Positive)**: Model predicted *Positive*, but the actual label was *Negative*. ❌ **Type I Error** (also called a "false alarm").
- **FN (False Negative)**: Model predicted *Negative*, but the actual label was *Positive*. ❌ **Type II Error** (also called a "miss").

## 2.2 Operational Logic — How Recall Works

The metric Recall follows a precise three-step evaluation pipeline:

1. **Step 1 — Isolate the Positive Class.** The model identifies which samples truly belong to the positive class in the ground truth (these are the rows in the *Actual Positive* row of the confusion matrix).
2. **Step 2 — Count Correct Detections.** Among these actual positives, count those the model correctly classified as positive (the **TP** count).
3. **Step 3 — Compute the Ratio.** Divide the count of correctly identified positives (TP) by the **total number of actual positives** (TP + FN). This total is sometimes called the **"Condition Positive"** count in ISO/IEC standards.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula a KTU student must memorize for Module 4 evaluation metrics. All metrics are derivable from the four cells of the binary confusion matrix.

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation | Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Recall (Sensitivity, TPR)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of actual positives correctly identified | $[0, 1]$ | dimensionless |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Fraction of predicted positives that are truly positive | $[0, 1]$ | dimensionless |
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Fraction of all correct predictions | $[0, 1]$ | dimensionless |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Fraction of actual negatives correctly identified | $[0, 1]$ | dimensionless |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall | $[0, 1]$ | dimensionless |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\frac{FP}{FP + TN}$ | Fall-out / false alarm rate | $[0, 1]$ | dimensionless |
| **Miss Rate (FNR)** | $\frac{FN}{TP + FN}$ | Complement of Recall | $[0, 1]$ | dimensionless |
| **Prevalence** | $\frac{TP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Fraction of actual positives in dataset | $[0, 1]$ | dimensionless |

### 2.3.1 Key Algebraic Relationships

The following identities are essential for cross-metric calculation problems in KTU examinations:

$$R = 1 - FNR$$

$$R + FNR = 1$$

$$P + FDR \neq 1 \quad \text{(where FDR} = 1 - P)$$

$$\text{F1} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Recall is **independent of TN and FP**. This means a model can achieve perfect Recall (R = 1) simply by predicting every single sample as *Positive* — even if every negative sample is misclassified! This is why Recall must **always** be interpreted alongside Precision.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **production ML systems**, Recall is preferred in scenarios where the **cost asymmetry** between False Negatives and False Positives is severe.

**Example — COVID-19 Screening Test:**
- Suppose there are 10,000 people, of which 100 are actually infected.
- A test that detects 95 of them and misses 5 has: $R = 95/100 = 0.95$.
- The same test may falsely flag 500 healthy people, giving a low Precision: $P = 95/595 \approx 0.16$.
- Yet, in public health policy, **catching 95 out of 100 infected individuals** is considered valuable, justifying Recall-optimized thresholds.

## 2.5 The Precision-Recall Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favorite Question:** "Explain the Precision-Recall trade-off in classification."

A single threshold (e.g., probability > 0.5) governs the classifier's decision boundary. As the threshold varies:

- **Lowering the threshold** → Model predicts *Positive* more aggressively → **Recall ↑, Precision ↓**
- **Raising the threshold** → Model predicts *Positive* more conservatively → **Precision ↑, Recall ↓**

This is the **fundamental trade-off** in probabilistic classifiers (Logistic Regression, Neural Networks, etc.). The optimal threshold depends on the **domain-specific cost function**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Recall from First Principles

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Conditional Probability Foundation

Recall is fundamentally a **conditional probability** — specifically, the probability that the model predicts *Positive* **given** that the ground truth is *Positive*.

Let $Y$ be the random variable representing the **true label** and $\hat{Y}$ represent the **predicted label**. Then:

$$R = P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1)$$

Applying the definition of conditional probability:

$$\begin{aligned}
P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1) &= \frac{P(\hat{Y} = 1 \cap Y = 1)}{P(Y = 1)} \\
&= \frac{\text{Number of cases where } \hat{Y} = 1 \text{ AND } Y = 1}{\text{Number of cases where } Y = 1} \\
&= \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
\end{aligned}$$

This derivation shows that **Recall is purely a function of the positive class** — the negative class (TN, FP) plays no role in its computation.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Miss Rate Complement

Since $P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1) + P(\hat{Y} = 0 \mid Y = 1) = 1$ (the model must predict either Positive or Negative):

$$\begin{aligned}
R + FNR &= 1 \\
\therefore \quad R &= 1 - FNR \\
&= 1 - \frac{FN}{TP + FN} \\
&= \frac{TP + FN - FN}{TP + FN} \\
&= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Recall in Multi-Class Settings

For an $N$-class classification problem, Recall is computed **per class** using the **One-vs-Rest (OvR)** strategy. For class $i$:

$$R_i = \frac{TP_i}{TP_i + FN_i}$$

The overall Recall can be aggregated in three ways:

| Aggregation Method | Formula | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Macro-Averaged Recall** | $R_{macro} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i$ | Treats all classes equally |
| **Micro-Averaged Recall** | $R_{micro} = \frac{\sum TP_i}{\sum (TP_i + FN_i)}$ | Aggregates globally, dominance by majority class |
| **Weighted-Averaged Recall** | $R_{weighted} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot R_i$ | Weights by class frequency |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> The following example is modeled after a typical KTU Part B (14-mark) numerical problem. It is essential that students practice the entire workflow end-to-end.

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A binary classifier was evaluated on a test set of **1,000 samples**. The resulting **Confusion Matrix** is given below:

|                 | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | 180 | 20 |
| **Actual Negative** | 50 | 750 |

**Required:**
1. Compute the **Recall**.
2. Compute the **Precision**.
3. Compute the **F1-Score**.
4. Compute the **Accuracy**.
5. Compute the **Specificity**.
6. If the threshold is lowered, the TP increases to 195 and FP increases to 120. Recompute Recall and Precision. Comment on the trade-off.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Extract values from the confusion matrix.**

$$\begin{aligned}
TP &= 180 \\
FN &= 20 \\
FP &= 50 \\
TN &= 750
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Recall.**

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
&= \frac{180}{180 + 20} \\
&= \frac{180}{200} \\
&= 0.90
\end{aligned}$$

**Recall = 0.90 (or 90%)** — The classifier correctly identified 90% of all actual positive samples.

**Step 3 — Compute Precision.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
&= \frac{180}{180 + 50} \\
&= \frac{180}{230} \\
&= 0.7826
\end{aligned}$$

**Precision = 0.7826 (≈ 78.26%)** — Of all samples the model flagged as positive, 78.26% were truly positive.

**Step 4 — Compute the F1-Score.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.7826 \times 0.90}{0.7826 + 0.90} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.7043}{1.6826} \\
&= 2 \cdot 0.4186 \\
&= 0.8372
\end{aligned}$$

**F1-Score = 0.8372 (≈ 83.72%)**

**Step 5 — Compute the Accuracy.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \\
&= \frac{180 + 750}{180 + 750 + 50 + 20} \\
&= \frac{930}{1000} \\
&= 0.93
\end{aligned}$$

**Accuracy = 0.93 (93%)**

**Step 6 — Compute Specificity.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Specificity} &= \frac{TN}{TN + FP} \\
&= \frac{750}{750 + 50} \\
&= \frac{750}{800} \\
&= 0.9375
\end{aligned}$$

**Specificity = 0.9375 (≈ 93.75%)**

**Step 7 — Re-evaluate after threshold reduction.**

New values: $TP' = 195$, $FP' = 120$, $FN' = 5$ (the 20 previously missed positives, 15 of which are now caught), $TN' = 680$.

$$\begin{aligned}
R' &= \frac{195}{195 + 5} = \frac{195}{200} = 0.975 \\
P' &= \frac{195}{195 + 120} = \frac{195}{315} = 0.619
\end{aligned}$$

**Trade-off Observation:** Recall increased from **0.90 → 0.975 (+8.33%)**, but Precision dropped from **0.7826 → 0.619 (−20.85%)**. This confirms the inverse relationship between Precision and Recall as the decision threshold is relaxed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Check:** Total sample count must remain constant. Original: $180 + 20 + 50 + 750 = 1000$. New: $195 + 5 + 120 + 680 = 1000$. ✅

## 3.3 Derivation of the F1-Score in Terms of Confusion Matrix

The F1-Score is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall. The harmonic mean is used (instead of arithmetic mean) because it **penalizes extreme values** — a model with R = 1.0 and P = 0.1 gets a low F1, not a misleadingly high score.

$$\begin{aligned}
F1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}
\end{aligned}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(TP+FP)(TP+FN)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
F1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{TP^2}{TP(TP+FN) + TP(TP+FP)} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{TP^2}{TP(2 \cdot TP + FN + FP)} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

This compact form is often used directly in KTU numerical problems for faster computation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Confusion Matrix Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the structural relationships between the four cells of a binary confusion matrix and the metrics derived from them.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Total Test Samples N"] --> B["Actual Positive: TP plus FN"]
    A --> C["Actual Negative: TN plus FP"]
    B --> D["TP: Correctly Predicted Positive"]
    B --> E["FN: Missed Positives Type II Error"]
    C --> F["TN: Correctly Predicted Negative"]
    C --> G["FP: False Alarms Type I Error"]
    D --> H["RECALL equals TP divided by TP plus FN"]
    D --> I["PRECISION equals TP divided by TP plus FP"]
    D --> J["F1 SCORE equals 2TP divided by 2TP plus FP plus FN"]
    F --> K["SPECIFICITY equals TN divided by TN plus FP"]
    D --> L["ACCURACY equals TP plus TN divided by N"]
    F --> L
    H --> M["Output Range 0 to 1"]
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow for Choosing Recall vs Precision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Classification Task"] --> Q1{"Cost of False Negative vs False Positive"}
    Q1 -->|"FN much worse e.g. Cancer Detection"| R1["Optimize for HIGH RECALL"]
    Q1 -->|"FP much worse e.g. Spam Filter Avoiding Important Email"| R2["Optimize for HIGH PRECISION"]
    Q1 -->|"Both equally costly"| R3["Optimize for HIGH F1 SCORE"]
    Q1 -->|"Class Imbalance Present"| R4["Use PR AUC or Balanced Accuracy"]
    R1 --> R1a["Minimize FN even if FP increases"]
    R2 --> R2a["Minimize FP even if FN increases"]
    R3 --> R3a["Balance both using harmonic mean"]
    R4 --> R4a["Avoid Accuracy Trap"]
```

## 4.3 Precision-Recall Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LowThreshold["LOW THRESHOLD REGION"]
        T1["Threshold 0.1"] --> V1["Recall HIGH near 1.0"]
        V1 --> V2["Precision LOW many false alarms"]
    end
    subgraph MidThreshold["MEDIUM THRESHOLD REGION"]
        T2["Threshold 0.5"] --> V3["Balanced Precision and Recall"]
    end
    subgraph HighThreshold["HIGH THRESHOLD REGION"]
        T3["Threshold 0.9"] --> V4["Precision HIGH very confident"]
        V4 --> V5["Recall LOW many missed positives"]
    end
    LowThreshold --> MidThreshold
    MidThreshold --> HighThreshold
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Metric Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input["Raw Predictions and Ground Truth Labels"] --> CM["Build Confusion Matrix"]
    CM --> Extract["Extract TP, FN, FP, TN"]
    Extract --> M1["Recall equals TP over TP plus FN"]
    Extract --> M2["Precision equals TP over TP plus FP"]
    Extract --> M3["F1 Score equals 2TP over 2TP plus FP plus FN"]
    Extract --> M4["Specificity equals TN over TN plus FP"]
    Extract --> M5["Accuracy equals TP plus TN over Total"]
    M1 --> Report["Generate Evaluation Report"]
    M2 --> Report
    M3 --> Report
    M4 --> Report
    M5 --> Report
    Report --> Decision["Threshold Tuning if needed"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define Recall in the context of a binary classification problem. State its formula and explain why it is also called the True Positive Rate.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Recall (R)** is defined as the ratio of the number of correctly predicted positive samples (True Positives) to the total number of actual positive samples in the dataset.

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

It is called the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** because it measures the probability that the classifier outputs a *Positive* prediction **given that the true label is actually Positive** — i.e., the rate of *true* positives among all *true* positives. The denominator (TP + FN) represents the *condition positive* count, i.e., every sample that should have been classified as positive.

Recall is dimensionless, lies in the range **[0, 1]**, and is most useful when the cost of a False Negative is high — such as in medical diagnosis and fraud detection.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Precision and Recall. Under what conditions should one prefer Recall over Precision?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Precision | Recall |
|---|---|---|
| **Question Answered** | Of items flagged Positive, how many are truly Positive? | Of all truly Positive items, how many were flagged? |
| **Formula** | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ |
| **Focus** | Quality of Positive predictions | Completeness of Positive detection |
| **Failure Mode** | Misses many positives (high FN) | Many false alarms (high FP) |

**When to prefer Recall:**
- When **False Negatives are more costly** than False Positives.
- **Examples:** Cancer screening (missing a cancer patient is fatal), security threat detection (missing a threat is catastrophic), fraud detection (missing fraud causes financial loss).
- When the **positive class is rare** but critical to detect, and the model is allowed to over-flag negatives (which can later be filtered by human review).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **KTU 2024 Scheme regulations**, Part B questions carry 14 marks each, divided into sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. The student must answer **ONE** of the two internal choice questions. Below, both choices are fully solved.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the Confusion Matrix. For a binary classification problem, derive the expression for **Recall** and explain its significance in evaluating classifier performance on imbalanced datasets.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Random Forest classifier was tested on a dataset of **2,000 customer churn records**. The confusion matrix obtained is as follows:

|                 | Predicted Churn | Predicted No Churn |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Churn** | 420 | 80 |
| **Actual No Churn** | 150 | 1,350 |

Compute: **(i) Recall, (ii) Precision, (iii) F1-Score, (iv) Accuracy**. Comment on whether the classifier is suitable for deployment in a customer retention campaign where the cost of missing a churner is **3× the cost** of a false alarm.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Definition and Derivation of Recall**

A **Confusion Matrix** is a tabular summary of a classifier's predictions versus the actual ground-truth labels. For a binary classifier, it is a 2×2 matrix with four cells: **TP, FP, TN, FN**.

**Derivation of Recall:**

Recall is the conditional probability $P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= P(\hat{Y} = 1 \mid Y = 1) \\
&= \frac{P(\hat{Y} = 1 \cap Y = 1)}{P(Y = 1)} \\
&= \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Significance in imbalanced datasets:**

In datasets where the positive class is rare (e.g., fraud = 1% of transactions), a trivial classifier that predicts *Negative* for every sample achieves 99% Accuracy. However, such a classifier has **Recall = 0** because it captures no positives. Recall exposes this failure, making it the preferred metric for imbalanced data. Recall is **invariant to class imbalance** in the sense that it specifically measures the model's ability to detect the minority class.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

**Step 1 — Extract values.**

$$TP = 420, \quad FN = 80, \quad FP = 150, \quad TN = 1{,}350$$

Verification: $420 + 80 + 150 + 1{,}350 = 2{,}000$ ✅

**(i) Recall:**

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{420}{420 + 80} = \frac{420}{500} = 0.84
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{R = 0.84 \text{ (84%)}}$$

**(ii) Precision:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{420}{420 + 150} = \frac{420}{570} = 0.7368
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 0.7368 \text{ (73.68%)}}$$

**(iii) F1-Score:**

$$\begin{aligned}
F1 &= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 420}{2 \times 420 + 150 + 80} \\
&= \frac{840}{840 + 230} = \frac{840}{1{,}070} = 0.7850
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{F1 \approx 0.7850 \text{ (78.50%)}}$$

**(iv) Accuracy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Acc} &= \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{420 + 1{,}350}{2{,}000} = \frac{1{,}770}{2{,}000} = 0.885
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Accuracy} = 0.885 \text{ (88.5%)}}$$

**Comment on Deployment Suitability:**

Given the cost structure: Cost(FN) = 3 × Cost(FP), the company should optimize for **Recall**, since False Negatives (missed churners) are 3× more expensive than False Alarms (wrongly flagged loyal customers).

- The classifier's Recall is **0.84**, which means it captures 84% of actual churners — leaving 16% (80 customers) uncaught.
- For every 100 churners, 16 are lost, which still represents a significant revenue loss.
- **Recommendation:** The classifier is **moderately suitable** for deployment. To improve Recall, the decision threshold should be **lowered** (accepting more False Positives) until R ≥ 0.90, which is a typical industry benchmark for churn campaigns. The cost of extra retention offers to loyal customers (FP) is justified by the 3× cost asymmetry.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to **scale cost ratios** when computing the expected loss. Always compute Expected Cost $= C_{FN} \cdot FN + C_{FP} \cdot FP$ and compare across thresholds. Marks are awarded for the *trade-off justification*, not just metric computation.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Many students confuse Precision and Recall by swapping FP and FN in the denominator. Memorize: **Recall's denominator has FN** (what was *missed*); **Precision's denominator has FP** (what was *wrongly flagged*).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Precision-Recall trade-off** with a suitable diagram. How does varying the decision threshold of a logistic regression classifier affect Recall and Precision? Justify your answer with a numerical example.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A medical diagnostic model for detecting a rare disease produces the following confusion matrix on a test set of **5,000 patients** (only 200 actually have the disease):

|                 | Predicted Disease | Predicted Healthy |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Disease** | 160 | 40 |
| **Actual Healthy** | 100 | 4,700 |

Calculate **Recall, Precision, Specificity, F1-Score, and Prevalence**. Also compute the **Miss Rate** and explain its clinical significance.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Precision-Recall Trade-off**

The **Precision-Recall trade-off** describes the inverse relationship between Precision and Recall as the decision threshold of a probabilistic classifier is varied.

- For **Logistic Regression**, the default threshold is **0.5** (predict Positive if $P(Y=1 \mid X) > 0.5$).
- **Lowering the threshold** (e.g., to 0.3) makes the classifier predict *Positive* more often → captures more True Positives (Recall ↑) but also flags more False Positives (Precision ↓).
- **Raising the threshold** (e.g., to 0.7) makes the classifier predict *Positive* only when very confident → fewer False Positives (Precision ↑) but more missed positives (Recall ↓).

**Numerical Justification:**

| Threshold | TP | FP | FN | Precision | Recall |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.3 (Liberal) | 170 | 200 | 30 | 0.459 | 0.85 |
| 0.5 (Default) | 160 | 100 | 40 | 0.615 | 0.80 |
| 0.7 (Strict) | 130 | 30 | 70 | 0.813 | 0.65 |

This table empirically demonstrates the trade-off. The optimal threshold is selected based on the **domain-specific cost function**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation**

**Step 1 — Extract values.**

$$TP = 160, \quad FN = 40, \quad FP = 100, \quad TN = 4{,}700, \quad N = 5{,}000$$

Verification: $160 + 40 + 100 + 4{,}700 = 5{,}000$ ✅

**Recall:**

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{160}{160 + 40} = \frac{160}{200} = 0.80$$

**Precision:**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{160}{160 + 100} = \frac{160}{260} = 0.6154$$

**Specificity:**

$$\text{Spec} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{4{,}700}{4{,}700 + 100} = \frac{4{,}700}{4{,}800} = 0.9792$$

**F1-Score:**

$$F1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{320}{320 + 100 + 40} = \frac{320}{460} = 0.6957$$

**Prevalence:**

$$\text{Prev} = \frac{TP + FN}{N} = \frac{200}{5{,}000} = 0.04 \text{ (4%)}$$

**Miss Rate (FNR):**

$$FNR = \frac{FN}{TP + FN} = \frac{40}{200} = 0.20$$

**Clinical Significance of Miss Rate:**

The **Miss Rate of 20%** indicates that **1 in every 5 diseased patients** is incorrectly classified as healthy. In a clinical setting, this is **unacceptably high** because undiagnosed patients miss critical early intervention. To reduce the Miss Rate, the decision threshold should be lowered, increasing Recall at the cost of Precision. Given that further confirmatory tests can filter False Positives, a Recall target of **≥ 0.95** is the recommended clinical benchmark.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #3:** Students often **misclassify the rows/columns** of the confusion matrix. Always read: *Row = Actual (Ground Truth)*, *Column = Predicted (Model Output)*. Reversing this leads to swapping TP with FP and FN with TN — costing **up to 4 marks**.
>
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #4:** In medical-domain numericals, students must explicitly **discuss the clinical context** (e.g., cost of missed diagnosis) to earn full marks. A purely numerical answer without domain commentary loses at least **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Recall**

- **Recall** is the **fraction of actual positives** correctly identified by the model: $R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$.
- Recall is **also called** Sensitivity, True Positive Rate (TPR), Hit Rate, or Detection Rate.
- **Range:** $[0, 1]$ — higher is better. Perfect Recall = 1.0.
- Recall is **independent of TN and FP** — a degenerate "predict-all-positive" model achieves R = 1.0 trivially.
- **Use Recall** when **False Negatives are more costly** than False Positives (cancer, fraud, security, defect detection).
- **Use Precision** when False Positives are more costly (spam filtering important emails, drug approval).
- **Use F1-Score** when both errors carry equal weight — it is the **harmonic mean** of P and R.
- **Precision-Recall Trade-off:** Lower threshold → Recall ↑, Precision ↓. Higher threshold → Recall ↓, Precision ↑.
- **Miss Rate (FNR)** is the **complement** of Recall: $FNR = 1 - R$.
- In **multi-class** settings, Recall is computed **per class** using One-vs-Rest; aggregate via macro, micro, or weighted averaging.
- **Confusion Matrix orientation** (KTU standard): Rows = *Actual*, Columns = *Predicted*.
- **Accuracy is misleading** for imbalanced datasets — always pair it with Recall and Precision.
- **Prevalence** $= \frac{TP + FN}{N}$ tells you the base rate of the positive class.
- **F1-Score in matrix form:** $F1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ — preferred for fast computation.
- **F-beta Score** generalizes F1: $F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 P + R}$; $\beta > 1$ weights Recall more, $\beta < 1$ weights Precision more.
- **Kerala KTU 2024 Scheme** expects students to (i) **derive formulas** from conditional probability, (ii) **compute all four metrics** from a given matrix, and (iii) **interpret** the result in a **domain-specific** engineering context.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accuracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Accuracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Machine Learning (ML), **Accuracy** is a standard **classification evaluation metric** that quantifies the proportion of correct predictions made by a classifier out of the total number of predictions. Mathematically, it represents the ratio of correctly classified instances (both positive and negative) to the overall number of instances evaluated.

Formally, for a classification model evaluated on a dataset of size $N$, accuracy is defined as:

$$Accuracy = \frac{\text{Number of Correct Predictions}}{\text{Total Number of Predictions}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Accuracy belongs to the family of **Performance Metrics** in Module 4 (Evaluation of Classifiers). It is derived directly from the **Confusion Matrix** and is one of the most intuitive yet deceptively simple metrics used in supervised classification.

---

### 1.2 Intuition & Real-World Analogy

**The Classroom Exam Analogy:**
Imagine a teacher who has graded **100 answer sheets** of a multiple-choice exam. Out of these 100 papers:
- The teacher correctly identified **88** papers — meaning she correctly recognized both the genuine answers and the cheating/spoiled ones.
- She made **12** mistakes.

Her **accuracy** would be:

$$Accuracy = \frac{88}{100} = 0.88 \text{ or } 88\%$$

So, **Accuracy answers the simplest question:** *"Out of all the predictions the model made, how many were actually correct?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Accuracy Alone is NOT Enough:**
> Consider a medical test for a rare disease that affects only **1 out of 1000** people. A naive model that simply predicts "No Disease" for everyone will achieve **99.9% accuracy**, yet it will completely fail to detect any actual positive cases. This phenomenon is known as the **Accuracy Paradox**, which KTU examiners frequently test.

---

### 1.3 The Confusion Matrix Foundation

Accuracy is computed directly from a **Confusion Matrix**, which categorizes predictions into four distinct outcomes:

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **TP** | True Positive (correctly predicted positive) |
| **TN** | True Negative (correctly predicted negative) |
| **FP** | False Positive — **Type I Error** |
| **FN** | False Negative — **Type II Error** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For binary classification, the universe of all predictions satisfies: $N = TP + TN + FP + FN$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Decomposition

To compute accuracy rigorously, the following operational logic is executed:

1. **Data Splitting:** The original dataset is partitioned into training and testing subsets (typically using an 80:20 or 70:30 ratio).
2. **Model Inference:** The trained classifier $f(x)$ generates predictions $\hat{y}_i$ for every test instance $x_i$.
3. **Prediction Comparison:** Each predicted label $\hat{y}_i$ is compared against the ground-truth label $y_i$ using a Boolean indicator function $\mathbb{1}(\hat{y}_i = y_i)$.
4. **Aggregation:** All boolean comparisons are summed and normalized by $N$.
5. **Final Metric:** The resulting scalar value lies in the closed interval $[0, 1]$.

---

### 2.2 Why Accuracy is Computed This Way

- **Symmetry Property:** Accuracy treats all four regions of the confusion matrix (TP, TN, FP, FN) equally. Each contributes a unit weight to the final computation.
- **Scale Invariance:** Since it is a ratio, accuracy is independent of the absolute size of the dataset, enabling fair comparison across models trained on different data scales.
- **Differentiability (in spirit):** While accuracy itself is non-differentiable (step-function), it serves as the conceptual goal that differentiable **loss functions** (e.g., cross-entropy) attempt to optimize.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Notation Summary | Units / Range |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Accuracy (ACC)** | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Sum of correct predictions over total predictions | Dimensionless, $[0, 1]$ or $[0\%, 100\%]$ |
| **Error Rate (ERR)** | $\frac{FP + FN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = 1 - ACC$ | Complement of accuracy | Dimensionless, $[0, 1]$ |
| **Misclassification Rate** | $\frac{FP + FN}{N}$ | Synonymous with error rate | Dimensionless, $[0, 1]$ |
| **Balanced Accuracy** | $\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{TP}{TP+FN} + \frac{TN}{TN+FP}\right)$ | Mean of sensitivity and specificity | Dimensionless, $[0, 1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KRUSHKAL-Wallis & STATISTICAL NOTE:** The sample accuracy $\hat{p}$ follows a **Binomial Distribution** $\hat{p} \sim \mathcal{B}(N, p)$ where $p$ is the true population accuracy. For large $N$, by the Central Limit Theorem, this converges to a Normal distribution:
> $$\hat{p} \approx \mathcal{N}\!\left(p, \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{N}}\right)$$
> This **confidence interval formulation** is a high-yield KTU 2024 topic.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical Diagnostics:** Initial screening tools where balanced accuracy is preferred, but raw accuracy serves as a baseline.
- **Spam Filtering:** Email classifiers use accuracy to gauge overall efficacy, though **Precision** is usually preferred to avoid losing legitimate emails.
- **Industrial Quality Control:** Manufacturing defect detection systems monitor accuracy as a top-level KPI.
- **Image Recognition:** General-purpose classifiers (e.g., MNIST digit recognition) where classes are roughly balanced.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Accuracy from the Confusion Matrix

**Given:** A binary classifier $f$ evaluated on $N$ test samples yields the following confusion matrix entries.

**Step 1: Define Total Number of Predictions**

$$N = TP + TN + FP + FN$$

**Step 2: Define the Number of Correct Predictions**

A prediction is *correct* if and only if it is either a True Positive or a True Negative. Therefore:

$$\text{Correct Predictions} = TP + TN$$

**Step 3: Form the Ratio**

Dividing correct predictions by the total predictions:

$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$

**Step 4: Express in Terms of Total $N$**

Since the denominator equals $N$, we can rewrite:

$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{N}$$

**Step 5: Express as a Percentage**

Multiplying by 100 yields the percentage form:

$$Accuracy \,(\%) = \frac{TP + TN}{N} \times 100\%$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Binomial Standard Error

The number of correct predictions $K$ follows a Binomial distribution: $K \sim \text{Binomial}(N, p)$.

**Step 1: Mean of $K$**

$$E[K] = Np$$

**Step 2: Variance of $K$**

$$Var(K) = Np(1-p)$$

**Step 3: Sample Accuracy Proportion**

Define $\hat{p} = \frac{K}{N}$.

**Step 4: Variance of $\hat{p}$**

$$Var(\hat{p}) = Var\!\left(\frac{K}{N}\right) = \frac{1}{N^2}Var(K) = \frac{p(1-p)}{N}$$

**Step 5: Standard Error**

$$SE(\hat{p}) = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{N}}$$

**Step 6: 95% Confidence Interval**

Using the Normal approximation, the 95% confidence interval for true accuracy is:

$$CI_{95\%} = \hat{p} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{N}}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A binary classifier is tested on 200 samples. The confusion matrix reports TP = 80, TN = 90, FP = 20, FN = 10. Compute the accuracy.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Verify total:
$$N = 80 + 90 + 20 + 10 = 200 \checkmark$$

**Step 2:** Sum correct predictions:
$$\text{Correct} = TP + TN = 80 + 90 = 170$$

**Step 3:** Compute accuracy:
$$Accuracy = \frac{170}{200} = 0.85 = 85\%$$

**Step 4:** Compute error rate:
$$ERR = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15 = 15\%$$

**Step 5:** Compute 95% CI:
$$SE = \sqrt{\frac{0.85 \times 0.15}{200}} = \sqrt{0.0006375} \approx 0.02525$$

$$CI_{95\%} = 0.85 \pm 1.96 \times 0.02525 = [0.8005, \; 0.8995]$$

---

### 3.4 The Accuracy Paradox — Analytical Proof

Consider a dataset where positive cases constitute only $1\%$ of the population. A trivial classifier that predicts **"Negative"** for all inputs will achieve:

$$Accuracy_{\text{trivial}} = \frac{TN + 0}{N} = \frac{99}{100} = 99\%$$

However, this model has **Recall = 0**, making it practically useless. This demonstrates that high accuracy does **not** imply a useful model under class imbalance.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Confusion Matrix to Accuracy Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Ground Truth Labels Y"] --> nodeB["Trained Classifier F of X"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Predicted Labels Y Hat"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Comparison Engine"]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE1["TP True Positive"]
    nodeD --> nodeE2["TN True Negative"]
    nodeD --> nodeE3["FP False Positive"]
    nodeD --> nodeE4["FN False Negative"]
    nodeE1 --> nodeF["Numerator TP plus TN"]
    nodeE2 --> nodeF
    nodeE3 --> nodeG["Denominator TP plus TN plus FP plus FN"]
    nodeE4 --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH["Accuracy Metric Output"]
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

### 4.2 Accuracy Computation Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph InputLayer["Input Stage"]
        inA["Dataset D of size N"]
    end

    subgraph ProcessingLayer["Processing Stage"]
        procA["Confusion Matrix Builder"]
        procB["Counter Module TP TN FP FN"]
        procC["Ratio Calculator"]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer["Output Stage"]
        outA["Scalar Accuracy Value"]
        outB["Percentage Display"]
        outC["Confidence Interval"]
    end

    inA --> procA
    procA --> procB
```
