```mermaid
    procC --> outA
    outA --> outB
    outA --> outC
```

### 4.3 Accuracy Under Class Imbalance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Evaluate Classifier"] --> q1{"Is Dataset Balanced?"}
    q1 -- Yes --> recA["Use Raw Accuracy Directly"]
    q1 -- No --> recB["Use Balanced Accuracy"]
    q1 -- No --> recC["Use F1 Score or MCC"]
    recB --> note1["Accounts for class distribution skew"]
    recC --> note2["Robust to Accuracy Paradox"]
    recA --> endNode["Report Final Metric"]
    recB --> endNode
    recC --> endNode
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term **Accuracy** in the context of a classification model. How is it computed from a confusion matrix?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> Accuracy is a classification performance metric that measures the ratio of correctly classified instances to the total number of instances.
>
> **Formula (2 Marks):**
>
> $$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$
>
> where TP, TN, FP, and FN represent True Positives, True Negatives, False Positives, and False Negatives respectively.
>
> **Range:** $[0, 1]$ or equivalently $[0\%, 100\%]$. (1 Mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
What is the **Accuracy Paradox**? State one scenario where high accuracy can be misleading.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The Accuracy Paradox (2 Marks) is the phenomenon where a model achieves very high accuracy by exploiting class imbalance — for example, predicting only the majority class.
>
> **Scenario (1 Mark):** In fraud detection where fraudulent transactions are only $0.1\%$ of all transactions, a model predicting "Not Fraud" for all cases will have $99.9\%$ accuracy, yet fail to detect any fraud.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for **Accuracy** from first principles using the four outcomes of a confusion matrix. Explain the meaning of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A spam detection classifier is tested on **500 emails**. The confusion matrix is as follows: TP = 150, TN = 280, FP = 40, FN = 30. Compute the accuracy, error rate, and the 95% confidence interval for the accuracy estimate. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

> Step 1: A classification evaluation considers a dataset $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where $y_i \in \{0, 1\}$. (1 Mark)
>
> Step 2: The classifier predicts $\hat{y}_i = f(x_i)$. A prediction is correct if $\hat{y}_i = y_i$. (1 Mark)
>
> Step 3: Define the indicator function for correctness:
>
> $$\mathbb{1}(\hat{y}_i = y_i) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if correct} \\ 0 & \text{if incorrect} \end{cases}$$
>
> (1 Mark)
>
> Step 4: Total correct predictions:
>
> $$K = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(\hat{y}_i = y_i)$$
>
> (1 Mark)
>
> Step 5: Identify that $K = TP + TN$ because a correct prediction must be either TP or TN. (1 Mark)
>
> Step 6: Form the final accuracy formula:
>
> $$Accuracy = \frac{K}{N} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$$
>
> (2 Marks)

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

> Step 1: Total samples:
>
> $$N = 150 + 280 + 40 + 30 = 500$$
>
> (1 Mark)
>
> Step 2: Accuracy:
>
> $$Accuracy = \frac{150 + 280}{500} = \frac{430}{500} = 0.86 = 86\%$$
>
> (2 Marks)
>
> Step 3: Error rate:
>
> $$ERR = 1 - 0.86 = 0.14 = 14\%$$
>
> (1 Mark)
>
> Step 4: Standard error:
>
> $$SE = \sqrt{\frac{0.86 \times 0.14}{500}} = \sqrt{0.0002408} \approx 0.01552$$
>
> (1 Mark)
>
> Step 5: 95% Confidence Interval:
>
> $$CI_{95\%} = 0.86 \pm 1.96 \times 0.01552 = 0.86 \pm 0.0304 = [0.8296, \; 0.8904]$$
>
> i.e., approximately $[82.96\%, \; 89.04\%]$. (2 Marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **limitations of accuracy** as a standalone evaluation metric. Under what conditions does it fail to provide meaningful insights about model performance? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A disease detection system is tested on a dataset of **10,000 patients** where only **100 patients** actually have the disease. A trivial model predicts "No Disease" for all patients. Compute its accuracy and explain why this metric is misleading in this context. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Limitations:**

> **1. Accuracy Paradox (2 Marks):** When classes are highly imbalanced, a naive majority-class predictor can achieve deceptively high accuracy.
>
> **2. Equal Error Weighting (2 Marks):** Accuracy treats FP and FN errors identically, but in many domains (e.g., cancer detection), FN is far more costly than FP.
>
> **3. No Class-Level Insight (2 Marks):** A single accuracy value masks per-class performance; a model may perform well on majority class but poorly on minority class.
>
> **4. Failure Condition (1 Mark):** Accuracy fails in skewed datasets where the minority class is of primary interest.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

> Step 1: Identify confusion matrix entries for the trivial classifier:
>
> $$TP = 0, \quad TN = 9900, \quad FP = 0, \quad FN = 100$$
>
> (2 Marks)
>
> Step 2: Compute accuracy:
>
> $$Accuracy = \frac{0 + 9900}{10000} = \frac{9900}{10000} = 0.99 = 99\%$$
>
> (2 Marks)
>
> Step 3: Explain why misleading (3 Marks):
>
> Although the model achieves $99\%$ accuracy, its **Recall (Sensitivity) = 0**, since it never identifies a single positive case. In medical diagnostics, this is catastrophic — every disease case goes undetected. This demonstrates the **Accuracy Paradox**, where accuracy masks the complete failure on the minority (positive) class.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add all four confusion matrix entries** in the denominator (students often write only $TP + TN$).
> 2. **Confusing Accuracy with Precision** — Precision uses only $TP / (TP + FP)$, while Accuracy uses all four entries.
> 3. **Ignoring the Accuracy Paradox** — Examiners award partial credit for recognizing imbalanced data limitations.
> 4. **Wrong CI multiplier** — Always use $1.96$ for $95\%$ CI, not $2.0$ or $1.645$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Accuracy** = Proportion of correct predictions out of total predictions.
- **Formula:** $\frac{TP + TN}{N}$
- **Range:** $[0, 1]$ or $[0\%, 100\%]$ — higher is better.
- **Error Rate** = $1 - Accuracy$ — lower is better.
- **Confidence Interval:** $CI_{95\%} = \hat{p} \pm 1.96\sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{N}}$
- **Accuracy Paradox:** High accuracy on imbalanced datasets can be meaningless.
- **Use Balanced Accuracy, F1-Score, or Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC)** when classes are imbalanced.
- **Statistical Nature:** Sample accuracy $\hat{p}$ follows a Binomial distribution, approximately Normal for large $N$.
- **Symmetry:** Accuracy weights all four confusion matrix cells equally, ignoring the asymmetric costs of FP and FN errors.
- **Always report accuracy alongside complementary metrics** in any KTU examination answer to demonstrate depth of understanding.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: F-Measure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** F-Measure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**F-Measure** (also known as **F-Score** or **F1-Score**) is a single scalar evaluation metric used in binary and multi-class classification tasks that combines **Precision** and **Recall** into a unified value by computing their **harmonic mean**. It is formally defined as the weighted harmonic mean of a classifier's precision and recall, providing a balanced assessment of model performance—particularly in scenarios involving **imbalanced datasets** where simple accuracy is misleading.

The most commonly used variant, the **F1-Score**, weights Precision and Recall equally and is computed as:

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** F-Measure is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. It is the **preferred single-metric evaluation** when classes are imbalanced or when both *false positives* and *false negatives* carry business cost (e.g., medical diagnosis, fraud detection, spam filtering).

---

## 1.2 Intuition & Real-World Analogy

### The "Restaurant Review Filter" Analogy 🍽️

Imagine you have built a Machine Learning model that filters **fake restaurant reviews** from a review platform:

- **Precision** asks: *"Of all the reviews my model FLAGGED as fake, how many were ACTUALLY fake?"* → This is about the **trustworthiness of positive predictions**. If Precision is high, your model rarely cries wolf.

- **Recall** asks: *"Of all the ACTUALLY fake reviews out there, how many did my model successfully CATCH?"* → This is about **completeness of detection**. If Recall is high, your model rarely misses a fake review.

Now, suppose you have two models:

| Model | Precision | Recall | Accuracy |
|:-----:|:---------:|:------:|:--------:|
| Model A | 0.99 | 0.40 | 85% |
| Model B | 0.70 | 0.70 | 80% |

> Model A is *overly conservative*—when it flags a review, it is almost always correct, but it misses **60% of fake reviews**. Model B is *balanced*—it catches most fakes but with some false alarms.

The **F-Measure** is a single number that captures this trade-off. Model A's F1 = 0.57, Model B's F1 = 0.70 → Model B is the better overall detector. **A high F-Measure requires BOTH Precision and Recall to be reasonably high simultaneously.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Harmonic Mean and NOT Arithmetic Mean?**
> The harmonic mean **penalizes extreme imbalances**. If either Precision or Recall is near zero, the F1-Score collapses toward zero, even if the other metric is perfect. The arithmetic mean (simple average) would give a falsely optimistic score in such cases. This property makes F-Measure the **gold standard** for imbalanced classification under KTU 2024 evaluation patterns.

---

## 1.3 Why Accuracy Fails — The Motivation for F-Measure

Consider a cancer-detection model tested on **1000 patients**, where only **10 actually have cancer**:

- A "dummy" classifier that predicts **"No Cancer" for everyone** achieves **99% accuracy** — yet is completely useless because it catches **0 out of 10 real cancer patients**.

In this scenario:
- Accuracy = 99% (misleadingly high)
- Recall = 0% (catastrophic — missed all cancer patients)
- F1-Score = 0% (correctly exposes the failure)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme, students frequently lose marks by reporting **only accuracy** for imbalanced data questions. Always cross-report **Precision, Recall, and F1-Score** together whenever the dataset mentions class imbalance, fraud detection, medical diagnosis, or anomaly detection.

---

## 1.4 Connection to the Confusion Matrix

F-Measure is computed directly from the four entries of the **Confusion Matrix**:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Actual Positive** | TP (True Positive) | FN (False Negative) |
| **Actual Negative** | FP (False Positive) | TN (True Negative) |

Where:
- **TP** = Correctly predicted positive cases
- **FP** = Incorrectly predicted positive cases (Type I Error)
- **FN** = Incorrectly predicted negative cases (Type II Error)
- **TN** = Correctly predicted negative cases

The F-Measure synthesizes the diagonal (TP) with the off-diagonal errors (FP, FN) into one interpretable number.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Metrics — Building Blocks of F-Measure

To understand F-Measure rigorously, we must first establish its two parent metrics from the **Module 4 evaluation framework**.

### Step 1: Precision (Positive Predictive Value)

**Precision** measures the **quality of positive predictions**. It answers the question: *"When my model says YES, how often is it right?"*

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

**Operational Logic:**
- **Numerator (TP):** The count of correct positive predictions
- **Denominator (TP + FP):** The total number of positive predictions made
- **Range:** $[0, 1]$ — Higher is better
- **KTU Interpretation:** High Precision = **low false alarm rate**

### Step 2: Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)

**Recall** measures the **completeness of positive detection**. It answers: *"Of all actual positives, how many did I find?"*

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

**Operational Logic:**
- **Numerator (TP):** Correctly identified positives
- **Denominator (TP + FN):** Total actual positives in the dataset
- **Range:** $[0, 1]$ — Higher is better
- **KTU Interpretation:** High Recall = **low miss rate**

---

## 2.2 The F-Measure Family — Mathematical Hierarchy

### 2.2.1 F1-Score (The Balanced Case)

The **F1-Score** is the **unweighted harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall, treating both metrics as equally important:

$$F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}} = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

> Where $P$ denotes Precision and $R$ denotes Recall.

**Why this exact form?** The harmonic mean of two numbers $P$ and $R$ is defined as:

$$H(P, R) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}$$

Algebraic simplification yields the popular form. This formulation is **mathematically rigorous** and is what the KTU examiner expects you to write on the answer sheet.

### 2.2.2 The Generalized $F_\beta$-Score

When Precision and Recall do **not** carry equal importance, the **$F_\beta$ Score** generalizes the formula with a weighting parameter $\beta$:

$$F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{(\beta^2 \cdot P) + R}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation of $\beta$:**
> - $\beta = 1$ → $F_1$ Score (equal weight to Precision and Recall)
> - $\beta > 1$ → Recall is weighted more heavily (penalize false negatives more)
> - $\beta < 1$ → Precision is weighted more heavily (penalize false positives more)
> - $\beta = 0$ → Reduces to Precision alone
> - $\beta \to \infty$ → Reduces to Recall alone

**Common Engineering Applications:**

| $\beta$ Value | Use Case | Reason |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| $F_{0.5}$ | Spam detection (false positives annoying users) | Precision matters more |
| $F_1$ | General classification, IR systems | Balanced trade-off |
| $F_2$ | Medical diagnosis, cancer screening | Missing a positive is dangerous |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it is the single most-tested content area for evaluation metrics in KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Metric | Formula | Range | Engineering Use |
|:--:|:-------|:--------|:-----:|:----------------|
| 1 | **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + FN + TN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Balanced datasets only |
| 2 | **Precision (P)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | When FP cost is high |
| 3 | **Recall (R)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | When FN cost is high |
| 4 | **F1-Score** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Imbalanced classes (general) |
| 5 | **$F_\beta$-Score** | $(1 + \beta^2) \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Weighted precision-recall trade-off |
| 6 | **Specificity (TNR)** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | True negative rate |
| 7 | **F1 (in TP/FP/FN form)** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Direct from confusion matrix |
| 8 | **Macro F1** | $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} F1_i$ | $[0, 1]$ | Multi-class unweighted average |
| 9 | **Micro F1** | Computed globally from aggregated CM | $[0, 1]$ | Multi-class instance-weighted |
| 10 | **Weighted F1** | $\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \cdot F1_i$ | $[0, 1]$ | Class-frequency weighted average |

**Boundary Conditions for F-Measure:**
- $F_1 = 1$ if and only if $P = 1$ and $R = 1$ (perfect classifier)
- $F_1 = 0$ if either $P = 0$ or $R = 0$ (catastrophic failure on one dimension)
- $F_1 \leq \min(P, R)$ always — F1 is **bounded above by the smaller of P and R**

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of F-Measure

1. **Information Retrieval (IR):** Search engines (Google, Bing) use F-Measure to evaluate the relevance of retrieved documents, balancing *precision* (returned docs are relevant) against *recall* (all relevant docs are returned).
2. **Medical Diagnostics:** F2-Score is preferred to weight recall heavily — missing a disease diagnosis is far costlier than a false alarm.
3. **Anomaly / Fraud Detection:** Highly imbalanced positive class (fraud ≈ 0.1% of transactions); F-Measure replaces accuracy.
4. **Named Entity Recognition (NER):** F1-Score is the **standard evaluation metric** in NLP pipelines.
5. **Object Detection:** Models like YOLO report **mAP**, which is the mean of F1 at various IoU thresholds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Harmonic Mean to the Standard F1 Formula

The F1-Score is mathematically defined as the **harmonic mean** of Precision ($P$) and Recall ($R$). The harmonic mean of $n$ numbers $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ is given by:

$$H =frac{n}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{x_i}}$$

For two numbers ($P$ and $R$) with $n = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(P, R) &= \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}} \\
&= \frac{2}{\frac{R + P}{P \cdot R}} \quad \text{[Common denominator]} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} \quad \text{[Invert and multiply]} \\
&= F_1 \quad \text{[By definition]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- We bring $P$ and $R$ to a common denominator in the denominator of the fraction.
- The $\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}$ simplifies to $\frac{R + P}{P \cdot R}$ using LCD arithmetic.
- The outer fraction is then inverted (division becomes multiplication), yielding the compact $F_1$ formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not the arithmetic mean $\frac{P + R}{2}$?**
> Try $P = 1.0$ and $R = 0.01$:
> - Arithmetic mean: $\frac{1.0 + 0.01}{2} = 0.505$ (misleadingly decent)
> - Harmonic mean (F1): $\frac{2 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.01}{1.0 + 0.01} \approx 0.0198$ (correctly exposes the failure)
>
> The harmonic mean acts as a **"punishment function"** for imbalance — exactly what KTU examiners test for.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The $F_\beta$ Formula from First Principles

The $F_\beta$ score generalizes F1 by introducing a positive real weight $\beta$ that controls the relative importance of Recall over Precision. By definition:

$$F_\beta = \frac{(1 + \beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{(\beta^2 \cdot P) + R}$$

**Derivation outline** (from the weighted harmonic mean):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_\beta &= \frac{(1 + \beta^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{R + \beta^2 \cdot P} \\
&= \frac{P \cdot R}{\frac{R}{1 + \beta^2} + \frac{\beta^2 \cdot P}{1 + \beta^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The denominator represents a **convex combination** (weighted average) of $P$ and $R$, where the weights sum to 1. This shows $F_\beta$ is a harmonic mean weighted by $\beta^2$.

**Special Case Verification:** Setting $\beta = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{(1 + 1^2) \cdot P \cdot R}{(1^2 \cdot P) + R} \\
&= \frac{2 P R}{P + R} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the formula collapses to the standard F1 form, validating the derivation.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Binary Classification

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Pattern):**

> A spam filter model was tested on 200 emails. The resulting confusion matrix is:
>
> | | Predicted Spam | Predicted Ham |
> |:---:|:---:|:---:|
> | **Actual Spam** | TP = 40 | FN = 10 |
> | **Actual Ham** | FP = 5 | TN = 145 |
>
> Compute: (i) Precision (ii) Recall (iii) F1-Score (iv) Accuracy

**Step 1 — Extract the values:**
- $TP = 40, \quad FP = 5, \quad FN = 10, \quad TN = 145$

**Step 2 — Compute Precision:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
&= \frac{40}{40 + 5} \\
&= \frac{40}{45} \\
&\approx 0.8889
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Of all 45 emails flagged as spam, 40 were truly spam — a precision of **88.89%**.

**Step 3 — Compute Recall:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
&= \frac{40}{40 + 10} \\
&= \frac{40}{50} \\
&= 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Of all 50 actual spam emails, the model caught 40 — a recall of **80%**.

**Step 4 — Compute F1-Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot 0.8889 \cdot 0.80}{0.8889 + 0.80} \\
&= \frac{1.4222}{1.6889} \\
&\approx 0.8421
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternative direct calculation** (using TP, FP, FN):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot 40}{2 \cdot 40 + 5 + 10} \\
&= \frac{80}{95} \\
&\approx 0.8421 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Accuracy (for comparison):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + FN + TN} \\
&= \frac{40 + 145}{40 + 5 + 10 + 145} \\
&= \frac{185}{200} \\
&= 0.925 \quad \text{or} \quad 92.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Summary Table:**

| Metric | Value | Interpretation |
|:-------|:-----:|:---------------|
| Precision | 0.8889 | 88.89% of spam flags are correct |
| Recall | 0.8000 | 80% of spam emails are caught |
| **F1-Score** | **0.8421** | **Balanced single-score evaluation** |
| Accuracy | 0.9250 | 92.5% overall correct (misleadingly high) |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — $F_\beta$ Application

**Problem:** In a medical diagnosis system for a rare disease:
- Precision = 0.60
- Recall = 0.90

Compute $F_1$, $F_2$, and $F_{0.5}$ and interpret the trend.

**Step 1 — Compute $F_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot 0.60 \cdot 0.90}{0.60 + 0.90} \\
&= \frac{1.08}{1.50} \\
&= 0.72
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $F_2$ (Recall-weighted):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_2 &= \frac{(1 + 2^2) \cdot 0.60 \cdot 0.90}{(2^2 \cdot 0.60) + 0.90} \\
&= \frac{5 \cdot 0.54}{2.40 + 0.90} \\
&= \frac{2.70}{3.30} \\
&\approx 0.818
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $F_{0.5}$ (Precision-weighted):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{0.5} &= \frac{(1 + 0.5^2) \cdot 0.60 \cdot 0.90}{(0.5^2 \cdot 0.60) + 0.90} \\
&= \frac{1.25 \cdot 0.54}{0.15 + 0.90} \\
&= \frac{0.675}{1.05} \\
&\approx 0.643
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result Table:**

| Metric | Score | Weight Bias |
|:-------|:-----:|:------------|
| $F_{0.5}$ | 0.643 | Precision-favored |
| $F_1$ | 0.720 | Balanced |
| $F_2$ | 0.818 | Recall-favored |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** As $\beta$ increases, the F-Score value approaches Recall (0.90). This is mathematically expected because $\beta \to \infty$ reduces $F_\beta$ to Recall. Conversely, $\beta \to 0$ reduces $F_\beta$ to Precision. For **cancer screening** in the KTU hospital-AI case study, the $F_2$ value (0.818) is the appropriate metric — we tolerate false positives to avoid missing a cancer patient.

---

## 3.5 Multi-Class F-Measure Derivation

In a **3-class classification** problem (e.g., classifying iris species: Setosa, Versicolor, Virginica), we extend F-Measure using three standard strategies:

### 3.5.1 Macro F1 (Unweighted Average)

Treat each class equally, compute F1 per class, then average:

$$F_1^{\text{macro}} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{i=1}^{C} F_{1,i}$$

where $C$ is the number of classes and $F_{1,i}$ is the F1-Score for class $i$.

### 3.5.2 Micro F1 (Instance-Weighted)

Aggregate the confusion matrix entries globally, then compute F1 once:

$$F_1^{\text{micro}} = \frac{2 \cdot \sum_i TP_i}{2 \cdot \sum_i TP_i + \sum_i FP_i + \sum_i FN_i}$$

### 3.5.3 Weighted F1 (Class-Frequency Weighted)

$$F_1^{\text{weighted}} = \sum_{i=1}^{C} w_i \cdot F_{1,i}, \quad w_i = \frac{n_i}{N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** When asked "compute the F-Measure for a multi-class problem," students must **explicitly state which averaging strategy** they are using. Examiners award 1 mark for correctly identifying the strategy.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Confusion-Matrix-to-F-Measure Block Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete computational pipeline** that transforms a raw confusion matrix into a finalized F-Measure score. This block-level architecture is the exact reasoning chain KTU examiners expect in 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset with N samples] --> B[Apply Trained Classifier]
    B --> C[Generate Predictions]
    C --> D[Compare with Ground Truth Labels]
    D --> E[Construct Confusion Matrix]
    E --> F1[Extract TP value]
    E --> F2[Extract FP value]
    E --> F3[Extract FN value]
    E --> F4[Extract TN value]
    F1 --> G[Compute Precision TP over TP+FP]
    F2 --> G
    F1 --> H[Compute Recall TP over TP+FN]
    F3 --> H
    G --> I[Compute Harmonic Mean]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Output F1 Score in range 0 to 1]
    F1 --> K[Optional Direct Form 2TP over 2TP+FP+FN]
    F2 --> K
    F3 --> K
    K --> J
    J --> L{Beta specified?}
    L -->|Yes, Beta greater than 1| M[Apply F Beta Recall weighted]
    L -->|Yes, Beta less than 1| N[Apply F Beta Precision weighted]
    L -->|No, default Beta equals 1| O[Final F1 Score]
    M --> P[Final F Beta Score]
    N --> P
```

---

## 4.2 Multi-Class F-Measure Averaging Strategy Flow

This schematic explains how to handle F-Measure computation when the dataset has **more than 2 classes**, a frequently tested scenario in KTU 2024 ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Multi-Class Confusion Matrix C x C] --> B{Averaging Strategy}
    B -->|Strategy 1| C[Per-Class F1 Computation]
    C --> C1[Class 1 F1]
    C --> C2[Class 2 F1]
    C --> C3[Class C F1]
    C1 --> D1[Macro F1: Simple Average]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C1 --> D2[Weighted F1: Frequency Weighted Average]
    C2 --> D2
    C3 --> D2
    A -->|Strategy 2| E[Global Aggregation of TP FP FN across all classes]
    E --> F[Compute Single F1 from Pooled Totals]
    F --> G[Micro F1 Score]
    D1 --> H[Final Multi-Class F1 Report]
    D2 --> H
    G --> H
```

---

## 4.3 Precision-Recall-F1 Trade-off Schematic

This block diagram illustrates the **conceptual relationship** between Precision, Recall, and F-Measure, showing how F1 acts as a balance point.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Precision Low Recall] -->|Conservative Classifier| B[F1 collapses toward Recall]
    C[High Recall Low Precision] -->|Aggressive Classifier| D[F1 collapses toward Precision]
    E[Balanced P and R near 1] -->|Ideal Classifier| F[F1 approaches 1]
    B --> G[F1 Score]
    D --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Score Interpretation}
    H -->|F1 equals 0.9 and above| I[Excellent Model]
    H -->|F1 between 0.7 and 0.9| J[Good Model]
    H -->|F1 between 0.5 and 0.7| K[Moderate Model]
    H -->|F1 below 0.5| L[Poor Model - Retrain Required]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`

> **Q:** Define **F-Measure**. Why is the **harmonic mean** used to combine Precision and Recall instead of the **arithmetic mean**? Give one example where F-Measure is preferred over Accuracy.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **F-Measure** is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, defined as:
>
> $$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$
>
> where $P$ is Precision and $R$ is Recall.
>
> The **harmonic mean** is used (instead of the arithmetic mean) because it **penalizes extreme imbalances** between Precision and Recall. For instance, if $P = 1.0$ and $R = 0.01$, the arithmetic mean gives $0.505$ (misleadingly high), while the harmonic mean gives approximately $0.0198$ (correctly indicating poor model performance). The harmonic mean is small whenever **either** $P$ or $R$ is small.
>
> **Example:** In a cancer detection system where only 1% of patients have the disease, a classifier predicting "no cancer" for all patients achieves 99% accuracy but **0% F1-Score** — correctly exposing its uselessness. Hence, F-Measure is preferred for **imbalanced medical datasets**. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** Explain the **$F_\beta$ Score**. What is the significance of the parameter $\beta$? Compare $F_1$, $F_2$, and $F_{0.5}$ briefly.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **$F_\beta$ Score** is a generalized form of the F-Measure that allows weighting Precision and Recall differently. It is defined as:
>
> $$F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$$
>
> The parameter $\beta$ controls the **relative importance of Recall over Precision**:
> - $\beta = 1$ → Equal weight ($F_1$, balanced)
> - $\beta > 1$ → Recall is weighted more heavily (e.g., $F_2$ for medical diagnosis)
> - $\beta < 1$ → Precision is weighted more heavily (e.g., $F_{0.5}$ for spam filtering)
>
> As $\beta \to \infty$, $F_\beta \to \text{Recall}$; as $\beta \to 0$, $F_\beta \to \text{Precision}$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **ONE full question** (with both sub-parts) out of the two choices provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Q4(b)]`

> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a **Confusion Matrix** for a binary classification problem. Define Precision, Recall, and F-Measure using its entries. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A diagnostic model for detecting a rare disease was tested on 500 patients. Out of these, 50 patients actually have the disease. The model correctly identified 40 disease cases. Additionally, it produced 20 false alarms. Compute the **Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Accuracy**. Comment on the model's performance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

### Part (a) — Confusion Matrix Structure and Definitions (7 marks)

A **Confusion Matrix** is a tabular visualization of a classifier's performance on a test set with known labels. For binary classification:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Actual Positive** | TP (True Positive) | FN (False Negative) |
| **Actual Negative** | FP (False Positive) | TN (True Negative) |

**Definitions from the matrix entries:**

- **Precision** (Positive Predictive Value):

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$$

Measures the accuracy of positive predictions.

- **Recall** (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate):

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$$

Measures the completeness of positive detection.

- **F-Measure** (Harmonic Mean of P and R):

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$$

Provides a single balanced score combining Precision and Recall. **(7 marks for diagram + formulas + explanation)**

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Total patients = 500
- Actual disease cases (positives) = 50
- Actual healthy cases (negatives) = 450
- Correctly identified disease cases (TP) = 40
- False alarms (FP) = 20
- False negatives (FN) = 50 − 40 = 10
- True negatives (TN) = 450 − 20 = 430

**Step 1 — Precision:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
&= \frac{40}{40 + 20} \\
&= \frac{40}{60} \\
&\approx 0.6667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Recall:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
&= \frac{40}{40 + 10} \\
&= \frac{40}{50} \\
&= 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — F1-Score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot 0.6667 \cdot 0.80}{0.6667 + 0.80} \\
&= \frac{1.0667}{1.4667} \\
&\approx 0.7273
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Accuracy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + FN + TN} \\
&= \frac{40 + 430}{500} \\
&= \frac{470}{500} \\
&= 0.94
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result Table:**

| Metric | Value | Interpretation |
|:-------|:-----:|:---------------|
| Precision | 0.6667 | 66.67% of disease flags are correct |
| Recall | 0.8000 | 80% of disease cases are caught |
| **F1-Score** | **0.7273** | **Balanced evaluation score** |
| Accuracy | 0.9400 | 94% (misleadingly high) |

**Comment on Performance:**
The model has **good Recall (0.80)**, meaning it correctly identifies most disease cases. However, its **Precision (0.67)** is moderate — about 1 in 3 alarms is a false alarm. The F1-Score of **0.7273** indicates a **reasonably balanced classifier**, though the false alarm rate could be improved (perhaps by adjusting the classification threshold). The high accuracy of 94% is misleading because the dataset is imbalanced (50 vs 450), and F1-Score provides a more honest assessment. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Alternative Choice]`

> **(a)** Define the **$F_\beta$ Score** mathematically and explain how the parameter $\beta$ controls the trade-off between Precision and Recall. Show that $F_1$ is a special case of $F_\beta$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a text classification system, Precision = 0.75 and Recall = 0.60. Compute the $F_1$, $F_2$, and $F_{0.5}$ scores. State which metric is most appropriate if (i) missing a relevant document is highly costly, and (ii) returning irrelevant documents is highly costly. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

### Part (a) — $F_\beta$ Definition and Special Case Derivation (7 marks)

The **$F_\beta$ Score** is defined as:

$$F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$$

**Trade-off controlled by $\beta$:**

- **$\beta > 1$** → Recall is weighted more heavily. Useful when false negatives (missed positives) are costlier than false positives.
- **$\beta < 1$** → Precision is weighted more heavily. Useful when false positives (false alarms) are costlier.
- **$\beta = 1$** → Equal weight to Precision and Recall — reduces to F1-Score.

**Special Case Proof ($F_1$ is a special case of $F_\beta$):**

Setting $\beta = 1$ in the $F_\beta$ formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= (1 + 1^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{1^2 \cdot P + R} \\
&= \frac{2 P R}{P + R}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is exactly the F1-Score formula. Hence, $F_1$ is a special case of $F_\beta$ with $\beta = 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

### Part (b) — Multi-$F_\beta$ Computation and Application (7 marks)

**Given:** $P = 0.75$, $R = 0.60$

**Step 1 — Compute $F_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot 0.75 \cdot 0.60}{0.75 + 0.60} \\
&= \frac{0.90}{1.35} \\
&\approx 0.6667
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $F_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_2 &= \frac{(1 + 2^2) \cdot 0.75 \cdot 0.60}{2^2 \cdot 0.75 + 0.60} \\
&= \frac{5 \cdot 0.45}{3.00 + 0.60} \\
&= \frac{2.25}{3.60} \\
&= 0.625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $F_{0.5}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{0.5} &= \frac{(1 + 0.5^2) \cdot 0.75 \cdot 0.60}{0.5^2 \cdot 0.75 + 0.60} \\
&= \frac{1.25 \cdot 0.45}{0.1875 + 0.60} \\
&= \frac{0.5625}{0.7875} \\
&\approx 0.7143
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Metric | Value | Weight Bias |
|:-------|:-----:|:------------|
| $F_2$ | 0.6250 | Recall-favored |
| $F_1$ | 0.6667 | Balanced |
| $F_{0.5}$ | 0.7143 | Precision-favored |

**Application Choice:**

(i) **Missing relevant documents is costly** (e.g., legal e-discovery, medical literature search) → Use **$F_2$ Score (0.6250)** — it weights Recall heavily, penalizing the model for missing relevant items.

(ii) **Returning irrelevant documents is costly** (e.g., user-facing search engine, premium content filtering) → Use **$F_{0.5}$ Score (0.7143)** — it weights Precision heavily, penalizing the model for showing irrelevant results. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Reporting only Accuracy** for imbalanced datasets — examiners deduct **2-3 marks** for ignoring F-Measure.
> 2. **Forgetting to state the averaging strategy** (Macro/Micro/Weighted) in multi-class F-Measure questions — a guaranteed **1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Confusing Precision and Recall formulas** — remember: Precision uses FP in the denominator, Recall uses FN.
> 4. **Using arithmetic mean instead of harmonic mean** — examiners specifically look for the harmonic mean derivation. Writing $\frac{P + R}{2}$ will get **zero marks** for F-Measure computation.
> 5. **Not simplifying fractions** — answers like $\frac{2 \cdot 0.6667 \cdot 0.80}{0.6667 + 0.80}$ without a final numerical value lose the final **1 mark**.
> 6. **Ignoring units and ranges** — F-Measure has no unit but is bounded in $[0, 1]$; mention this for full marks.
> 7. **Confusing F-Measure with F-Test** — these are entirely different concepts (statistical F-test vs. evaluation F-Measure).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — F-Measure (Module 4)**

- ✅ **F-Measure** is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall — never the arithmetic mean.
- ✅ The **F1-Score** formula: $F_1 = \frac{2 P R}{P + R} = \frac{2 TP}{2 TP + FP + FN}$
- ✅ **$F_\beta$ generalization**: $F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{P R}{\beta^2 P + R}$
- ✅ **$\beta = 1$** → F1 (balanced); **$\beta > 1$** → Recall-heavy; **$\beta < 1$** → Precision-heavy
- ✅ F1 ranges in **$[0, 1]$**: 1 = perfect, 0 = catastrophic failure on at least one dimension
- ✅ F1 is **bounded above by $\min(P, R)$** — a strict mathematical property
- ✅ **Use F-Measure when:**
    - Classes are **imbalanced** (fraud, medical, anomaly detection)
    - Both **FP and FN costs** matter
    - A **single balanced metric** is needed for model comparison
- ✅ **Do NOT use F-Measure when:**
    - Classes are **balanced** AND cost of FP and FN are equal → Accuracy suffices
    - The application is **multi-label ranking** → use MAP, NDCG instead
- ✅ **Multi-class averaging strategies:** Macro (equal weight), Micro (instance-weighted), Weighted (class-frequency weighted)
- ✅ **F1 collapses to 0** if EITHER Precision = 0 OR Recall = 0 — this is its most important property
- ✅ KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Always quote the **confusion matrix values** explicitly before substituting
- ✅ Examiner pet-topic: **"Why harmonic mean and not arithmetic mean?"** — memorize the $P=1.0, R=0.01$ counter-example

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (ROC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (ROC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) Curve**: A ROC curve is a two-dimensional graphical evaluation plot used in **binary classification** problems, where the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** is plotted on the **Y-axis** and the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** is plotted on the **X-axis**, as the **discrimination threshold** of a classifier is varied from **0 to 1**. It illustrates the trade-off between **sensitivity** and **specificity** across all possible decision thresholds.

The ROC curve was originally developed during **World War II** for the analysis of radar signals — hence the name "Receiver Operating Characteristic." In modern Machine Learning, it is the **standard visual diagnostic tool** for assessing the **separability capability** of any probabilistic binary classifier (e.g., Logistic Regression, SVM with probability output, Random Forest, Neural Networks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Definition to Memorize**: The Area Under the ROC Curve (**AUC**) represents the **probability** that a randomly chosen **positive instance** is ranked higher by the classifier than a randomly chosen **negative instance**. An AUC of **1.0** denotes a **perfect classifier**, while an AUC of **0.5** represents a **random guess** (equivalent to the diagonal line $y = x$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **customs officer at an international airport** whose job is to identify potential **smugglers (positives)** from among thousands of **innocent travelers (negatives)**. You have a suspicion meter (the classifier) that gives every passenger a score from 0 to 1.

- If you set your **threshold very low** (e.g., 0.1) — you catch *every* smuggler, but you also wrongly flag many innocent travelers (**high TPR**, **high FPR**).
- If you set your **threshold very high** (e.g., 0.95) — you barely bother anyone, but you miss most smugglers (**low TPR**, **low FPR**).

The **ROC curve** is the complete map of *all* possible trade-offs you can make by sliding that threshold from 0 to 1. The **better** your suspicion meter, the more the curve **bows toward the top-left corner** of the plot. The **AUC** is essentially a single number summarizing how good your "sniff test" is — the larger, the better.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics Referenced by the ROC

| Symbol | Full Name | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **TP** | True Positives | Correctly predicted positive cases |
| **FP** | False Positives | Incorrectly predicted positive cases (Type I Error) |
| **TN** | True Negatives | Correctly predicted negative cases |
| **FN** | False Negatives | Missed positive cases (Type II Error) |
| **TPR** | True Positive Rate | Sensitivity, Recall, Hit Rate |
| **FPR** | False Positive Rate | Fall-out, 1 - Specificity |
| **AUC** | Area Under Curve | Threshold-invariant performance index |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ROC and not just Accuracy?** Accuracy fails on **imbalanced datasets** (e.g., fraud detection where 99.9% of transactions are legitimate). A classifier predicting "no fraud" always achieves 99.9% accuracy but is useless. The ROC curve, through TPR and FPR, isolates classifier performance **independent of class prevalence** and the specific operating threshold.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Building Blocks: Confusion Matrix Revisited

The ROC curve is built on top of the **2×2 Confusion Matrix**, which tabulates classifier predictions against ground-truth labels for a binary problem.

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | TP | FN |
| **Actual Negative** | FP | TN |

## 2.2 The Two Axes of the ROC Plot — Operational Meaning

The ROC curve sweeps the **decision threshold** $\tau \in [0, 1]$ and records two values at each $\tau$:

**Step 1 — Classify by threshold:**
For every instance $i$ with predicted probability $\hat{p}_i$:
$$\text{Prediction} = \begin{cases} \text{Positive} & \text{if } \hat{p}_i \geq \tau \\ \text{Negative} & \text{if } \hat{p}_i < \tau \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Count TP, FP, TN, FN at threshold $\tau$.**

**Step 3 — Compute the two axis quantities:**

$$\text{TPR}(\tau) = \frac{TP(\tau)}{TP(\tau) + FN(\tau)} = \text{Sensitivity} = \text{Recall}$$

$$\text{FPR}(\tau) = \frac{FP(\tau)}{FP(\tau) + TN(\tau)} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$$

**Step 4 — Plot the point $\big(\text{FPR}(\tau),\ \text{TPR}(\tau)\big)$ on the 2D plane.**

**Step 5 — Repeat for every candidate $\tau$ (in practice, one per unique score in the dataset) and connect the points.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Diagonal Reference Line**: The line $y = x$ (from $(0,0)$ to $(1,1)$) represents a **random classifier**. Any curve **strictly above** this diagonal indicates a classifier performing **better than random**. A curve **below** the diagonal indicates that the labels are systematically **inverted** (the classifier is anti-correlated with truth).

## 2.3 AUC — The Single-Number Performance Summary

The Area Under the ROC Curve is computed as the **integral of TPR with respect to FPR** over the full range:

$$\text{AUC} = \int_{0}^{1} \text{TPR}(\text{FPR})\ d(\text{FPR})$$

A practical rank-based estimator (used by scikit-learn, etc.) is the **Mann-Whitney U statistic**:

$$\text{AUC} = \frac{\sum_{i \in \text{Pos}} \sum_{j \in \text{Neg}} \mathbf{1}\!\left[\hat{p}_i > \hat{p}_j\right]}{N_{\text{Pos}} \cdot N_{\text{Neg}}}$$

where $\mathbf{1}[\cdot]$ is the indicator function, $N_{\text{Pos}}$ is the number of positive samples, and $N_{\text{Neg}}$ is the number of negative samples.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sensitivity (TPR / Recall)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of actual positives correctly identified |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\dfrac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of actual negatives correctly identified |
| **Fall-out (FPR)** | $\dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Fraction of actual negatives wrongly flagged |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Of those flagged positive, how many are truly positive |
| **F1-Score** | $\dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall |
| **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | Total correct predictions (misleading on imbalanced data) |
| **Youden's J Statistic** | $\text{TPR} + \text{TNR} - 1$ | $[0, 1]$ | Optimal threshold criterion: $\tau^* = \arg\max_\tau J(\tau)$ |
| **AUC** | $\int_0^1 \text{TPR}(\text{FPR})\ d(\text{FPR})$ | $[0, 1]$ | Threshold-invariant ranking quality |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule**: In all written exam answers, use the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\text{AUC} = \mid \int \ldots \mid$) instead of the bare pipe character `|`, as the KTU digital answer-sheet parser will mis-render the latter.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of the ROC Curve

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Compare competing diagnostic tests (e.g., cancer screening models) regardless of disease prevalence |
| **Credit Risk Scoring** | Evaluate loan default classifiers without retuning thresholds for every economic cycle |
| **Anomaly / Intrusion Detection** | Quantify detection capability under extreme class imbalance (attacks $\ll$ normal traffic) |
| **Information Retrieval** | Compare ranking models in search engines via AUC as a single quality index |
| **Spam Filtering** | Trade-off between blocking legitimate mail vs. missing spam |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix Worked Example — Constructing a ROC Curve by Hand

This is the **exact procedure** the KTU examiner expects you to write on paper. We use a tiny dataset so all arithmetic is fully visible.

**Given (Test Set, Ground Truth Labels, and Classifier Probabilities):**

| Instance | True Label $y_i$ | Predicted Probability $\hat{p}_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | Positive (1) | 0.92 |
| B | Negative (0) | 0.85 |
| C | Positive (1) | 0.78 |
| D | Negative (0) | 0.60 |
| E | Positive (1) | 0.40 |
| F | Negative (0) | 0.15 |
| G | Positive (1) | 0.10 |

**Totals:** $P = 4$ positives (A, C, E, G), $N = 3$ negatives (B, D, F).

**Step 1 — Sort by descending predicted probability** (this is the canonical ROC construction order):

| Rank | Instance | $\hat{p}_i$ | True Label |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | 0.92 | 1 |
| 2 | B | 0.85 | 0 |
| 3 | C | 0.78 | 1 |
| 4 | D | 0.60 | 0 |
| 5 | E | 0.40 | 1 |
| 6 | F | 0.15 | 0 |
| 7 | G | 0.10 | 1 |

**Step 2 — Add the standard $(0, 0)$ starting point** (threshold $> \max(\hat{p})$, so nothing is classified positive yet):

| Point | $\tau$ | Cumulative TP | Cumulative FP | TPR = TP/4 | FPR = FP/3 | (FPR, TPR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Start | 1.00 | 0 | 0 | 0/4 = 0.00 | 0/3 = 0.00 | **(0.00, 0.00)** |
| After A | 0.92 | 1 | 0 | 1/4 = 0.25 | 0/3 = 0.00 | **(0.00, 0.25)** |
| After B | 0.85 | 1 | 1 | 1/4 = 0.25 | 1/3 ≈ 0.33 | **(0.33, 0.25)** |
| After C | 0.78 | 2 | 1 | 2/4 = 0.50 | 1/3 ≈ 0.33 | **(0.33, 0.50)** |
| After D | 0.60 | 2 | 2 | 2/4 = 0.50 | 2/3 ≈ 0.67 | **(0.67, 0.50)** |
| After E | 0.40 | 3 | 2 | 3/4 = 0.75 | 2/3 ≈ 0.67 | **(0.67, 0.75)** |
| After F | 0.15 | 3 | 3 | 3/4 = 0.75 | 3/3 = 1.00 | **(1.00, 0.75)** |
| After G | 0.10 | 4 | 3 | 4/4 = 1.00 | 3/3 = 1.00 | **(1.00, 1.00)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The staircase rule**: Each true positive causes a **vertical step up** (TPR increases, FPR fixed), and each false positive causes a **horizontal step right** (FPR increases, TPR fixed). The plot therefore looks like a **staircase** rising from $(0,0)$ to $(1,1)$.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step AUC Derivation (Trapezoidal Rule)

For the dataset above, the AUC is the **sum of trapezoid areas** under the staircase.

**Step 1 — Identify the trapezoids** between consecutive points along the FPR axis:

| Trapezoid | FPR span $\Delta x$ | Average TPR (height) | Area |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_1$ | $0.00 \to 0.00$ | $(0.00 + 0.25)/2 = 0.125$ | $0 \cdot 0.125 = 0.000$ |
| $T_2$ | $0.00 \to 0.33$ | $(0.25 + 0.25)/2 = 0.250$ | $0.333 \cdot 0.250 \approx 0.0833$ |
| $T_3$ | $0.33 \to 0.33$ | $(0.25 + 0.50)/2 = 0.375$ | $0 \cdot 0.375 = 0.000$ |
| $T_4$ | $0.33 \to 0.67$ | $(0.50 + 0.50)/2 = 0.500$ | $0.333 \cdot 0.500 \approx 0.1667$ |
| $T_5$ | $0.67 \to 0.67$ | $(0.50 + 0.75)/2 = 0.625$ | $0 \cdot 0.625 = 0.000$ |
| $T_6$ | $0.67 \to 1.00$ | $(0.75 + 0.75)/2 = 0.750$ | $0.333 \cdot 0.750 \approx 0.2500$ |
| $T_7$ | $1.00 \to 1.00$ | $(0.75 + 1.00)/2 = 0.875$ | $0 \cdot 0.875 = 0.000$ |

**Step 2 — Sum the areas:**

$$\text{AUC} = \sum_{k=1}^{7} T_k = 0 + 0.0833 + 0 + 0.1667 + 0 + 0.2500 + 0$$

$$\boxed{\text{AUC} \approx 0.5000}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> An AUC of **0.5** here confirms the classifier is barely better than random on this tiny toy set — it correctly ranks 2 of the 4 positives above their paired negatives. With more data and a stronger model, the curve bows toward the top-left and AUC approaches 1.

## 3.3 Optimal Threshold via Youden's J Statistic

Youden's J balances sensitivity and specificity by maximizing the vertical distance from the diagonal:

$$J(\tau) = \text{TPR}(\tau) + \text{TNR}(\tau) - 1 = \text{TPR}(\tau) - \text{FPR}(\tau)$$

The optimal threshold is:

$$\tau^{*} = \arg\max_{\tau} J(\tau)$$

For our table above, $J$ at each unique threshold is:

| Threshold $\tau$ | TPR | FPR | $J = \text{TPR} - \text{FPR}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $> 0.92$ | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 0.92 | 0.25 | 0.00 | **0.25** |
| 0.85 | 0.25 | 0.33 | -0.08 |
| 0.78 | 0.50 | 0.33 | 0.17 |
| 0.60 | 0.50 | 0.67 | -0.17 |
| 0.40 | 0.75 | 0.67 | 0.08 |
| 0.15 | 0.75 | 1.00 | -0.25 |
| $\leq 0.10$ | 1.00 | 1.00 | 0.00 |

$$\boxed{\tau^{*} = 0.92, \quad J_{\max} = 0.25}$$

This matches our KTU intuition: at $\tau = 0.92$, the model captures 25% of positives with zero false alarms — the cleanest operating point on this small dataset.

## 3.4 Proof of Equivalence: AUC = P(score(+) > score(-))

**Statement**: For a score function $s(\mathbf{x})$, the AUC equals the probability that a randomly drawn positive instance is ranked above a randomly drawn negative instance.

**Derivation:**

$$\text{AUC} = \int_{0}^{1} \text{TPR}(\text{FPR})\, d(\text{FPR})$$

Substitute the rank-based definition. Let $s^{+}$ be a random positive score and $s^{-}$ a random negative score. Define the indicator $\mathbf{1}[s^{+} > s^{-}]$. The expectation over independent draws is:

$$\mathbb{P}(s^{+} > s^{-}) = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\mathbf{1}[s^{+} > s^{-}]\right] = \frac{1}{N_{+} N_{-}} \sum_{i \in \text{Pos}} \sum_{j \in \text{Neg}} \mathbf{1}\!\left[\hat{p}_i > \hat{p}_j\right]$$

This expectation is mathematically identical to the area under the ROC curve. **QED.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ROC Curve Construction — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **algorithmic pipeline** used to construct a ROC curve from a trained classifier and a labelled test set.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trained Classifier with Probabilities] --> B[Load Test Set with True Labels]
    B --> C[Step 1: Sort Instances by Descending Score]
    C --> D[Step 2: Initialize Cumulative TP and FP to Zero]
    D --> E[Step 3: For Each Instance in Sorted Order]
    E --> F{Is True Label Positive?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Increment Cumulative TP]
    F -- No --> H[Increment Cumulative FP]
    G --> I[Compute TPR equals TP over P]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Compute FPR equals FP over N]
    J --> K[Append Point FPR, TPR to Curve]
    K --> L{More Instances?}
    L -- Yes --> E
    L -- No --> M[Step 4: Connect All Points into Staircase]
    M --> N[Step 5: Compute AUC via Trapezoidal Integration]
    N --> O[Step 6: Find Optimal Tau via Youden J Statistic]
    O --> P[Final Output: ROC Curve, AUC, Optimal Threshold]
```

## 4.2 Classifier Performance Comparison — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input]
        X[Independent Test Set]
    end
    subgraph MODELS[Candidate Classifiers]
        M1[Model A: Logistic Regression]
        M2[Model B: Random Forest]
        M3[Model C: SVM with RBF Kernel]
    end
    subgraph METRICS[Per-Model Probability Scores]
        S1[Sorted Score List A]
        S2[Sorted Score List B]
        S3[Sorted Score List C]
    end
    subgraph ROCGEN[ROC Construction Engine]
        R1[ROC Curve A]
        R2[ROC Curve B]
        R3[ROC Curve C]
    end
    subgraph COMPARE[Comparison Layer]
        AUC1[AUC_A]
        AUC2[AUC_B]
        AUC3[AUC_C]
        BEST[Best Model equals argmax AUC]
    end
    X --> M1 --> S1 --> R1 --> AUC1
    X --> M2 --> S2 --> R2 --> AUC2
    X --> M3 --> S3 --> R3 --> AUC3
    AUC1 --> BEST
    AUC2 --> BEST
    AUC3 --> BEST
```

## 4.3 Decision Threshold Sweep — Operating Point Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Threshold Tau Sweep] --> T1[Tau equals 0.0: Predict All Positive]
    T --> T2[Tau equals 0.5: Default]
    T --> T3[Tau equals 1.0: Predict All Negative]
    T1 --> R1[TPR equals 1, FPR equals 1: Top Right Corner]
    T2 --> R2[Mid Point on Curve]
    T3 --> R3[TPR equals 0, FPR equals 0: Origin]
    R1 --> DIAG[Diagonal: Random Classifier]
    R2 --> DIAG
    R3 --> DIAG
    DIAG --> OPT[Optimal Point: Maximize Youden J]
    OPT --> OUT[Selected Tau Star]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Mermaid diagrams**: The blocks labelled `INPUT`, `MODELS`, etc. are **subgraph clusters**. Every node ID is purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `M1`, `R2`, `BEST`) — never a reserved keyword — as required by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]**
**Define the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve. What does the area under the ROC curve (AUC) represent?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The **ROC curve** is a two-dimensional plot in which the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** is plotted on the Y-axis against the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the X-axis as the decision threshold of a binary classifier is varied from 0 to 1.
>
> The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** is a scalar value in $[0, 1]$ that measures the **overall ranking quality** of the classifier. It is mathematically equivalent to the probability that a randomly chosen positive instance is ranked higher than a randomly chosen negative instance by the classifier. An AUC of **1.0** indicates a **perfect classifier**, while **0.5** indicates performance equivalent to **random guessing**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model Question Bank]**
**Differentiate between TPR and FPR in the context of ROC analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **True Positive Rate (TPR)** is also known as **Sensitivity** or **Recall** and measures the fraction of actual positive instances correctly identified by the classifier. It is computed as $TPR = \dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$.
>
> **False Positive Rate (FPR)** is also known as the **Fall-out** and measures the fraction of actual negative instances that are incorrectly classified as positive. It is computed as $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$.
>
> The ROC curve plots TPR against FPR to illustrate the trade-off between **catching positives (TPR)** and **avoiding false alarms (FPR)** as the threshold varies.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction of a ROC curve. Discuss the significance of the area under the ROC curve (AUC) in evaluating classifier performance.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Explain the step-by-step procedure to construct a ROC curve from a binary classifier's output.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Obtain Probabilities**: Run the trained classifier on a labelled test set to obtain predicted probabilities $\hat{p}_i$ for every instance.
2. **Sort Descending**: Sort all instances in **decreasing order** of $\hat{p}_i$.
3. **Initialize Counters**: Set cumulative $TP = 0$ and cumulative $FP = 0$. Let $P$ = total positives, $N$ = total negatives in the test set.
4. **Sweep Threshold**: Starting at the top of the sorted list, for each instance:
   - If the true label is **positive** → increment $TP$.
   - If the true label is **negative** → increment $FP$.
5. **Record Point**: At each step, record the point $\left(\dfrac{FP}{N},\ \dfrac{TP}{P}\right)$.
6. **Plot & Connect**: Plot all points and connect them to form a **staircase** from $(0,0)$ to $(1,1)$.
7. **Sketch**: The curve bows toward the **top-left corner** for good classifiers; a random classifier lies along the diagonal $y = x$.

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**What is AUC? Discuss its significance and interpretation.**

**Model Solution:**

The **Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)** is the integral of TPR with respect to FPR over the full range:

$$\text{AUC} = \int_{0}^{1} \text{TPR}(\text{FPR})\, d(\text{FPR})$$

**Significance:**

1. **Threshold-Independent**: AUC summarises classifier performance across **all possible thresholds**, removing the need to commit to a single operating point.
2. **Ranking Interpretation**: AUC equals the probability $P(\hat{p}_{+} > \hat{p}_{-})$ — i.e., the likelihood that the model ranks a random positive above a random negative.
3. **Robust to Class Imbalance**: Unlike accuracy, AUC is **invariant to class prior** changes, making it the preferred metric for imbalanced datasets.
4. **Discrimination Index**:
   - $\text{AUC} = 1.0$ → **Perfect** classifier.
   - $0.9 \leq \text{AUC} < 1.0$ → **Excellent**.
   - $0.8 \leq \text{AUC} < 0.9$ → **Good**.
   - $0.7 \leq \text{AUC} < 0.8$ → **Fair**.
   - $0.5 \leq \text{AUC} < 0.7$ → **Poor**.
   - $\text{AUC} = 0.5$ → **Random**.
5. **Model Comparison**: The classifier with the **largest AUC** is preferred when comparing multiple models on the same test set.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Adapted]** For a binary classification problem, the following predictions and true labels are given:

| Instance | True Label $y_i$ | Predicted Probability $\hat{p}_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0.95 |
| 2 | 0 | 0.80 |
| 3 | 1 | 0.70 |
| 4 | 0 | 0.55 |
| 5 | 1 | 0.40 |
| 6 | 0 | 0.30 |
| 7 | 1 | 0.20 |

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**Plot the ROC curve and compute the AUC using the trapezoidal rule.**

**Model Solution:**

Total positives $P = 4$ (instances 1, 3, 5, 7), total negatives $N = 3$ (instances 2, 4, 6).

**Sorted by descending score** (already in this order). Sweep:

| Threshold $\tau$ | Cumulative TP | Cumulative FP | TPR = TP/4 | FPR = FP/3 | (FPR, TPR) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $> 0.95$ | 0 | 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | (0.00, 0.00) |
| 0.95 | 1 | 0 | 0.25 | 0.00 | (0.00, 0.25) |
| 0.80 | 1 | 1 | 0.25 | 0.33 | (0.33, 0.25) |
| 0.70 | 2 | 1 | 0.50 | 0.33 | (0.33, 0.50) |
| 0.55 | 2 | 2 | 0.50 | 0.67 | (0.67, 0.50) |
| 0.40 | 3 | 2 | 0.75 | 0.67 | (0.67, 0.75) |
| 0.30 | 3 | 3 | 0.75 | 1.00 | (1.00, 0.75) |
| $\leq 0.20$ | 4 | 3 | 1.00 | 1.00 | (1.00, 1.00) |

**AUC via trapezoidal rule:**

$$\text{AUC} = (0)(0.125) + (0.333)(0.250) + (0)(0.375) + (0.333)(0.500) + (0)(0.625) + (0.333)(0.750) + (0)(0.875)$$

$$\text{AUC} = 0 + 0.0833 + 0 + 0.1667 + 0 + 0.2500 + 0 = 0.5000$$

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks:**
**Find the optimal threshold using Youden's J statistic. Comment on classifier performance.**

**Model Solution:**

Youden's $J(\tau) = \text{TPR}(\tau) - \text{FPR}(\tau)$ at each unique threshold:

| $\tau$ | TPR | FPR | $J$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.95 | 0.25 | 0.00 | **0.25** |
| 0.80 | 0.25 | 0.33 | -0.08 |
| 0.70 | 0.50 | 0.33 | 0.17 |
| 0.55 | 0.50 | 0.67 | -0.17 |
| 0.40 | 0.75 | 0.67 | 0.08 |
| 0.30 | 0.75 | 1.00 | -0.25 |
| 0.20 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 0.00 |

$$\boxed{\tau^{*} = 0.95, \quad J_{\max} = 0.25}$$

**Comment**: With AUC = 0.5, the classifier performs **no better than random guessing** on this small dataset. The optimal threshold $\tau = 0.95$ captures 25% of positives with **zero false alarms**, but the model is clearly **under-trained** or the **features are weak**. In practice, one would engineer new features, tune hyperparameters, or collect more data.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing ROC axes**: Writing "ROC plots accuracy vs. threshold" is a **common 2-mark blunder**. The axes are **TPR (Y)** and **FPR (X)** — memorize this.
> 2. **Forgetting to sort descending**: The ROC construction **must** begin by sorting scores in **descending** order. Without sorting, the staircase is meaningless.
> 3. **Using accuracy on imbalanced data**: Examiners explicitly test whether you know that accuracy is **misleading** under class imbalance and that AUC is preferred.
> 4. **Mis-computing TPR/FPR denominators**: TPR uses $TP + FN = P$ (total positives), and FPR uses $FP + TN = N$ (total negatives). Mixing up the denominators costs a full sub-part.
> 5. **Not drawing the diagonal $y = x$**: Always include the **random-guess diagonal** in your ROC sketch — the examiner awards 1 mark for this reference line.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **ROC curve** = plot of **TPR (Y-axis)** vs. **FPR (X-axis)** as the threshold $\tau$ sweeps from $0$ to $1$.
- ✅ **TPR** = Sensitivity = Recall = $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$.
- ✅ **FPR** = $1 -$ Specificity = $\dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$.
- ✅ Construction = **sort by score → sweep threshold → record $(FPR, TPR)$ → connect as staircase**.
- ✅ **AUC** $\in [0, 1]$; **1** = perfect, **0.5** = random, **< 0.5** = inverted.
- ✅ AUC = $P(\text{positive score} > \text{negative score})$ — the **Mann-Whitney U** interpretation.
- ✅ **AUC is threshold-invariant** and **robust to class imbalance** — preferred over accuracy for skewed datasets.
- ✅ **Youden's J** = $\text{TPR} - \text{FPR}$; optimal $\tau^{*} = \arg\max J(\tau)$.
- ✅ The **diagonal line $y = x$** is the **random-classifier baseline**; curves above it are useful, curves below it indicate inverted predictions.
- ✅ **Comparison rule**: When comparing classifiers, the one with the **larger AUC** is the better ranker **only if their ROC curves do not cross**. If they cross, prefer the curve with the larger AUC in the **operationally relevant FPR region**.
- ✅ **Precision-Recall (PR) curves** are preferred over ROC when the **positive class is rare** AND the focus is on positive-class performance (since PR curves are sensitive to class prevalence, unlike ROC).
- ✅ KTU 2024 expects you to: (1) **derive** the staircase construction, (2) **compute** AUC via trapezoidal rule, (3) **find** the optimal threshold using Youden's J, and (4) **interpret** the curve for real engineering use-cases.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Area Under Curve (AUC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET495 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Area Under Curve (AUC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Area Under the Curve (AUC)** in machine learning refers to the area beneath the **Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve**, which is a fundamental evaluation metric for **binary classification models**. The ROC curve plots the **True Positive Rate (TPR)** on the y-axis against the **False Positive Rate (FPR)** on the x-axis at various classification threshold settings.

$$AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$$

The AUC value quantifies the **overall discriminative ability** of a classifier across all possible decision thresholds, providing a single scalar measure of performance independent of any specific threshold choice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** AUC represents the probability that a randomly selected positive instance will be ranked higher by the classifier than a randomly selected negative instance. It is a threshold-invariant, rank-based performance metric.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **medical diagnostic test** that needs to distinguish between **diseased patients (positives)** and **healthy individuals (negatives)**. A perfect test would rank every diseased patient ahead of every healthy person on a risk score. **AUC = 1.0** means this perfect separation is achieved.

If the test were no better than a **coin flip** (random guessing), it would rank a positive ahead of a negative only 50% of the time, giving **AUC = 0.5**.

Think of AUC as a **"discrimination scorecard"**:
- A student taking a competitive exam where every correct-answer scorer (positive) is ranked above every incorrect-answer scorer (negative) gets **AUC = 1.0**.
- A student who ranks them randomly gets **AUC = 0.5**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Independence:** Unlike accuracy, precision, or recall (which depend on a chosen cutoff), AUC evaluates the model's **inherent ranking capability**, making it robust to class imbalance and threshold selection.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Summary

| Metric | Full Name | Formula | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TPR** | True Positive Rate (Recall/Sensitivity) | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | [0, 1] |
| **FPR** | False Positive Rate (1 - Specificity) | $\frac{FP}{FP+TN}$ | [0, 1] |
| **AUC** | Area Under ROC Curve | $\int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$ | [0, 1] |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The ROC Curve — Construction Logic

The ROC curve is constructed by systematically varying the **classification threshold** $t$ of a model that outputs continuous probability scores:

**Step 1:** Train a probabilistic classifier (e.g., Logistic Regression) producing scores $s_i \in [0,1]$ for each instance.

**Step 2:** Sort all instances by descending score.

**Step 3:** Sweep threshold $t$ from $\infty$ (every prediction is negative) down to $-\infty$ (every prediction is positive).

**Step 4:** At each threshold, compute TPR and FPR.

**Step 5:** Plot FPR on x-axis, TPR on y-axis. Connect points to form the ROC curve.

### 2.2 AUC Interpretation Levels

**Geometric Interpretation:** AUC is the literal geometric area under the ROC curve in the unit square $[0,1] \times [0,1]$.

**Probabilistic Interpretation:** For a randomly chosen pair of (positive, negative) instances, AUC equals the probability that the classifier assigns a higher score to the positive instance.

**Statistical Interpretation:** AUC is equivalent to the normalized **Mann-Whitney U statistic**:

$$AUC = \frac{U}{n_+ \cdot n_-}$$

where $n_+$ is the number of positives and $n_-$ is the number of negatives.

### 2.3 Why AUC Matters — Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "How" and "Why" Matter:**
> - **Threshold-invariant:** One number summarizes performance across all operating points.
> - **Imbalance-robust:** In heavily skewed datasets (e.g., fraud detection where 99.9% are legitimate), accuracy is misleading but AUC remains meaningful.
> - **Calibration-free:** AUC depends only on the ranking order of predictions, not on the absolute probability values.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| $TPR$ | True Positive Rate (Sensitivity, Recall) | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ |
| $FPR$ | False Positive Rate | $\frac{FP}{FP+TN}$ |
| $TNR$ | True Negative Rate (Specificity) | $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ |
| $FNR$ | False Negative Rate (Miss Rate) | $\frac{FN}{FN+TP}$ |
| $PPV$ | Positive Predictive Value (Precision) | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ |
| $AUC$ | Area Under ROC Curve | $\int_{0}^{1} TPR \, d(FPR)$ |
| $U$ | Mann-Whitney U Statistic | $\sum_{i=1}^{n_+} R_i - \frac{n_+(n_+ + 1)}{2}$ |
| $n_+, n_-$ | Count of positives, negatives | Given by confusion matrix |

### 2.5 AUC Interpretation Table

| AUC Value | Classifier Quality | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $AUC = 1.0$ | **Perfect** | All positives ranked above all negatives |
| $0.9 \le AUC < 1.0$ | **Excellent** | Strong discrimination |
| $0.8 \le AUC < 0.9$ | **Good** | Reliable classifier |
| $0.7 \le AUC < 0.8$ | **Fair** | Acceptable but weak |
| $0.5 \le AUC < 0.7$ | **Poor** | Limited practical value |
| $AUC = 0.5$ | **Random** | Equivalent to coin flip |
| $AUC < 0.5$ | **Worse than random** | Invert predictions to improve |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Medical Diagnostics:** Comparing diagnostic tests for diseases (cancer screening, COVID-19 testing).
- **Credit Scoring:** Ranking loan applicants by default probability.
- **Spam Detection:** Evaluating email classifiers under varying threshold strictness.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Industrial fault detection with rare positive events.
- **Information Retrieval:** Ranking document relevance where AUC is closely related to **Average Precision (AP)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: AUC from Mann-Whitney U Statistic

**Problem Setup:** Given $n_+$ positive instances and $n_-$ negative instances with classifier scores, prove:

$$AUC = \frac{U}{n_+ \cdot n_-}$$

where $U$ counts the number of discordant pairs (positive score < negative score).

**Step 1 — Define the Ranking Function**

Assign ranks $R_i$ to all $N = n_+ + n_-$ instances when sorted in descending order of classifier score. The sum of all ranks is:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i = \frac{N(N+1)}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Express $R_+$ (Sum of Positive Ranks)**

Let $R_+ = \sum_{i \in \text{positives}} R_i$ denote the sum of ranks assigned to positive instances.

**Step 3 — Count Concordant Pairs**

A pair (positive, negative) is **concordant** if the positive is ranked higher. The number of concordant pairs is:

$$C = R_+ - \frac{n_+(n_+ + 1)}{2}$$

> **Logic:** Among the $R_+$ total rank positions occupied by positives, the first positive contributes $n_+ - 1$ concordant pairs (vs other positives, excluded), the second contributes $n_+ - 2$, etc. Subtracting the within-class pairs leaves only cross-class concordant pairs.

**Step 4 — Total Number of Positive-Negative Pairs**

$$N_{\text{pairs}} = n_+ \cdot n_-$$

**Step 5 — Define Mann-Whitney U for Negatives**

By symmetry, the number of discordant pairs is:

$$D = R_- - \frac{n_-(n_- + 1)}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Establish the AUC Identity**

Since every pair is either concordant or discordant:

$$C + D = n_+ \cdot n_-$$

The AUC is defined as the fraction of concordant pairs:

$$AUC = \frac{C}{n_+ \cdot n_-}$$

**Step 7 — Final Closed Form**

Substituting $C = R_+ - \frac{n_+(n_+ + 1)}{2}$:

$$\boxed{AUC = \frac{R_+ - \frac{n_+(n_+ + 1)}{2}}{n_+ \cdot n_-} = \frac{U}{n_+ \cdot n_-}}$$

This is the **Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney equivalence**, which is the standard rank-based formulation used in non-parametric statistics.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Computing AUC Manually

**Given Data:**

| Instance | True Label | Classifier Score |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 1 (Positive) | 0.92 |
| $P_2$ | 1 (Positive) | 0.78 |
| $P_3$ | 1 (Positive) | 0.61 |
| $N_1$ | 0 (Negative) | 0.85 |
| $N_2$ | 0 (Negative) | 0.55 |
| $N_3$ | 0 (Negative) | 0.43 |

**Step 1 — Sort by Score (Descending)**

| Rank | Instance | Label | Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_1$ | +1 | 0.92 |
| 2 | $N_1$ | 0 | 0.85 |
| 3 | $P_2$ | +1 | 0.78 |
| 4 | $P_3$ | +1 | 0.61 |
| 5 | $N_2$ | 0 | 0.55 |
| 6 | $N_3$ | 0 | 0.43 |

**Step 2 — Count Concordant Pairs**

We have $n_+ = 3$ positives, $n_- = 3$ negatives, so $3 \times 3 = 9$ total pairs.

List all positive-negative pairs and check concordance (positive score > negative score):

- $(P_1, N_1)$: $0.92 > 0.85$ → **Concordant** ✓
- $(P_1, N_2)$: $0.92 > 0.55$ → **Concordant** ✓
- $(P_1, N_3)$: $0.92 > 0.43$ → **Concordant** ✓
- $(P_2, N_1)$: $0.78 < 0.85$ → **Discordant** ✗
- $(P_2, N_2)$: $0.78 > 0.55$ → **Concordant** ✓
- $(P_2, N_3)$: $0.78 > 0.43$ → **Concordant** ✓
- $(P_3, N_1)$: $0.61 < 0.85$ → **Discordant** ✗
- $(P_3, N_2)$: $0.61 > 0.55$ → **Concordant** ✓
- $(P_3, N_3)$: $0.61 > 0.43$ → **Concordant** ✓

**Step 3 — Compute AUC**

$$AUC = \frac{\text{Concordant Pairs}}{\text{Total Pairs}} = \frac{7}{9} \approx 0.778$$

**Step 4 — Verify Using Mann-Whitney Formula**

$R_+ = 1 + 3 + 4 = 8$ (sum of ranks of positives)

$$U = R_+ - \frac{n_+(n_+ + 1)}{2} = 8 - \frac{3 \times 4}{2} = 8 - 6 = 2$$

$$AUC = \frac{U}{n_+ \cdot n_-} = \frac{2}{9} \approx 0.222$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Discrepancy Note:** $U$ in the classical Mann-Whitney statistic counts *discordant* pairs, while the ROC-AUC counts *concordant* pairs. The relationship is:
> $$AUC = 1 - \frac{U_{\text{discordant}}}{n_+ \cdot n_-} = 1 - \frac{2}{9} = \frac{7}{9} \approx 0.778$$
> Both formulations are equivalent and widely used.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step ROC Curve Construction (Trapezoidal Rule)

The AUC can be computed numerically using the **trapezoidal rule** over sorted (FPR, TPR) points:

$$AUC = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \frac{(TPR_{i+1} + TPR_i)}{2} \cdot (FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)$$

where $k$ is the number of threshold points sorted by increasing FPR.

**Logic:** The trapezoidal rule approximates the integral $\int TPR \, d(FPR)$ by summing the area of trapezoids formed between consecutive ROC points. Each trapezoid has parallel sides $TPR_i$ and $TPR_{i+1}$ with width $(FPR_{i+1} - FPR_i)$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: From Classifier Scores to AUC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Trained Binary Classifier] --> B[Generate Probability Scores]
    B --> C[Sort Instances by Score Descending]
    C --> D[Sweep Threshold t from 1.0 to 0.0]
    D --> E[At Each t Compute TP, FP, TN, FN]
    E --> F[Calculate TPR and FPR]
    F --> G[Plot ROC Points FPR vs TPR]
    G --> H[Connect Points to Form ROC Curve]
    H --> I[Apply Trapezoidal Integration]
    I --> J[Scalar AUC Value 0 to 1]
    J --> K{AUC Interpretation}
    K --> K1[AUC = 1.0 Perfect]
    K --> K2[AUC 0.8 to 1.0 Excellent]
    K --> K3[AUC 0.5 Random Baseline]
    K --> K4[AUC less than 0.5 Invert Predictions]
```
