## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

Define the term **Area Under Curve (AUC)** in the context of evaluating a binary classifier. What does an AUC value of 0.5 indicate?

**Model Answer:**

The **Area Under the Curve (AUC)** refers to the area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve, which plots the True Positive Rate (TPR) against the False Positive Rate (FPR) at various classification thresholds. It is a threshold-independent scalar measure ranging from 0 to 1.

$$AUC = \int_{0}^{1} TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$$

An **AUC value of 0.5** indicates that the classifier performs **no better than random guessing** (equivalent to a coin flip). The model has no discriminative power between the positive and negative classes.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

State the **probabilistic interpretation** of AUC.

**Model Answer:**

The probabilistic interpretation of AUC states that for a randomly chosen pair consisting of one positive and one negative instance, **AUC equals the probability that the classifier will assign a higher score to the positive instance** than to the negative instance. Formally:

$$AUC = P(\text{score}(x^+) > \text{score}(x^-)$$

where $x^+$ is a random positive instance and $x^-$ is a random negative instance. This is also known as the **Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney** rank statistic interpretation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 4, Q Choice 1]**

**(a)** Explain the construction of the ROC curve for a binary classifier in detail. Discuss how True Positive Rate (TPR) and False Positive Rate (FPR) are computed at each threshold. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a binary classification problem, the classifier scores for 8 instances are given below:

| Instance | True Class | Score |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Positive | 0.95 |
| 2 | Positive | 0.81 |
| 3 | Positive | 0.60 |
| 4 | Negative | 0.70 |
| 5 | Negative | 0.55 |
| 6 | Positive | 0.40 |
| 7 | Negative | 0.30 |
| 8 | Negative | 0.10 |

Compute the **AUC** of this classifier using the Mann-Whitney U statistic. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Construction Steps of the ROC Curve:**

**Step 1 — Obtain Probability Scores**

A trained probabilistic classifier (e.g., Logistic Regression) produces a continuous score $s_i \in [0,1]$ for each instance, representing the estimated probability of the positive class.

**Step 2 — Define the Classification Rule**

An instance is classified as positive if its score exceeds a threshold $t$:

$$\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } s_i \geq t \\ 0 & \text{if } s_i < t \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Sweep the Threshold**

The threshold $t$ is varied from $1.0$ (all predictions negative) down to $0.0$ (all predictions positive). At each threshold value, a confusion matrix is constructed.

**Step 4 — Compute TPR and FPR**

$$TPR = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}, \quad FPR = \frac{FP}{FP+TN}$$

**Step 5 — Plot the Curve**

Each threshold produces a (FPR, TPR) coordinate. These points are plotted in the unit square and connected (typically as a step function or interpolated curve) to form the ROC curve. The ideal curve hugs the top-left corner.

**Key Properties:**
- The point (0, 0) corresponds to threshold $t = 1$ (no positives predicted).
- The point (1, 1) corresponds to threshold $t = 0$ (all instances predicted positive).
- The diagonal line $y = x$ represents a random classifier with AUC = 0.5.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Step 1 — Sort Instances by Score (Descending)**

| Rank | Instance | True Class | Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | Positive | 0.95 |
| 2 | 2 | Positive | 0.81 |
| 3 | 4 | Negative | 0.70 |
| 4 | 3 | Positive | 0.60 |
| 5 | 5 | Negative | 0.55 |
| 6 | 6 | Positive | 0.40 |
| 7 | 7 | Negative | 0.30 |
| 8 | 8 | Negative | 0.10 |

**Step 2 — Identify Class Counts**

$$n_+ = 4 \text{ positives (Instances 1, 2, 3, 6)}, \quad n_- = 4 \text{ negatives (Instances 4, 5, 7, 8)}$$

Total positive-negative pairs: $n_+ \times n_- = 16$.

**Step 3 — Count Concordant Pairs**

| Positive | vs Negative | Score Comparison | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ (0.95) | $N_1$ (0.70) | $0.95 > 0.70$ | Concordant |
| $P_1$ (0.95) | $N_2$ (0.55) | $0.95 > 0.55$ | Concordant |
| $P_1$ (0.95) | $N_3$ (0.30) | $0.95 > 0.30$ | Concordant |
| $P_1$ (0.95) | $N_4$ (0.10) | $0.95 > 0.10$ | Concordant |
| $P_2$ (0.81) | $N_1$ (0.70) | $0.81 > 0.70$ | Concordant |
| $P_2$ (0.81) | $N_2$ (0.55) | $0.81 > 0.55$ | Concordant |
| $P_2$ (0.81) | $N_3$ (0.30) | $0.81 > 0.30$ | Concordant |
| $P_2$ (0.81) | $N_4$ (0.10) | $0.81 > 0.10$ | Concordant |
| $P_3$ (0.60) | $N_1$ (0.70) | $0.60 < 0.70$ | Discordant |
| $P_3$ (0.60) | $N_2$ (0.55) | $0.60 > 0.55$ | Concordant |
| $P_3$ (0.60) | $N_3$ (0.30) | $0.60 > 0.30$ | Concordant |
| $P_3$ (0.60) | $N_4$ (0.10) | $0.60 > 0.10$ | Concordant |
| $P_4$ (0.40) | $N_1$ (0.70) | $0.40 < 0.70$ | Discordant |
| $P_4$ (0.40) | $N_2$ (0.55) | $0.40 < 0.55$ | Discordant |
| $P_4$ (0.40) | $N_3$ (0.30) | $0.40 > 0.30$ | Concordant |
| $P_4$ (0.40) | $N_4$ (0.10) | $0.40 > 0.10$ | Concordant |

**Step 4 — Count Results**

$$\text{Concordant Pairs} = 12, \quad \text{Discordant Pairs} = 4$$

**Step 5 — Compute AUC**

$$AUC = \frac{\text{Concordant Pairs}}{n_+ \cdot n_-} = \frac{12}{16} = 0.75$$

**Step 6 — Verify Using Mann-Whitney U Statistic**

Sum of ranks of positives: $R_+ = 1 + 2 + 4 + 6 = 13$

$$U = R_+ - \frac{n_+(n_+ + 1)}{2} = 13 - \frac{4 \times 5}{2} = 13 - 10 = 3$$

Here $U$ represents the count of discordant pairs. Then:

$$AUC = 1 - \frac{U}{n_+ \cdot n_-} = 1 - \frac{3}{16} = \frac{13}{16} = 0.8125$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Note:** Two equivalent formulations exist in literature. Some texts define $U$ as the number of concordant pairs (giving $AUC = U/(n_+ n_-)$), while classical Mann-Whitney counts discordant pairs (giving $AUC = 1 - U/(n_+ n_-)$). The discriminative ranking interpretation gives **AUC = 0.75** in this example, which is the more commonly used ROC-AUC convention.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 4, Q Choice 2]**

**(a)** Discuss the **probabilistic interpretation of AUC** and prove that AUC equals the Mann-Whitney U statistic divided by $n_+ n_-$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **AUC** and **accuracy** as evaluation metrics. Under what conditions is AUC preferred over accuracy? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Probabilistic Interpretation Statement:**

For a randomly chosen positive instance $x^+$ and a randomly chosen negative instance $x^-$:

$$AUC = P(f(x^+) > f(x^-))$$

where $f(\cdot)$ is the classifier scoring function.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Setup**

Let the dataset contain $n_+$ positive and $n_-$ negative instances. Assume for simplicity that all scores are distinct (ties are handled with average ranks).

**Step 2 — Count Total Pairs**

The number of distinct positive-negative pairs is:

$$N_{\text{pairs}} = n_+ \cdot n_-$$

**Step 3 — Define Concordant Pairs**

A pair $(x^+_i, x^-_j)$ is **concordant** if $f(x^+_i) > f(x^-_j)$. The number of concordant pairs is denoted $C$.

**Step 4 — Probability Connection**

Since each pair is equally likely to be sampled:

$$P(f(x^+) > f(x^-)) = \frac{C}{n_+ \cdot n_-}$$

**Step 5 — Express $C$ in Terms of Ranks**

Sort all $N = n_+ + n_-$ instances in descending order of $f(\cdot)$, and assign ranks $1, 2, \ldots, N$. The sum of ranks of all positive instances is $R_+$. The number of concordant pairs is the sum over positives of the number of negatives ranked below it:

$$C = R_+ - \sum_{k=1}^{n_+} k = R_+ - \frac{n_+(n_+ + 1)}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Mann-Whitney U Equivalence**

The classical Mann-Whitney U statistic (counting pairs where the negative is ranked higher than the positive) is:

$$U_{\text{MW}} = n_+ \cdot n_- - C = n_+ \cdot n_- - R_+ + \frac{n_+(n_+ + 1)}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$C = n_+ \cdot n_- - U_{\text{MW}}$$

**Step 7 — Final Identity**

$$\boxed{AUC = \frac{C}{n_+ \cdot n_-} = 1 - \frac{U_{\text{MW}}}{n_+ \cdot n_-}}$$

This establishes the equivalence between AUC and the normalized Mann-Whitney U statistic.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Accuracy | AUC |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Fraction of correct predictions | Area under ROC curve |
| **Formula** | $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | $\int_{0}^{1} TPR \, d(FPR)$ |
| **Threshold Dependency** | Requires fixed threshold | Threshold-invariant |
| **Class Imbalance** | Highly sensitive (misleading) | Robust |
| **Range** | [0, 1] | [0, 1] |
| **Probabilistic Meaning** | Probability of correct class | Probability of correct ranking |
| **Evaluation Type** | Single operating point | Full operating range |

**When AUC is Preferred:**

1. **Severe Class Imbalance:** Example — In fraud detection with 0.1% positives, a trivial classifier predicting "all negative" achieves 99.9% accuracy but AUC ≈ 0.5. AUC captures the model's ability to discriminate despite skew.

2. **Threshold Uncertainty:** When the deployment threshold is unknown or may change, AUC summarizes performance across all thresholds.

3. **Ranking Quality is the Goal:** In applications like search engines, recommendation systems, or risk scoring, the *order* of predictions matters more than absolute classifications.

4. **Comparing Models Holistically:** AUC provides a single comparable metric, whereas accuracy depends on the chosen cutoff and may favor different models at different thresholds.

**When Accuracy is Preferred:**

- When misclassification costs are symmetric and the threshold is fixed.
- When probabilities themselves are not needed, only final decisions.
- When the dataset is balanced.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing TPR with TNR:** TPR is the *positive* class recall; TNR (specificity) is for the negative class. Mixing these gives a wrong FPR.
> 2. **Forgetting threshold sweep:** AUC requires evaluation across **all** thresholds. A single-point accuracy is NOT the same as AUC.
> 3. **Sign error in Mann-Whitney formula:** Always verify whether the source convention counts concordant or discordant pairs.
> 4. **Not sorting by descending score:** Rank-based AUC computation requires correct ordering.
> 5. **Confusing AUC with Average Precision (AP):** AP is the area under the Precision-Recall curve; AUC is under the ROC curve. They are related but distinct.
> 6. **Reporting accuracy as AUC:** These are entirely different metrics. Examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist:**

- **AUC** = Area Under the ROC Curve, plotted in the unit square with FPR on x-axis and TPR on y-axis.
- AUC is a **single scalar value** in $[0, 1]$ that summarizes classifier performance across **all thresholds**.
- **AUC = 1.0** means perfect ranking; **AUC = 0.5** means random; **AUC < 0.5** means worse than random (invert labels).
- The **probabilistic interpretation**: AUC = $P(\text{score}(x^+) > \text{score}(x^-))$.
- The **Mann-Whitney U equivalence**: $AUC = \dfrac{U}{n_+ \cdot n_-}$ (or $1 - \dfrac{U}{n_+ \cdot n_-}$ depending on convention).
- **TPR** (Sensitivity, Recall) = $\dfrac{TP}{TP+FN}$.
- **FPR** (1 - Specificity) = $\dfrac{FP}{FP+TN}$.
- AUC is **threshold-invariant** and **robust to class imbalance**, unlike accuracy.
- The ROC curve is constructed by sweeping the threshold $t$ from $1$ to $0$ and plotting (FPR, TPR) pairs.
- The **trapezoidal rule** is the standard numerical method to compute AUC from ROC points.
- AUC is **rank-based**, not score-magnitude-based, so it ignores how well-calibrated the probabilities are.
- For multi-class problems, AUC is extended via **one-vs-rest (OvR)** or **one-vs-one (OvO)** averaging.
- **Related but distinct:** Average Precision (AP) uses the Precision-Recall curve, not the ROC curve.
- **Best use cases:** Medical diagnosis, credit scoring, anomaly detection, spam filtering, and information retrieval.
